We prove that keeping the sample points safe in the buffered region guarantees collision avoidance for the entire continuous robot surface. The framework is validated on a 7-degree-of-freedom manipulator in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: Global full-body collision avoidance for manipulators via Poisson Safety Functions (PSFs): a single smooth CBF synthesized by solving Poisson's equation on a buffered, Pontryagin-differenced free space, enabling real-time multi-constraint QP safety filter; validated on a 7-DOF manipulator.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional CBF-based safety filters require many constraints to ensure full-body safety, which incurs theoretical complexity and computational burden. A single global safety function can provide scalable guarantees with reduced solver load.
AI.method: 1) Use environmental occupancy to sample the manipulator surface at a prescribed resolution. 2) Shrink free space with a Pontryagin difference according to this resolution to create a buffered domain. 3) Solve Poisson's equation on this domain to obtain a globally smooth safety function (Poisson Safety Function) for the entire environment. 4) Evaluate the safety function at sampled points to yield task-space CBF constraints. 5) Enforce these constraints in real-time via a multi-constraint quadratic program (QP) safety filter. 6) Prove that safety of sample points in the buffered region guarantees collision avoidance for the entire continuous robot surface. 7) Validate on a 7-DOF manipulator in dynamic environments.
AI.result: Derives a single global CBF from the PSF construction, enabling a unified, smooth safety function for the environment. Demonstrates real-time feasibility of enforcing multiple CBF constraints via a QP safety filter and confirms collision avoidance in dynamic scenarios with experiments or simulations on a 7-DOF manipulator.
AI.conclusion: Poisson Safety Function-based full-body safety provides a scalable, smooth, and verifiable collision avoidance framework for manipulators in dynamic environments, reducing the need for numerous per-constraint CBFs while guaranteeing safety through a unified global safety function.

title: Clinically-Informed Modeling for Pediatric Brain Tumor Classification from Whole-Slide Histopathology Images
comment: Accepted at the IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI), 2026
summary: Accurate diagnosis of pediatric brain tumors, starting with histopathology, presents unique challenges for deep learning, including severe data scarcity, class imbalance, and fine-grained morphologic overlap across diagnostically distinct subtypes. While pathology foundation models have advanced patch-level representation learning, their effective adaptation to weakly supervised pediatric brain tumor classification under limited data remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce an expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning framework for pediatric brain tumor diagnosis from whole-slide images (WSI). Our approach integrates contrastive learning into slide-level multiple instance learning (MIL) to explicitly regularize the geometry of slide-level representations during downstream fine-tuning. We propose both a general supervised contrastive setting and an expert-guided variant that incorporates clinically informed hard negatives targeting diagnostically confusable subtypes. Through comprehensive experiments on pediatric brain tumor WSI classification under realistic low-sample and class-imbalanced conditions, we demonstrate that contrastive fine-tuning yields measurable improvements in fine-grained diagnostic distinctions. Our experimental analyses reveal complementary strengths across different contrastive strategies, with expert-guided hard negatives promoting more compact intra-class representations and improved inter-class separation. This work highlights the importance of explicitly shaping slide-level representations for robust fine-grained classification in data-scarce pediatric pathology settings.

AI.tldr: Expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning for pediatric brain tumor WSI classification using slide-level MIL; improves fine-grained accuracy under data scarcity and imbalance.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity and class-imbalance in pediatric pathology; need robust slide-level representations during weakly supervised learning.
AI.method: Combine contrastive learning with slide-level MIL for WSIs. Provide two settings: general supervised contrastive; expert-guided variant with clinically informed hard negatives for confusable subtypes; regularize slide-level representation geometry during fine-tuning.
AI.result: In low-sample, imbalanced scenarios, contrastive fine-tuning yields measurable gains in fine-grained distinctions. Expert-guided hard negatives produce more compact intra-class clusters and better inter-class separation; complementary strengths observed across contrastive strategies.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly shaping slide-level representations via contrastive objectives within MIL improves robustness of fine-grained pediatric brain tumor classification under data scarcity; suggests integrating expert knowledge with foundation-model-inspired representations for pathology tasks.

title: Adaptive Test-Time Compute Allocation with Evolving In-Context Demonstrations
summary: While scaling test-time compute can substantially improve model performance, existing approaches either rely on static compute allocation or sample from fixed generation distributions. In this work, we introduce a test-time compute allocation framework that jointly adapts where computation is spent and how generation is performed. Our method begins with a warm-up phase that identifies easy queries and assembles an initial pool of question-response pairs from the test set itself. An adaptive phase then concentrates further computation on unresolved queries while reshaping their generation distributions through evolving in-context demonstrations -- conditioning each generation on successful responses from semantically related queries rather than resampling from a fixed distribution. Experiments across math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines while consuming substantially less inference-time compute.

AI.tldr: A two-phase test-time compute framework that jointly allocates compute and adapts generation, using a warm-up phase to form an initial pool of QA pairs from the test set and an adaptive phase that concentrates on unresolved queries while evolving in-context demonstrations from semantically related successes, yielding better performance with less inference compute across math, coding, and reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies of static compute allocations and fixed-generation distributions by enabling dynamic, content-aware test-time computation that can steer both where to spend compute and how to generate responses.
AI.method: Two-phase approach: (1) warm-up phase identifies easy queries and builds an initial pool of question–response pairs from the test data; (2) adaptive phase focuses additional computation on unresolved queries and reshapes generation through evolving in-context demonstrations, conditioning on successful responses from semantically related queries instead of resampling from a fixed distribution.
AI.result: Consistent improvement over strong baselines across math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks, with substantial reductions in inference-time compute.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing test-time compute allocation and generation strategy with dynamic in-context demonstrations is effective and generalizes across domains, outperforming static or fixed-distribution baselines while reducing computational cost.

title: Forget, Then Recall: Learnable Compression and Selective Unfolding via Gist Sparse Attention
summary: Scaling large language models to long contexts is challenging due to the quadratic computational cost of full attention. Mitigation approaches include KV-cache selection or compression techniques. We instead provide an effective and end-to-end learnable bridge between the two without requiring architecture modification. In particular, our key insight is that interleaved gist compression tokens -- which provide a learnable summary of sets of raw tokens -- can serve as routing signals for sparse attention. Building on this, we introduce selective unfolding via GSA, which first compresses the context into gist tokens, then selects the most relevant gists, and subsequently restores the corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. This yields a simple coarse-to-fine mechanism that combines compact global representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence. We further incorporate this process directly into training in an end-to-end fashion, avoiding the need for external retrieval modules. In addition, we extend the framework hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction, enabling multi-resolution context access with logarithmic per-step decoding complexity. Empirical results on LongBench and RAG benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms other compression baselines as well as inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from $8\times$ to $32\times$. The code is available at: https://github.com/yuzhenmao/gist-sparse-attention/

AI.tldr: A learnable gist-based sparse attention mechanism (GSA) compresses long contexts into gist tokens, selects relevant gists, and expands back to raw chunks, enabling 8–32× context compression with end-to-end training and hierarchical gist-of-gist for multi-resolution access, outperforming baselines on LongBench and RAG.
AI.motivation: Scaling LLMs to long contexts is limited by quadratic full-attention; need an end-to-end, architecture-agnostic approach that provides efficient long-range routing without external retrieval modules.
AI.method: Introduce interleaved gist compression tokens as learnable summaries that route sparse attention. Develop selective unfolding via GSA: (1) compress context into gist tokens, (2) select the most relevant gists, (3) restore corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. Train end-to-end without external retrieval. Extend the framework with recursive gist-of-gist for multi-resolution access, achieving logarithmic per-step decoding complexity.
AI.result: Empirical results on LongBench and RAG show consistent outperformance of compression baselines and inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from 8× to 32×.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end, gist-driven sparse attention enables scalable long-context models with coarse-to-fine retrieval and hierarchical multi-resolution access, offering substantial efficiency gains without architecture changes; code is released.

title: How VLAs (Really) Work In Open-World Environments
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) have been extensively used in robotics applications, achieving great success in various manipulation problems. More recently, VLAs have been used in long-horizon tasks and evaluated on benchmarks, such as BEHAVIOR1K (B1K), for solving complex household chores. The common metric for measuring progress in such benchmarks is success rate or partial score based on satisfaction of progress-agnostic criteria, meaning only the final states of the objects are considered, regardless of the events that lead to such states. In this paper, we argue that using such evaluation protocols say little about safety aspects of operation and can potentially exaggerate reported performance, undermining core challenges for future real-world deployment. To this end, we conduct a thorough analysis of state-of-the-art models on the B1K Challenge and evaluate policies in terms of robustness via reproducibility and consistency of performance, safety aspects of policies operations, task awareness, and key elements leading to the incompletion of tasks. We then propose evaluation protocols to capture safety violations to better measure the true performance of the policies in more complex and interactive scenarios. At the end, we discuss the limitations of the existing VLAs and motivate future research.

AI.tldr: Critiques success-only metrics of vision-language-action models on BEHAVIOR1K and proposes safety-aware evaluation protocols to better assess robustness and real-world deployability.
AI.motivation: Current final-state success criteria can mask safety issues and process-level failures; robust, reproducible, and safe operation is essential for real-world deployment of VLAs in interactive tasks.
AI.method: Analyze state-of-the-art VLAs using the BEHAVIOR1K benchmark, evaluating robustness (reproducibility and consistency), safety of policies, task awareness, and factors behind task incompletion; propose evaluation protocols that explicitly capture safety violations; discuss limitations and future directions.
AI.result: The study reveals that existing metrics may overstate performance and overlook safety concerns; the proposed protocols aim to reveal safety violations and provide a more reliable assessment of policy performance in complex, interactive scenarios; limitations of current VLAs and evaluation practices are discussed with guidance for future work.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation of VLAs should incorporate safety- and interaction-aware protocols to reflect true performance and readiness for real-world use; addressing the identified limitations is needed to advance safe, reliable long-horizon manipulation.

title: Optimizing Diffusion Priors with a Single Observation
summary: While diffusion priors generate high-quality posterior samples across many inverse problems, they are often trained on limited training sets or purely simulated data, thus inheriting the errors and biases of these underlying sources. Current approaches to finetuning diffusion models rely on a large number of observations with varying forward operators, which can be difficult to collect for many applications, and thus lead to overfitting when the measurement set is small. We propose a method for tuning a prior from only a single observation by combining existing diffusion priors into a single product-of-experts prior and identifying the exponents that maximize the Bayesian evidence. We validate our method on real-world inverse problems, including black hole imaging, where the true prior is unknown a priori, and image deblurring with text-conditioned priors. We find that the evidence is often maximized by priors that extend beyond those trained on a single dataset. By generalizing the prior through exponent weighting, our approach enables posterior sampling from both tempered and combined diffusion models, yielding more flexible priors that improve the trustworthiness of the resulting posterior image distribution.

AI.tldr: A product-of-experts diffusion-prior framework that uses exponent weights optimized to maximize Bayesian evidence to tune from a single observation, yielding more flexible and trustworthy posteriors across inverse problems.
AI.motivation: Diffusion priors trained on limited or biased data inherit errors; fine-tuning typically requires many observations and diverse forward operators, which is often impractical. A single-observation tuning method aims to reduce overfitting and data collection burden.
AI.method: Form a prior as a product of existing diffusion priors with exponent weights. Optimize exponents by maximizing Bayesian evidence given one observation. Enable sampling from tempered/combined priors and validate on real inverse problems (e.g., black hole imaging, text-conditioned deblurring).
AI.result: Evidence-maximizing exponents tend to favor priors beyond those trained on a single dataset, improving the trustworthiness of the posterior image distribution and enabling flexible posterior sampling.
AI.conclusion: Exponent-weighted product-of-experts priors generalize diffusion priors from limited data, enabling robust and adaptable posterior inference across problems by tempering and combining priors to avoid overfitting and improve posterior quality.

title: HypEHR: Hyperbolic Modeling of Electronic Health Records for Efficient Question Answering
summary: Electronic health record (EHR) question answering is often handled by LLM-based pipelines that are costly to deploy and do not explicitly leverage the hierarchical structure of clinical data. Motivated by evidence that medical ontologies and patient trajectories exhibit hyperbolic geometry, we propose HypEHR, a compact Lorentzian model that embeds codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic space and answers queries via geometry-consistent cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads. HypEHR is pretrained with next-visit diagnosis prediction and hierarchy-aware regularization to align representations with the ICD ontology. On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yuyuliu11037/HypEHR.

AI.tldr: HypEHR is a compact Lorentzian hyperbolic model for EHR QA that embeds codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic space and uses geometry-aware cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads; it pretrains on next-visit diagnosis and applies hierarchy-aware regularization to align with ICD, achieving competitive results with far fewer parameters on MIMIC-IV EHR-QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: EHR QA pipelines typically rely on costly large language models and fail to exploit the inherent hierarchical structure of clinical data. Hyperbolic geometry is well-suited to capture hierarchies such as the ICD ontology and patient trajectories.
AI.method: HypEHR embeds codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic (Lorentzian) space and performs geometry-consistent cross-attention using type-specific pointer heads. It is pretrained with a next-visit diagnosis prediction objective and includes hierarchy-aware regularization to align representations with the ICD ontology.
AI.result: On two MIMIC-IV–based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches the performance of LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic geometry and hierarchy-aware pretraining yield effective, parameter-efficient EHR-QA, highlighting the benefit of leveraging clinical hierarchies and trajectories over purely large-language-model approaches.

title: Validating a Deep Learning Algorithm to Identify Patients with Glaucoma using Systemic Electronic Health Records
summary: We evaluated whether a glaucoma risk assessment (GRA) model trained on All of Us national data can identify patients at high probability of glaucoma using only systemic electronic health records (EHR) at an independent institution. In this cross-sectional study, 20,636 Stanford patients seen from November 2013 to January 2024 were included (15% with glaucoma). A pretrained GRA model was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort and tested on a held-out set using demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, laboratory results, and physical examination measurements as inputs. The best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Calibration was consistent with clinical risk: the highest prediction decile showed the greatest glaucoma diagnosis rate (65.7%) and treatment rate (57.0%). Performance improved with more trainable layers up to 15 and with additional data. An EHR-only GRA model may enable scalable and accessible pre-screening without specialized imaging.

AI.tldr: A glaucoma risk assessment (GRA) model pretrained on All of Us data and fine-tuned on Stanford EHRs can identify glaucoma with AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657 on a held-out cohort; top decile risk aligns with higher glaucoma and treatment rates, suggesting EHR-only screening is feasible.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable pre-screening for glaucoma using routinely collected systemic EHR data without imaging.
AI.method: Cross-sectional study; 20,636 Stanford patients (2013-2024); 15% with glaucoma. A pretrained GRA model from All of Us was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort and tested on a held-out set using inputs: demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, laboratory results, and physical examination measurements. Best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Calibration consistent with clinical risk; highest prediction decile glaucoma diagnosis rate 65.7% and treatment rate 57.0%. Performance improved with up to 15 trainable layers and with more data.
AI.result: AUROC 0.883; PPV 0.657. Calibration aligns with risk, with higher incidence in top decile. More trainable layers up to 15 improved performance; more data further improved.
AI.conclusion: An EHR-only glaucoma risk assessment model may enable scalable, accessible pre-screening without the need for specialized imaging.

title: CorridorVLA: Explicit Spatial Constraints for Generative Action Heads via Sparse Anchors
summary: Vision--Language--Action (VLA) models often use intermediate representations to connect multimodal inputs with continuous control, yet spatial guidance is often injected implicitly through latent features. We propose $CorridorVLA$, which predicts sparse spatial anchors as incremental physical changes (e.g., $Δ$-positions) and uses them to impose an explicit tolerance region in the training objective for action generation. The anchors define a corridor that guides a flow-matching action head: trajectories whose implied spatial evolution falls outside it receive corrective gradients, while minor deviations from contacts and execution noise are permitted. On the more challenging LIBERO-Plus benchmark, CorridorVLA yields consistent gains across both SmolVLA and GR00T, improving success rate by $3.4\%$--$12.4\%$ over the corresponding baselines; notably, our GR00T-Corr variant reaches a success rate of $83.21\%$. These results indicate that action-aligned physical cues can provide direct and interpretable constraints for generative action policies, complementing spatial guidance encoded in visual or latent forms. Code is available at https://github.com/corridorVLA.

AI.tldr: CorridorVLA introduces sparse, incremental spatial anchors to carve an explicit tolerance corridor for action generation in vision-language-action models, improving success rates on LIBERO-Plus across SmolVLA and GR00T baselines, with a GR00T-Corr variant achieving 83.21% success.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of explicit spatial guidance in VLA models, which typically rely on latent features to guide control. The goal is to provide interpretable, action-aligned cues that directly constrain the generated trajectories.
AI.method: Predict sparse spatial anchors as Δ-positions to define a corridor (tolerance region). Use these anchors in a flow-matching action head so that trajectories outside the corridor receive corrective gradients; allow small deviations due to contacts and execution noise. Evaluate on LIBERO-Plus against SmolVLA and GR00T baselines; report improvements in success rate.
AI.result: On LIBERO-Plus, CorridorVLA yields consistent gains across both SmolVLA and GR00T, with improvements of 3.4%–12.4% in success rate. The GR00T-Corr variant reaches 83.21% success.
AI.conclusion: Action-aligned physical cues can provide direct, interpretable constraints for generative action policies, complementing existing spatial guidance encoded in visuals or latent representations. The approach is implementable with public code.

title: Foveated Reasoning: Stateful, Action-based Visual Focusing for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models benefit from high-resolution images, but the increase in visual-token count incurs high compute overhead. Humans resolve this tension via foveation: a coarse view guides "where to look", while selectively acquired high-acuity evidence refines "what to think". We introduce Foveated Reasoner, an autoregressive vision-language framework that unifies foveation and reasoning within a single decoding trajectory. Starting from a low-resolution view, the model triggers foveation only when needed, retrieves high-resolution evidence from selected regions, and injects it back into the same decoding trajectory. We train the method with a two-stage pipeline: coldstart supervision to bootstrap foveation behavior, followed by reinforcement learning to jointly improve evidence acquisition and task accuracy while discouraging trivial "see-everything" solutions. Experiments show that the method learns effective foveation policies and achieves stronger accuracy under tight visual-token budgets across multiple vision-language benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A foveated, autoregressive vision-language model that uses dynamic, region-based high-resolution evidence within a single decoding trajectory, trained with coldstart supervision and reinforcement learning, achieving better accuracy under limited visual-token budgets.
AI.motivation: High-resolution images boost V-L tasks but incur high compute due to many tokens. Humans use foveation to focus resources; the paper aims to replicate this efficiency at inference time without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Unifies foveation and reasoning in one autoregressive decoding path. Start from a coarse image, trigger foveation as needed, fetch high-res evidence from selected regions, and inject it back into the same decoding trajectory. Two-stage training: coldstart supervision to bootstrap foveation policies, followed by reinforcement learning to improve evidence acquisition and task accuracy while discouraging fully dense observation.
AI.result: The model learns effective foveation policies and achieves higher accuracy under tight visual-token budgets across multiple vision-language benchmarks, outperforming baselines that do not use dynamic foveation.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, selective high-resolution evidence integrated into a single decoding process yields efficiency and accuracy gains in vision-language tasks; RL-based optimization mitigates trivial policy shortcuts and enables budget-aware inference.

title: Who Defines Fairness? Target-Based Prompting for Demographic Representation in Generative Models
summary: Text-to-image(T2I) models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E have made generative AI widely accessible, yet recent studies reveal that these systems often replicate societal biases, particularly in how they depict demographic groups across professions. Prompts such as 'doctor' or 'CEO' frequently yield lighter-skinned outputs, while lower-status roles like 'janitor' show more diversity, reinforcing stereotypes. Existing mitigation methods typically require retraining or curated datasets, making them inaccessible to most users. We propose a lightweight, inference-time framework that mitigates representational bias through prompt-level intervention without modifying the underlying model. Instead of assuming a single definition of fairness, our approach allows users to select among multiple fairness specifications-ranging from simple choices such as a uniform distribution to more complex definitions informed by a large language model(LLM) that cites sources and provides confidence estimates. These distributions guide the construction of demographic specific prompt variants in the corresponding proportions, and we evaluate alignment by auditing adherence to the declared target and measuring the resulting skin tone distribution rather than assuming uniformity as 'fairness'. Across 36 prompts spanning 30 occupations and 6 non-occupational contexts, our method shifts observed skin-tone outcomes in directions consistent with the declared target, and reduces deviation from targets when the target is defined directly in skin-tone space(fallback). This work demonstrates how fairness interventions can be made transparent, controllable, and usable at inference time, directly empowering users of generative AI.

AI.tldr: An inference-time, prompt-level fairness intervention for text-to-image models that uses user-selected fairness specifications to generate demographic-specific prompt variants in given proportions, auditing against declared targets and skin-tone distributions; demonstrates bias mitigation across 36 prompts and 30 occupations across 6 contexts without retraining.
AI.motivation: To make bias mitigation in T2I models accessible without retraining, by giving users transparent, controllable mechanisms to specify fairness targets and audit outcomes.
AI.method: At inference, construct demographic-specific prompt variants according to a user-chosen target distribution (uniform or LLM-informed with citations). Combine variants in corresponding proportions to form inputs for the base model without modifying it; audit alignment by comparing outputs to the declared target and by measuring the skin-tone distribution.
AI.result: The approach yields alignment with the declared targets and reduces deviation from targets, across 36 prompts spanning 30 occupations and 6 non-occupational contexts, when targets are defined in skin-tone space (fallback) or other spaces.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time, prompt-level fairness control can be effective, transparent, and usable, empowering users to influence the fairness of generative AI outputs without retraining.

title: ILDR: Geometric Early Detection of Grokking
summary: Grokking describes a delayed generalization phenomenon in which a neural network achieves perfect training accuracy long before validation accuracy improves, followed by an abrupt transition to strong generalization. Existing detection signals are indirect: weight norm reflects parameter-space regularization and consistently lags the transition, while GrokFast's slow gradient EMA, used without gradient amplification, is unstable across seeds with standard deviation exceeding mean lead time. We propose the Inter/Intra-class Distance Ratio (ILDR), a geometric metric computed on second-to-last layer representations as the ratio of inter-class centroid separation to intra-class scatter. ILDR provides an early detection signal: it rises and crosses a threshold at 2.5 times its baseline before the grokking transition appears in validation accuracy, indicating early geometric reorganization in representation space. Grounded in Fisher's linear discriminant criterion, ILDR requires no eigendecomposition and runs in O(|C|^2 + N). It is evaluated exclusively on held-out data, making it robust to memorization effects. Across modular arithmetic and permutation group composition (S5), ILDR leads the grokking transition by 9 to 73 percent of the training budget, with lead time increasing with task algebraic complexity. Over eight random seeds, ILDR leads by 950 +/- 250 steps with a coefficient of variation of 26 percent, and post-grokking variance drops by 1696 times, consistent with a sharp phase transition in representation space. Using ILDR as an early stopping trigger reduces training by 18.6 percent on average. Optimizer interventions triggered at the ILDR threshold demonstrate bidirectional control over the transition, suggesting ILDR tracks representational conditions underlying generalization rather than a downstream correlate.

AI.tldr: ILDR is a geometry-based early signal that predicts grokking by monitoring second-to-last layer representations; it anticipates the transition by crossing a threshold well before validation accuracy improves.
AI.motivation: Grokking shows delayed generalization with abrupt transition; existing signals are indirect and unreliable; there is need for a robust, efficient early predictor that reflects representation-space reorganization and is robust to memorization.
AI.method: Compute ILDR = inter-class centroid distance divided by intra-class scatter on second-to-last layer representations, on held-out data; no eigendecomposition; compute as O(|C|^2 + N); threshold at 2.5 baseline; test across modular arithmetic and S5; analyze seeds; measure lead time and variance; use as early stopping and as trigger for optimizer interventions.
AI.result: ILDR crosses threshold ~2.5x baseline before grokking; leads the transition by 9-73% of training budget; lead time 950±250 steps; CV 26%; post-grokking variance drops by 1696x; early stopping reduces training by 18.6%; optimizer-based interventions can bidirectionally control the transition.
AI.conclusion: ILDR captures representational conditions underlying generalization, offers a robust, efficient, and controllable early predictor for grokking; it improves training efficiency and provides insight into the geometry of representation space.

title: Reasoning About Traversability: Language-Guided Off-Road 3D Trajectory Planning
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) enable high-level semantic reasoning for end-to-end autonomous driving, particularly in unstructured environments, existing off-road datasets suffer from language annotations that are weakly aligned with vehicle actions and terrain geometry. To address this misalignment, we propose a language refinement framework that restructures annotations into action-aligned pairs, enabling a VLM to generate refined scene descriptions and 3D future trajectories directly from a single image. To further encourage terrain-aware planning, we introduce a preference optimization strategy that constructs geometry-aware hard negatives and explicitly penalizes trajectories inconsistent with local elevation profiles. Furthermore, we propose off-road-specific metrics to quantify traversability compliance and elevation consistency, addressing the limitations of conventional on-road evaluation. Experiments on the ORAD-3D benchmark demonstrate that our approach reduces average trajectory error from 1.01m to 0.97m, improves traversability compliance from 0.621 to 0.644, and decreases elevation inconsistency from 0.428 to 0.322, highlighting the efficacy of action-aligned supervision and terrain-aware optimization for robust off-road driving.

AI.tldr: Action-aligned language refinement and terrain-aware optimization improve off-road VLM-based planning by aligning annotations with actions and elevation, yielding better trajectory accuracy, traversability, and elevation consistency.
AI.motivation: Off-road datasets have language annotations weakly aligned with vehicle actions and terrain geometry, hindering robust VLM-based planning in unstructured environments.
AI.method: Refine annotations into action-aligned pairs; use a preference optimization to create geometry-aware hard negatives; penalize trajectories violating local elevation profiles; introduce off-road-specific metrics for traversability and elevation consistency.
AI.result: On ORAD-3D, average trajectory error reduced from 1.01m to 0.97m; traversability compliance improved from 0.621 to 0.644; elevation inconsistency decreased from 0.428 to 0.322.
AI.conclusion: Action-aligned supervision and terrain-aware optimization effectively boost robustness of VLM-driven off-road planning, with tailored evaluation metrics addressing on-road biases.

title: Leveraging Multimodal LLMs for Built Environment and Housing Attribute Assessment from Street-View Imagery
summary: We present a novel framework for automatically evaluating building conditions nationwide in the United States by leveraging large language models (LLMs) and Google Street View (GSV) imagery. By fine-tuning Gemma 3 27B on a modest human-labeled dataset, our approach achieves strong alignment with human mean opinion scores (MOS), outperforming even individual raters on SRCC and PLCC relative to the MOS benchmark. To enhance efficiency, we apply knowledge distillation, transferring the capabilities of Gemma 3 27B to a smaller Gemma 3 4B model that achieves comparable performance with a 3x speedup. Further, we distill the knowledge into a CNN-based model (EfficientNetV2-M) and a transformer (SwinV2-B), delivering close performance while achieving a 30x speed gain. Furthermore, we investigate LLMs' capabilities for assessing an extensive list of built environment and housing attributes through a human-AI alignment study and develop a visualization dashboard that integrates LLM assessment outcomes for downstream analysis by homeowners. Our framework offers a flexible and efficient solution for large-scale building condition assessment, enabling high accuracy with minimal human labeling effort.

AI.tldr: A framework using LLMs and Google Street View for nationwide building-condition assessment. Fine-tunes Gemma 3 27B on a labeled dataset, aligns with human MOS, and uses knowledge distillation to smaller models (Gemma 3 4B) and then to CNN/Transformer backbones (EfficientNetV2-M, SwinV2-B) for speed gains. Also studies broad built-environment attributes via human-AI alignment and provides a visualization dashboard for homeowners. Claims scalable, accurate results with minimal labeling.
AI.motivation: Address the need for scalable, consistent, and cost-effective assessment of building conditions across the United States by leveraging public imagery and LLMs to approximate human judgments with minimal labeling.
AI.method: Fine-tune Gemma 3 27B on a modest labeled dataset to predict building-condition qualities aligned with MOS. Evaluate using SRCC and PLCC against MOS. Apply knowledge distillation to transfer to a smaller Gemma 3 4B model (3x speedup). Further distill into a CNN (EfficientNetV2-M) and a transformer (SwinV2-B) to achieve comparable accuracy with ~30x speedup. Conduct a human-AI alignment study for assessing a broad set of built-environment attributes. Develop a visualization dashboard integrating LLM assessments for downstream homeowner analysis.
AI.result: The 27B model achieves strong alignment with MOS, outperforming individual raters on SRCC and PLCC. The 4B model retains similar performance with a 3x speedup. Distilled models (EfficientNetV2-M and SwinV2-B) closely match performance with a 30x speedup. Expanded evaluation across a wide set of attributes via a human-AI alignment study. A visualization dashboard is created for downstream homeowner use.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a flexible, efficient, and scalable approach for large-scale building-condition assessment with high accuracy and low labeling effort.

title: Active Data
summary: In some complex domains, certain problem-specific decompositions can provide advantages over monolithic designs by enabling comprehension and specification of the design. In this paper we present an intuitive and tractable approach to reasoning over large and complex data sets. Our approach is based on Active Data, i.e., data as atomic objects that actively interact with environments. We describe our intuition about how this bottom-up approach improves designs confronting computational and conceptual complexity. We describe an implementation of the base Active Data concepts within the air traffic flow management domain and discuss performance for this implementation.

AI.tldr: Bottom-up Active Data approach to manage complexity by treating data as active atomic objects; demonstrated in air traffic flow management with a feasibility/performance discussion.
AI.motivation: Address the difficulty of reasoning about large, complex data sets and problem-specific decompositions; propose a bottom-up, active-data paradigm to improve design comprehension and specification.
AI.method: Introduce Active Data as atomic objects that actively interact with environments; describe intuition, and implement base Active Data concepts in air traffic flow management; discuss performance of the implementation.
AI.result: Provides performance discussion for the implemented Active Data approach; no quantitative metrics stated in abstract, but demonstrates feasibility and potential advantages in managing complexity.
AI.conclusion: Bottom-up Active Data approach can improve designs facing computational and conceptual complexity; domain-specific implementation in air traffic flow management substantiates the concept.

title: Clinically Interpretable Sepsis Early Warning via LLM-Guided Simulation of Temporal Physiological Dynamics
summary: Timely and interpretable early warning of sepsis remains a major clinical challenge due to the complex temporal dynamics of physiological deterioration. Traditional data-driven models often provide accurate yet opaque predictions, limiting physicians' confidence and clinical applicability. To address this limitation, we propose a Large Language Model (LLM)-guided temporal simulation framework that explicitly models physiological trajectories prior to disease onset for clinically interpretable prediction. The framework consists of a spatiotemporal feature extraction module that captures dynamic dependencies among multivariate vital signs, a Medical Prompt-as-Prefix module that embeds clinical reasoning cues into LLMs, and an agent-based post-processing component that constrains predictions within physiologically plausible ranges. By first simulating the evolution of key physiological indicators and then classifying sepsis onset, our model offers transparent prediction mechanisms that align with clinical judgment. Evaluated on the MIMIC-IV and eICU databases, the proposed method achieves superior AUC scores (0.861-0.903) across 24-4-hour pre-onset prediction tasks, outperforming conventional deep learning and rule-based approaches. More importantly, it provides interpretable trajectories and risk trends that can assist clinicians in early intervention and personalized decision-making in intensive care environments.

AI.tldr: LLM-guided temporal simulation framework for interpretable sepsis prediction using simulated physiological trajectories, with spatiotemporal features, clinical-reasoning prompts, and agent-based plausibility checks; achieves AUC 0.861-0.903 for 24-4h pre-onset predictions on MIMIC-IV/eICU, outperforming DL and rule-based methods.
AI.motivation: Sepsis onset is governed by complex, dynamic physiological changes. Traditional models are often accurate but opaque, limiting clinical trust and adoption. There is a need for interpretable, trajectory-based predictions aligned with clinical reasoning.
AI.method: A three-part framework: (1) spatiotemporal feature extraction to capture dynamic dependencies among multivariate vitals; (2) Medical Prompt-as-Prefix (MPP) module that injects clinical reasoning cues into a large language model to guide interpretation and forecasting; (3) agent-based post-processing to constrain predictions within physiologically plausible bounds. The system first simulates the evolution of key physiological indicators leading up to onset, then performs sepsis onset classification, yielding interpretable trajectories and risk trends.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MIMIC-IV and eICU datasets shows superior AUC scores ranging from 0.861 to 0.903 for 24 to 4 hours pre-onset prediction windows, outperforming conventional deep learning and rule-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides transparent, trajectory-based predictions that align with clinical judgment, potentially aiding early intervention and personalized decision-making in ICUs.

title: FingerViP: Learning Real-World Dexterous Manipulation with Fingertip Visual Perception
summary: The current practice of dexterous manipulation generally relies on a single wrist-mounted view, which is often occluded and limits performance on tasks requiring multi-view perception. In this work, we present FingerViP, a learning system that utilizes a visuomotor policy with fingertip visual perception for dexterous manipulation. Specifically, we design a vision-enhanced fingertip module with an embedded miniature camera and install the modules on each finger of a multi-fingered hand. The fingertip cameras substantially improve visual perception by providing comprehensive, multi-view feedback of both the hand and its surrounding environment. Building on the integrated fingertip modules, we develop a diffusion-based whole-body visuomotor policy conditioned on a third-view camera and multi-view fingertip vision, which effectively learns complex manipulation skills directly from human demonstrations. To improve view-proprioception alignment and contact awareness, each fingertip visual feature is augmented with its corresponding camera pose encoding and per-finger joint-current encoding. We validate the effectiveness of the multi-view fingertip vision and demonstrate the robustness and adaptability of FingerViP on various challenging real-world tasks, including pressing buttons inside a confined box, retrieving sticks from an unstable support, retrieving objects behind an occluding curtain, and performing long-horizon cabinet opening and object retrieval, achieving an overall success rate of 80.8%. All hardware designs and code will be fully open-sourced.

AI.tldr: FingerViP introduces fingertip cameras on each finger and a diffusion-based whole-body visuomotor policy that fuses third-view and fingertip vision to achieve robust dexterous manipulation (80.8% success) with open-source hardware and code.
AI.motivation: Current dexterous manipulation relies on a single wrist-mounted view, which is often occluded, limiting perception and performance for tasks requiring multi-view perception.
AI.method: Design a vision-enhanced fingertip module with embedded miniature cameras on each finger; fuse multi-view fingertip vision with a third-view camera in a diffusion-based whole-body visuomotor policy trained from human demonstrations; augment fingertip features with camera pose encoding and per-finger joint-current encoding.
AI.result: Validation shows effectiveness of multi-view fingertip vision, with robust and adaptable manipulation across diverse tasks including pressing buttons in a box, retrieving sticks from unstable support, retrieving objects behind occlusion, and long-horizon cabinet opening; overall success rate 80.8%.
AI.conclusion: Hardware designs and code will be fully open-sourced, demonstrating viability of fingertip-vision augmented dexterous manipulation and enabling broader adoption and extension.

title: Pretrain Where? Investigating How Pretraining Data Diversity Impacts Geospatial Foundation Model Performance
summary: New geospatial foundation models introduce a new model architecture and pretraining dataset, often sampled using different notions of data diversity. Performance differences are largely attributed to the model architecture or input modalities, while the role of the pretraining dataset is rarely studied. To address this research gap, we conducted a systematic study on how the geographic composition of pretraining data affects a model's downstream performance. We created global and per-continent pretraining datasets and evaluated them on global and per-continent downstream datasets. We found that the pretraining dataset from Europe outperformed global and continent-specific pretraining datasets on both global and local downstream evaluations. To investigate the factors influencing a pretraining dataset's downstream performance, we analysed 10 pretraining datasets using diversity across continents, biomes, landcover and spectral values. We found that only spectral diversity was strongly correlated with performance, while others were weakly correlated. This finding establishes a new dimension of diversity to be accounted for when creating a high-performing pretraining dataset. We open-sourced 7 new pretraining datasets, pretrained models, and our experimental framework at https://github.com/kerner-lab/pretrain-where.

AI.tldr: Pretraining data geography matters: Europe-origin data yields better downstream performance than global or continent-tuned data; spectral diversity is the strongest predictor of performance; authors release 7 datasets and models and an experimental framework.
AI.motivation: There is limited understanding of how the geographic composition of pretraining data influences downstream performance in geospatial foundation models. Prior work emphasizes model architecture and modalities; a systematic study isolating pretraining data effects is needed.
AI.method: Constructed global and per-continent pretraining datasets and evaluated them on global and per-continent downstream datasets. Analyzed 10 pretraining datasets across diversity factors—continents, biomes, landcover, and spectral values. Open-sourced 7 pretraining datasets, pretrained models, and experimental framework at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.result: The Europe pretraining dataset outperformed global and continent-specific pretraining datasets on both global and local downstream evaluations. Among diversity factors, spectral diversity showed a strong correlation with downstream performance, while diversity in continents, biomes, and landcover were weakly correlated. The work releases 7 new pretraining datasets, pretrained models, and an experimental framework.
AI.conclusion: Spectral diversity is a key, underappreciated dimension of pretraining dataset design for geospatial foundation models. When constructing pretraining data, spectral diversity should be prioritized to enhance downstream performance; several open datasets and models are made available.

title: InVitroVision: a Multi-Modal AI Model for Automated Description of Embryo Development using Natural Language
summary: The application of artificial intelligence (AI) in IVF has shown promise in improving consistency and standardization of decisions, but often relies on annotated data and does not make use of the multimodal nature of IVF data. We investigated whether foundational vision-language models can be fine-tuned to predict natural language descriptions of embryo morphology and development. Using a publicly available embryo time-lapse dataset, we fine-tuned PaliGemma-2, a multi-modal vision-language model, with only 1,000 images and corresponding captions, describing embryo morphology, embryonic cell cycle and developmental stage. Our results show that the fine-tuned model, InVitroVision, outperformed a commercial model, ChatGPT 5.2, and base models in overall metrics, with performance improving with larger training datasets. This study demonstrates the potential of foundational vision-language models to generalize to IVF tasks with limited data, enabling the prediction of natural language descriptions of embryo morphology and development. This approach may facilitate the use of large language models to retrieve information and scientific evidence from relevant publications and guidelines, and has implications for few-shot adaptation to multiple downstream tasks in IVF.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning a vision-language model on a small embryo time-lapse dataset enables InVitroVision to generate natural-language descriptions of embryo morphology and development, outperforming a commercial model and base models, with gains scaling with training data.
AI.motivation: Leverage multimodal foundations to describe IVF-relevant morphology in natural language, reducing reliance on large labeled datasets and enabling retrieval of evidence from publications using LLMs.
AI.method: Fine-tuned PaliGemma-2, a multi-modal vision-language model, on 1,000 image-caption pairs from a publicly available embryo time-lapse dataset describing morphology, cell cycle, and developmental stage; evaluated against ChatGPT 5.2 and base models using standard metrics; observed performance improvements with increased data.
AI.result: InVitroVision outperformed a commercial model and base models on overall metrics; performance improved as the training data size increased.
AI.conclusion: Foundational vision-language models can generalize to IVF tasks with limited data, enabling natural-language descriptions of embryo morphology and development; adds potential for LLM-assisted retrieval of evidence and supports few-shot adaptation to related IVF tasks.

title: Unsupervised Learning of Inter-Object Relationships via Group Homomorphism
summary: While current deep learning models achieve high performance by learning statistical correlations from vast datasets,which stands in stark contrast to human learning. They lack the flexibility of humans-particularly preverbal infants-to autonomously acquire the underlying structure of the world from limited experience and adapt to novel situations. In this study, we propose an unsupervised representation learning method based on a hierarchical relationship in group operations, rather than statistical independence, aiming to build a computational model of the cognitive development of infants. The proposed model features an integrated architecture that simultaneously performs object segmentation and the extraction of motion laws from dynamic image sequences. By introducing the Homomorphism from algebra as a structural constraint within a neural network, the model structurally separates pixel-level changes into meaningful, decomposed transformation components, such as translation and deformation. Using interaction scenes (chasing and evading tasks) based on developmental science findings, we experimentally demonstrate that the model can segment multiple objects into individual slots without any ground-truth labels. Furthermore, we confirmed that relative movements between objects, such as approaching or receding, are accurately mapped and structured into a one-dimensional additive latent space. These results suggest that by introducing algebraic geometric constraints rather than relying solely on statistical correlation learning, physically interpretable "disentangled representations" can be acquired. This study contributes to the understanding of the process by which infants internalize environmental laws as structures and provides a new perspective for constructing artificial systems with developmental intelligence.

AI.tldr: Proposes unsupervised, development-inspired representation learning using algebraic homomorphism as a structural prior to disentangle object segmentation and motion laws in dynamic scenes, achieving label-free segmentation and a 1D latent space for relative motions.
AI.motivation: Move beyond purely statistical learning to capture underlying environmental structure as infants do, enabling interpretable, physically grounded representations without labeled data.
AI.method: Integrates a neural network with an algebraic homomorphism constraint to structurally decompose pixel changes into components such as translation and deformation; jointly learns object segmentation and motion-law extraction from dynamic sequences; validates on chased/evaded interaction scenes inspired by developmental psychology.
AI.result: The model segments multiple objects into slots without ground-truth labels and maps relative motions (approach/recede) into a one-dimensional additive latent space, demonstrating physically interpretable, disentangled representations.
AI.conclusion: Algebraic-geometric constraints can yield disentangled, interpretable representations and provide a developmental account of how infants internalize environmental laws, offering a new direction for constructing developmental AI systems.

title: PREVENT-JACK: Context Steering for Swarms of Long Heavy Articulated Vehicles
comment: 32 pages, 7 figures, 4 videos; submitted to the Swarm Robotics collection of the Nature Portfolio Journal Robotics (NPJ Robot)
summary: In this paper, we aim to extend the traditional point-mass-like robot representation in swarm robotics and instead study a swarm of long Heavy Articulated Vehicles (HAVs). HAVs are kinematically constrained, elongated, and articulated, introducing unique challenges. Local, decentralized coordination of these vehicles is motivated by many real-world applications. Our approach, Prevent-Jack, introduces the sparsely covered context steering framework in robotics. It fuses six local behaviors, providing guarantees against jackknifing and collisions at the cost of potential dead- and livelocks, tested for vehicles with up to ten trailers. We highlight the importance of the Evade Attraction behavior for deadlock prevention using a parameter study, and use 15,000 simulations to evaluate the swarm performance. Our extensive experiments and the results show that both the dead- and livelocks occur more frequently in larger swarms and denser scenarios, affecting a peak average of 27%/31% of vehicles. We observe that larger swarms exhibit increased waiting, while smaller swarms show increased evasion.

AI.tldr: Extends swarm robotics from point-mass models to long articulated heavy vehicles (HAVs) and introduces Prevent-Jack with sparsely covered context steering, fusing six local behaviors to prevent jackknifing and collisions. Although it provides safety guarantees, it can incur dead- and livelocks, especially in larger or denser swarms; evaluated via 15,000 simulations with up to ten trailers, showing up to 27% of vehicles deadlocked and 31% livelocked at peak.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of point-mass-based swarm models for kinematically constrained, articulated vehicles. Understand decentralized coordination under articulation-induced constraints and quantify safety vs. deadlock risks in HAV swarms.
AI.method: Proposes Prevent-Jack, a sparsely covered context-steering framework that combines six local behaviors to guarantee avoidance of jackknifing and collisions. Includes Evade Attraction for deadlock mitigation. Evaluated on HAVs with up to ten trailers, across 15,000 simulations varying swarm size and density.
AI.result: The framework achieves safety against jackknifing and collisions under the proposed scheme but introduces potential dead- and livelocks. Deadlocks and livelocks increase with swarm size and density, affecting a peak average of 27% (deadlock) and 31% (livelock) of vehicles. Larger swarms show more waiting; smaller swarms show more evasion.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized coordination for HAV swarms is feasible with guarantees against some failure modes, but scalability introduces nontrivial deadlock risks. The Evade Attraction helps but is not sufficient to eliminate deadlocks/livelocks; future work should address stronger deadlock resolution, parameter sensitivity, and real-world validation.

title: Materialistic RIR: Material Conditioned Realistic RIR Generation
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 Findings. Project page: https://mahnoor-fatima-saad.github.io/MatRIR.html
summary: Rings like gold, thuds like wood! The sound we hear in a scene is shaped not only by the spatial layout of the environment but also by the materials of the objects and surfaces within it. For instance, a room with wooden walls will produce a different acoustic experience from a room with the same spatial layout but concrete walls. Accurately modeling these effects is essential for applications such as virtual reality, robotics, architectural design, and audio engineering. Yet, existing methods for acoustic modeling often entangle spatial and material influences in correlated representations, which limits user control and reduces the realism of the generated acoustics. In this work, we present a novel approach for material-controlled Room Impulse Response (RIR) generation that explicitly disentangles the effects of spatial and material cues in a scene. Our approach models the RIR using two modules: a spatial module that captures the influence of the spatial layout of the scene, and a material module that modulates this spatial RIR according to a user-specified material configuration. This explicitly disentangled design allows users to easily modify the material configuration of a scene and observe its impact on acoustics without altering the spatial structure or scene content. Our model provides significant improvements over prior approaches on both acoustic-based metrics (up to +16% on RTE) and material-based metrics (up to +70%). Furthermore, through a human perceptual study, we demonstrate the improved realism and material sensitivity of our model compared to the strongest baselines.

AI.tldr: A two-module, disentangled approach to RIR generation that separates spatial layout from material cues, enabling material-controlled acoustics with improved objective and perceptual performance.
AI.motivation: Existing acoustic modeling methods entangle spatial and material influences in correlated representations, hindering user control and realism. A disentangled model could allow independent manipulation of material properties without changing geometry.
AI.method: Propose a two-module architecture: a spatial module that encodes the scene's geometry and a material module that modulates the spatial RIR according to a user-specified material configuration, enabling explicit control over material effects while preserving spatial content.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on acoustic-based metrics (up to +16% on RTE) and material-based metrics (up to +70%). Human perceptual study shows improved realism and material sensitivity compared to strongest baselines.
AI.conclusion: A disentangled, material-controlled RIR generation framework offers precise material manipulation without altering spatial structure, improving both objective metrics and perceptual realism, with broad applicability in VR, robotics, architecture, and audio engineering.

title: Mind the Prompt: Self-adaptive Generation of Task Plan Explanations via LLMs
summary: Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into complex software systems enables the generation of human-understandable explanations of opaque AI processes, such as automated task planning. However, the quality and reliability of these explanations heavily depend on effective prompt engineering. The lack of a systematic understanding of how diverse stakeholder groups formulate and refine prompts hinders the development of tools that can automate this process. We introduce COMPASS (COgnitive Modelling for Prompt Automated SynthesiS), a proof-of-concept self-adaptive approach that formalises prompt engineering as a cognitive and probabilistic decision-making process. COMPASS models unobservable users' latent cognitive states, such as attention and comprehension, uncertainty, and observable interaction cues as a POMDP, whose synthesised policy enables adaptive generation of explanations and prompt refinements. We evaluate COMPASS using two diverse cyber-physical system case studies to assess the adaptive explanation generation and their qualities, both quantitatively and qualitatively. Our results demonstrate the feasibility of COMPASS integrating human cognition and user profile's feedback into automated prompt synthesis in complex task planning systems.

AI.tldr: COMPASS formalizes prompt engineering as a cognitive, probabilistic decision-making problem (a POMDP) to adapt explanations and prompt refinements in LLM-assisted task planning, validated on two cyber-physical system case studies as feasible.
AI.motivation: Diverse stakeholders formulate prompts in varied ways; there is no systematic framework to automate and adapt prompt generation based on user cognition and feedback, hindering reliable explanations of opaque AI processes.
AI.method: Model latent user states (attention, comprehension, uncertainty) and observable cues as a POMDP; synthesize a policy that guides adaptive explanation generation and prompt refinement; evaluate on two cyber-physical system case studies using quantitative and qualitative measures.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility: COMPASS can integrate human cognitive factors and user feedback into automated prompt synthesis, producing adaptive explanations and refined prompts in CPS contexts, with supportive quantitative and qualitative assessments.
AI.conclusion: A proof-of-concept that cognitive-modelling-based prompt engineering can automate adaptive explanations in complex task-planning systems; indicates potential for broader applicability and avenues for enhancement.

title: Domain-Aware Hierarchical Contrastive Learning for Semi-Supervised Generalization Fault Diagnosis
summary: Fault diagnosis under unseen operating conditions remains highly challenging when labeled data are scarce. Semi-supervised domain generalization fault diagnosis (SSDGFD) provides a practical solution by jointly exploiting labeled and unlabeled source domains. However, existing methods still suffer from two coupled limitations. First, pseudo-labels for unlabeled domains are typically generated primarily from knowledge learned on the labeled source domain, which neglects domain-specific geometric discrepancies and thus induces systematic cross-domain pseudo-label bias. Second, unlabeled samples are commonly handled with a hard accept-or-discard strategy, where rigid thresholding causes imbalanced sample utilization across domains, while hard-label assignment for uncertain samples can easily introduce additional noise. To address these issues, we propose a unified framework termed domain-aware hierarchical contrastive learning (DAHCL) for SSDGFD. Specifically, DAHCL introduces a domain-aware learning (DAL) module to explicitly capture source-domain geometric characteristics and calibrate pseudo-label predictions across heterogeneous source domains, thereby mitigating cross-domain bias in pseudo-label generation. In addition, DAHCL develops a hierarchical contrastive learning (HCL) module that combines dynamic confidence stratification with fuzzy contrastive supervision, enabling uncertain samples to contribute to representation learning without relying on unreliable hard labels. In this way, DAHCL jointly improves the quality of supervision and the utilization of unlabeled samples. Furthermore, to better reflect practical industrial scenarios, we incorporate engineering noise into the SSDGFD evaluation protocol. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that...

AI.tldr: A unified domain-aware hierarchical contrastive framework (DAHCL) for semi-supervised domain-generalization fault diagnosis (SSDGFD) tackles cross-domain pseudo-label bias and inefficient use of unlabeled data via (1) domain-aware learning to calibrate pseudo-labels across source domains, and (2) hierarchical contrastive learning with dynamic confidence stratification and fuzzy supervision, plus engineering-noise–augmented evaluation; validated on three benchmarks showing improved supervision quality and sample utilization.
AI.motivation: Fault diagnosis under unseen operating conditions with scarce labeled data. Existing SSDGFD methods struggle due to cross-domain pseudo-label bias (labels influenced by the labeled source domain) and a hard accept/discard strategy for unlabeled samples, leading to imbalanced use and noisy hard labels.
AI.method: Propose DAHCL with two modules: (1) Domain-aware Learning (DAL) to explicitly capture source-domain geometric characteristics and calibrate pseudo-label predictions across heterogeneous source domains; (2) Hierarchical Contrastive Learning (HCL) combining dynamic confidence stratification with fuzzy contrastive supervision to allow uncertain samples to contribute without relying on unreliable hard labels. Evaluation protocol incorporates engineering noise to reflect practical industrial scenarios.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that DAHCL improves both supervision quality and unlabeled sample utilization under SSDGFD, outperforming baseline approaches in the presence of domain shifts and noisy conditions.
AI.conclusion: DAHCL effectively mitigates cross-domain bias in pseudo-label generation and enhances the use of unlabeled data, yielding better generalization for fault diagnosis under unseen operating conditions in industrial-like environments.

title: Learn Weightlessness: Imitate Non-Self-Stabilizing Motions on Humanoid Robot
summary: The integration of imitation and reinforcement learning has enabled remarkable advances in humanoid whole-body control, facilitating diverse human-like behaviors. However, research on environment-dependent motions remains limited. Existing methods typically enforce rigid trajectory tracking while neglecting physical interactions with the environment. We observe that humans naturally exploit a "weightless" state during non-self-stabilizing (NSS) motions--selectively relaxing specific joints to allow passive body--environment contact, thereby stabilizing the body and completing the motion. Inspired by this biological mechanism, we design a weightlessness-state auto-labeling strategy for dataset annotation; and we propose the Weightlessness Mechanism (WM), a method that dynamically determines which joints to relax and to what level, together enabling effective environmental interaction while executing target motions. We evaluate our approach on 3 representative NSS tasks: sitting on chairs of varying heights, lying down on beds with different inclinations, and leaning against walls via shoulder or elbow. Extensive experiments in simulation and on the Unitree G1 robot demonstrate that our WM method, trained on single-action demonstrations without any task-specific tuning, achieves strong generalization across diverse environmental configurations while maintaining motion stability. Our work bridges the gap between precise trajectory tracking and adaptive environmental interaction, offering a biologically-inspired solution for contact-rich humanoid control.

AI.tldr: Biologically-inspired weightlessness mechanism enabling selective joint relaxation to exploit passive body-environment contact during NSS motions, enabling robust cross-environment generalization from single-action demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between rigid trajectory tracking and environment-dependent control; humans exploit a weightless state to stabilize via contact, suggesting a data-annotation and control strategy to leverage such interactions in humanoid control.
AI.method: Introduce a weightlessness-state auto-labeling strategy for dataset annotation and propose Weightlessness Mechanism (WM) that dynamically decides which joints to relax and by how much in real-time. Train on single-action demonstrations without task-specific tuning and evaluate on 3 NSS tasks (chair sitting, bed lying with varying inclinations, wall leaning) in simulation and on Unitree G1.
AI.result: WM enables effective environmental interaction while preserving motion stability, generalizes across diverse environmental configurations, and does not require task-specific tuning, demonstrated in simulation and hardware.
AI.conclusion: Bridges precise trajectory tracking with adaptive environmental interaction using a biologically-inspired approach, offering a practical solution for contact-rich humanoid control and broader applicability to NSS tasks.

title: HyperFM: An Efficient Hyperspectral Foundation Model with Spectral Grouping
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, to be published in CVPR 2026 findings, Code and data are publicly available on https://github.com/umbc-sanjaylab/HyperFM
summary: The NASA PACE mission provides unprecedented hyperspectral observations of ocean color, aerosols, and clouds, offering new insights into how these components interact and influence Earth's climate and air quality. Its Ocean Color Instrument measures light across hundreds of finely spaced wavelength bands, enabling detailed characterization of features such as phytoplankton composition, aerosol properties, and cloud microphysics. However, hyperspectral data of this scale is large, complex, and difficult to label, requiring specialized processing and analysis techniques. Existing foundation models, which have transformed computer vision and natural language processing, are generally trained on standard RGB imagery and therefore struggle to interpret the continuous spectral signatures captured by PACE. While recent advances have introduced hyperspectral foundation models, they are typically trained on cloud-free observations and often remain limited to single-sensor datasets due to spectral inconsistencies across instruments. Moreover, existing models tend to be parameter-heavy and computationally expensive, limiting scalability and adoption in operational settings. To address these challenges, we introduce HyperFM, a parameter-efficient hyperspectral foundation model that leverages intra-group and inter-group spectral attention along with hybrid parameter decomposition to better capture spectral spatial relationships while reducing computational cost. HyperFM demonstrates consistent performance improvements over existing hyperspectral foundation models and task-specific state-of-the-art methods across four benchmark downstream atmospheric cloud property retrieval tasks. To support further research, we additionally release HyperFM250K, a large-scale hyperspectral dataset from the PACE mission that includes both clear and cloudy scenes.

AI.tldr: HyperFM is a parameter-efficient hyperspectral foundation model for NASA PACE data, employing intra-group and inter-group spectral attention with hybrid parameter decomposition to capture spectral-spatial relationships. It outperforms existing hyperspectral foundation models and task-specific SOTA on four cloud-related retrieval tasks, and the authors release HyperFM250K, a large hyperspectral dataset from PACE including clear and cloudy scenes.
AI.motivation: The field faces massive, labeled hyperspectral data that are difficult to process and label; existing foundation models are RGB-centric or cloud-free/specifically tuned, limiting cross-sensor transfer and scalability. A parameter-efficient, hyperspectral foundation model is needed to exploit spectral detail, handle multi-instrument spectral variability, and enable operational deployment.
AI.method: Proposes HyperFM, a hyperspectral foundation model that uses intra-group and inter-group spectral attention and hybrid parameter decomposition to efficiently model spectral-spatial relationships. Designed for multi-sensor spectral consistency, reduces parameter count while maintaining performance. Trained/validated on hyperspectral data from the PACE mission; evaluated on four benchmark cloud property retrieval tasks; includes release of a large-scale dataset HyperFM250K (clear and cloudy scenes).
AI.result: HyperFM demonstrates consistent performance improvements over existing hyperspectral foundation models and task-specific state-of-the-art methods across four downstream atmospheric cloud property retrieval tasks.
AI.conclusion: HyperFM offers a scalable, efficient hyperspectral foundation modeling approach for PACE data, enabling improved retrieval of cloud properties and other atmospheric variables; the HyperFM250K dataset supports broader research and benchmarking.

title: Propensity Inference: Environmental Contributors to LLM Behaviour
summary: Motivated by loss of control risks from misaligned AI systems, we develop and apply methods for measuring language models' propensity for unsanctioned behaviour. We contribute three methodological improvements: analysing effects of changes to environmental factors on behaviour, quantifying effect sizes via Bayesian generalised linear models, and taking explicit measures against circular analysis. We apply the methodology to measure the effects of 12 environmental factors (6 strategic in nature, 6 non-strategic) and thus the extent to which behaviour is explained by strategic aspects of the environment, a question relevant to risks from misalignment. Across 23 language models and 11 evaluation environments, we find approximately equal contributions from strategic and non-strategic factors for explaining behaviour, do not find strategic factors becoming more or less influential as capabilities improve, and find some evidence for a trend for increased sensitivity to goal conflicts. Finally, we highlight a key direction for future propensity research: the development of theoretical frameworks and cognitive models of AI decision-making into empirically testable forms.

AI.tldr: Three methodological advances for measuring unsanctioned AI behavior; use of Bayesian generalized linear models; analysis of environmental factor changes and explicit avoidance of circular analysis; across 23 models and 11 environments, strategic and non-strategic factors contribute roughly equally to behavior with no clear trend as capabilities grow; evidence for increasing sensitivity to goal conflicts; emphasizes developing empirically testable cognitive frameworks for AI decision-making.
AI.motivation: Mitigate loss-of-control risks from misaligned text-based AI systems by quantifying propensity for unsanctioned behavior and separating strategic from non-strategic environmental influences; address methodological gaps such as circular analysis.
AI.method: Three improvements: (1) study how changes in environmental factors affect behavior; (2) quantify effect sizes using Bayesian generalized linear models; (3) explicitly counter circular analysis; empirical application to 12 environmental factors (6 strategic, 6 non-strategic) across 23 language models and 11 evaluation environments, to assess how much of behavior is explained by strategic vs non-strategic environment.
AI.result: Strategic and non-strategic factors contribute roughly equally to explaining behavior; no evidence that strategic influence grows or shrinks with model capabilities; some evidence of increased sensitivity to goal conflicts as capabilities evolve.
AI.conclusion: Calls for theoretical frameworks and cognitive models of AI decision-making to be translated into empirically testable forms; future propensity research should integrate rigorous theoretical grounding to improve understanding of unsanctioned behavior risk.

title: IRIS: Interpolative Rényi Iterative Self-play for Large Language Model Fine-Tuning
summary: Self-play fine-tuning enables large language models to improve beyond supervised fine-tuning without additional human annotations by contrasting annotated responses with self-generated ones. Many existing methods rely on a fixed divergence regime. SPIN is closely related to a KL-based regime, SPACE to a Jensen-Shannon-style objective via noise contrastive estimation, and SPIF to $χ^2$-regularized self-play. Since these divergences exhibit different strengths depending on the distributional gap between model and target, no single choice appears to provide favorable learning dynamics across training stages. We propose IRIS (Interpolative Rényi Iterative Self-play), a Rényi-based self-play fine-tuning framework with a continuously adjustable objective. IRIS decomposes into two independent tilted risk terms over annotated and synthetic data, with exponential importance weights controlled by the order parameter $α$. We show that several self-play objectives can be interpreted as limiting or representative regimes at particular values of $α$, providing a unified theoretical perspective on these methods. An adaptive order schedule further adjusts $α$ to the distributional gap, shifting from sharper importance weighting early in training to smoother refinement near convergence. Theoretically, we establish the fixed-point property of IRIS and analyze how $α$ controls gradient concentration. Experiments on Zephyr-7B and Qwen2.5-3B across ten benchmarks show that IRIS improves upon baselines, reaching 44.57\% average score with gains across iterations. In our setting, IRIS with only 26$k$ annotated samples surpasses standard supervised fine-tuning trained on the full 200$k$ dataset.

AI.tldr: IRIS: a Rényi-based self-play fine-tuning framework that interpolates between self-play divergences via an adjustable α parameter, enabling adaptive weighting of annotated vs. synthetic data.
