It unifies SPIN, SPACE, and SPIF as limiting cases, and via an adaptive schedule improves data efficiency and performance, achieving strong results on Zephyr-7B and Qwen2.5-3B with fewer annotations and compelling fixed-point/gradient-concentration properties.
AI.motivation: Divergence choice in self-play fine-tuning strongly affects learning dynamics and data efficiency. A single fixed objective may underperform across training stages due to changing distributional gaps between model and target. A unified, tunable framework can adapt to this gap and improve learning efficiency.
AI.method: IRIS defines two tilted risk terms over annotated and synthetic data, each with exponential importance weights controlled by the order parameter α (Rényi-inspired). The overall objective is a combination of these two risks, allowing α to interpolate between different divergences. An adaptive order schedule modulates α over training to transition from sharp weighting early to smoother refinement near convergence. The framework supports interpreting several existing self-play objectives as special cases. Theoretical results include a fixed-point property and analysis of how α affects gradient concentration.
AI.result: Theoretical: IRIS has a fixed-point property and characterizes gradient concentration as a function of α. Empirical: across Zephyr-7B and Qwen2.5-3B over ten benchmarks, IRIS outperforms baselines, achieving an average score of 44.57%. In scarce annotation regimes, IRIS with 26k annotated samples surpasses standard supervised fine-tuning trained on 200k data.
AI.conclusion: IRIS provides a unified, tunable framework for self-play fine-tuning that can cover existing objectives as limiting cases, while offering adaptive optimization dynamics that improve data efficiency and performance. This supports more effective LLM fine-tuning with fewer annotations and better early-stage learning dynamics.

title: RPG: Robust Policy Gating for Smooth Multi-Skill Transitions in Humanoid Fighting
summary: Humanoid robots have demonstrated impressive motor skills in a wide range of tasks, yet whole-body control for humanlike long-time, dynamic fighting remains particularly challenging due to the stringent requirements on agility and stability. While imitation learning enables robots to execute human-like fighting skills, existing approaches often rely on switching among multiple single-skill policies or employing a general policy to imitate input reference motions. These strategies suffer from instability when transitioning between skills, as the mismatch of initial and terminal states across skills or reference motions introduces out-of-domain disturbances, resulting in unsmooth or unstable behaviors. In this work, we propose RPG, a hybrid expert policy framework, for smooth and stable humanoid multi-skills transition. Our approach incorporates motion transition randomization and temporal randomization to train a unified policy that generates agile fighting actions with stability and smoothness during skill transitions. Furthermore, we design a control pipeline that integrates walking/running locomotion with fighting skills, allowing humanlike long-time combat of arbitrary duration that can be seamlessly interrupted or transit action policies at any time. Extensive experiments in simulation demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, and real-world deployment on the Unitree G1 humanoid robot further validates its robustness and applicability.

AI.tldr: A unified hybrid expert policy (RPG) with motion-transition and temporal randomization enables smooth, stable multi-skill humanoid fighting by integrating locomotion and combat skills, validated in simulation and on Unitree G1.
AI.motivation: Long-time, dynamic humanoid fighting requires agile and stable whole-body control. Existing imitation strategies either switch between multiple single-skill policies or imitate reference motions with a general policy, leading to instability due to initial/terminal state mismatch and out-of-domain disturbances during skill transitions.
AI.method: Propose RPG, a hybrid expert policy framework that uses motion transition randomization and temporal randomization to train a single unified policy capable of executing multiple skills with smooth transitions. The control pipeline integrates walking/running locomotion with fighting skills, enabling long-time combat of arbitrary duration with the ability to interrupt or switch action policies at any time.
AI.result: Extensive simulations demonstrate effectiveness; hardware experiments on the Unitree G1 humanoid robot validate robustness and applicability in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables smooth, stable long-duration multi-skill humanoid control for fighting, with potential applicability to broader multi-skill robotic tasks and more natural human-robot interaction during dynamic tasks.

title: WFM: 3D Wavelet Flow Matching for Ultrafast Multi-Modal MRI Synthesis
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at MIDL 2026 (Poster)
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable quality in multi-modal MRI synthesis, but their computational cost (hundreds of sampling steps and separate models per modality) limits clinical deployment. We observe that this inefficiency stems from an unnecessary starting point: diffusion begins from pure noise, discarding the structural information already present in available MRI sequences. We propose WFM (Wavelet Flow Matching), which instead learns a direct flow from an informed prior, the mean of conditioning modalities in wavelet space, to the target distribution. Because the source and target share underlying anatomy and differ primarily in contrast, this formulation enables accurate synthesis in just 1-2 integration steps. A single 82M-parameter model with class conditioning synthesizes all four BraTS modalities (T1, T1c, T2, FLAIR), replacing four separate diffusion models totaling 326M parameters. On BraTS 2024, WFM achieves 26.8 dB PSNR and 0.94 SSIM, within 1-2 dB of diffusion baselines, while running 250-1000x faster (0.16-0.64s vs. 160s per volume). This speed-quality trade-off makes real-time MRI synthesis practical for clinical workflows. Code is available at https://github.com/yalcintur/WFM.

AI.tldr: WFM is a wavelet-space flow-matching method for MRI synthesis that replaces diffusion with a deterministic flow from an informed prior, enabling 1-2 integration steps and a single model to generate all BraTS modalities with substantially reduced parameters and runtime.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based multi-modal MRI synthesis, though high quality, is computationally heavy due to long sampling chains and separate models per modality. Exploiting shared anatomy and using informative priors can drastically reduce cost and enable clinical deployment.
AI.method: WFM learns a direct flow from the mean of conditioning modalities in wavelet space (an informed prior) to the target distribution, reducing the synthesis to a few integration steps. A single 82M-parameter model with class conditioning handles all four BraTS modalities (T1, T1c, T2, FLAIR), replacing four diffusion models (total 326M parameters).
AI.result: On BraTS 2024, WFM achieves 26.8 dB PSNR and 0.94 SSIM, within 1-2 dB of diffusion baselines, while running 250-1000x faster (0.16-0.64s per volume vs. 160s). This makes real-time MRI synthesis more feasible in clinical workflows.
AI.conclusion: Wavelet-informed flow matching substantially improves efficiency for multi-modal MRI synthesis, preserving high image quality with far fewer parameters and drastically reduced runtime; the approach generalizes to multiple modalities and supports real-time clinical deployment; code is publicly available.

title: AI Governance under Political Turnover: The Alignment Surface of Compliance Design
summary: Governments are increasingly interested in using AI to make administrative decisions cheaper, more scalable, and more consistent. But for probabilistic AI to be incorporated into public administration it must be embedded in a compliance layer that makes decisions reviewable, repeatable, and legally defensible. That layer can improve oversight by making departures from law easier to detect. But it can also create a stable approval boundary that political successors learn to navigate while preserving the appearance of lawful administration. We develop a formal model in which institutions choose the scale of automation, the degree of codification, and safeguards on iterative use. The model shows when these systems become vulnerable to strategic use from within government, why reforms that initially improve oversight can later increase that vulnerability, and why expansions in AI use may be difficult to unwind. Making AI usable can thus make procedures easier for future governments to learn and exploit.

AI.tldr: A formal institutional model analyzes how embedding probabilistic AI in a compliance layer for public administration affects oversight, vulnerability to internal strategic manipulation, and the ease of unwinding AI adoption; findings show that oversight-friendly reforms can backfire, expansions can entrench exploitable paths, and making AI usable facilitates learning and manipulation by future governments.
AI.motivation: Governments seek cheaper, scalable, and consistent administrative decisions but must ensure legal defensibility and oversight. A compliance layer is proposed to make probabilistic AI decisions reviewable, repeatable, and legally defensible, yet such a layer can also create stable boundaries that can be navigated by political actors. The paper investigates the dynamic institutional effects of implementing AI with codified rules and safeguards.
AI.method: Formal institutional/game-theoretic model where institutions choose three levers: (i) the scale of automation, (ii) the degree of codification, and (iii) safeguards on iterative use. The model analyzes how these choices interact with incentives of actors inside government to affect oversight, detectability of deviations, and the possibility of strategic use. It derives conditions under which AI systems become vulnerable, explains how reforms can have unintended consequences over time, and discusses path dependence and difficulty of unwinding AI adoption.
AI.result: The model shows that AI-enabled administrative systems can be exploited by internal strategic actors; initially beneficial reforms that improve oversight can later increase vulnerability due to the stabilization of new procedural boundaries. Expansions in AI use create hard-to-unwind path dependencies, making rollback politically and institutionally challenging. Increasing AI usability lowers the friction for future governments to learn and manipulate procedures.
AI.conclusion: Policy implications emphasize designing adaptive, robust oversight that remains hard to game, and anticipating dynamic effects where improvements in accountability may inadvertently create exploitable channels. Strategies should include flexible safeguards, explicit unwind plans, and transparency to prevent future manipulation as AI adoption expands.

title: Data-Driven Open-Loop Simulation for Digital-Twin Operator Decision Support in Wastewater Treatment
summary: Wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) need digital-twin-style decision support tools that can simulate plant response under prescribed control plans, tolerate irregular and missing sensing, and remain informative over 12-36 h planning horizons. Meeting these requirements with full-scale plant data remains an open engineering-AI challenge. We present CCSS-RS, a controlled continuous-time state-space model that separates historical state inference from future control and exogenous rollout. The model combines typed context encoding, gain-weighted forcing of prescribed and forecast drivers, semigroup-consistent rollouts, and Student-t plus hurdle outputs for heavy-tailed and zero-inflated WWTP sensor data. On the public Avedøre full-scale benchmark, with 906,815 timesteps, 43% missingness, and 1-20 min irregular sampling, CCSS-RS achieves RMSE 0.696 and CRPS 0.349 at H=1000 across 10,000 test windows. This reduces RMSE by 40-46% relative to Neural CDE baselines and by 31-35% relative to simplified internal variants. Four case studies using a frozen checkpoint on test data demonstrate operational value: oxygen-setpoint perturbations shift predicted ammonium by -2.3 to +1.4 over horizons 300-1000; a smoothed setpoint plan ranks first in multi-criterion screening; context-only sensor outages raise monitored-variable RMSE by at most 10%; and ammonium, nitrate, and oxygen remain more accurate than persistence throughout the rollout. These results establish CCSS-RS as a practical learned simulator for offline scenario screening in industrial wastewater treatment, complementary to mechanistic models.

AI.tldr: CCSS-RS is a controlled continuous-time state-space model for WWTPs that isolates historical state inference from future control/rollout, handling irregular/missing data and heavy-tailed, zero-inflated sensor outputs; achieves strong predictive accuracy and demonstrates operational value on a full-scale benchmark, outperforming Neural CDE baselines and simplified variants.
AI.motivation: Need robust, informative digital-twin style simulators for WWTPs that operate under incomplete sensing and under 12-36 h horizons for offline scenario screening.
AI.method: Developed CCSS-RS with typed context encoding, gain-weighted forcing of prescribed/forecast drivers, semigroup-consistent rollouts, and output distributions combining Student-t and hurdle components; separates historical inference from future rollout in a continuous-time state-space formulation; validated on Avedøre benchmark with heavy missingness and irregular sampling.
AI.result: RMSE 0.696 and CRPS 0.349 at horizon H=1000 across 10k test windows on 906,815 timesteps with 43% missing; 40-46% RMSE reduction versus Neural CDE baselines and 31-35% versus simplified internal variants.
AI.conclusion: CCSS-RS provides a practical learned simulator for offline WWTP scenario screening, complementary to mechanistic models.

title: A Deployable Embodied Vision-Language Navigation System with Hierarchical Cognition and Context-Aware Exploration
summary: Bridging the gap between embodied intelligence and embedded deployment remains a key challenge in intelligent robotic systems, where perception, reasoning, and planning must operate under strict constraints on computation, memory, energy, and real-time execution. In vision-language navigation (VLN), existing approaches often face a fundamental trade-off between strong reasoning capabilities and efficient deployment on real-world platforms. In this paper, we present a deployable embodied VLN system that achieves both high efficiency and robust high-level reasoning on real-world robotic platforms. To achieve this, we decouple the system into three asynchronous modules: a real-time perception module for continuous environment sensing, a memory integration module for spatial-semantic aggregation, and a reasoning module for high-level decision making. We incrementally construct a cognitive memory graph to encode scene information, which is further decomposed into subgraphs to enable reasoning with a vision-language model (VLM). To further improve navigation efficiency and accuracy, we also leverage the cognitive memory graph to formulate the exploration problem as a context-aware Weighted Traveling Repairman Problem (WTRP), which minimizes the weighted waiting time of viewpoints. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world robotic platforms demonstrate improved navigation success and efficiency over existing VLN approaches, while maintaining real-time performance on resource-constrained hardware.

AI.tldr: A deployable embodied VLN system decouples perception, memory, and reasoning, using a cognitive memory graph decomposed into subgraphs and a context-aware Weighted Traveling Repairman Problem to improve efficiency and high-level reasoning on real-world platforms.
AI.motivation: Address the fundamental trade-off in vision-language navigation between strong reasoning capabilities and efficient deployment under real-time, resource-constrained conditions; enable robust navigation on real robotic platforms.
AI.method: Three asynchronous modules: real-time perception for continuous sensing, memory integration for spatial-semantic aggregation, and reasoning for high-level decisions; incremental cognitive memory graph encoding scene information and decomposed subgraphs; reasoning via a vision-language model on subgraphs; exploration framed as context-aware WTRP to minimize weighted viewpoint waiting time.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved navigation success and efficiency over existing VLN approaches and retains real-time performance on resource-constrained hardware in both simulation and real-world robotic experiments.
AI.conclusion: A modular, graph-based memory and reasoning framework enables efficient, high-level decision making for deployable VLN, bridging perception, memory, and planning under tight resource constraints.

title: Reinforcing 3D Understanding in Point-VLMs via Geometric Reward Credit Assignment
summary: Point-Vision-Language Models promise to empower embodied agents with executable spatial reasoning, yet they frequently succumb to geometric hallucination where predicted 3D structures contradict the observed 2D reality. We identify a key cause of this failure not as a representation bottleneck but as a structural misalignment in reinforcement learning, where sparse geometric tokens are drowned out by noisy and broadcasted sequence-level rewards. To resolve this causal dilution, we propose Geometric Reward Credit Assignment, a framework that disentangles holistic supervision into field-specific signals and routes them exclusively to their responsible token spans. This mechanism transforms vague feedback into precise gradient updates and effectively turns generic policy optimization into targeted structural alignment. Furthermore, we internalize physical constraints via a Reprojection-Consistency term which serves as a cross-modal verifier to penalize physically impossible geometries. Validated on a calibrated benchmark derived from ShapeNetCore, our approach bridges the reliability gap by boosting 3D KPA from 0.64 to 0.93, increasing 3D bounding box intersection over union to 0.686, and raising reprojection consistency scores to 0.852. Crucially, these gains are achieved while maintaining robust 2D localization performance, marking a meaningful step from plausible textual outputs toward physically verifiable spatial predictions.

AI.tldr: Geometric Reward Credit Assignment aligns reinforcement-learning signals with geometry in Point-Vision-Language Models, improving 3D accuracy and reprojection consistency while preserving 2D localization; validated on a ShapeNetCore-based benchmark.
AI.motivation: Address geometric hallucination (inconsistent 3D structures) caused by misaligned sparse geometric tokens and dense sequence-level rewards in RL for PVLMs; aim to achieve physically verifiable spatial predictions.
AI.method: Geometric Reward Credit Assignment disentangles holistic supervision into field-specific signals and routes them to the responsible token spans; adds a Reprojection-Consistency term as a cross-modal verifier to penalize impossible geometries, enabling targeted gradient updates.
AI.result: On a ShapeNetCore-derived benchmark, 3D KPA improves from 0.64 to 0.93; 3D bounding box IoU increases to 0.686; reprojection consistency rises to 0.852; 2D localization performance remains robust.
AI.conclusion: The approach substantially improves reliability of geometric reasoning in PVLMs, moving toward physically verifiable spatial predictions while preserving 2D localization capabilities.

title: Agentic AI for Personalized Physiotherapy: A Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Video Training and Real-Time Pose Correction
comment: 3 pages, 2 figures, submitted to ICDH IEEE conference
summary: At-home physiotherapy compliance remains critically low due to a lack of personalized supervision and dynamic feedback. Existing digital health solutions rely on static, pre-recorded video libraries or generic 3D avatars that fail to account for a patient's specific injury limitations or home environment. In this paper, we propose a novel Multi-Agent System (MAS) architecture that leverages Generative AI and computer vision to close the tele-rehabilitation loop. Our framework consists of four specialized micro-agents: a Clinical Extraction Agent that parses unstructured medical notes into kinematic constraints; a Video Synthesis Agent that utilizes foundational video generation models to create personalized, patient-specific exercise videos; a Vision Processing Agent for real-time pose estimation; and a Diagnostic Feedback Agent that issues corrective instructions. We present the system architecture, detail the prototype pipeline using Large Language Models and MediaPipe, and outline our clinical evaluation plan. This work demonstrates the feasibility of combining generative media with agentic autonomous decision-making to scale personalized patient care safely and effectively.

AI.tldr: Proposes a four-agent multi-agent system that uses generative AI and computer vision to create personalized at-home physiotherapy videos, estimate patient pose in real-time, and give corrective feedback, addressing low home exercise compliance.
AI.motivation: Target low adherence in home-based physiotherapy due to lack of personalized supervision and dynamic feedback; current digital solutions rely on static libraries or generic avatars that fail to account for patient-specific injuries and home environments.
AI.method: A four-maction micro-agent MAS: Clinical Extraction Agent parses unstructured medical notes into kinematic constraints; Video Synthesis Agent generates patient-specific exercise videos using foundational video generation models; Vision Processing Agent performs real-time pose estimation; Diagnostic Feedback Agent issues corrective instructions. Prototype pipeline leverages Large Language Models and MediaPipe; system architecture and pipeline are described; clinical evaluation plan outlined.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported yet. The work demonstrates feasibility of integrating generative media with autonomous, agentic decision-making and outlines a prototype pipeline and clinical evaluation plan.
AI.conclusion: The study suggests that combining generative media with MAS can scale personalized tele-rehabilitation safely and effectively, indicating potential for improved adherence and outcomes pending clinical validation.

title: Sink-Token-Aware Pruning for Fine-Grained Video Understanding in Efficient Video LLMs
summary: Video Large Language Models (Video LLMs) incur high inference latency due to a large number of visual tokens provided to LLMs. To address this, training-free visual token pruning has emerged as a solution to reduce computational costs; however, existing methods are primarily validated on Multiple-Choice Question Answering (MCQA) benchmarks, where coarse-grained cues often suffice. In this work, we reveal that these methods suffer a sharp performance collapse on fine-grained understanding tasks requiring precise visual grounding, such as hallucination evaluation. To explore this gap, we conduct a systematic analysis and identify sink tokens--semantically uninformative tokens that attract excessive attention--as a key obstacle to fine-grained video understanding. When these sink tokens survive pruning, they distort the model's visual evidence and hinder fine-grained understanding. Motivated by these insights, we propose Sink-Token-aware Pruning (SToP), a simple yet effective plug-and-play method that introduces a sink score to quantify each token's tendency to behave as a sink and applies this score to existing spatial and temporal pruning methods to suppress them, thereby enhancing video understanding. To validate the effectiveness of SToP, we apply it to state-of-the-art pruning methods (VisionZip, FastVid, and Holitom) and evaluate it across diverse benchmarks covering hallucination, open-ended generation, compositional reasoning, and MCQA. Our results demonstrate that SToP significantly boosts performance, even when pruning up to 90% of visual tokens.

AI.tldr: SToP: a plug-and-play sink-token-aware pruning method that suppresses semantically inert sink tokens during pruning, yielding robust, high-quality video understanding under aggressive token pruning (up to 90%).
AI.motivation: Current training-free visual token pruning degrades performance on fine-grained video tasks that require precise visual grounding. Sink tokens attract attention but carry little information, causing distortion when pruned. A robust pruning strategy is needed that preserves informative tokens for fine-grained understanding.
AI.method: Introduce a sink score for each token reflecting its tendency to act as a sink. Integrate this score with existing spatial/temporal pruning methods (VisionZip, FastVid, Holitom) as a plug-and-play module to suppress sink tokens during pruning.
AI.result: SToP substantially improves performance across hallucination, open-ended generation, compositional reasoning, and MCQA benchmarks, remaining effective even when pruning up to 90% of visual tokens.
AI.conclusion: SToP is a simple, effective, plug-and-play enhancement to current video-LMM pruning pipelines, enabling robust, fine-grained video understanding under aggressive token pruning.

title: A Replicable Robotics Awareness Method Using LLM-Enabled Robotics Interaction: Evidence from a Corporate Challenge
comment: 10 pages, 8 Figures, to be submitted for journal per-review
summary: Large language models are increasingly being explored as interfaces between humans and robotic systems, yet there remains limited evidence on how such technologies can be used not only for interaction, but also as a structured means of introducing robotics to non-specialist users in real organizational settings. This paper introduces and evaluates a challenge-based method for robotics awareness, implemented through an LLM-enabled humanoid robot activity conducted with employees of AD Ports Group in the United Arab Emirates. In the event, participants engaged with a humanoid robot in a logistics-inspired task environment using voice commands interpreted through an LLM-based control framework. The activity was designed as a team-based, role-driven experience intended to expose participants to embodied AI and human-robot collaboration without requiring prior robotics expertise. To evaluate the approach, a post-event survey remained open for 16 days and collected 102 responses. Results indicate strong overall reception, with high satisfaction (8.46/10), increased interest in robotics and AI (4.47/5), and improved understanding of emerging forms of human-robot collaboration (4.45/5). Participants who interacted directly with the robot also reported natural interaction (4.37/5) and a strong sense that interaction became easier as the activity progressed (4.74/5). At the same time, lower ratings for reliability and predictability point to important technical and design challenges for future iterations. The findings suggest that challenge-based, LLM-enabled humanoid interaction can serve as a promising and replicable method for robotics awareness in industrial and operational environments.

AI.tldr: LLM-enabled humanoid robot activity provides promising robotics awareness in industrial settings, with high user satisfaction and interest, but reliability/predictability require improvement.
AI.motivation: to assess whether LLM-driven humanoid interaction can introduce robotics to non-specialists in real organizations, beyond mere interaction.
AI.method: Field deployment with AD Ports Group employees in the United Arab Emirates. A team-based, role-driven activity in a logistics-inspired task environment. Voice commands interpreted via an LLM-based control framework. Post-event survey collected over 16 days with 102 responses.
AI.result: Participants reported high satisfaction (8.46/10), increased interest in robotics/AI (4.47/5), and improved understanding of emerging human–robot collaboration (4.45/5). Those who interacted directly with the robot reported natural interaction (4.37/5) and a sense that interaction became easier over time (4.74/5). Reliability and predictability ratings were lower, signaling technical/design challenges for future iterations.
AI.conclusion: Challenge-based, LLM-enabled humanoid interaction appears to be a promising and replicable method for robotics awareness in industrial/operational environments, with clear potential for broader adoption after addressing reliability and design issues.

title: WildSplatter: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting with Appearance Control from Unconstrained Images
summary: We propose WildSplatter, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model for unconstrained images with unknown camera parameters and varying lighting conditions. 3DGS is an effective scene representation that enables high-quality, real-time rendering; however, it typically requires iterative optimization and multi-view images captured under consistent lighting with known camera parameters. WildSplatter is trained on unconstrained photo collections and jointly learns 3D Gaussians and appearance embeddings conditioned on input images. This design enables flexible modulation of Gaussian colors to represent significant variations in lighting and appearance. Our method reconstructs 3D Gaussians from sparse input views in under one second, while also enabling appearance control under diverse lighting conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing pose-free 3DGS methods on challenging real-world datasets with varying illumination.

AI.tldr: WildSplatter is a fast, feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting model that reconstructs 3D Gaussians from sparse views in under 1s on unconstrained images with unknown camera parameters and varying lighting, enabling controllable appearance under diverse illumination and outperforming pose-free 3DGS baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard 3D Gaussian Splatting achieves real-time quality but relies on iterative optimization, multi-view data with fixed lighting, and known camera parameters. There is a need for fast, pose-free, unconstrained 3D scene reconstruction from unconstrained photo collections with lighting variation.
AI.method: Train on unconstrained photo collections and jointly learn 3D Gaussian parameters and appearance embeddings conditioned on input images. Use appearance-conditioned color modulation to capture lighting variations. Reconstruct Gaussians in a feed-forward pass from sparse input views in under one second, enabling appearance control under diverse lighting.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms existing pose-free 3DGS methods on challenging real-world datasets with varying illumination.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables fast, unconstrained 3D scene reconstruction with appearance control from sparse views under diverse lighting, broadening the applicability of 3D Gaussian Splatting to unconstrained imagery.

title: Multi-Agent Empowerment and Emergence of Complex Behavior in Groups
summary: Intrinsic motivations are receiving increasing attention, i.e. behavioral incentives that are not engineered, but emerge from the interaction of an agent with its surroundings. In this work we study the emergence of behaviors driven by one such incentive, empowerment, specifically in the context of more than one agent. We formulate a principled extension of empowerment to the multi-agent setting, and demonstrate its efficient calculation. We observe that this intrinsic motivation gives rise to characteristic modes of group-organization in two qualitatively distinct environments: a pair of agents coupled by a tendon, and a controllable Vicsek flock. This demonstrates the potential of intrinsic motivations such as empowerment to not just drive behavior for only individual agents but also higher levels of behavioral organization at scale.

AI.tldr: Extends the empowerment intrinsic-motivation concept to multi-agent systems, provides an efficient computational method, and demonstrates emergent group-organization modes in a tendon-coupled pair and a controllable Vicsek flock, highlighting scalability of intrinsic motivation to collective behavior.
AI.motivation: Explore whether intrinsic motivations, specifically empowerment, can drive coherent, scalable group behavior in multi-agent settings without external rewards or explicit coordination.
AI.method: Formulate a principled multi-agent extension of empowerment, derive an efficient computation for it, and validate it in two environments: a tendon-coupled pair of agents and a controllable Vicsek flock, analyzing resulting group-organization modes.
AI.result: Empowerment induces characteristic modes of group organization in both environments, demonstrating that intrinsic motivation can guide scalable collective behavior and that the proposed multi-agent empowerment is computationally tractable.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic motivations like empowerment can-scale to orchestrate coordination and organization across multiple agents, enabling emergent collective behavior without external control or rewards.

title: HARBOR: Automated Harness Optimization
summary: Long-horizon language-model agents are dominated, in lines of code and in operational complexity, not by their underlying model but by the harness that wraps it: context compaction, tool caching, semantic memory, trajectory reuse, speculative tool prediction, and the glue that binds the model to a sandboxed execution environment. We argue that harness design is a first-class machine-learning problem and that automated configuration search dominates manual stacking once the flag space exceeds a handful of bits. We defend this claim in two steps. First, we formalize automated harness optimization as constrained noisy Bayesian optimization over a mixed-variable, cost-heterogeneous configuration space with cold-start-corrected rewards and a posterior chance-constrained safety check, and give a reference solver, HARBOR (Harness Axis-aligned Regularized Bayesian Optimization Routine), built from a block-additive SAAS surrogate, multi-fidelity cost-aware acquisition, and TuRBO trust regions. Second, we instantiate the problem in a flag-gated harness over a production coding agent and report a controlled four-round manual-tuning case study against a fixed task suite and an end-to-end HARBOR run. The formulation itself is task-class agnostic: the configuration space, reward correction, acquisition, and safety check apply to any agent harness with a bounded flag space and a reproducible task suite.

AI.tldr: Harness design for long-horizon language-model agents is the bottleneck, dominated by the configuration and tooling rather than the model itself; the paper formalizes automated harness optimization using Bayesian optimization and introduces HARBOR, then demonstrates it on a flag-gated production agent with a four-round tuning study, claiming task-class agnostic applicability.
AI.motivation: Harness complexity (context compaction, tool caching, memory, trajectory reuse, etc.) constitutes a first-class ML problem; automated configuration search dominates manual stacking once the flag space is nontrivial.
AI.method: Formalize automated harness optimization as constrained noisy Bayesian optimization over a mixed-variable, cost-heterogeneous configuration space with cold-start-corrected rewards and a posterior chance-constrained safety check. Propose HARBOR (Harness Axis-aligned Regularized Bayesian Optimization Routine) built from a block-additive SAAS surrogate, multi-fidelity cost-aware acquisition, and TuRBO trust regions.
AI.result: Application to a production, flag-gated harness for a coding agent with a controlled four-round manual-tuning experiment vs a fixed task suite and an end-to-end HARBOR run.
AI.conclusion: The approach is task-class agnostic: the configuration space, reward correction, acquisition, and safety checks extend to any agent harness with bounded flag space and a reproducible task suite.

title: From Noise to Intent: Anchoring Generative VLA Policies with Residual Bridges
summary: Bridging high-level semantic understanding with low-level physical control remains a persistent challenge in embodied intelligence, stemming from the fundamental spatiotemporal scale mismatch between cognition and action. Existing generative VLA policies typically adopt a "Generation-from-Noise" paradigm, which disregards this disparity, leading to representation inefficiency and weak condition alignment during optimization. In this work, we propose ResVLA, an architecture that shifts the paradigm to "Refinement-from-Intent." Recognizing that robotic motion naturally decomposes into global intent and local dynamics, ResVLA utilizes spectral analysis to decouple control into a deterministic low-frequency anchor and a stochastic high-frequency residual. By anchoring the generative process on the predicted intent, our model focuses strictly on refining local dynamics via a residual diffusion bridge. Extensive simulation experiments show that ResVLA achieves competitive performance, strong robustness to language and robot embodiment perturbations, and faster convergence than standard generative baselines. It also demonstrates strong performance in real-world robot experiments.

AI.tldr: ResVLA reframes generation-from-noise policies as refinement-from-intent, using spectral decomposition to separate a deterministic low-frequency anchor (intent) from a stochastic high-frequency residual, enabling robust, faster-converging visuomotor planning in both simulation and real robots.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent spatiotemporal scale mismatch between high-level semantic understanding and low-level physical control, which undermines representation efficiency and condition alignment in generation-from-noise VLA policies.
AI.method: Decompose control spectrally into a low-frequency deterministic anchor driven by predicted intent and a high-frequency stochastic residual. Anchor generation on intent; refine local dynamics via a residual diffusion bridge in a ResVLA architecture.
AI.result: In simulation, ResVLA matches or exceeds baselines, shows strong robustness to language and embodiment perturbations, converges faster than standard generative methods, and demonstrates success in real-world robot experiments.
AI.conclusion: ResVLA offers a viable refinement-from-intent paradigm for embodied, language-conditioned control, improving data efficiency, robustness, and real-world applicability by decoupling global intent from local dynamics.

title: SpatiO: Adaptive Test-Time Orchestration of Vision-Language Agents for Spatial Reasoning
summary: Understanding visual scenes requires not only recognizing objects but also reasoning about their spatial relationships. Unlike general vision-language tasks, spatial reasoning requires integrating multiple inductive biases, such as 2D appearance cues, depth signals, and geometric constraints, whose reliability varies across contexts. This suggests that effective spatial reasoning requires \emph{spatial adaptability}: the ability to flexibly coordinate different reasoning strategies depending on the input. However, most existing approaches rely on a single reasoning pipeline that implicitly learns a fixed spatial prior, limiting their ability to adapt under distribution changes. Multi-agent systems offer a promising alternative by aggregating diverse reasoning trajectories, but prior attempts in spatial reasoning primarily employ homogeneous agents, restricting the diversity of inductive biases they can leverage. In this work, we introduce \textbf{\textsc{SpatiO}}, a heterogeneous multi-agent framework for spatial reasoning that coordinates multiple vision-language specialists with complementary inductive biases. To enable effective collaboration, we propose \textbf{Test-Time Orchestration (TTO)}, an optimization mechanism that dynamically evaluates and reweights agents based on their observed reliability during inference, without modifying model parameters. Extensive experiments on diverse spatial reasoning benchmarks, including 3DSRBench, STVQA-7k, CV-Bench, and Omni3D-Bench, demonstrate that \textsc{SpatiO} consistently improves spatial reasoning performance over both closed-source and open-source baselines.

AI.tldr: SpatiO is a heterogeneous multi-agent framework for spatial reasoning that uses Test-Time Orchestration to dynamically reweight diverse vision-language specialists, improving spatial reasoning across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Spatial reasoning requires flexible coordination of different inductive biases (2D cues, depth, geometry) and adapts to distribution shifts; single fixed pipelines underexploit available priors.
AI.method: SpatiO coordinates multiple vision-language specialists with complementary inductive biases; Test-Time Orchestration (TTO) evaluates and reweights agents at inference time based on observed reliability, without changing model parameters.
AI.result: Across benchmarks (3DSRBench, STVQA-7k, CV-Bench, Omni3D-Bench), SpatiO yields consistent performance gains over both closed-source and open-source baselines on spatial reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration with dynamic, parameter-free orchestration improves spatial reasoning and adapts to varying contexts, suggesting broader applicability of TTO in multi-agent vision-language systems.

title: Trust but Verify: Introducing DAVinCI -- A Framework for Dual Attribution and Verification in Claim Inference for Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable fluency and versatility across a wide range of NLP tasks, yet they remain prone to factual inaccuracies and hallucinations. This limitation poses significant risks in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, law, and scientific communication, where trust and verifiability are paramount. In this paper, we introduce DAVinCI - a Dual Attribution and Verification framework designed to enhance the factual reliability and interpretability of LLM outputs. DAVinCI operates in two stages: (i) it attributes generated claims to internal model components and external sources; (ii) it verifies each claim using entailment-based reasoning and confidence calibration. We evaluate DAVinCI across multiple datasets, including FEVER and CLIMATE-FEVER, and compare its performance against standard verification-only baselines. Our results show that DAVinCI significantly improves classification accuracy, attribution precision, recall, and F1-score by 5-20%. Through an extensive ablation study, we isolate the contributions of evidence span selection, recalibration thresholds, and retrieval quality. We also release a modular DAVinCI implementation that can be integrated into existing LLM pipelines. By bridging attribution and verification, DAVinCI offers a scalable path to auditable, trustworthy AI systems. This work contributes to the growing effort to make LLMs not only powerful but also accountable.

AI.tldr: DAVinCI introduces a Dual Attribution and Verification framework for LLM outputs. It attributes claims to internal model components and external sources, then verifies them via entailment reasoning and confidence calibration. Evaluated on FEVER and CLIMATE-FEVER, it yields 5-20% improvements and enables modular, auditable AI pipelines.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations and factual inaccuracies in LLMs pose risk in high-stakes domains. There is a need for interpretable, verifiable reasoning that traces claims to internal and external evidence, enabling trustable AI systems.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (i) attribution of generated claims to internal components and external sources; (ii) verification of each claim using entailment-based reasoning and confidence calibration. Includes ablation studies on evidence span selection, recalibration thresholds, and retrieval quality. Evaluated on FEVER and CLIMATE-FEVER; provides a modular implementation for integration into existing LLM pipelines.
AI.result: DAVinCI substantially improves classification accuracy, attribution precision, recall, and F1-score by 5-20% across evaluated datasets. Ablations identify contributions from evidence span selection, recalibration thresholds, and retrieval quality. A modular implementation is released for integration into LLM pipelines.
AI.conclusion: By uniting attribution with verification, DAVinCI offers a scalable path to auditable, trustworthy AI systems and contributes to the broader effort to develop accountable, high-trust LLMs.

title: SCM: Sleep-Consolidated Memory with Algorithmic Forgetting for Large Language Models
summary: We present SCM (Sleep-Consolidated Memory), a research preview of a memory architecture for large language models that draws on neuroscientific principles to address a fundamental limitation in current systems: the absence of persistent, structured, and biologically plausible memory. Existing approaches rely on truncating context windows, growing vector databases without bound, or tiered storage systems that lack consolidation and forgetting mechanisms. SCM implements five core components inspired by human memory: a limited-capacity working memory, multi-dimensional importance tagging, offline sleep-stage consolidation with distinct NREM and REM phases, intentional value-based forgetting, and a computational self-model enabling introspection. Across a standardized benchmark suite of eight tests, the prototype achieves perfect recall accuracy over ten-turn conversations while reducing memory noise by 90.9% through adaptive forgetting. Memory search latency remains below one millisecond even with hundreds of stored concepts. This work establishes the architectural foundations for memory systems that consolidate, prioritize, and forget, offering a testable platform for advancing LLM memory research.

AI.tldr: Neuroscience-inspired memory architecture for LLMs (SCM) featuring limited-capacity working memory, multi-dimensional importance tagging, sleep-based consolidation with distinct NREM/REM phases, value-based forgetting, and a self-model for introspection; demonstrates perfect recall on ten-turn conversations, large reductions in memory noise, and sub-millisecond search latency.
AI.motivation: Address the fundamental limitation of current LLM systems lacking persistent, structured, and biologically plausible memory: reliance on truncated context windows, unbounded vector stores, or tiered storage without consolidation or forgetting.
AI.method: Five core components: (1) limited-capacity working memory, (2) multi-dimensional importance tagging, (3) offline sleep-stage consolidation with distinct NREM and REM phases, (4) intentional value-based forgetting, and (5) a computational self-model enabling introspection. Evaluation uses a standardized benchmark suite of eight tests to measure recall, noise reduction, and latency.
AI.result: Perfect recall accuracy over ten-turn conversations; memory noise reduced by 90.9% via adaptive forgetting; memory search latency remains below 1 millisecond even with hundreds of stored concepts.
AI.conclusion: Establishes architectural foundations for memory systems that consolidate, prioritize, and forget, offering a testable platform for advancing LLM memory research.

title: Ufil: A Unified Framework for Infrastructure-based Localization
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, this work was submitted to IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC) 2026
summary: Infrastructure-based localization enhances road safety and traffic management by providing state estimates of road users. Development is hindered by fragmented, application-specific stacks that tightly couple perception, tracking, and middleware. We introduce Ufil, a Unified Framework for Infrastructure-Based Localization with a standardized object model and reusable multi-object tracking components. Ufil offers interfaces and reference implementations for prediction, detection, association, state update, and track management, allowing researchers to improve components without reimplementing the pipeline. Ufil is open-source C++/ROS 2 software with documentation and executable examples. We demonstrate Ufil by integrating three heterogeneous data sources into a single localization pipeline combining (i) vehicle onboard units broadcasting ETSI ITS-G5 Cooperative Awareness Messages, (ii) a lidar-based roadside sensor node, and (iii) an in-road sensitive surface layer. The pipeline runs unchanged in the CARLA simulator and a small-scale CAV testbed, demonstrating Ufil's scale-independent execution model. In a three-lane highway scenario with 423 and 355 vehicles in simulation and testbed, respectively, the fused system achieves lane-level lateral accuracy with mean lateral position RMSEs of 0.31 m in CARLA and 0.29 m in the CPM Lab, and mean absolute orientation errors around 2.2°. Median end-to-end latencies from sensing to fused output remain below 100 ms across all modalities in both environments.

AI.tldr: Ufil is an open-source, ROS 2 framework that standardizes object modeling and multi-object tracking for infrastructure-based localization, enabling modular fusion of heterogeneous data sources with sub-100 ms latency and lane-level accuracy in simulation and a lab testbed.
AI.motivation: Fragmented, tightly coupled perception–tracking–middleware stacks hinder scalable, reproducible infrastructure-based localization; a standardized, reusable component set is needed to accelerate research and deployment.
AI.method: Define a unified object model and interfaces for prediction, detection, data association, state update, and track management; provide reference implementations; integrate three heterogeneous data sources (ETSI ITS-G5 CAM, lidar roadside sensor, in-road surface layer); demonstrate across CARLA and CPM Lab with C++/ROS 2.
AI.result: Achieves lane-level lateral RMSE of ~0.31 m (CARLA) and ~0.29 m (CPM Lab); mean orientation error ~2.2°; median end-to-end latency <100 ms across modalities in both environments; pipeline remains unchanged between simulation and testbed.
AI.conclusion: Ufil enables scalable, reproducible infrastructure-based localization research by enabling component reuse and cross-domain deployment; demonstrated scale-independent execution and effective fusion of heterogeneous sensors.

title: A Probabilistic Framework for Improving Dense Object Detection in Underwater Image Data via Annealing-Based Data Augmentation
summary: Object detection models typically perform well on images captured in controlled environments with stable lighting, water clarity, and viewpoint, but their performance degrades substantially in real-world underwater settings characterized by high variability and frequent occlusions. In this work, we address these challenges by introducing a novel data augmentation framework designed to improve robustness in dense and unconstrained underwater scenes. Using the DeepFish dataset, which contains images of fish in natural environments, we first generate bounding box annotations from provided segmentation masks to construct a custom detection dataset. We then propose a pseudo-simulated annealing-based augmentation algorithm, inspired by the copy-paste strategy of Deng et al. [1], to synthesize realistic crowded fish scenarios. Our approach improves spatial diversity and object density during training, enabling better generalization to complex scenes. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms a baseline YOLOv10 model, particularly on a challenging test set of manually annotated images collected from live-stream footage in the Florida Keys. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of our augmentation strategy for improving detection performance in dense, real-world underwater environments.

AI.tldr: A data-augmentation framework using pseudo-simulated annealing and copy-paste inspired synthesis improves detection robustness for dense, unconstrained underwater scenes by converting segmentation masks in DeepFish into bounding boxes and augmenting training data, outperforming a YOLOv10 baseline on live-stream Florida Keys data.
AI.motivation: Underwater object detection suffers from high variability, occlusions, and clutter. Standard augmentation fails to capture realistic dense scenes, risking poor generalization to real underwater environments.
AI.method: Convert segmentation masks from the DeepFish dataset into bounding boxes to create a custom detection dataset. Propose a pseudo-simulated annealing-based augmentation algorithm, inspired by copy-paste strategies, to synthesize realistic crowded fish scenarios and increase spatial diversity and object density during training.
AI.result: The augmented training improves detection performance and significantly outperforms a baseline YOLOv10 model, especially on a challenging test set of manually annotated live-stream footage from the Florida Keys.
AI.conclusion: The proposed augmentation framework enhances detector robustness in dense, real-world underwater environments and demonstrates promising generalization to unconstrained underwater scenes; further work could explore broader datasets, ablation studies, and computational trade-offs.

title: Align Generative Artificial Intelligence with Human Preferences: A Novel Large Language Model Fine-Tuning Method for Online Review Management
comment: Accepted to Information Systems Research (ISR). This is a preliminary version
summary: Online reviews have played a pivotal role in consumers' decision-making processes. Existing research has highlighted the significant impact of managerial review responses on customer relationship management and firm performance. However, a large portion of online reviews remains unaddressed due to the considerable human labor required to respond to the rapid growth of online reviews. While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in a range of tasks, they are general-purpose models and may not align well with domain-specific human preferences. To tailor these general generative AI models to domain-specific applications, finetuning is commonly employed. Nevertheless, several challenges persist in finetuning with domain-specific data, including hallucinations, difficulty in representing domain-specific human preferences, and over conservatism in offline policy optimization. To address these challenges, we propose a novel preference finetuning method to align an LLM with domain-specific human preferences for generating online review responses. Specifically, we first identify the source of hallucination and propose an effective context augmentation approach to mitigate the LLM hallucination. To represent human preferences, we propose a novel theory-driven preference finetuning approach that automatically constructs human preference pairs in the online review domain. Additionally, we propose a curriculum learning approach to further enhance preference finetuning. To overcome the challenge of over conservatism in existing offline preference finetuning method, we propose a novel density estimation-based support constraint method to relax the conservatism, and we mathematically prove its superior theoretical guarantees. Extensive evaluations substantiate the superiority of our proposed preference finetuning method.

AI.tldr: A novel preference finetuning framework aligning LLMs to domain-specific human preferences for generating online review responses, addressing hallucination, domain-preference representation, and offline policy conservatism via context augmentation, theory-driven preference pair construction, curriculum learning, and density-estimation-based relaxation with theoretical guarantees; empirically validated.
AI.motivation: Online reviews heavily influence consumer decisions; scalable, high-quality responses are needed. General-purpose LLMs often misalign with domain-specific human preferences, and offline RL-based finetuning suffers from hallucinations, misrepresentation of preferences, and over-conservatism. There is a need for domain-tailored finetuning methods.
AI.method: Identify hallucination sources; apply context augmentation to mitigate hallucination; automatically construct domain-specific human preference pairs via theory-driven method; employ curriculum learning to guide training; introduce density-estimation-based support constraint to relax conservatism; provide theoretical guarantees; extensive experiments demonstrate improvements.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show superior alignment to domain-specific human preferences, reduced hallucinations, improved response quality, and better handling of online review domains compared to baselines and existing offline preference finetuning methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed preference finetuning framework effectively aligns LLMs with domain-specific human preferences for online review responses, with solid theoretical grounding and strong empirical performance, and offers a scalable solution to automated review response generation.

title: LAF-Based Evaluation and UTTL-Based Learning Strategies with MIATTs
summary: In many real-world machine learning (ML) applications, the true target cannot be precisely defined due to ambiguity or subjectivity information. To address this challenge, under the assumption that the true target for a given ML task is not assumed to exist objectively in the real world, the EL-MIATTs (Evaluation and Learning with Multiple Inaccurate True Targets) framework has been proposed. Bridging theory and practice in implementing EL-MIATTs, in this paper, we develop two complementary mechanisms: LAF (Logical Assessment Formula)-based evaluation algorithms and UTTL (Undefinable True Target Learning)-based learning strategies with MIATTs, which together enable logically coherent and practically feasible modeling under uncertain supervision. We first analyze task-specific MIATTs, examining how their coverage and diversity determine their structural property and influence downstream evaluation and learning. Based on this understanding, we formulate LAF-grounded evaluation algorithms that operate either on original MIATTs or on ternary targets synthesized from them, balancing interpretability, soundness, and completeness. For model training, we introduce UTTL-grounded learning strategies using Dice and cross-entropy loss functions, comparing per-target and aggregated optimization schemes. We also discuss how the integration of LAF and UTTL bridges the gap between logical semantics and statistical optimization. Together, these components provide a coherent pathway for implementing EL-MIATTs, offering a principled foundation for developing ML systems in scenarios where the notion of "ground truth" is inherently uncertain. An application of this work's results is presented as part of the study available at https://www.qeios.com/read/EZWLSN.

AI.tldr: Presents EL-MIATTs (Evaluation and Learning with Multiple Inaccurate True Targets) with two complementary mechanisms—LAF-based evaluation and UTTL-based learning—for handling uncertainty in target definitions. It analyzes how MIATT coverage and diversity affect evaluation and learning, proposes LAF-grounded evaluation on original MIATTs or ternary-synthesized targets, and introduces UTTL-grounded learning using Dice and cross-entropy losses with per-target versus aggregated optimization. The work bridges logical semantics and statistical optimization, offering a principled pathway for implementing EL-MIATTs and an online application link.
AI.motivation: In many ML tasks, the true target is ambiguous or subjective, so there is no objective ground truth. A framework that jointly reasons over multiple inaccurate targets and aligns logical semantics with statistical optimization is needed to train robust models under uncertain supervision.
AI.method: 1) Analyze task-specific MIATTs to understand how coverage and diversity shape structure and downstream tasks. 2) Formulate LAF-grounded evaluation algorithms operating on original MIATTs or on ternary targets synthesized from them, balancing interpretability, soundness, and completeness. 3) Develop UTTL-grounded learning strategies using Dice and cross-entropy losses, comparing per-target and aggregated optimization schemes. 4) Discuss integration of LAF and UTTL to connect logical semantics with optimization.
AI.result: The abstract outlines a cohesive framework and methodology for implementing EL-MIATTs, with theoretical development and an application link provided; specific empirical results are not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: EL-MIATTs offer a principled foundation for ML systems under uncertain supervision by combining logical assessment with statistical optimization. The paper presents compatible evaluation and learning mechanisms and points to an online study as demonstration.

title: MISTY: High-Throughput Motion Planning via Mixer-based Single-step Drifting
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Multi-modal trajectory generation is essential for safe autonomous driving, yet existing diffusion-based planners suffer from high inference latency due to iterative neural function evaluations. This paper presents MISTY (Mixer-based Inference for Single-step Trajectory-drifting Yield), a high-throughput generative motion planner that achieves state-of-the-art closed-loop performance with pure single-step inference. MISTY integrates a vectorized Sub-Graph encoder to capture environment context, a Variational Autoencoder to structure expert trajectories into a compact 32-dimensional latent manifold, and an ultra-lightweight MLP-Mixer decoder to eliminate quadratic attention complexity. Importantly, we introduce a latent-space drifting loss that shifts the complex distribution evolution entirely to the training phase. By formulating explicit attractive and repulsive forces, this mechanism empowers the model to synthesize novel, proactive maneuvers, such as active overtaking, that are virtually absent from the raw expert demonstrations. Extensive evaluations on the nuPlan benchmark demonstrate that MISTY achieves state-of-the-art results on the challenging Test14-hard split, with comprehensive scores of 80.32 and 82.21 in non-reactive and reactive settings, respectively. Operating at over 99 FPS with an end-to-end latency of 10.1 ms, MISTY offers an order-of-magnitude speedup over iterative diffusion planners while while achieving significantly robust generation.

AI.tldr: A high-throughput single-step generative motion planner (MISTY) combining vectorized environment encoding, a 32-dimensional latent VAE for expert trajectories, and an ultra-lightweight MLP-Mixer decoder, with latent-space drifting loss to shift distribution evolution into training; achieves state-of-the-art nuPlan results with 99+ FPS latency.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference latency in diffusion-based multi-modal trajectory planners while maintaining or improving performance, enabling fast and proactive maneuvers in autonomous driving.
AI.method: Use a vectorized Sub-Graph encoder for environment context, a Variational Autoencoder to compress expert trajectories into a 32D latent space, and an ultra-lightweight MLP-Mixer decoder. Introduce a latent-space drifting loss that transfers distribution evolution to training and define attractive/repulsive forces to promote proactive maneuvers like overtaking.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuPlan Test14-hard in non-reactive (80.32) and reactive (82.21) settings, with end-to-end latency of 10.1 ms and over 99 FPS, offering an order-of-magnitude speedup over iterative diffusion planners and improved robustness.
AI.conclusion: Single-step, diffusion-free trajectory generation via latent-space drift and lightweight decoding enables high-throughput, robust motion planning that can produce novel proactive maneuvers absent in raw data.

title: Sparse Forcing: Native Trainable Sparse Attention for Real-time Autoregressive Diffusion Video Generation
summary: We introduce Sparse Forcing, a training-and-inference paradigm for autoregressive video diffusion models that improves long-horizon generation quality while reducing decoding latency. Sparse Forcing is motivated by an empirical observation in autoregressive diffusion rollouts: attention concentrates on a persistent subset of salient visual blocks, forming an implicit spatiotemporal memory in the KV cache, and exhibits a locally structured block-sparse pattern within sliding windows. Building on this observation, we propose a trainable native sparsity mechanism that learns to compress, preserve, and update these persistent blocks while restricting computation within each local window to a dynamically selected local neighborhood. To make the approach practical at scale for both training and inference, we further propose Persistent Block-Sparse Attention (PBSA), an efficient GPU kernel that accelerates sparse attention and memory updates for low-latency, memory-efficient decoding. Experiments show that Sparse Forcing improves the VBench score by +0.26 over Self-Forcing on 5-second text-to-video generation while delivering a 1.11-1.17x decoding speedup and 42% lower peak KV-cache footprint. The gains are more pronounced on longer-horizon rollouts, delivering improved visual quality with +0.68 and +2.74 VBench improvements, and 1.22x and 1.27x speedups on 20-second and 1-minute generations, respectively.

AI.tldr: Sparse Forcing introduces trainable native sparsity and a Persistent Block-Sparse Attention (PBSA) kernel for autoregressive video diffusion, achieving better long-horizon quality and lower latency by exploiting a persistent, locally structured KV-cache block sparsity.
AI.motivation: Attention in autoregressive diffusion rollouts concentrates on a persistent subset of visual blocks, creating an implicit spatiotemporal memory in the KV cache with a local block-sparse pattern. Exploiting this structure can reduce computation and improve decoding efficiency.
AI.method: A trainable sparsity mechanism compresses, preserves, and updates persistent blocks while restricting per-window computation to a chosen local neighborhood; PBSA GPU kernel accelerates sparse attention and KV-cache updates for low-latency decoding.
AI.result: On 5-second text-to-video generation, Sparse Forcing improves VBench by +0.26 over Self-Forcing, with 1.11-1.17x decoding speedup and 42% lower peak KV-cache footprint. Gains are larger for longer horizons: +0.68 and +2.74 in VBench, and 1.22x and 1.27x speedups for 20-second and 1-minute generations.
AI.conclusion: Sparse Forcing provides a scalable, practical approach for training and inference in autoregressive video diffusion by leveraging persistent local sparsity; the PBSA kernel enables efficient, low-latency decoding, with pronounced benefits at longer horizons.

title: ReCAPA: Hierarchical Predictive Correction to Mitigate Cascading Failures
summary: Vision-Language-Action systems follow instructions to execute multi-step tasks in multimodal environments. Recent VLA approaches typically rely on post-hoc correction mechanisms or operate under fixed task decompositions and alignment schemes. However, once an intermediate step is mis-specified, local errors propagate through subsequent steps and eventually accumulate into cascading failures. To mitigate this compounding effect, we propose Predictive Alignment and Planning Architecture, a framework that uses prediction and contrast to adjust deviations across three levels: actions, subgoals, and trajectories. Semantic alignment is enforced at all levels using a Sinkhorn-based module and a Score-field module. The predictive correction and alignment jointly update the action generator during training, enabling it to adjust fine-grained steps to remain aligned with the overall intent. We further introduce two new metrics to quantify error propagation and recovery processes in tasks, capturing how mistakes spread and fade over long-horizon execution. Experiments show that ReCAPA achieves competitive results on embodied agent benchmarks such as VisualAgentBench, MineDojo, and AI2-THOR, outperforming strong proprietary and open-source Large Language Model baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReCAPA, a predictive alignment and planning framework for Vision-Language-Action tasks that mitigates cascading errors by aligning actions, subgoals, and trajectories through prediction, contrast, and semantic alignment modules (Sinkhorn and Score-field). Introduces two metrics for error propagation and recovery and achieves competitive results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cascading errors in multi-step VLA systems arise when a mis-specified intermediate step propagates and compounds across subsequent steps, especially under fixed task decompositions and alignment schemes. There is a need for predictive correction and cross-level alignment to maintain adherence to overall intent.
AI.method: Introduce Predictive Alignment and Planning Architecture (ReCAPA) that uses prediction and contrast to adjust deviations across actions, subgoals, and trajectories. Enforce semantic alignment at all levels via a Sinkhorn-based module and a Score-field module. Train the action generator with predictive correction and alignment to adapt fine-grained steps to the global objective. Propose two new metrics to quantify error propagation and recovery in long-horizon tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows ReCAPA achieving competitive performance on embodied benchmarks such as VisualAgentBench, MineDojo, and AI2-THOR, outperforming strong proprietary and open-source LLM baselines.
AI.conclusion: ReCAPA provides a robust framework to mitigate cascading errors in VLA by predictive alignment across multiple levels, improving long-horizon execution and generalization across diverse embodied AI benchmarks.

title: X2-N: A Transformable Wheel-legged Humanoid Robot with Dual-mode Locomotion and Manipulation
summary:
Wheel-legged robots combine the efficiency of wheeled locomotion with the versatility of legged systems, enabling rapid traversal over both continuous and discrete terrains. However, conventional designs typically employ fixed wheels as feet and limited degrees of freedom (DoFs) at the hips, resulting in reduced stability and mobility during legged locomotion compared to humanoids with flat feet. In addition, most existing platforms lack a full upper body with arms, which limits their ability to perform dexterous manipulation tasks.
  In this letter, we present X2-N, a high-DoF transformable robot with dual-mode locomotion and manipulation. X2-N can operate in both humanoid and wheel-legged forms and transform seamlessly between them through joint reconfiguration. We further propose a reinforcement learning (RL)-based whole-body control framework tailored to this morphology, enabling unified control across hybrid locomotion, transformation, and manipulation. We validate X2-N in a range of challenging locomotion and manipulation tasks, including dynamic skating-like motion, stair climbing and package delivery. Results demonstrate high locomotion efficiency, strong terrain adaptability, and stable loco-manipulation performance of X2-N, highlighting its potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Introduces X2-N, a high-DoF transformable wheel-legged robot with dual-mode locomotion and full upper body, controlled by an RL-based whole-body controller; demonstrated on dynamic skating-like motion, stair climbing, and package delivery.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap between efficient wheeled locomotion and versatile legged manipulation by providing high DoF in an integrated humanoid-wheeled morphology with seamless transformation, enabling robust locomotion and dexterous manipulation on mixed terrains.
AI.method: Design and realize X2-N with reconfigurable joints enabling seamless morphing between humanoid and wheel-legged forms; develop a reinforcement learning-based whole-body control framework that unifies locomotion, transformation, and manipulation across modes; validate on diverse tasks.
AI.result: X2-N achieves high locomotion efficiency, broad terrain adaptability, and stable loco-manipulation performance; demonstrates successful cross-mode transformation and competent manipulation in challenging tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility and potential of transformable, dual-mode humanoid/wheeled robots with unified RL control for real-world deployment, while leaving open challenges in hardware complexity, energy efficiency, and reliability across rapid morphing and real-world variability.

title: UAU-Net: Uncertainty-aware Representation Learning and Evidential Classification for Facial Action Unit Detection
summary: Facial action unit (AU) detection remains challenging because it involves heterogeneous, AU-specific uncertainties arising at both the representation and decision stages. Recent methods have improved discriminative feature learning, but they often treat the AU representations as deterministic, overlooking uncertainty caused by visual noise, subject-dependent appearance variations, and ambiguous inter-AU relationships, all of which can substantially degrade robustness. Meanwhile, conventional point-estimation classifiers often provide poorly calibrated confidence, producing overconfident predictions, especially under the severe label imbalance typical of AU datasets. We propose UAU-Net, an Uncertainty-aware AU detection framework that explicitly models uncertainty at both stages. At the representation stage, we introduce CV-AFE, a conditional VAE (CVAE)-based AU feature extraction module that learns probabilistic AU representations by jointly estimating feature means and variances across multiple spatio-temporal scales; conditioning on AU labels further enables CV-AFE to capture uncertainty associated with inter-AU dependencies. At the decision stage, we design AB-ENN, an Asymmetric Beta Evidential Neural Network for multi-label AU detection, which parameterizes predictive uncertainty with Beta distributions and mitigates overconfidence via an asymmetric loss tailored to highly imbalanced binary labels. Extensive experiments on BP4D and DISFA show that UAU-Net achieves strong AU detection performance, and further analyses indicate that modeling uncertainty in both representation learning and evidential prediction improves robustness and reliability.

AI.tldr: UAU-Net combines probabilistic AU representations via a CVAE-based CV-AFE module and an evidential, asymmetric Beta classifier AB-ENN to improve uncertainty estimation and calibration in multi-label facial AU detection, showing gains on BP4D and DISFA.
AI.motivation: AU detection faces representation-level and decision-level uncertainties due to visual noise, subject-specific appearance variation, and ambiguous inter-AU relationships, plus severe label imbalance causing overconfident predictions. Uncertainty modeling aims to improve robustness and calibration.
AI.method: CV-AFE: a conditional VAE-based AU feature extractor that learns probabilistic AU representations (means and variances) across multiple spatio-temporal scales, conditioned on AU labels to capture inter-AU dependencies. AB-ENN: Asymmetric Beta Evidential Neural Network that parameterizes predictive uncertainty with Beta distributions and uses an asymmetric loss to mitigate overconfidence under imbalanced binary labels in a multi-label setting.
AI.result: On BP4D and DISFA, UAU-Net achieves strong AU detection performance. Analyses show that modeling uncertainty in both representation learning and evidential prediction improves robustness and reliability, with ablations highlighting the contribution of each component.
AI.conclusion: Modeling uncertainty at both representation and decision stages yields more robust and reliable facial AU detection; UAU-Net demonstrates effectiveness for multi-label AU detection under challenging conditions.

title: Robustness Analysis of POMDP Policies to Observation Perturbations
summary: Policies for Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) are often designed using a nominal system model. In practice, this model can deviate from the true system during deployment due to factors such as calibration drift or sensor degradation, leading to unexpected performance degradation. This work studies policy robustness against deviations in the POMDP observation model. We introduce the Policy Observation Robustness Problem: to determine the maximum tolerable deviation in a POMDP's observation model that guarantees the policy's value remains above a specified threshold. We analyze two variants: the sticky variant, where deviations are dependent on state and actions, and the non-sticky variant, where they can be history-dependent. We show that the Policy Observation Robustness Problem can be formulated as a bi-level optimization problem in which the inner optimization is monotonic in the size of the observation deviation. This enables efficient solutions using root-finding algorithms in the outer optimization. For the non-sticky variant, we show that when policies are represented with finite-state controllers (FSCs) it is sufficient to consider observations which depend on nodes in the FSC rather than full histories. We present Robust Interval Search, an algorithm with soundness and convergence guarantees, for both the sticky and non-sticky variants. We show this algorithm has polynomial time complexity in the non-sticky variant and at most exponential time complexity in the sticky variant. We provide experimental results validating and demonstrating the scalability of implementations of Robust Interval Search to POMDP problems with tens of thousands of states. We also provide case studies from robotics and operations research which demonstrate the practical utility of the problem and algorithms.

AI.tldr: Policy Observation Robustness Problem in POMDPs quantifies how much the observation model can deviate before a policy's value falls below a threshold; introduces Robust Interval Search for sticky and non-sticky deviations, with FSC-based efficiency for the non-sticky case and polynomial/exponential complexity guarantees; validated on large-scale POMDPs and robotics/OR case studies.
AI.motivation: Practical deployment of POMDP-based policies is sensitive to observation-model drift (calibration, sensor degradation). A formal robustness notion with guarantees is needed to ensure performance under such deviations.
AI.method: Formulate as a bi-level optimization: inner problem is monotone in deviation size; outer problem solved via root finding. For non-sticky deviations, prove sufficiency of observations dependent on FSC nodes. Develop Robust Interval Search with soundness and convergence guarantees for both variants; analyze computational complexity (polynomial in non-sticky; at most exponential in sticky).
AI.result: Robust Interval Search provides sound, convergent estimates of maximal tolerable observation deviation. The algorithm scales to POMDPs with tens of thousands of states and offers practical utility through experiments and robotics/OR case studies.
