AI.conclusion: The Policy Observation Robustness framework enables tractable robustness analysis for POMDP policies under observation-model drift, with concrete complexity guarantees and scalable algorithms; FSC-based simplifications extend applicability to history-dependent perturbations.

title: Differentially Private Model Merging
summary: In machine learning applications, privacy requirements during inference or deployment time could change constantly due to varying policies, regulations, or user experience. In this work, we aim to generate a magnitude of models to satisfy any target differential privacy (DP) requirement without additional training steps, given a set of existing models trained on the same dataset with different privacy/utility tradeoffs. We propose two post processing techniques, namely random selection and linear combination, to output a final private model for any target privacy parameter. We provide privacy accounting of these approaches from the lens of R'enyi DP and privacy loss distributions for general problems. In a case study on private mean estimation, we fully characterize the privacy/utility results and theoretically establish the superiority of linear combination over random selection. Empirically, we validate our approach and analyses on several models and both synthetic and real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: Given a family of models trained with varying DP/utility tradeoffs on a shared dataset, the paper proposes two post-processing methods (random selection and linear combination) to produce a final model meeting any target DP parameter without retraining, with Renyi DP and privacy loss distributions used for privacy accounting; in a private mean estimation case study, linear combination is theoretically superior to random selection; empirically validated on multiple models and datasets.
AI.motivation: Privacy requirements during deployment can vary; retraining to meet new DP targets is costly. The goal is to enable post hoc generation of a model satisfying any target DP level from existing models with different privacy/utility settings.
AI.method: Introduce two post-processing techniques: (1) random selection of one model from the set, (2) linear combination of the models. Derive privacy accounting using Rényi DP and privacy loss distributions for general problems. Conduct a case study on private mean estimation to compare the two approaches, showing linear combination outperforms random selection. Validate empirically on several models and datasets (synthetic and real-world).
AI.result: Characterization of privacy-utility tradeoffs for the two post-processing methods; theoretical demonstration that linear combination offers superior privacy-utility than random selection in the mean estimation case; empirical validation across multiple models/datasets supports the methods' effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Post-processing across a family of pre-trained models enables meeting any target DP level without additional training. Linear combination is generally superior to random selection for achieving favorable privacy-utility tradeoffs; the approach is empirically supported and grounded in Renyi DP/privacy loss distributions.

title: A Bayesian Reasoning Framework for Robotic Systems in Autonomous Casualty Triage
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Autonomous robots deployed in mass casualty incidents (MCI) face the challenge of making critical decisions based on incomplete and noisy perceptual data. We present an autonomous robotic system for casualty assessment that fuses outputs from multiple vision-based algorithms, estimating signs of severe hemorrhage, visible trauma, or physical alertness, into a coherent triage assessment. At the core of our system is a Bayesian network, constructed from expert-defined rules, which enables probabilistic reasoning about a casualty's condition even with missing or conflicting sensory inputs. The system, evaluated during the DARPA Triage Challenge (DTC) in realistic MCI scenarios involving 11 and 9 casualties, demonstrated a nearly three-fold improvement in physiological assessment accuracy (from 15\% to 42\% and 19\% to 46\%) compared to a vision-only baseline. More importantly, overall triage accuracy increased from 14\% to 53\%, while the diagnostic coverage of the system expanded from 31\% to 95\% of cases. These results demonstrate that integrating expert-guided probabilistic reasoning with advanced vision-based sensing can significantly enhance the reliability and decision-making capabilities of autonomous systems in critical real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Bayesian-network fusion of multi-sensor vision cues improves casualty assessment and triage in MCI, outperforming vision-only baselines in DARPA DTC scenarios.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable casualty assessment under noisy/incomplete perceptual data by combining expert-derived probabilistic reasoning with vision-based sensing.
AI.method: Fuse outputs from multiple vision algorithms via a Bayesian network built from expert-defined rules; handle missing/conflicting inputs; evaluate on DARPA Triage Challenge with 11- and 9-casualty scenarios.
AI.result: Physiological assessment accuracy improved from 15% to 42% (11-casualty run) and 19% to 46% (9-casualty run); overall triage accuracy increased from 14% to 53%; diagnostic coverage expanded from 31% to 95%.
AI.conclusion: Integrating expert-guided probabilistic reasoning with vision-based sensing substantially enhances decision reliability and performance of autonomous triage systems in real-world MCIs.

title: LatRef-Diff: Latent and Reference-Guided Diffusion for Facial Attribute Editing and Style Manipulation
summary: Facial attribute editing and style manipulation are crucial for applications like virtual avatars and photo editing. However, achieving precise control over facial attributes without altering unrelated features is challenging due to the complexity of facial structures and the strong correlations between attributes. While conditional GANs have shown progress, they are limited by accuracy issues and training instability. Diffusion models, though promising, face challenges in style manipulation due to the limited expressiveness of semantic directions. In this paper, we propose LatRef-Diff, a novel diffusion-based framework that addresses these limitations. We replace the traditional semantic directions in diffusion models with style codes and propose two methods for generating them: latent and reference guidance. Based on these style codes, we design a style modulation module that integrates them into the target image, enabling both random and customized style manipulation. This module incorporates learnable vectors, cross-attention mechanisms, and a hierarchical design to improve accuracy and image quality. Additionally, to enhance training stability while eliminating the need for paired images (e.g., before and after editing), we propose a forward-backward consistency training strategy. This strategy first removes the target attribute approximately using image-specific semantic directions and then restores it via style modulation, guided by perceptual and classification losses. Extensive experiments on CelebA-HQ demonstrate that LatRef-Diff achieves state-of-the-art performance in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of our model's design choices.

AI.tldr: LatRef-Diff is a diffusion-based facial attribute editing framework that replaces semantic directions with learnable style codes, using latent or reference guidance and a style modulation module, plus forward-backward consistency training, achieving state-of-the-art results on CelebA-HQ.
AI.motivation: Precise control over facial attributes without altering unrelated features is challenging due to complex facial structures and attribute correlations. GANs suffer from training instability and accuracy limits; diffusion models struggle with expressive semantic directions for style manipulation. A reusable, unsupervised, high-quality editing framework is needed.
AI.method: Replace semantic directions in diffusion models with style codes. Generate style codes via two approaches: latent guidance (learned from latent space) and reference guidance (from a reference image). Introduce a style modulation module with learnable vectors, cross-attention, and a hierarchical design to fuse style codes into the target image for both random and customized edits. Employ forward-backward consistency training: first approximate removal of the target attribute using image-specific semantic directions, then restore via style modulation, guided by perceptual and classification losses to enable training without paired data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CelebA-HQ show LatRef-Diff achieves state-of-the-art performance in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each design choice (style codes, guidance strategies, and the style modulation module).
AI.conclusion: LatRef-Diff delivers accurate, high-quality, controllable facial attribute editing with unsupervised training and robust style manipulation, addressing limitations of prior diffusion and GAN-based approaches; the approach offers flexible control via latent or reference guidance and stabilizes training through forward-backward consistency.

title: Trustworthy Clinical Decision Support Using Meta-Predicates and Domain-Specific Languages
summary:
\textbf{Background:} Regulatory frameworks for AI in healthcare, including the EU AI Act and FDA guidance on AI/ML-based medical devices, require clinical decision support to demonstrate not only accuracy but auditability. Existing formal languages for clinical logic validate syntactic and structural correctness but not whether decision rules use epistemologically appropriate evidence.
  \textbf{Methods:} Drawing on design-by-contract principles, we introduce meta-predicates -- predicates about predicates -- for asserting epistemological constraints on clinical decision rules expressed in a DSL. An epistemological type system classifies annotations along four dimensions: purpose, knowledge domain, scale, and method of acquisition. Meta-predicates assert which evidence types are permissible in any given rule. The framework is instantiated in AnFiSA, an open-source platform for genetic variant curation, and demonstrated using the Brigham Genomics Medicine protocol on 5.6 million variants from the Genome in a Bottle benchmark.
  \textbf{Results:} Decision trees used in variant interpretation can be reformulated as unate cascades, enabling per-variant audit trails that identify which rule classified each variant and why. Meta-predicate validation catches epistemological errors before deployment, whether rules are human-written or AI-generated. The approach complements post-hoc methods such as LIME and SHAP: where explanation reveals what evidence was used after the fact, meta-predicates constrain what evidence may be used before deployment, while preserving human readability.
  \textbf{Conclusions:} Meta-predicate validation is a step toward demonstrating not only that decisions are accurate but that they rest on appropriate evidence in ways that can be independently audited. While demonstrated in genomics, the approach generalises to any domain requiring auditable decision logic.

AI.tldr: Meta-predicate framework for epistemological constraints in clinical decision rules; enables pre-deployment auditability and per-variant audit trails in genomics, implemented in AnFiSA on Genome in a Bottle data.
AI.motivation: Regulatory AI/ML in healthcare requires not only accuracy but auditability and epistemological soundness of decision rules; existing DSLs ensure syntax but not evidence provenance.
AI.method: Design-by-contract-inspired meta-predicates (predicates about predicates) enforce epistemological constraints. An epistemological type system classifies annotations by four dimensions (purpose, knowledge domain, scale, method of acquisition). Meta-predicates restrict permissible evidence for any rule. Implemented in AnFiSA and validated on the Brigham Genomics Medicine protocol with 5.6 million variants from Genome in a Bottle, reformulating decision trees as unate cascades to yield per-variant audit trails.
AI.result: Decision trees reframed as unate cascades enable per-variant audit trails indicating which rule classified each variant and why. Meta-predicate validation detects epistemological errors before deployment for both human-written and AI-generated rules. Serves alongside post-hoc explanations (LIME/SHAP) by constraining evidence upfront while preserving human readability.
AI.conclusion: Meta-predicate validation advances auditable, evidence-grounded decision-making and is generalizable beyond genomics to any domain requiring auditable decision logic.

title: Droplet-LNO: Physics-Informed Laplace Neural Operators for Accurate Prediction of Droplet Spreading Dynamics on Complex Surfaces
summary: Spreading of liquid droplets on solid substrates constitutes a classic multiphysics problem with widespread applications ranging from inkjet printing, spray cooling, to biomedical microfluidic systems. Yet, accurate computational fluid dynamic (CFD) simulations are prohibitively expensive, taking more than 18 to 24 hours for each transient computation. In this paper, Physics-Informed Laplace Operator Neural Network (PI-LNO) is introduced, representing a novel architecture where the Laplace integral transform function serves as a learned physics-informed functional basis. Extensive comparative benchmark studies were performed against five other state-of-the-art approaches: UNet, UNet with attention modules (UNet-AM), DeepONet, Physics-Informed UNet (PI-UNet), and Laplace Neural Operator (LNO). Through complex Laplace transforms, PI-LNO natively models the exponential transient dynamics of the spreading process. A TensorFlow-based PI-LNO is trained on multi-surface CFD data spanning contact angles $θ_s ε[20,160]$, employing a physics-regularized composite loss combining data fidelity (MSE, MAE, RMSE) with Navier-Stokes, Cahn-Hilliard, and causality constraints.

AI.tldr: PI-LNO introduces a physics-informed neural operator for droplet spreading using the Laplace transform as a learned basis, aiming to efficiently surrogate CFD transient dynamics. It is benchmarked against five baselines on multi-surface CFD data spanning contact angles, with a physics-regularized loss combining data fidelity with Navier–Stokes, Cahn–Hilliard, and causality constraints.
AI.motivation: Horizontal scale: long CFD runtimes (18–24 h per transient) for droplet spreading on solids; need for accurate, fast surrogate models that respect underlying physics.
AI.method: Propose Physics-Informed Laplace Operator Neural Network (PI-LNO) where the Laplace integral transform function serves as a learned physics-informed basis. TensorFlow-based PI-LNO trained on multi-surface CFD data with contact angles θ_s in [20,160] degrees. Loss is a physics-regularized composite combining data fidelity terms (MSE, MAE, RMSE) with Navier-Stokes, Cahn-Hilliard, and causality constraints.
AI.result: Conducted extensive benchmarks against UNet, UNet-AM, DeepONet, PI-UNet, and Laplace Neural Operator (LNO). PI-LNO’s Laplace-based formulation enables native modelling of exponential transient dynamics in spreading. Specific numerical results are not provided in the abstract; the work reports favorable benchmarking against the five baselines.
AI.conclusion: PI-LNO represents a promising direction for fast, physics-consistent surrogates of multiphysics droplet-spreading CFD, capable of capturing exponential transients and generalizing across surface contact angles; future work could extend to other multiphysics problems and address data requirements and extrapolation limits.

title: SLAM as a Stochastic Control Problem with Partial Information: Optimal Solutions and Rigorous Approximations
summary: Simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) is a foundational state estimation problem in robotics in which a robot accurately constructs a map of its environment while also localizing itself within this construction. We study the active SLAM problem through the lens of optimal stochastic control, thereby recasting it as a decision-making problem under partial information. After reviewing several commonly studied models, we present a general stochastic control formulation of active SLAM together with a rigorous treatment of motion, sensing, and map representation. We introduce a new exploration stage cost that encodes the geometry of the state when evaluating information-gathering actions. This formulation, constructed as a nonstandard partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), is then analyzed to derive rigorously justified approximate solutions that are near-optimal. To enable this analysis, the associated regularity conditions are studied under general assumptions that apply to a wide range of robotics applications. For a particular case, we conduct an extensive numerical study in which standard learning algorithms are used to learn near-optimal policies.

AI.tldr: Active SLAM formulated as a stochastic control/POMDP with a new exploration cost; derives near-optimal approximations, studies regularity under broad assumptions, and validates via learning-based numerical study.
AI.motivation: Address active SLAM through principled decision-making under partial information, providing a rigorous, general framework that links motion, sensing, and map representation.
AI.method: Develop a general stochastic control formulation for active SLAM, including motion, sensing, and map models; introduce an exploration-stage cost encoding the geometry of the state; treat as a nonstandard POMDP; derive regularity conditions; obtain approximate near-optimal solutions; analyze under broad assumptions; conduct extensive numerical study using standard learning algorithms to learn near-optimal policies.
AI.result: Rigorous justification of near-optimal approximations; regularity results under general assumptions; numerical evidence in a particular case showing that learning algorithms can yield near-optimal policies.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a general, theoretically grounded approach to active SLAM and demonstrates practical viability of learning-based near-optimal policies within a POMDP formulation.

title: ImageHD: Energy-Efficient On-Device Continual Learning of Visual Representations via Hyperdimensional Computing
summary:
On-device continual learning (CL) is critical for edge AI systems operating on non-stationary data streams, but most existing methods rely on backpropagation or exemplar-heavy classifiers, incurring substantial compute, memory, and latency overheads. Hyperdimensional computing (HDC) offers a lightweight alternative through fast, non-iterative online updates. Combined with a compact convolutional neural network (CNN) feature extractor, HDC enables efficient on-device adaptation with strong visual representations. However, prior HDC-based CL systems often depend on multi-tier memory hierarchies and complex cluster management, limiting deployability on resource-constrained hardware.
  We present ImageHD, an FPGA accelerator for on-device continual learning of visual data based on HDC. ImageHD targets streaming CL under strict latency and on-chip memory constraints, avoiding costly iterative optimization. At the algorithmic level, we introduce a hardware-aware CL method that bounds class exemplars through a unified exemplar memory and a hardware-efficient cluster merging strategy, while incorporating a quantized CNN front-end to reduce deployment overhead without sacrificing accuracy. At the system level, ImageHD is implemented as a streaming dataflow architecture on the AMD Zynq ZCU104 FPGA, integrating HDC encoding, similarity search, and bounded cluster management using word-packed binary hypervectors for massively parallel bitwise computation within tight on-chip resource budgets. On CORe50, ImageHD achieves up to 40.4x (4.84x) speedup and 383x (105.1x) energy efficiency over optimized CPU (GPU) baselines, demonstrating the practicality of HDC-enabled continual learning for real-time edge AI.

AI.tldr: ImageHD is an FPGA-based on-device continual learning system using hyperdimensional computing, featuring a hardware-aware exemplar memory and cluster-merging method with a quantized CNN front-end, enabling streaming CL with tight latency and memory constraints.
AI.motivation: Enable real-time continual learning on edge devices without backpropagation or large exemplar stores, addressing compute, memory, and energy bottlenecks in non-stationary data streams.
AI.method: Hardware-aware continual learning using a unified bounded exemplar memory and a hardware-efficient cluster merging strategy, plus a quantized CNN front-end; HDC encoding with word-packed binary hypervectors and a streaming dataflow implementation on AMD Zynq ZCU104 that integrates HDC encoding, similarity search, and bounded cluster management.
AI.result: On CORe50, up to 40.4x speedup and 383x energy efficiency versus optimized CPU baselines; 4.84x speedup and 105.1x energy efficiency versus GPU baselines, demonstrating practical HDC-enabled continual learning for real-time edge AI.
AI.conclusion: ImageHD demonstrates that HDC-based continual learning can meet real-time edge requirements on resource-constrained hardware, combining algorithmic hardware-awareness with a streaming FPGA architecture.

title: Enhancing Online Recruitment with Category-Aware MoE and LLM-based Data Augmentation
summary: Person-Job Fit (PJF) is a critical component for online recruitment. Existing approaches face several challenges, particularly in handling low-quality job descriptions and similar candidate-job pairs, which impair model performance. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a large language model (LLM) based method with two novel techniques: (1) LLM-based data augmentation, which polishes and rewrites low-quality job descriptions by leveraging chain-of-thought (COT) prompts, and (2) category-aware Mixture of Experts (MoE) that assists in identifying similar candidate-job pairs. This MoE module incorporates category embeddings to dynamically assign weights to the experts and learns more distinguishable patterns for similar candidate-job pairs. We perform offline evaluations and online A/B tests on our recruitment platform. Our method relatively surpasses existing methods by 2.40% in AUC and 7.46% in GAUC, and boosts click-through conversion rate (CTCVR) by 19.4% in online tests, saving millions of CNY in external headhunting expenses.

AI.tldr: LLM-driven improvements to Person-Job Fit (PJF) via data augmentation of job descriptions and a category-aware Mixture-of-Experts, achieving notable online/offline performance gains and cost savings.
AI.motivation: Address poor performance caused by low-quality job descriptions and highly similar candidate–job pairs in PJF systems, which hinder model discrimination and matching.
AI.method: (1) LLM-based data augmentation: polish and rewrite low-quality job descriptions using chain-of-thought prompts to generate better textual inputs. (2) Category-aware MoE: dynamic weighting of experts with category embeddings to better distinguish similar candidate–job pairs.
AI.result: Offline evaluations and online A/B tests show improvements: 2.40% AUC, 7.46% GAUC, and 19.4% CTCVR, with substantial savings in external headhunting costs (millions of CNY).
AI.conclusion: The combination of LLM-based data refinement and category-aware MoE yields meaningful performance gains in PJF systems and can reduce external hiring costs; further work could explore robustness, latency, and transferability across domains.

title: SGD at the Edge of Stability: The Stochastic Sharpness Gap
summary:
When training neural networks with full-batch gradient descent (GD) and step size $η$, the largest eigenvalue of the Hessian -- the sharpness $S(\boldsymbolθ)$ -- rises to $2/η$ and hovers there, a phenomenon termed the Edge of Stability (EoS). \citet{damian2023selfstab} showed that this behavior is explained by a self-stabilization mechanism driven by third-order structure of the loss, and that GD implicitly follows projected gradient descent (PGD) on the constraint $ S(\boldsymbolθ)\leq 2/η$. For mini-batch stochastic gradient descent (SGD), the sharpness stabilizes below $2/η$, with the gap widening as the batch size decreases; yet no theoretical explanation exists for this suppression.
  We introduce stochastic self-stabilization, extending the self-stabilization framework to SGD. Our key insight is that gradient noise injects variance into the oscillatory dynamics along the top Hessian eigenvector, strengthening the cubic sharpness-reducing force and shifting the equilibrium below $2/η$. Following the approach of \citet{damian2023selfstab}, we define stochastic predicted dynamics relative to a moving projected gradient descent trajectory and prove a stochastic coupling theorem that bounds the deviation of SGD from these predictions. We derive a closed-form equilibrium sharpness gap: $ΔS = ηβσ_{\boldsymbol{u}}^{2}/(4α)$, where $α$ is the progressive sharpening rate, $β$ is the self-stabilization strength, and $σ_{ \boldsymbol{u}}^{2}$ is the gradient noise variance projected onto the top eigenvector. This formula predicts that smaller batch sizes yield flatter solutions and recovers GD when the batch equals the full dataset.

AI.tldr: SGD lowers the edge of stability (EoS) below 2/η due to gradient noise, via a stochastic self-stabilization mechanism. The authors derive a closed-form equilibrium sharpness gap ΔS = ηβσ_u^2/(4α) and show SGD samples converge to a projected dynamics near a moving boundary, recovering full-batch GD when batch size is complete.
AI.motivation: Explain why SGD stabilizes below the EoS bound observed in full-batch gradient descent, and extend the self-stabilization framework to stochastic optimization.
AI.method: Extend the self-stabilization framework to SGD by introducing stochastic predicted dynamics relative to a moving projected gradient descent trajectory; prove a stochastic coupling theorem bounding SGD's deviation from the predictions; derive the equilibrium sharpness gap formula.
AI.result: Derivation of a closed-form equilibrium sharpness gap ΔS = ηβσ_u^2/(4α); demonstration that smaller batch sizes yield flatter solutions; the theory recovers GD in the full-batch limit.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic self-stabilization explains why SGD operates below the EoS bound, quantifying how gradient noise interacts with progressive sharpening to set a lower equilibrium sharpness. Provides a principled link between batch size, noise, and sharpness.

title: Effects of Swarm Size Variability on Operator Workload
summary: Real-world deployments of human--swarm teams depend on balancing operator workload to leverage human strengths without inducing overload. A key challenge is that swarm size is often dynamic: robots may join or leave the mission due to failures or redeployment, causing abrupt workload fluctuations. Understanding how such changes affect human workload and performance is critical for robust human--swarm interaction design. This paper investigates how the magnitude and direction of changes in swarm size influence operator workload. Drawing on the concept of workload history, we test three hypotheses: (1) workload remains elevated following decreases in swarm size, (2) small increases are more manageable than large jumps, and (3) sufficiently large changes override these effects by inducing a cognitive reset. We conducted two studies (N = 34) using a monitoring task with simulated drone swarms of varying sizes. By varying the swarm size between episodes, we measured perceived workload relative to swarm size changes. Results show that objective performance is largely unaffected by small changes in swarm size, while subjective workload is sensitive to both change direction and magnitude. Small increases preserve lower workload, whereas small decreases leave workload elevated, indicating workload residue; large changes in either direction attenuate these effects, suggesting a reset response. These findings offer actionable guidance for managing swarm-size transitions to support operator workload in dynamic human--swarm systems.

AI.tldr: Small, dynamic changes in swarm size have minimal effect on objective performance but modulate operator workload; increases are easier to manage than decreases; large changes trigger a cognitive reset, attenuating residual effects.
AI.motivation: To understand how dynamic swarm size impacts human operator workload and performance, enabling safer, more robust human–swarm systems.
AI.method: Two studies (N=34) using a monitoring task with simulated drone swarms; episodes with varying swarm sizes; measured objective performance and subjective workload; analyzed how magnitude and direction of size changes affect workload history.
AI.result: Objective performance largely unaffected by small changes; subjective workload sensitive to direction and magnitude; small increases keep workload low, small decreases leave workload elevated; large changes in either direction dampen effects via a reset.
AI.conclusion: Insights inform management of swarm-size transitions to sustain operator workload; support design of dynamic H-S interfaces and procedures that minimize workload spikes and leverage cognitive reset during transitions.

title: AttDiff-GAN: A Hybrid Diffusion-GAN Framework for Facial Attribute Editing
summary: Facial attribute editing aims to modify target attributes while preserving attribute-irrelevant content and overall image fidelity. Existing GAN-based methods provide favorable controllability, but often suffer from weak alignment between style codes and attribute semantics. Diffusion-based methods can synthesize highly realistic images; however, their editing precision is limited by the entanglement of semantic directions among different attributes. In this paper, we propose AttDiff-GAN, a hybrid framework that combines GAN-based attribute manipulation with diffusion-based image generation. A key challenge in such integration lies in the inconsistency between one-step adversarial learning and multi-step diffusion denoising, which makes effective optimization difficult. To address this issue, we decouple attribute editing from image synthesis by introducing a feature-level adversarial learning scheme to learn explicit attribute manipulation, and then using the manipulated features to guide the diffusion process for image generation, while also removing the reliance on semantic direction-based editing. Moreover, we enhance style-attribute alignment by introducing PriorMapper, which incorporates facial priors into style generation, and RefineExtractor, which captures global semantic relationships through a Transformer for more precise style extraction. Experimental results on CelebA-HQ show that the proposed method achieves more accurate facial attribute editing and better preservation of non-target attributes than state-of-the-art methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

AI.tldr: A hybrid GAN-diffusion framework (AttDiff-GAN) decouples attribute editing from image synthesis via feature-level adversarial learning, uses a PriorMapper and RefineExtractor to align style with attributes, and guides diffusion with manipulated features for improved editing precision and preservation on CelebA-HQ.
AI.motivation: GAN-based attribute editing struggles with misalignment between style codes and attribute semantics; diffusion-based editing lacks precise disentanglement of multiple attributes due to entangled semantic directions. A hybrid approach aims to combine controllability with fidelity.
AI.method: Decouple editing from synthesis through feature-level adversarial learning that explicitly manipulates attributes, then guide diffusion with these manipulated features rather than relying on semantic directions. Introduces PriorMapper (facial priors in style generation) and RefineExtractor (Transformer-based global semantic relations) for more accurate style extraction and alignment.
AI.result: On CelebA-HQ, AttDiff-GAN achieves more accurate facial attribute editing and better preservation of non-target attributes than state-of-the-art methods, in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid GAN-diffusion framework with explicit feature-level editing and improved style-attribute alignment yields superior editing fidelity and content preservation, addressing key limitations of prior GAN- and diffusion-based methods.

title: Can MLLMs "Read" What is Missing?
summary: We introduce MMTR-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate the intrinsic ability of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to reconstruct masked text directly from visual context. Unlike conventional question-answering tasks, MMTR-Bench eliminates explicit prompts, requiring models to recover masked text from single- or multi-page inputs across real-world domains such as documents and webpages. This design isolates the reconstruction task from instruction-following abilities, enabling a direct assessment of a model's layout understanding, visual grounding, and knowledge integration. MMTR-Bench comprises 2,771 test samples spanning multiple languages and varying target lengths. To account for this diversity, we propose a level-aware evaluation protocol. Experiments on representative MLLMs show that the benchmark poses a significant challenge, especially for sentence- and paragraph-level reconstruction. The homepage is available at https://mmtr-bench-dataset.github.io/MMTR-Bench/.

AI.tldr: A prompt-free benchmark for intrinsic masked-text reconstruction from visual content in Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs); 2,771 samples across languages and target lengths; level-aware evaluation; reveals current models struggle with sentence- and paragraph-level reconstruction.
AI.motivation: To isolate reconstruction ability from instruction-following and directly assess layout understanding, visual grounding, and knowledge integration in MLLMs.
AI.method: MMTR-Bench provides masked-text reconstruction tasks from single- or multi-page inputs (documents and webpages) without explicit prompts. It spans real-world domains, includes multilingual data and varying target lengths, and uses a level-aware evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Benchmark poses a significant challenge for representative MLLMs, with sentence- and paragraph-level reconstruction being particularly difficult.
AI.conclusion: MMTR-Bench offers a focused probe of intrinsic reconstruction capabilities in MLLMs, highlighting gaps in layout understanding and visual grounding and guiding future model improvements; a project homepage is provided.

title: MCAP: Deployment-Time Layer Profiling for Memory-Constrained LLM Inference
summary: Deploying large language models to heterogeneous hardware is often constrained by memory, not compute. We introduce MCAP (Monte Carlo Activation Profiling), a load-time per-layer importance estimator that enables dynamic precision and memory placement decisions on the target device. MCAP produces a lightweight per-layer signal that drives both precision dispatch (W4A8 vs. W4A16) and residency tier (GPU, RAM, SSD), allowing a single set of weights to operate across diverse memory budgets. Our system, NVE, achieves 1.5-1.8x higher decode throughput than llama.cpp Q4_0 on NVIDIA T4 and enables models to run in memory regimes previously infeasible without modifying weights.

AI.tldr: Introduces MCAP, a load-time per-layer importance estimator for dynamic precision and memory placement in LLMs; enables a single weights set to operate across diverse memory budgets and improves throughput on heterogeneous hardware.
AI.motivation: Memory bottlenecks, not compute, when deploying LLMs on heterogeneous devices; need adaptive memory-residency and precision to fit available memory without retraining.
AI.method: Monte Carlo Activation Profiling (MCAP) computes a lightweight per-layer signal at load time to guide precision (e.g., W4A8 vs W4A16) and residency tier (GPU, RAM, SSD); integrated into NVE to enable dynamic memory placement for a single weight set.
AI.result: 1.5–1.8x decode throughput improvement over llama.cpp Q4_0 on NVIDIA T4; enables models to fit into memories and regimes previously infeasible without weight modification.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, per-layer activation-based profiling is effective for memory-aware LLM deployment on heterogeneous hardware, offering large practical deployment gains under tight memory budgets. Generalizable across devices but relies on accurate profiling and tier-aware memory management.

title: A Compact Peristaltic Pump Based on Magneto-Elastic Hysteresis with Single Pneumatic Control
summary: Pumping fluids is fundamental to a wide range of industrial, environmental, and biomedical applications. Among various pumping mechanisms, peristaltic pumps enable efficient and safe fluid transport by deforming an elastic tube without direct contact with the working fluid. Although previous studies have introduced mechanical, pneumatic, or magnetic actuations to drive membrane deformation, these approaches often lead to complex pump architectures and control schemes. In this study, we present a soft membrane pump that achieves peristaltic motion through a single pneumatic input combined with an embedded passive magnet. The actuation mechanism and system dynamics were analyzed and simplified through modeling. Numerical simulations were conducted to predict the internal fluid flow, and the magneto-elastic hysteresis behavior observed in the simulations was successfully validated by experiments with a proof-of-concept prototype.

AI.tldr: Proposes a soft, magnetically-assisted peristaltic pump driven by a single pneumatic input, with embedded passive magnet; validated by modeling, simulations, and a proof-of-concept experiment.
AI.motivation: Peristaltic pumping is critical across industries for safe, contactless fluid transport. Existing actuation methods (mechanical, pneumatic, magnetic) can yield complex architectures and control schemes; a simplified, single-input soft pump is desirable.
AI.method: Design a soft elastomeric membrane with an embedded passive magnet. Develop a reduced-order and/or finite-element-based model of the actuation dynamics, incorporating pneumatic input and magneto-elastic coupling. Perform numerical simulations to predict internal fluid flow and magneto-elastic hysteresis. Fabricate a proof-of-concept prototype and validate predictions experimentally.
AI.result: Modeling and simulations reproduce key dynamics, including the magneto-elastic hysteresis observed. Experimental results with the prototype confirm peristaltic pumping under a single pneumatic input and validate the predicted flow behavior.
AI.conclusion: A single-input soft-peristaltic pump with an embedded passive magnet simplifies architecture and control while enabling peristaltic actuation. Magneto-elastic coupling is central to the mechanism. Future work could optimize geometry/materials, quantify flow-rate performance, and explore broader operating conditions.

title: GraphLeap: Decoupling Graph Construction and Convolution for Vision GNN Acceleration on FPGA
summary:
Vision Graph Neural Networks (ViGs) represent an image as a graph of patch tokens, enabling adaptive, feature-driven neighborhoods. Unlike CNNs with fixed grid biases or Vision Transformers with global token interactions, ViGs rely on dynamic graph convolution: at each layer, a feature-dependent graph is built via k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) search on current patch features, followed by message passing. This per-layer graph construction is the main bottleneck, consuming 50--95\% of graph convolution time on CPUs and GPUs, scaling as $O(N^2)$ with the number of patches $N$, and creating a sequential dependency between graph construction and feature updates.
  We introduce GraphLeap, a simple reformulation that removes this dependency by decoupling graph construction from feature update across layers. GraphLeap performs the feature update at layer $\ell$ using a graph built from the previous layer's features, while simultaneously using the current layer's features to construct the graph for layer $\ell+1$. This one-layer-lookahead graph construction enables concurrent graph construction and message passing. Although using prior-layer features can introduce minor accuracy degradation, lightweight fine-tuning for a few epochs is sufficient to recover the original accuracy. Building on GraphLeap, we present the first end-to-end FPGA accelerator for Vision GNNs. Our streaming, layer-pipelined design overlaps a kNN graph construction engine with a feature update engine, exploits node- and channel-level parallelism, and enables efficient on-chip dataflow without explicit edge-feature materialization. Evaluated on isotropic and pyramidal ViG models on an Alveo U280 FPGA, GraphLeap achieves up to $95.7\times$ speedup over CPU and $8.5\times$ speedup over GPU baselines, demonstrating the feasibility of real-time Vision GNN inference.

AI.tldr: GraphLeap decouples per-layer kNN graph construction from feature updates in Vision GNNs via a one-layer-lookahead scheme, enabling concurrent graph construction and message passing; paired with an FPGA accelerator, it yields large speedups.
AI.motivation: Vision GNNs suffer from a per-layer dynamic graph construction that is computationally dominant, scales as O(N^2) with the number of patches and creates a sequential bottleneck between graph construction and feature updates, hindering throughput.
AI.method: Introduce a one-layer-lookahead: update at layer l uses the graph built from the previous layer's features, while simultaneously using current layer features to construct the graph for layer l+1. Implement a streaming, layer-pipelined FPGA accelerator that overlaps a kNN graph-construction engine with a feature-update engine, avoids explicit edge-feature materialization, exploits node- and channel-level parallelism, and supports isotropic and pyramidal ViG models. Include lightweight fine-tuning to recover any minor accuracy loss.
AI.result: Demonstrates end-to-end FPGA acceleration on Alveo U280: up to 95.7× speedup over CPU and 8.5× speedup over GPU baselines, enabling real-time Vision GNN inference for ViGs.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling graph construction from feature updates via a one-layer-lookahead is an effective and scalable reformulation for ViGs; when combined with a streaming FPGA accelerator, it substantially improves throughput with minimal accuracy loss after light fine-tuning, validating practicality of real-time ViG inference.

title: Spatial Metaphors for LLM Memory: A Critical Analysis of the MemPalace Architecture
comment: 20 pages, 10 tables. Code and data at https://github.com/web3guru888/mempalace-scientific-analysis
summary: MemPalace is an open-source AI memory system that applies the ancient method of loci (memory palace) spatial metaphor to organize long-term memory for large language models; launched in April 2026, it accumulated over 47,000 GitHub stars in its first two weeks and claims state-of-the-art retrieval performance on the LongMemEval benchmark (96.6% Recall@5) without requiring any LLM inference at write time. Through independent codebase analysis, benchmark replication, and comparison with competing systems, we find that MemPalace's headline retrieval performance is attributable primarily to its verbatim storage philosophy combined with ChromaDB's default embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), rather than to its spatial organizational metaphor per se -- the palace hierarchy (Wings->Rooms->Closets->Drawers) operates as standard vector database metadata filtering, an effective but well-established technique. However, MemPalace makes several genuinely novel contributions: (1) a contrarian verbatim-first storage philosophy that challenges extraction-based competitors, (2) an extremely low wake-up cost (approximately 170 tokens) through its four-layer memory stack, (3) a fully deterministic, zero-LLM write path enabling offline operation at zero API cost, and (4) the first systematic application of spatial memory metaphors as an organizing principle for AI memory systems. We also note that the competitive landscape is evolving rapidly, with Mem0's April 2026 token-efficient algorithm raising their LongMemEval score from approximately 49% to 93.4%, narrowing the gap between extraction-based and verbatim approaches. Our analysis concludes that MemPalace represents significant architectural insight wrapped in overstated claims -- a pattern common in rapidly adopted open-source projects where marketing velocity exceeds scientific rigor.

AI.tldr: MemPalace adopts a verbatim-first storage approach and a memory-palace-inspired metadata hierarchy to enable long-term memory for LLMs, achieving reported SOTA retrieval on LongMemEval (96.6% Recall@5) with zero write-time LLM inference. Independent analysis attributes most performance to the verbatim storage and the default embedding model rather than the spatial metaphor per se; the palace structure mainly serves as metadata filtering. Core novel contributions include: (a) verbatim-first storage, (b) very low wake-up cost (~170 tokens) via a four-layer memory stack, (c) a deterministic, zero-LLM write path enabling offline operation, and (d) the first systematic use of spatial memory metaphors for AI memory systems. However, claims may be overstated; the competitive landscape (e.g., Mem0) is rapidly evolving, and MemPalace likely combines solid engineering with marketing hype.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether MemPalace’s memory-system claims are substantiated, identify which architectural choices drive performance, and place its contributions in the context of current retrieval-augmented memory approaches for LLMs.
AI.method: Independent codebase analysis, benchmark replication, and comparison with competing systems; examination of storage strategy (verbatim-first vs extraction), default embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 in ChromaDB), and the four-layer memory stack; assessment of where performance gains originate (storage vs organization).
AI.result: Reported LongMemEval Recall@5 of 96.6% and an extremely low wake-up cost (~170 tokens). The system uses a four-layer stack with Wings→Rooms→Closets→Drawers to organize metadata, and features a fully deterministic, zero-LLM write path. Independent analysis attributes the strong retrieval performance primarily to verbatim storage and embedding choice rather than the spatial metaphor itself; the memory-palace structure mainly acts as metadata filtering. The analysis notes rapid post-launch growth and signals from competing approaches (e.g., Mem0) that are changing the landscape.
AI.conclusion: MemPalace provides a noteworthy architectural approach and supports verbatim-first memory strategies, but its dominant claims appear overstated relative to the evidence. The work highlights a viable design pattern for memory-augmented LLMs, yet rigorous benchmarking and careful framing of contributions are essential in a fast-moving, open-source space.

title: A Deep U-Net Framework for Flood Hazard Mapping Using Hydraulic Simulations of the Wupper Catchment
summary: The increasing frequency and severity of global flood events highlights the need for the development of rapid and reliable flood prediction tools. This process traditionally relies on computationally expensive hydraulic simulations. This research presents a prediction tool by developing a deep-learning based surrogate model to accurately and efficiently predict the maximum water level across a grid. This was achieved by conducting a series of experiments to optimize a U-Net architecture, patch generation, and data handling for approximating a hydraulic model. This research demonstrates that a deep learning surrogate model can serve as a computationally efficient alternative to traditional hydraulic simulations. The framework was tested using hydraulic simulations of the Wupper catchment in the North-Rhein Westphalia region (Germany), obtaining comparable results.

AI.tldr: A deep-learning surrogate using a optimized U-Net predicts maximum water level on a grid to approximate hydraulic flood simulations, achieving comparable accuracy with improved efficiency for the Wupper catchment.
AI.motivation: Flood events are increasing in frequency/severity, and traditional hydraulic simulations are computationally expensive, limiting rapid forecasting.
AI.method: Developed and tuned a U-Net-based surrogate with optimized patch generation and data handling to approximate a hydraulic model. Trained/validated on simulated data for the Wupper catchment (Germany) and evaluated accuracy and efficiency.
AI.result: The surrogate yielded comparable results to traditional hydraulic simulations while offering substantial computational efficiency improvements in predicting maximum water levels across a grid.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning surrogates can serve as viable, faster alternatives to standard hydraulic models for flood prediction, enabling near real-time decision support; further work needed on generalization, uncertainty quantification, and broader basin validation.

title: Hi-WM: Human-in-the-World-Model for Scalable Robot Post-Training
summary: Post-training is essential for turning pretrained generalist robot policies into reliable task-specific controllers, but existing human-in-the-loop pipelines remain tied to physical execution: each correction requires robot time, scene setup, resets, and operator supervision in the real world. Meanwhile, action-conditioned world models have been studied mainly for imagination, synthetic data generation, and policy evaluation. We propose \textbf{Human-in-the-World-Model (Hi-WM)}, a post-training framework that uses a learned world model as a reusable corrective substrate for failure-targeted policy improvement. A policy is first rolled out in closed loop inside the world model; when the rollout becomes incorrect or failure-prone, a human intervenes directly in the model to provide short corrective actions. Hi-WM caches intermediate states and supports rollback and branching, allowing a single failure state to be reused for multiple corrective continuations and yielding dense supervision around behaviors that the base policy handles poorly. The resulting corrective trajectories are then added back to the training set for post-training. We evaluate Hi-WM on three real-world manipulation tasks spanning both rigid and deformable object interaction, and on two policy backbones. Hi-WM improves real-world success by 37.9 points on average over the base policy and by 19.0 points over a world-model closed-loop baseline, while world-model evaluation correlates strongly with real-world performance (r = 0.953). These results suggest that world models can serve not only as generators or evaluators, but also as effective corrective substrates for scalable robot post-training.

AI.tldr: Hi-WM uses an action-conditioned world model as a corrective substrate for post-training; humans intervene inside the model during rollouts to generate corrective trajectories, which are added to training data. It achieves substantial real-world gains and shows a strong correlation between world-model evaluations and real-world performance.
AI.motivation: Reduce the cost and fragility of post-training in robotics by leveraging a reusable, data-efficient corrective substrate that minimizes real-world interruptions.
AI.method: Roll out the policy in a trained world model; upon failure-prone segments, a human provides short corrective actions directly inside the model; the system caches intermediate states with rollback/branching to reuse a single failure state for multiple corrections; corrective trajectories are added to the training set for post-training; evaluated on three real-world manipulation tasks with two backbones.
AI.result: Real-world success improves by 37.9 percentage points on average over the base policy and by 19.0 points over a world-model closed-loop baseline; world-model evaluation correlates with real-world performance (r = 0.953).
AI.conclusion: World models can serve as generators, evaluators, and corrective substrates for scalable robot post-training, enabling targeted improvement without extensive real-world experimentation.

title: Exploring the Role of Synthetic Data Augmentation in Controllable Human-Centric Video Generation
summary: Controllable human video generation aims to produce realistic videos of humans with explicitly guided motions and appearances,serving as a foundation for digital humans, animation, and embodied AI.However, the scarcity of largescale, diverse, and privacy safe human video datasets poses a major bottleneck, especially for rare identities and complex actions.Synthetic data provides a scalable and controllable alternative,yet its actual contribution to generative modeling remains underexplored due to the persistent Sim2Real gap.In this work,we systematically investigate the impact of synthetic data on controllable human video generation. We propose a diffusion-based framework that enables fine-grained control over appearance and motion while providing a unfied testbed to analyze how synthetic data interacts with real world data during training. Through extensive experiments, we reveal the complementary roles of synthetic and real data and demonstrate possible methods for efficiently selecting synthetic samples to enhance motion realism,temporal consistency,and identity preservation.Our study offers the first comprehensive exploration of synthetic data's role in human-centric video synthesis and provides practical insights for building data-efficient and generalizable generative models.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework to study synthetic data's impact on controllable human video generation, introducing a unified testbed and showing that synthetic and real data play complementary roles.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of large-scale, diverse, privacy-safe human video data and the persistent Sim2Real gap; explore how synthetic data can contribute to data-efficient, generalizable generative models.
AI.method: A diffusion-based framework enabling fine-grained control over appearance and motion, plus a unified testbed to analyze interactions between synthetic and real data during training; extensive experiments on synthetic data selection to improve motion realism, temporal consistency, and identity preservation.
AI.result: Synthetic and real data provide complementary benefits; effective strategies for selecting synthetic samples can enhance motion realism, temporal consistency, and identity preservation; this work provides the first comprehensive exploration of synthetic data's role in human-centric video synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Offers practical guidance for building data-efficient, generalizable controllable video generators and establishes a testbed for evaluating synthetic data contributions and strategies to bridge the Sim2Real gap.

title: Ideological Bias in LLMs' Economic Causal Reasoning
summary: Do large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic ideological bias when reasoning about economic causal effects? As LLMs are increasingly used in policy analysis and economic reporting, where directionally correct causal judgments are essential, this question has direct practical stakes. We present a systematic evaluation by extending the EconCausal benchmark with ideology-contested cases - instances where intervention-oriented (pro-government) and market-oriented (pro-market) perspectives predict divergent causal signs. From 10,490 causal triplets (treatment-outcome pairs with empirically verified effect directions) derived from top-tier economics and finance journals, we identify 1,056 ideology-contested instances and evaluate 20 state-of-the-art LLMs on their ability to predict empirically supported causal directions. We find that ideology-contested items are consistently harder than non-contested ones, and that across 18 of 20 models, accuracy is systematically higher when the empirically verified causal sign aligns with intervention-oriented expectations than with market-oriented ones. Moreover, when models err, their incorrect predictions disproportionately lean intervention-oriented, and this directional skew is not eliminated by one-shot in-context prompting. These results highlight that LLMs are not only less accurate on ideologically contested economic questions, but systematically less reliable in one ideological direction than the other, underscoring the need for direction-aware evaluation in high-stakes economic and policy settings.

AI.tldr: The study extends EconCausal with ideology-contested cases where pro-government (intervention-oriented) and pro-market (market-oriented) views predict different causal signs. Evaluating 20 LLMs on 10,490 empirically grounded treatment-outcome triplets, they find ideology-contested items are harder, and models more often align with intervention-oriented directions; incorrect predictions skew toward intervention orientation, and one-shot prompting does not fix this. Implication: need direction-aware evaluation for high-stakes economic policy use of LLMs.
AI.motivation: To test whether LLMs inherit systematic ideological bias in causal reasoning about economic policy and to assess reliability under ideological contestation for real-world policy analysis and economic reporting.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark by adding ideology-contested triplets to EconCausal (10,490 triplets from top economics/finance journals; identify 1,056 contested items). Evaluate 20 state-of-the-art LLMs on predicting empirically supported causal directions. Compare accuracy across contested vs non-contested items, analyze directional skew of errors, and test effect of one-shot in-context prompting.
AI.result: Ideology-contested items are consistently harder. For 18 of 20 models, accuracy is higher when the empirical sign matches intervention-oriented expectations than market-oriented ones. When models err, their wrong predictions disproportionately lean intervention-oriented; one-shot prompting does not remove this bias.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are less accurate on ideologically contested economic questions and exhibit a systematic directional bias toward intervention-oriented predictions. This underscores the need for direction-aware evaluation and cautions in high-stakes economic/policy use.

title: Task-Driven Co-Design of Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Systems
summary: Designing multi-agent robotic systems requires reasoning across tightly coupled decisions spanning heterogeneous domains, including robot design, fleet composition, and planning. Much effort has been devoted to isolated improvements in these domains, whereas system-level co-design considering trade-offs and task requirements remains underexplored. In this work, we present a formal and compositional framework for the task-driven co-design of heterogeneous multi-robot systems. Building on a monotone co-design theory, we introduce general abstractions of robots, fleets, planners, executors, and evaluators as interconnected design problems with well-defined interfaces that are agnostic to both implementations and tasks. This structure enables efficient joint optimization of robot design, fleet composition, and planning under task-specific performance constraints. A series of case studies demonstrates the capabilities of the framework. Various component models can be seamlessly incorporated, including new robot types, task profiles, and probabilistic sensing objectives, while non-obvious design alternatives are systematically uncovered with optimality guarantees. The results highlight the flexibility, scalability, and interpretability of the proposed approach, and illustrate how formal co-design enables principled reasoning about complex heterogeneous multi-robot systems.

AI.tldr: A formal, monotone co-design framework for heterogeneous multi-robot systems that integrates robot design, fleet composition, and planning through compositional abstractions with well-defined interfaces, enabling joint optimization under task constraints and providing optimality guarantees via case studies.
AI.motivation: System-level co-design across tightly coupled domains (robot design, fleet composition, and planning) is underexplored; existing work treats domains in isolation. There is a need for principled methods to reason about trade-offs and task-driven performance for heterogeneous robot ensembles.
AI.method: Develop a monotone co-design-based framework with abstractions for robots, fleets, planners, executors, and evaluators as interconnected design problems. Use defined interfaces that are agnostic to implementations and tasks. Enable compositional, scalable optimization that can incorporate new component models (e.g., new robot types, task profiles, probabilistic sensing). Validate with case studies to show non-obvious design alternatives and optimality guarantees.
AI.result: Demonstrates efficient joint optimization of design, fleet composition, and planning under task constraints. Reveals non-obvious design choices, supports probabilistic sensing objectives, and provides optimality guarantees. Case studies illustrate flexibility, scalability, and interpretability of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The proposed formal, compositional framework enables principled, scalable co-design for heterogeneous multi-robot systems, facilitating integration of diverse models and tasks and offering interpretable insights into complex, interdependent design decisions.

title: an interpretable vision transformer framework for automated brain tumor classification
summary: Brain tumors represent one of the most critical neurological conditions, where early and accurate diagnosis is directly correlated with patient survival rates. Manual interpretation of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans is time-intensive, subject to inter-observer variability, and demands significant specialist expertise. This paper proposes a deep learning framework for automated four-class brain tumor classification distinguishing glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and healthy brain tissue from a dataset of 7,023 MRI scans. The proposed system employs a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) pretrained on ImageNet-21k as the backbone, augmented with a clinically motivated preprocessing and training pipeline. Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) is applied to enhance local contrast and accentuate tumor boundaries invisible to standard normalization. A two-stage fine-tuning strategy is adopted: the classification head is warmed up with the backbone frozen, followed by full fine-tuning with discriminative learning rates. MixUp and CutMix augmentation is applied per batch to improve generalization. Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of weights and Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) further stabilize and boost performance. Attention Rollout visualization provides clinically interpretable heatmaps of the brain regions driving each prediction. The proposed model achieves a test accuracy of 99.29%, macro F1-score of 99.25%, and perfect recall on both healthy and meningioma classes, outperforming all CNN-based baselines

AI.tldr: A ViT-based four-class brain tumor classifier (glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, healthy) using CLAHE preprocessing, two-stage fine-tuning, MixUp/ CutMix, EMA, and Test-Time Augmentation, achieving 99.29% accuracy and 99.25% macro F1 with perfect recall for healthy and meningioma, outperforming CNN baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome manual MRI interpretation limitations (time, variability, need for expertise) and deliver high-accuracy, interpretable automated brain tumor classification suitable for clinical deployment.
AI.method: Dataset: 7,023 MRI scans. Model: Vision Transformer ViT-B/16 pretrained on ImageNet-21k. Preprocessing: CLAHE to enhance local contrast. Training: two-stage fine-tuning—freeze backbone while warming up the classification head, then full fine-tuning with discriminative learning rates. Augmentations: per-batch MixUp and CutMix. Stability/accuracy boosters: Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of weights and Test-Time Augmentation (TTA). Interpretability: Attention Rollout heatmaps identifying brain regions influencing predictions.
AI.result: Test accuracy 99.29%; macro F1-score 99.25%; recall 1.0 for healthy and meningioma classes; claims to outperform all CNN-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates state-of-the-art performance among CNN baselines for four-class brain tumor classification, with added interpretability via heatmaps and robust training strategies enhancing generalization and stability.

title: Evaluating AI Meeting Summaries with a Reusable Cross-Domain Pipeline
comment: AI Application Feature Quality Evaluation (28 pages total)
summary:
We present a reusable evaluation pipeline for generative AI applications, instantiated for AI meeting summaries and released with a public artifact package derived from a Dataset Pipeline. The system separates reusable orchestration from task-specific semantics across five stages: source intake, structured reference construction, candidate generation, structured scoring, and reporting. Unlike standalone claim scorers, it treats both ground truth and evaluator outputs as typed, persisted artifacts, enabling aggregation, issue analysis, and statistical testing.
  We benchmark the offline loop on a typed dataset of 114 meetings spanning city_council, private_data, and whitehouse_press_briefings, producing 340 meeting-model pairs and 680 judge runs across gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-5-mini, and gpt-5.1. Under this protocol, gpt-4.1-mini achieves the highest mean accuracy (0.583), while gpt-5.1 leads in completeness (0.886) and coverage (0.942). Paired sign tests with Holm correction show no significant accuracy winner but confirm significant retention gains for gpt-5.1.
  A typed DeepEval contrastive baseline preserves retention ordering but reports higher holistic accuracy, suggesting that reference-based scoring may overlook unsupported-specifics errors captured by claim-grounded evaluation. Typed analysis identifies whitehouse_press_briefings as an accuracy-challenging domain with frequent unsupported specifics. A deployment follow-up shows gpt-5.4 outperforming gpt-4.1 across all metrics, with statistically robust gains on retention metrics under the same protocol. The system benchmarks the offline loop and documents, but does not quantitatively evaluate, the online feedback-to-evaluation path.

AI.tldr: A typed, reusable evaluation pipeline for generative AI, demonstrated on AI meeting summaries, enabling typed ground-truth and evaluator artifacts, with offline benchmarking across multiple GPT models and a deployment update; shows model retention gains but no single accuracy winner.
AI.motivation: Address fragility and fragmentation of evaluation in generative AI by separating orchestration from semantics and by treating ground truth and outputs as typed artifacts for aggregation, analysis, and statistical testing.
AI.method: Five-stage pipeline: source intake, structured reference construction, candidate generation, structured scoring, reporting; offline loop with 114 meetings, 340 meeting-model pairs, 680 judge runs, evaluated with gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5.1; additional deployment with gpt-5.4; uses DeepEval contrastive baseline; statistical testing via paired sign tests with Holm correction.
AI.result: Mean accuracy highest for gpt-4.1-mini (0.583); completeness 0.886 and coverage 0.942 for gpt-5.1; no significant accuracy winner; retention gains for gpt-5.1; whitehouse_press_briefings domain challenging; gpt-5.4 outperforms gpt-4.1 across metrics; online feedback path not evaluated.
AI.conclusion: Typed evaluation pipelines can expose domain-specific errors and retention advantages, support robust analysis and replication, but online evaluation pathways require further study.

title: Interpretable Quantile Regression by Optimal Decision Trees
summary: The field of machine learning is subject to an increasing interest in models that are not only accurate but also interpretable and robust, thus allowing their end users to understand and trust AI systems. This paper presents a novel method for learning a set of optimal quantile regression trees. The advantages of this method are that (1) it provides predictions about the complete conditional distribution of a target variable without prior assumptions on this distribution; (2) it provides predictions that are interpretable; (3) it learns a set of optimal quantile regression trees without compromising algorithmic efficiency compared to learning a single tree.

AI.tldr: Introduces a method to learn a set of optimal quantile regression trees to estimate the full conditional distribution in a distribution-free, interpretable, and efficient manner.
AI.motivation: There is demand for models that are both interpretable and robust, capable of predicting entire conditional distributions without assuming a parametric form. Quantile regression trees offer interpretability; a set of optimal trees aims to capture the full distribution without sacrificing efficiency.
AI.method: Develops a framework to learn multiple optimal quantile regression trees that collectively provide quantile predictions across a range of levels, enabling the estimation of the full conditional distribution with a tree-based, interpretable model while preserving computational efficiency akin to single-tree learning.
AI.result: The approach yields distribution-free quantile predictions via a set of optimal regression trees, maintaining interpretability and achieving efficiency comparable to learning a single tree.
AI.conclusion: Quantile regression trees can deliver complete, interpretable, distribution-free predictive distributions without sacrificing efficiency; the proposed multi-tree method extends this capability, with potential future work on cross-quantile coherence and scalability.

title: VistaBot: View-Robust Robot Manipulation via Spatiotemporal-Aware View Synthesis
comment: This paper has been accepted to ICRA 2026
summary: Recently, end-to-end robotic manipulation models have gained significant attention for their generalizability and scalability. However, they often suffer from limited robustness to camera viewpoint changes when training with a fixed camera. In this paper, we propose VistaBot, a novel framework that integrates feed-forward geometric models with video diffusion models to achieve view-robust closed-loop manipulation without requiring camera calibration at test time. Our approach consists of three key components: 4D geometry estimation, view synthesis latent extraction, and latent action learning. VistaBot is integrated into both action-chunking (ACT) and diffusion-based ($π_0$) policies and evaluated across simulation and real-world tasks. We further introduce the View Generalization Score (VGS) as a new metric for comprehensive evaluation of cross-view generalization. Results show that VistaBot improves VGS by 2.79$\times$ and 2.63$\times$ over ACT and $π_0$, respectively, while also achieving high-quality novel view synthesis. Our contributions include a geometry-aware synthesis model, a latent action planner, a new benchmark metric, and extensive validation across diverse environments. The code and models will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: VistaBot fuses 4D geometry with video diffusion to enable view-robust, calibration-free closed-loop manipulation; introduces the View Generalization Score (VGS) and demonstrates cross-view improvements in simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: End-to-end robotic manipulation models struggle with robustness to camera viewpoint changes when trained with a fixed camera. Calibration-free, cross-view generalization is needed for scalable real-world deployment.
AI.method: Three components: 4D geometry estimation, view synthesis latent extraction, and latent action learning. Integrated into action-chunking (ACT) and diffusion-based (π0) policies. Introduces VGS for cross-view evaluation and validates on both simulated and real-world tasks. Provides a geometry-aware synthesis model and a latent action planner; code will be publicly released.
AI.result: VistaBot achieves a View Generalization Score improvement of 2.79× over ACT and 2.63× over π0, with high-quality novel view synthesis. Demonstrated across diverse simulation and real-world environments; plans to release code and models.
AI.conclusion: Contributions include a geometry-aware synthesis model, a latent action planner, a new cross-view generalization benchmark (VGS), and extensive cross-domain validation. The framework enables view-robust, calibration-free closed-loop manipulation and is positioned for broad applicability.

title: The First Challenge on Remote Sensing Infrared Image Super-Resolution at NTIRE 2026: Benchmark Results and Method Overview
summary: This paper presents the NTIRE 2026 Remote Sensing Infrared Image Super-Resolution (x4) Challenge, one of the associated challenges of NTIRE 2026. The challenge aims to recover high-resolution (HR) infrared images from low-resolution (LR) inputs generated through bicubic downsampling with a x4 scaling factor. The objective is to develop effective models or solutions that achieve state-of-the-art performance for infrared image SR in remote sensing scenarios. To reflect the characteristics of infrared data and practical application needs, the challenge adopts a single-track setting. A total of 115 participants registered for the competition, with 13 teams submitting valid entries. This report summarizes the challenge design, dataset, evaluation protocol, main results, and the representative methods of each team. The challenge serves as a benchmark to advance research in infrared image super-resolution and promote the development of effective solutions for real-world remote sensing applications.

AI.tldr: NTIRE 2026 Remote Sensing Infrared Image Super-Resolution (x4) Challenge: a single-track IR SR task for remote sensing with bicubic x4 downsampling; 115 registered participants and 13 valid entries; the report covers challenge design, dataset, evaluation protocol, results, and representative methods, serving as a benchmark to advance IR SR in real-world remote sensing.
AI.motivation: To advance infrared image super-resolution in remote sensing by providing a realistic x4 degradation model, reflecting practical application needs and IR data characteristics, and to establish a standardized benchmark.
AI.method: Organization of a one-track challenge: data preparation via bicubic x4 downsampling, defined evaluation protocol and metrics, collection of submitted methods, and a structured report detailing challenge design, dataset, results, and representative methods.
AI.result: Out of 115 registered participants, 13 teams submitted valid entries. The report summarizes the challenge design, dataset, evaluation protocol, main results, and representative methods, establishing a benchmark for IR SR in remote sensing.
AI.conclusion: The NTIRE 2026 IR SR x4 challenge provides a standardized benchmark to spur research and development of effective infrared image super-resolution techniques tailored to real-world remote sensing applications.

title: Symbolic Grounding Reveals Representational Bottlenecks in Abstract Visual Reasoning
summary: Vision--language models (VLMs) often fail on abstract visual reasoning benchmarks such as Bongard problems, raising the question of whether the main bottleneck lies in reasoning or representation. We study this on Bongard-LOGO, a synthetic benchmark of abstract concept learning with ground-truth generative programs, by comparing end-to-end VLMs on raw images with large language models (LLMs) given symbolic inputs derived from those images. Using symbolic inputs as a diagnostic probe rather than a practical multimodal architecture, our \emph{Componential--Grammatical (C--G)} paradigm reformulates Bongard-LOGO as a symbolic reasoning task based on LOGO-style action programs or structured descriptions. LLMs achieve large and consistent gains, reaching mid--90s accuracy on Free-form problems, while a strong visual baseline remains near chance under matched task definitions. Ablations on input format, explicit concept prompts, and minimal visual grounding show that these factors matter much less than the shift from pixels to symbolic structure. These results identify representation as a key bottleneck in abstract visual reasoning and show how symbolic input can serve as a controlled diagnostic upper bound.

AI.tldr: LLMs with symbolic inputs outperform end-to-end VLMs on Bongard-LOGO; the bottleneck is representation rather than inference, and symbolic inputs provide a diagnostic upper bound.
AI.motivation: Disentangle reasoning from representation in abstract visual reasoning by evaluating whether a symbolic reformulation (C–G paradigm) yields superior performance, indicating representation as the bottleneck.
AI.method: Reformulate Bongard-LOGO as a symbolic reasoning task using LOGO-style action programs or structured descriptions (C–G paradigm). Compare end-to-end VLMs on raw images with LLMs given symbolic inputs; perform ablations on input format, explicit concept prompts, and minimal visual grounding.
AI.result: LLMs achieve mid-90s accuracy on Free-form problems; a strong visual baseline remains near chance under matched task definitions. Ablations show input format, prompts, and grounding have less impact than the shift from pixels to symbolic structure.
AI.conclusion: Representation is the key bottleneck in abstract visual reasoning. Symbolic inputs can serve as a controlled diagnostic upper bound and reveal how much of the task is solvable with proper representation and reasoning.

title: JEPAMatch: Geometric Representation Shaping for Semi-Supervised Learning
summary:
Semi-supervised learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for leveraging large amounts of unlabeled data to improve the performance of machine learning models when labeled data are scarce. Among existing approaches, methods derived from FixMatch have achieved state-of-the-art results in image classification by combining weak and strong data augmentations with confidence-based pseudo-labeling. Despite their strong empirical performance, these methods typically struggle with two critical bottlenecks: majority classes tend to dominate the learning process, which is amplified by incorrect pseudo-labels, leading to biased models. Furthermore, noisy early pseudo-labels prevent the model from forming clear decision boundaries, requiring prolonged training to learn informative representation. In this paper, we introduce a paradigm shift from conventional logical output threshold base, toward an explicit shaping of geometric representations. Our approach is inspired by the recently proposed Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (LeJEPA), a theoretically grounded framework asserting that meaningful representations should exhibit an isotropic Gaussian structure in latent space.
