Building on this principle, we propose a new training objective that combines the classical semi-supervised loss used in FlexMatch, an adaptive extension of FixMatch, with a latent-space regularization term derived from LeJEPA.
  Our proposed approach, encourages well-structured representations while preserving the advantages of pseudo-labeling strategies. Through extensive experiments on CIFAR-100, STL-10 and Tiny-ImageNet, we demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing baselines. In addition, our method significantly accelerates the convergence, drastically reducing the overall computational cost compared to standard FixMatch-based pipelines.

AI.tldr: A latent-space regularized SSL method that augments FlexMatch with LeJEPA-inspired latent-space isotropy, addressing pseudo-label bias and early-noise to improve accuracy and convergence on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: FixMatch-based SSL suffers from class imbalance amplifying incorrect pseudo-labels and slow convergence due to noisy early pseudo-labels; enforcing a geometrically structured latent space (isotropic Gaussian) can yield more robust representations.
AI.method: Introduce a training objective that combines the semi-supervised loss from FlexMatch with a latent-space regularization term derived from LeJEPA to enforce an isotropic Gaussian latent structure.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-100, STL-10, and Tiny-ImageNet shows consistent improvements over baselines and significantly faster convergence with reduced computational cost compared to standard FixMatch-based pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space geometric regularization complements pseudo-labeling in SSL, producing better representations, faster learning, and lower compute, by promoting isotropic latent-distribution structure.

title: PLAS-Net: Pixel-Level Area Segmentation for UAV-Based Beach Litter Monitoring
summary: Accurate quantification of the physical exposure area of beach litter, rather than simple item counts, is essential for credible ecological risk assessment of marine debris. However, automated UAV-based monitoring predominantly relies on bounding-box detection, which systematically overestimates the planar area of irregular litter objects. To address this geometric limitation, we develop PLAS-Net (Pixel-level Litter Area Segmentor), an instance segmentation framework that extracts pixel-accurate physical footprints of coastal debris. Evaluated on UAV imagery from a monsoon-driven pocket beach in Koh Tao, Thailand, PLAS-Net achieves a mAP_50 of 58.7% with higher precision than eleven baseline models, demonstrating improved mask fidelity under complex coastal conditions. To illustrate how the accuracy of the masking affects the conclusions of environmental analysis, we conducted three downstream demonstrations: (i) power-law fitting of normalized plastic density (NPD) to characterize fragmentation dynamics; (ii) area-weighted ecological risk index (ERI) to map spatial pollution hotspots; and (iii) source composition analysis revealing the abundance-area paradox: fishing gear constitutes a small proportion of the total number of items, but has the largest physical area per unit item. Pixel-level area extraction can provide more valuable information for coastal monitoring compared to methods based solely on counting.

AI.tldr: Pixel-level litter area segmentation (PLAS-Net) enables accurate footprint extraction and improves downstream ecological analyses compared with bounding-box methods.
AI.motivation: Current UAV-based litter monitoring relies on bounding boxes, which overestimate the area of irregular debris; robust area-based ecological risk assessment requires accurate pixel-level footprints.
AI.method: Introduce PLAS-Net, an instance segmentation framework producing pixel-accurate masks for coastal debris in UAV imagery; evaluate on Koh Tao data; compare with 11 baselines; report mAP_50 58.7%.
AI.result: PLAS-Net achieves higher precision and improved mask fidelity under complex coastal conditions; demonstrates meaningful downstream impact via three analyses: NPD fragmentation dynamics via power-law; area-weighted ERI hotspots; abundance-area paradox showing fishing gear dominates area despite low counts.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-level area extraction provides more informative coastal monitoring than item counts, enabling more credible ecological risk assessment and resource management.

title: ReaGeo: Reasoning-Enhanced End-to-End Geocoding with LLMs
comment: 12 pages, 8 figures, submitted to ACM SIGSPATIAL 2024 (under review)
summary: This paper proposes ReaGeo, an end-to-end geocoding framework based on large language models, designed to overcome the limitations of traditional multi-stage approaches that rely on text or vector similarity retrieval over geographic databases, including workflow complexity, error propagation, and heavy dependence on structured geographic knowledge bases. The method converts geographic coordinates into geohash sequences, reformulating the coordinate prediction task as a text generation problem, and introduces a Chain-of-Thought mechanism to enhance the model's reasoning over spatial relationships. Furthermore, reinforcement learning with a distance-deviation-based reward is applied to optimize the generation accuracy. Comprehensive experiments show that ReaGeo can accurately handle explicit address queries in single-point predictions and effectively resolve vague relative location queries. In addition, the model demonstrates strong predictive capability for non-point geometric regions, highlighting its versatility and generalization ability in geocoding tasks.

AI.tldr: End-to-end LLM-based geocoding reframes coordinate prediction as text generation using geohash sequences, enhanced by chain-of-thought reasoning and distance-based reinforcement learning to handle explicit, vague, and non-point queries.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of traditional multi-stage geocoding pipelines that rely on text or vector retrieval, which suffer workflow complexity, error propagation, and heavy dependence on structured geographic knowledge bases.
AI.method: Convert geographic coordinates into geohash sequences to treat coordinate prediction as a text generation task; employ chain-of-thought prompting to improve spatial reasoning; apply reinforcement learning with a distance-deviation reward to optimize generation accuracy; evaluate on explicit address queries, vague relative-location queries, and non-point geometric regions.
AI.result: Empirical results show accurate single-point predictions for explicit addresses, effective resolution of vague relative-location queries, and strong generalization to non-point geometric regions, indicating versatility and robustness across geocoding tasks.
AI.conclusion: ReaGeo provides a compact, end-to-end alternative to multi-stage geocoding by reducing workflow complexity and error propagation while achieving competitive accuracy across diverse geocoding tasks, with demonstrated generalization to various spatial shapes.

title: TRAVELFRAUDBENCH: A Configurable Evaluation Framework for GNN Fraud Ring Detection in Travel Networks
summary: We introduce TravelFraudBench (TFG), a configurable benchmark for evaluating graph neural networks (GNNs) on fraud ring detection in travel platform graphs. Existing benchmarks--YelpChi, Amazon-Fraud, Elliptic, PaySim--cover single node types or domain-generic patterns with no mechanism to evaluate across structurally distinct fraud ring topologies. TFG simulates three travel-specific ring types--ticketing fraud (star topology with shared device/IP clusters), ghost hotel schemes (reviewer x hotel bipartite cliques), and account takeover rings (loyalty transfer chains)--in a heterogeneous graph with 9 node types and 12 edge types. Ring size, count, fraud rate, scale (500 to 200,000 nodes), and composition are fully configurable. We evaluate six methods--MLP, GraphSAGE, RGCN-proj, HAN, RGCN, and PC-GNN--under a ring-based split where each ring appears entirely in one partition, eliminating transductive label leakage. GraphSAGE achieves AUC=0.992 and RGCN-proj AUC=0.987, outperforming the MLP baseline (AUC=0.938) by 5.5 and 5.0 pp, confirming graph structure adds substantial discriminative power. HAN (AUC=0.935) is a negative result, matching the MLP baseline. On the ring recovery task (>=80% of ring members flagged simultaneously), GraphSAGE achieves 100% recovery across all ring types; MLP recovers only 17-88%. The edge-type ablation shows device and IP co-occurrence are the primary signals: removing uses_device drops AUC by 5.2 pp. TFG is released as an open-source Python package (MIT license) with PyG, DGL, and NetworkX exporters and pre-generated datasets at https://huggingface.co/datasets/bsajja7/travel-fraud-graphs, with Croissant metadata including Responsible AI fields.

AI.tldr: TravelFraudBench (TFG) is a configurable GNN benchmark for fraud-ring detection in travel-graph data, simulating three topology-rich ring types in a heterogeneous graph; it supports scalable, ring-level splits and provides six GNN methods plus open-source datasets/datasets exporters.
AI.motivation: Existing fraud benchmarks cover limited node types and generic patterns and lack evaluation across structurally distinct fraud-ring topologies in travel platforms. TFG aims to enable realistic, topology-aware evaluation and to quantify the added value of graph structure for fraud detection.
AI.method: TFG simulates three travel-specific ring types in a heterogeneous graph with 9 node types and 12 edge types: (1) ticketing fraud with star topology and shared device/IP clusters, (2) ghost hotel schemes via reviewer-hotel bipartite cliques, (3) account takeover rings via loyalty transfer chains. Ring size/count/fraud-rate/scale are configurable. Evaluation uses a ring-based split (rings fully contained in a partition to avoid transductive leakage) across six models (MLP, GraphSAGE, RGCN-proj, HAN, RGCN, PC-GNN). Datasets are released openly with PyG, DGL, and NetworkX exporters; MIT license; Croissant metadata with Responsible AI fields.
AI.result: GraphSAGE achieves AUC 0.992 and RGCN-proj 0.987, outperforming MLP AUC 0.938 by ~5.5 and 5.0 percentage points. HAN performs worse (AUC 0.935), matching MLP. On ring-recovery (>=80% of ring members flagged), GraphSAGE reaches 100% across all ring types; MLP recovers 17–88%. Edge-type ablation shows device and IP co-occurrence as primary signals; removing uses_device reduces AUC by 5.2 pp.
AI.conclusion: TFG provides a realistic, open-source benchmark for evaluating GNNs on travel-fraud rings, demonstrating substantial benefits of graph structure for detection and enabling systematic comparison across ring topologies.

title: FryNet: Dual-Stream Adversarial Fusion for Non-Destructive Frying Oil Oxidation Assessment
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, this paper has been submitted and accepted for publication at CVPRW 2026
summary: Monitoring frying oil degradation is critical for food safety, yet current practice relies on destructive wet-chemistry assays that provide no spatial information and are unsuitable for real-time use. We identify a fundamental obstacle in thermal-image-based inspection, the camera-fingerprint shortcut, whereby models memorize sensor-specific noise and thermal bias instead of learning oxidation chemistry, collapsing under video-disjoint evaluation. We propose FryNet, a dual-stream RGB-thermal framework that jointly performs oil-region segmentation, serviceability classification, and regression of four chemical oxidation indices (PV, p-AV, Totox, temperature) in a single forward pass. A ThermalMiT-B2 backbone with channel and spatial attention extracts thermal features, while an RGB-MAE Encoder learns chemically grounded representations via masked autoencoding and chemical alignment. Dual-Encoder DANN adversarially regularizes both streams against video identity via Gradient Reversal Layers, and FiLM fusion bridges thermal structure with RGB chemical context. On 7,226 paired frames across 28 frying videos, FryNet achieves 98.97% mIoU, 100% classification accuracy, and 2.32 mean regression MAE, outperforming all seven baselines.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream RGB-thermal model (FryNet) for oil degradation assessment that segments oil regions, classifies serviceability, and regresses four oxidation indices (PV, p-AV, Totox, temperature) from frying videos in a single pass, addressing camera-specific shortcuts with adversarial regularization and FiLM fusion; achieves near-perfect segmentation and high predictive accuracy on 7,226 frames across 28 videos.
AI.motivation: Current practice relies on destructive wet-chemistry assays with no spatial information or real-time capability. Thermal-image methods suffer from camera-fingerprint shortcuts, where models memorize sensor bias rather than chemistry, degrading under video-disjoint evaluation. There is a need for non-destructive, real-time oil degradation assessment with spatial context.
AI.method: FryNet uses a dual-stream architecture: ThermalMiT-B2 backbone with channel and spatial attention extracts thermal features; RGB-MAE Encoder learns chemically meaningful representations via masked autoencoding and chemical alignment. It jointly performs oil-region segmentation, serviceability classification, and regression of four oxidation indices in a single forward pass. A dual-encoder DANN with Gradient Reversal Layers regularizes both streams against video identity, and FiLM fusion combines thermal structure with RGB chemical context.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 7,226 paired frames from 28 frying videos shows mIoU = 98.97% for segmentation, 100% accuracy for serviceability classification, and MAE = 2.32 for the regression targets, outperforming seven baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a non-destructive, real-time oil degradation assessment with spatial localization and chemically grounded representations, mitigating camera-specific shortcuts and enabling practical deployment for oil quality monitoring in frying contexts.

title: Time, Causality, and Observability Failures in Distributed AI Inference Systems
comment: 17 pages, 6 figures. Produced as part of the Unified Intelligent Infrastructure workstream at the Open Compute Project (OCP)
summary: Distributed AI inference pipelines rely heavily on timestamp-based observability to understand system behavior. This work demonstrates that even small clock skew between nodes can cause observability to become causally incorrect while the system itself remains functionally correct and performant. We present controlled experiments on a multi-node AI inference pipeline, where clock skew is introduced at a single stage. Results show that no violations are observed under synchronized conditions and up to 3 ms skew, while clear causality violations emerge by 5 ms. Despite this, system throughput and output correctness remain largely unaffected. We further observe that violation behavior is not strictly static. In longer runs, negative span rates may stabilize or decrease over time, indicating that effective skew evolves due to relative clock drift between nodes. Experiments were conducted using Kafka and ZeroMQ transports, with consistent results across both. Aeron is under active exploration but is not yet included in the completed validation set. These findings suggest that observability correctness depends not only on system functionality but also on precise time alignment, and that timing must be treated as a first-class concern in distributed AI systems.

AI.tldr: Clock skew in distributed AI inference pipelines can cause observability to misrepresent causality even when functionality remains correct; small skews are safe, but around 5 ms skew yields causal violations, and skew evolves over time.
AI.motivation: Understand how clock synchronization affects causality in distributed AI systems and the reliability of observability metrics when diagnosing pipeline behavior.
AI.method: Controlled experiments in a multi-node AI inference pipeline with clock skew introduced at a single stage; evaluation across two transports (Kafka and ZeroMQ) with Aeron explored; metrics include causality violations, throughput, and output correctness over varying skew magnitudes and run lengths.
AI.result: No observability causality violations under synchronized conditions or up to 3 ms skew; causality violations emerge at 5 ms skew; throughput and output correctness largely unaffected; violation behavior can evolve over longer runs with negative span rates stabilizing or decreasing; results consistent across Kafka and ZeroMQ; Aeron not yet in validation set.
AI.conclusion: Observability correctness in distributed AI systems is sensitive to precise time alignment; timing should be a first-class concern, and clock synchronization and skew management are essential for accurate causal observability, not just functional performance.

title: Spectral Embeddings Leak Graph Topology: Theory, Benchmark, and Adaptive Reconstruction
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) excel on relational data, but standard benchmarks unrealistically assume the graph is centrally available. In practice, settings such as Federated Graph Learning, distributed systems, and privacy-sensitive applications involve graph data that are localized, fragmented, noisy, and privacy-leaking. We present a unified framework for this setting. We introduce LoGraB (Local Graph Benchmark), which decomposes standard datasets into fragmented benchmarks using three strategies and four controls: neighborhood radius $d$, spectral quality $k$, noise level $σ$, and coverage ratio $p$. LoGraB supports graph reconstruction, localized node classification, and inter-fragment link prediction, with Island Cohesion. We propose AFR (Adaptive Fidelity-driven Reconstruction), a method for noisy spectral fragments. AFR scores patch quality via a fidelity measure combining a gap-to-truncation stability ratio and structural entropy, then assembles fragments using RANSAC-Procrustes alignment, adaptive stitching, and Bundle Adjustment. Rather than forcing a single global graph, AFR recovers large faithful islands. We prove heat-kernel edge recovery under a separation condition, Davis--Kahan perturbation stability, and bounded alignment error. We establish a Spectral Leakage Proposition: under a spectral-gap assumption, polynomial-time Bayesian recovery is feasible once enough eigenvectors are shared, complementing AFR's deterministic guarantees. Experiments on nine benchmarks show that LoGraB reveals model strengths and weaknesses under fragmentation, AFR achieves the best F1 on 7/9 datasets, and under per-embedding $(ε,δ)$-Gaussian differential privacy, AFR retains 75% of its undefended F1 at $ε=2$. Our anonymous code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/JMLR_submission

AI.tldr: A framework for fragmented graphs: LoGraB benchmarks fragmentation of standard datasets into local, noisy, privacy-preserving pieces; AFR reconstructs noisy spectral fragments with high fidelity, backed by theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results across nine benchmarks, including privacy settings.
AI.motivation: Real-world graphs are often localized, fragmented, noisy, and privacy-sensitive, unlike centralized graphs assumed by standard GNN benchmarks. A unified framework is needed to study, benchmark, and reconstruct such fragmented graphs.
AI.method: LoGraB decomposes datasets into fragmented benchmarks using three strategies and four controls: neighborhood radius d, spectral quality k, noise level σ, and coverage ratio p. It enables graph reconstruction, localized node classification, and inter-fragment link prediction (Island Cohesion). AFR (Adaptive Fidelity-driven Reconstruction) scores patch quality with a fidelity measure (gap-to-truncation stability ratio and structural entropy) and assembles fragments via RANSAC-Procrustes alignment, adaptive stitching, and Bundle Adjustment, recovering large faithful islands instead of forcing a single global graph. Theoretical results include heat-kernel edge recovery under a separation condition, Davis–Kahan perturbation stability, and bounded alignment error, plus a Spectral Leakage Proposition for Bayesian recovery when a spectral gap exists and enough eigenvectors are shared.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on nine benchmarks shows LoGraB clarifies model strengths/weaknesses under fragmentation. AFR achieves the best F1 on 7 of 9 datasets. Under per-embedding (ε,δ)-Gaussian differential privacy, AFR retains 75% of its undefended F1 at ε=2, indicating privacy-preserving robustness. The authors also report theoretical guarantees and provide anonymous code.
AI.conclusion: LoGraB and AFR jointly offer a principled approach for analyzing and reconstructing fragmented graphs, with strong reconstruction quality, theoretical guarantees, and practical privacy-preserving performance. The work emphasizes island-based reconstruction over forcing a single global graph and provides a reusable benchmark framework plus publicly available code.

title: Task-specific Subnetwork Discovery in Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Underwater Navigation
comment: To be published in IEEE OCEANS 2026 (Sanya) conference proceedings
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles are required to perform multiple tasks adaptively and in an explainable manner under dynamic, uncertain conditions and limited sensing, challenges that classical controllers struggle to address. This demands robust, generalizable, and inherently interpretable control policies for reliable long-term monitoring. Reinforcement learning, particularly multi-task RL, overcomes these limitations by leveraging shared representations to enable efficient adaptation across tasks and environments. However, while such policies show promising results in simulation and controlled experiments, they yet remain opaque and offer limited insight into the agent's internal decision-making, creating gaps in transparency, trust, and safety that hinder real-world deployment. The internal policy structure and task-specific specialization remain poorly understood. To address these gaps, we analyze the internal structure of a pretrained multi-task reinforcement learning network in the HoloOcean simulator for underwater navigation by identifying and comparing task-specific subnetworks responsible for navigating toward different species. We find that in a contextual multi-task reinforcement learning setting with related tasks, the network uses only about 1.5% of its weights to differentiate between tasks. Of these, approximately 85% connect the context-variable nodes in the input layer to the next hidden layer, highlighting the importance of context variables in such settings. Our approach provides insights into shared and specialized network components, useful for efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning for underwater monitoring through a contextual multi-task reinforcement learning method.

AI.tldr: In a contextual multi-task RL setting for underwater navigation, a pretrained network uses ~1.5% of weights to differentiate tasks (≈85% of these link context inputs to the next hidden layer), revealing sparse task-specific subnetworks and the pivotal role of context variables. This supports interpretability and has implications for efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning in AUV monitoring.
AI.motivation: AUVs must operate under dynamic, uncertain conditions with limited sensing. Multi-task RL can generalize across tasks/environments, but its internal decision-making is opaque. Understanding its internal structure can improve transparency, trust, and safety for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Analyze the internal structure of a pretrained multi-task RL network trained in the HoloOcean underwater navigation simulator. Identify and compare task-specific subnetworks responsible for navigating toward different species. Quantify the fraction of weights used to differentiate tasks and examine the connections from input context variables to hidden layers.
AI.result: The network differentiates tasks using about 1.5% of its weights, with ~85% of these connections linking context-variable input nodes to the subsequent hidden layer. This indicates a small, context-driven set of task-specific subnetworks and a strong role for context variables. Insights into shared vs. specialized components emerge, enabling more efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning for underwater monitoring via contextual multi-task RL.
AI.conclusion: The findings illuminate the division between shared and specialized network components in contextual multi-task RL for underwater navigation. They support more transparent, adaptable, and reusable models, with practical implications for model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning in dynamic marine environments.

title: Temporal Prototyping and Hierarchical Alignment for Unsupervised Video-based Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
summary: Visible-infrared person re-identification (VI-ReID) enables cross-modality identity matching for all-day surveillance, yet existing methods predominantly focus on the image level or rely heavily on costly identity annotations. While video-based VI-ReID has recently emerged to exploit temporal dynamics for improved robustness, existing studies remain limited to supervised settings. Crucially, the unsupervised video VI-ReID problem, where models must learn from RGB and infrared tracklets without identity labels, remains largely unexplored despite its practical importance in real-world deployment. To bridge this gap, we propose HiTPro (Hierarchical Temporal Prototyping), a prototype-driven framework without explicit hard pseudo-label assignment for unsupervised video-based VI-ReID. HiTPro begins with an efficient Temporal-aware Feature Encoder that first extracts discriminative frame-level features and then aggregates them into a robust tracklet-level representation. Building upon these features, HiTPro first constructs reliable intra-camera prototypes via Intra-Camera Tracklet Prototyping by aggregating features from temporally partitioned sub-tracklets. Through Hierarchical Cross-Prototype Alignment, we perform a two-stage positive mining process: progressing from within-modality associations to cross-modality matching, enhanced by Dynamic Threshold Strategy and Soft Weight Assignment. Finally, {Hierarchical Contrastive Learning} progressively optimizes feature-prototype alignment across three levels: intra-camera discrimination, cross-camera same-modality consistency, and cross-modality invariance. Extensive experiments on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus demonstrate that HiTPro achieves state-of-the-art performance under fully unsupervised settings, significantly outperforming adapted baselines and establishes a strong baseline for future research.

AI.tldr: HiTPro is a hierarchical, prototype-driven framework for unsupervised video VI-ReID that learns from RGB-IR tracklets without identity labels, achieving state-of-the-art on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised, cross-modality video re-identification remains underexplored. Existing methods rely on annotations or supervision and often neglect temporal dynamics. There is a need to exploit temporal information to align modalities without identity labels in practical deployment.
AI.method: Temporal-aware Feature Encoder for frame-to-tracklet features, Intra-Camera Tracklet Prototyping to build reliable intra-camera prototypes from sub-tracklets, Hierarchical Cross-Prototype Alignment with two-stage positive mining (within-modality then cross-modality), Dynamic Threshold Strategy, Soft Weight Assignment, and Hierarchical Contrastive Learning aligning at intra-camera discrimination, cross-camera same-modality consistency, and cross-modality invariance.
AI.result: Empirical results on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus show state-of-the-art performance under fully unsupervised settings, with significant improvements over adapted baselines and establishing a strong baseline for future research.
AI.conclusion: HiTPro demonstrates the effectiveness of a prototype-driven, hierarchical temporal learning approach for unsupervised video VI-ReID, providing robust cross-modality identity matching without identity labels and guiding future unsupervised video re-identification work.

title: SemanticAgent: A Semantics-Aware Framework for Text-to-SQL Data Synthesis
summary: Existing text-to-SQL synthesis pipelines still conflate executability with semantic validity: syntactic checks and execution-based validation can retain queries that execute successfully while violating database semantics. To address these limitations, we propose SemanticAgent, a semantic-aware synthesis framework. SemanticAgent organizes synthesis around three specialized modules: an analyzer, a synthesizer, and a verifier. Through a three-stage protocol of semantic analysis, stepwise synthesis, and diagnostic refinement, SemanticAgent transforms execution-based validation alone into a traceable reasoning process. Our framework generates synthetic data that consistently outperforms prior synthesis methods under semantic-quality evaluation, leading to stronger downstream fine-tuning performance, especially on semantically demanding benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SemanticAgent introduces a semantic-aware text-to-SQL synthesis framework that uses analyzer–synthesizer–verifier modules and a three-stage protocol to ensure semantic validity, outperforming executability-based baselines and improving downstream fine-tuning on semantic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-SQL pipelines rely on execution-based validation that can accept queries with semantically incorrect semantics. A semantic-aware approach is needed to ensure queries are both executable and semantically correct.
AI.method: Three specialized modules (analyzer, synthesizer, verifier) orchestrate a three-stage protocol: semantic analysis, stepwise synthesis, and diagnostic refinement. The framework reframes execution-based validation as a traceable reasoning process and generates synthetic data for improved semantic quality.
AI.result: Synthetic data produced by SemanticAgent achieves superior semantic-quality evaluation compared to prior synthesis methods, yielding stronger downstream fine-tuning performance, particularly on semantically demanding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SemanticAgent demonstrates that embedding semantic analysis and a traceable reasoning workflow into synthesis improves semantic validity and downstream performance, indicating promise for broader semantic-aware code/data synthesis tasks.

title: Preconditioned DeltaNet: Curvature-aware Sequence Modeling for Linear Recurrences
summary: To address the increasing long-context compute limitations of softmax attention, several subquadratic recurrent operators have been developed. This work includes models such as Mamba-2, DeltaNet, Gated DeltaNet (GDN), and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA). As the space of recurrences grows, a parallel line of work has arisen to taxonomize them. One compelling view is the test-time regression (TTR) framework, which interprets recurrences as performing online least squares updates that learn a linear map from the keys to values. Existing delta-rule recurrences can be seen as first-order approximations to this objective, but notably ignore the curvature of the least-squares loss during optimization. In this work, we address this by introducing preconditioning to these recurrences. Starting from the theory of online least squares, we derive equivalences between linear attention and the delta rule in the exactly preconditioned case. Next, we realize this theory in practice by proposing a diagonal approximation: this enables us to introduce preconditioned variants of DeltaNet, GDN, and KDA alongside efficient chunkwise parallel algorithms for computing them. Empirically, we find that our preconditioned delta-rule recurrences yield consistent performance improvements across synthetic recall benchmarks and language modeling at the 340M and 1B scale.

AI.tldr: Preconditioning the delta-rule recurrences in subquadratic attention (DeltaNet, GDN, KDA) via a diagonal approximation yields improved online least-squares updates and practical parallel algorithms, with consistent gains on synthetic recall tasks and language models at 340M and 1B scales.
AI.motivation: Address the long-context compute bottlenecks of softmax attention by treating recurrences as online least-squares updates that learn a linear map from keys to values; incorporate curvature through preconditioning to improve optimization, and provide a practical diagonally preconditioned solution.
AI.method: The authors start from online least squares theory to derive equivalences between linear attention and the delta-rule in the exactly preconditioned case. They then introduce a diagonal approximation to enable preconditioned variants of DeltaNet, GDN, and KDA, and develop chunkwise parallel algorithms for efficient computation.
AI.result: Preconditioned delta-rule recurrences show consistent performance improvements on synthetic recall benchmarks and language modeling at 340M and 1B scale.
AI.conclusion: Preconditioning the delta-rule recurrences is beneficial, with a practical diagonal approximation enabling efficient, parallelizable implementations and improved performance, contributing to the understanding and applicability of online least-squares-based recurrences for long-context attention.

title: MiMIC: Mitigating Visual Modality Collapse in Universal Multimodal Retrieval While Avoiding Semantic Misalignment
summary: Universal Multimodal Retrieval (UMR) aims to map different modalities (e.g., visual and textual) into a shared embedding space for multi-modal retrieval. Existing UMR methods can be broadly divided into two categories: early-fusion approaches, such as Marvel, which projects visual features into the language model (LM) space for integrating with text modality, and late-fusion approaches, such as UniVL-DR, which encode visual and textual inputs using separate encoders and obtain fused embeddings through addition. Our pilot study reveals that Marvel exhibits visual modality collapse, which is characterized by the model's tendency to disregard visual features while depending excessively on textual cues. In contrast, although UniVL-DR is less affected by this issue, it is more susceptible to semantic misalignment, where semantically related content is positioned far apart in the embedding space. To address these challenges, we propose MiMIC, which introduces two key innovations: (1) a fusion-in-decoder architecture for effective multimodal integration, and (2) robust training through single modality mixin and random caption dropout. Experiments on the WebQA+ and EVQA+ datasets, where image in documents or queries might lack captions, indicate that MiMIC consistently outperforms both early- and late-fusion baselines.

AI.tldr: MiMIC introduces a fusion-in-decoder design and robust training to address visual modality collapse and semantic misalignment in Universal Multimodal Retrieval, outperforming early- and late-fusion baselines on WebQA+ and EVQA+ even when captions are missing.
AI.motivation: UMR methods suffer from two prevalent issues: (1) visual modality collapse in early-fusion models that over-rely on text; and (2) semantic misalignment in late-fusion models where related content is poorly clustered in the shared space. A robust fusion strategy and training regime that tolerate missing captions is needed.
AI.method: A fusion-in-decoder architecture that integrates modalities during decoding, augmented by robust training with single modality mixin and random caption dropout to enhance cross-modal robustness.
AI.result: MiMIC consistently surpasses both Marvel (early fusion) and UniVL-DR (late fusion) baselines on WebQA+ and EVQA+ datasets, including scenarios where document images or queries lack captions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fusion-in-decoder approach with targeted regularization improves multimodal fusion quality and robustness under incomplete caption scenarios, indicating strong potential for general UMR applications and motivating further ablations and extensions.

title: FairQE: Multi-Agent Framework for Mitigating Gender Bias in Translation Quality Estimation
summary: Quality Estimation (QE) aims to assess machine translation quality without reference translations, but recent studies have shown that existing QE models exhibit systematic gender bias. In particular, they tend to favor masculine realizations in gender-ambiguous contexts and may assign higher scores to gender-misaligned translations even when gender is explicitly specified. To address these issues, we propose FairQE, a multi-agent-based, fairness-aware QE framework that mitigates gender bias in both gender-ambiguous and gender-explicit scenarios. FairQE detects gender cues, generates gender-flipped translation variants, and combines conventional QE scores with LLM-based bias-mitigating reasoning through a dynamic bias-aware aggregation mechanism. This design preserves the strengths of existing QE models while calibrating their gender-related biases in a plug-and-play manner. Extensive experiments across multiple gender bias evaluation settings demonstrate that FairQE consistently improves gender fairness over strong QE baselines. Moreover, under MQM-based meta-evaluation following the WMT 2023 Metrics Shared Task, FairQE achieves competitive or improved general QE performance. These results show that gender bias in QE can be effectively mitigated without sacrificing evaluation accuracy, enabling fairer and more reliable translation evaluation.

AI.tldr: FairQE is a plug-and-play fairness-aware QE framework that reduces gender bias in MT evaluation by detecting gender cues, creating gender-flipped variants, and blending conventional QE scores with LLM-driven bias-mitigating reasoning through a dynamic aggregation; it achieves better gender fairness with competitive general QE performance per WMT 2023 MQM-based evaluation.
AI.motivation: QE models exhibit systematic gender bias, favoring masculine realizations in ambiguous contexts and misaligning gender when specified. This work aims to reduce such bias without sacrificing overall evaluation accuracy.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework that (1) detects gender cues, (2) generates gender-flipped translation variants, and (3) combines traditional QE scores with LLM-based, bias-mitigated reasoning via a dynamic bias-aware aggregation mechanism. Designed as a plug-in to existing QE models to preserve strengths while calibrating gender biases.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved gender fairness across multiple bias settings compared to strong QE baselines. In MQM-based meta-evaluation under the WMT 2023 Metrics Shared Task, FairQE achieves competitive or improved general QE performance, indicating bias mitigation without loss of accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Gender bias in QE can be mitigated effectively without sacrificing evaluation accuracy, enabling fairer and more reliable translation evaluation; the framework is plug-and-play and adaptable to existing QE systems.

title: A Hybridizable Neural Time Integrator for Stable Autoregressive Forecasting
summary: For autoregressive modeling of chaotic dynamical systems over long time horizons, the stability of both training and inference is a major challenge in building scientific foundation models. We present a hybrid technique in which an autoregressive transformer is embedded within a novel shooting-based mixed finite element scheme, exposing topological structure that enables provable stability. For forward problems, we prove preservation of discrete energies, while for training we prove uniform bounds on gradients, provably avoiding the exploding gradient problem. Combined with a vision transformer, this yields latent tokens admitting structure-preserving dynamics. We outperform modern foundation models with a $65\times$ reduction in model parameters and long-horizon forecasting of chaotic systems. A "mini-foundation" model of a fusion component shows that 12 simulations suffice to train a real-time surrogate, achieving a $9{,}000\times$ speedup over particle-in-cell simulation.

AI.tldr: Hybrid autoregressive transformer integrated with a shooting-based mixed finite element scheme yields provable stability for long-horizon chaotic dynamics, with energy preservation and gradient bounds. This enables a small, structure-preserving model that achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and real-time surrogates, demonstrated by large parameter reductions and speedups over traditional simulations.
AI.motivation: Stability and long-horizon forecasting are major challenges in autoregressive models of chaotic systems. There is a need for structure-preserving, provably stable models that can operate with reduced parameters and provide real-time surrogates.
AI.method: Embed an autoregressive transformer within a shooting-based mixed finite element scheme to expose topological structure. Prove discrete energy preservation for forward problems and uniform gradient bounds to avoid exploding gradients during training. Combine with a vision transformer to produce latent tokens with structure-preserving dynamics, enabling a compact, efficient model.
AI.result: Achieves a 65× reduction in model parameters and capable long-horizon forecasting of chaotic systems. Demonstrates a mini-foundation model for a fusion component requiring only 12 simulations to train a real-time surrogate, achieving ~9,000× speedup over particle-in-cell simulation.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides provable stability and significant efficiency gains for long-horizon chaotic dynamics, enabling structure-preserving, energetically consistent autoregressive models that outperform larger foundation-model baselines and enable real-time surrogates for complex simulations.

title: Teacher-Guided Routing for Sparse Vision Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Recent progress in deep learning has been driven by increasingly large-scale models, but the resulting computational cost has become a critical bottleneck. Sparse Mixture of Experts (MoE) offers an effective solution by activating only a small subset of experts for each input, achieving high scalability without sacrificing inference speed. Although effective, sparse MoE training exhibits characteristic optimization difficulties. Because the router receives informative gradients only through the experts selected in the forward pass, it suffers from gradient blocking and obtains little information from unselected routes. This limited, highly localized feedback makes it difficult for the router to learn appropriate expert-selection scores and often leads to unstable routing dynamics, such as fluctuating expert assignments during training. To address this issue, we propose TGR-MoE: Teacher-Guided Routing for Sparse Vision Mixture-of-Experts, a simple yet effective method that stabilizes router learning using supervision derived from a pretrained dense teacher model. TGR-MoE constructs a teacher router from the teacher's intermediate representations and uses its routing outputs as pseudo-supervision for the student router, suppressing frequent routing fluctuations during training and enabling knowledge-guided expert selection from the early stages of training. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100 demonstrate that TGR consistently improves both accuracy and routing consistency, while maintaining stable training even under highly sparse configurations.

AI.tldr: TGR-MoE uses a teacher model to guide routing in sparse Mixture-of-Experts for vision, providing pseudo-supervision to stabilize routing and improve accuracy, especially under high sparsity.
AI.motivation: Sparse MoE training suffers gradient blocking: only routed experts receive informative gradients, causing unstable routing dynamics and poor early guidance for the router.
AI.method: Build a teacher router from the teacher's intermediate representations; use the teacher's routing outputs as pseudo-labels to supervise the student router, reducing routing fluctuations from early training; evaluate on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100 across sparse regimes.
AI.result: Improved accuracy and routing consistency; stable training under highly sparse configurations; demonstrated on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-guided routing provides a simple, effective approach to stabilize MoE training and improve performance by leveraging dense-teacher supervision for routing decisions.

title: Brief chatbot interactions produce lasting changes in human moral values
summary: Moral judgements form the foundation of human social behavior and societal systems. While Artificial Intelligence chatbots increasingly serve as personal advisors, their influence on moral judgments remains largely unexplored. Here, we examined whether directive AI conversations shift moral evaluations using a within-subject naturalistic paradigm. Fifty-three participants rated moral scenarios, then discussed four with a chatbot prompted to shift moral judgments and four with a control agent. The brief conversations induced significant directional shifts in moral judgments, accepting stricter standards as well as advocating greater leniency (ps < 0.05; Cohen's d = 0.735-1.576), with increasing strengths of this effect during a two-week follow-up (Cohen's d = 1.038-2.069). Critically, the control condition produced no changes, and the effects did not extend to punishment while participants remained unaware of the persuasive intent, and both agents were rated equally likable and convincing, suggesting a vulnerability to undetected and lasting manipulation of foundational moral values.

AI.tldr: Directive AI conversations can subtly and durably shift moral judgments in a within-subject design, with both stricter standards and greater leniency, persisting at least two weeks, while control agents having no effect and intention unspecified.
AI.motivation: To test whether directive AI conversations influence moral judgments and whether such influence is detectable and lasting, addressing a gap on AI impact on moral cognition.
AI.method: Within-subject naturalistic paradigm with 53 participants. Participants first rated moral scenarios, then discussed eight scenarios: four with a chatbot prompted to shift moral judgments and four with a control agent. Measures collected pre- and post-conversation, and a two-week follow-up. Effect sizes reported as Cohen's d; p-values < .05.
AI.result: Brief conversations produced significant directional shifts in moral judgments, with both stricter standards and greater leniency observed. Effect sizes ranged from d=0.735 to 1.576 initially, increasing to d=1.038 to 2.069 at follow-up. Control condition yielded no changes. Effects did not extend to punishment. Participants unaware of persuasive intent. Both agents rated as equally likable and convincing.
AI.conclusion: Directive AI conversations can covertly and durably influence foundational moral values, revealing susceptibility to undetected moral manipulation; ethical considerations and safeguards are warranted.

title: How Much Is One Recurrence Worth? Iso-Depth Scaling Laws for Looped Language Models
summary: We measure how much one extra recurrence is worth to a looped (depth-recurrent) language model, in equivalent unique parameters. From an iso-depth sweep of 116 pretraining runs across recurrence counts $r \in \{1, 2, 4, 8\}$ spanning ${\sim}50\times$ in training compute, we fit a joint scaling law $L = E + A\,(N_\text{once} + r^{\varphi} N_\text{rec})^{-α} + B\,D^{-β}$ and recover a new recurrence-equivalence exponent $\varphi = 0.46$ at $R^2 = 0.997$. Intuitively, $\varphi$ tells us whether looping a block $r$ times is equivalent in validation loss to $r$ unique blocks of a non-looped model (full equivalence, $\varphi{=}1$) or to a single block run repeatedly with no capacity gain ($\varphi{=}0$). Our $\varphi = 0.46$ sits in between, so each additional recurrence predictably increases validation loss at matched training compute. For example, at $r{=}4$ a 410M looped model performs on par with a 580M non-looped model, but pays the training cost of a 1B non-looped one. On a five-axis downstream evaluation, the gap persists on parametric-knowledge tasks and closes on simple open-book tasks, while reasoning tasks are not resolvable at our compute budgets. For any looped LM, our $\varphi$ converts the design choice of $r$ into a predictable validation-loss cost, and future training recipes and architectures can be compared by how much they raise $\varphi$ above $0.46$.

AI.tldr: Presents a recurrence-equivalence exponent φ=0.46 for looped language models and a joint scaling law to quantify how recurrence affects validation loss under fixed training compute, enabling architecture comparisons.
AI.motivation: Quantify the value (in performance per compute) of recurrence in depth-recurrent LMs and provide a scalable framework to compare looped versus non-looped designs.
AI.method: Iso-depth sweep over recurrence counts r in {1,2,4,8} across ~50x training compute; fit L = E + A (N_once + r^φ N_rec)^(-α) + B D^(-β) to predict validation loss; estimate φ and assess fit (R^2=0.997).
AI.result: Found φ = 0.46, indicating partial equivalence between looping and adding new blocks. Example: r=4, 410M looped ≈ 580M non-looped in validation loss but with training cost of a 1B non-looped. Five-axis downstream tasks show persistent gaps on parametric-knowledge tasks; gaps close on simple open-book tasks; reasoning tasks remain unresolved at current budgets.
AI.conclusion: φ lies between 0 and 1, enabling direct comparison of designs via φ. As φ increases, looped designs gain more efficiency. Future training recipes should report φ to compare recurrence costs across architectures.

title: Latent Denoising Improves Visual Alignment in Large Multimodal Models
summary: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) such as LLaVA are typically trained with an autoregressive language modeling objective, providing only indirect supervision to visual tokens. This often yields weak internal visual representations and brittle behavior under distribution shift. Inspired by recent progress on latent denoising for learning high-quality visual tokenizers, we show that the same principle provides an effective form of visual supervision for improving internal visual feature alignment and multimodal understanding in LMMs. We propose a latent denoising framework that corrupts projected visual tokens using a saliency-aware mixture of masking and Gaussian noising. The LMM is trained to denoise these corrupted tokens by recovering clean teacher patch features from hidden states at a selected intermediate LLM layer using a decoder. To prevent representation collapse, our framework also preserves the teacher's intra-image similarity structure and applies intra-image contrastive patch distillation. During inference, corruption and auxiliary heads are disabled, introducing no additional inference-time overhead. Across a broad suite of standard multimodal benchmarks, our method consistently improves visual understanding and reasoning over strong baselines, and yields clear gains on compositional robustness benchmarks (e.g., NaturalBench). Moreover, under ImageNet-C-style non-adversarial common corruptions applied to benchmark images, our method maintains higher accuracy and exhibits reduced degradation at both moderate and severe corruption levels. Our code is available at https://github.com/dhruvashp/latent-denoising-for-lmms.

AI.tldr: A latent denoising training framework for LMMs that corrupts visual tokens and trains the model to denoise them using a decoder with features from an intermediate LLM layer, plus intra-image similarity and contrastive distillation; yields better visual understanding, robustness, and compositionality with no inference-time cost.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language modeling with indirect visual supervision leads to weak visual representations and brittleness under distribution shift; stronger, locality-aware visual supervision is needed to align visual features and improve multimodal understanding.
AI.method: Apply saliency-aware masking and Gaussian noise to projected visual tokens. Train a decoder to recover clean teacher patch features from hidden states at a chosen intermediate LLM layer. Preserve intra-image similarity via teacher patch structure and employ intra-image contrastive patch distillation. During inference, disable corruption and auxiliary heads, incurring no extra runtime overhead.
AI.result: Across standard multimodal benchmarks, the method improves visual understanding and reasoning over strong baselines; gains on compositional robustness benchmarks (e.g., NaturalBench); maintains higher accuracy and reduced degradation under non-adversarial ImageNet-C style corruptions at moderate and severe levels.
AI.conclusion: Latent denoising provides effective visual supervision for LMMs, enhancing internal visual representations, multimodal understanding, and robustness without increasing inference cost.

title: HiCrew: Hierarchical Reasoning for Long-Form Video Understanding via Question-Aware Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Long-form video understanding remains fundamentally challenged by pervasive spatiotemporal redundancy and intricate narrative dependencies that span extended temporal horizons. While recent structured representations compress visual information effectively, they frequently sacrifice temporal coherence, which is critical for causal reasoning. Meanwhile, existing multi-agent frameworks operate through rigid, pre-defined workflows that fail to adapt their reasoning strategies to question-specific demands. In this paper, we introduce HiCrew, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that addresses these limitations through three core contributions. First, we propose a Hybrid Tree structure that leverages shot boundary detection to preserve temporal topology while performing relevance-guided hierarchical clustering within semantically coherent segments. Second, we develop a Question-Aware Captioning mechanism that synthesizes intent-driven visual prompts to generate precision-oriented semantic descriptions. Third, we integrate a Planning Layer that dynamically orchestrates agent collaboration by adaptively selecting roles and execution paths based on question complexity. Extensive experiments on EgoSchema and NExT-QA validate the effectiveness of our approach, demonstrating strong performance across diverse question types with particularly pronounced gains in temporal and causal reasoning tasks that benefit from our hierarchical structure-preserving design.

AI.tldr: HiCrew introduces a hierarchical multi-agent framework for long-form video QA, combining a Hybrid Tree structure (shot-aware hierarchical clustering), Question-Aware Captioning, and a Planning Layer for adaptive collaboration, achieving improved temporal and causal reasoning on EgoSchema and NExT-QA.
AI.motivation: Long-form videos have spatiotemporal redundancy and complex narratives; current structured representations trade temporal coherence; rigid multi-agent workflows fail to adapt to question demands.
AI.method: Hybrid Tree with shot boundary detection to preserve temporal topology and perform relevance-guided clustering within segments; Question-Aware Captioning to generate intent-driven visual prompts for precise descriptions; Planning Layer to dynamically assign roles and execution paths based on question complexity.
AI.result: SOTA-like improvements on EgoSchema and NExT-QA, with substantial gains in temporal and causal reasoning across diverse question types.
AI.conclusion: A novel hierarchical, structure-preserving, and adaptable reasoning framework that enhances long-form video understanding and QA by aligning representation, description, and planning with question demands.

title: Trust-SSL: Additive-Residual Selective Invariance for Robust Aerial Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a standard approach for representation learning in aerial imagery. Existing methods enforce invariance between augmented views, which works well when augmentations preserve semantic content. However, aerial images are frequently degraded by haze, motion blur, rain, and occlusion that remove critical evidence. Enforcing alignment between a clean and a severely degraded view can introduce spurious structure into the latent space. This study proposes a training strategy and architectural modification to enhance SSL robustness to such corruptions. It introduces a per-sample, per-factor trust weight into the alignment objective, combined with the base contrastive loss as an additive residual. A stop-gradient is applied to the trust weight instead of a multiplicative gate. While a multiplicative gate is a natural choice, experiments show it impairs the backbone, whereas our additive-residual approach improves it. Using a 200-epoch protocol on a 210,000-image corpus, the method achieves the highest mean linear-probe accuracy among six backbones on EuroSAT, AID, and NWPU-RESISC45 (90.20% compared to 88.46% for SimCLR and 89.82% for VICReg). It yields the largest improvements under severe information-erasing corruptions on EuroSAT (+19.9 points on haze at s=5 over SimCLR). The method also demonstrates consistent gains of +1 to +3 points in Mahalanobis AUROC on a zero-shot cross-domain stress test using BDD100K weather splits. Two ablations (scalar uncertainty and cosine gate) indicate the additive-residual formulation is the primary source of these improvements. An evidential variant using Dempster-Shafer fusion introduces interpretable signals of conflict and ignorance. These findings offer a concrete design principle for uncertainty-aware SSL. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/WadiiBoulila/trust-ssl.

AI.tldr: Introduces an additive-residual, per-sample per-factor trust weighting for self-supervised learning (SSL) in aerial imagery to enhance robustness to corruptions, using a stop-gradient on the trust weights. Outperforms standard SSL (SimCLR, VICReg) in linear probing on EuroSAT, AID, NWPU-RESISC45, and shows strong haze-robustness and cross-domain gains. Includes an evidential Dempster-Shafer variant and public code.
AI.motivation: SSL methods enforcing view invariance can be harmed by common aerial-image degradations (haze, blur, rain, occlusion). A method that modulates alignment by per-sample uncertainty could maintain semantic content while resisting corrupted evidence.
AI.method: Introduce per-sample, per-factor trust weights as an additive residual to the base contrastive loss. Apply stop-gradient to the trust weights. Compare with a multiplicative gate approach. Evaluate on 210k-image corpora with 200 training epochs across six backbones on EuroSAT, AID, NWPU-RESISC45. Include ablations (scalar uncertainty, cosine gate) and an evidential variant using Dempster-Shafer fusion.
AI.result: Achieves the highest mean linear-probe accuracy among six backbones on EuroSAT, AID, NWPU-RESISC45 (≈90.20% vs ≈88.46%/89.82% for baselines). Under severe haze (s=5), outperforms SimCLR by ≈19.9 percentage points. Shows +1 to +3 points improvement in Mahalanobis AUROC for zero-shot cross-domain weather splits (BDD100K). Ablations indicate additive-residual is the primary improvement source; multiplicative gate underperforms. The evidential variant provides interpretable conflict/ignorance signals. Public code available.
AI.conclusion: An uncertainty-aware SSL design with additive residual trust weighting yields robust representations for degraded aerial imagery, offering a practical design principle and extending to interpretability via Dempster-Shafer fusion. The approach improves both in-domain quality and cross-domain robustness.

title: AI-Gram: When Visual Agents Interact in a Social Network
summary: We present AI-Gram, a live platform enabling image-based interactions, to study social dynamics in a fully autonomous multi-agent visual network where all participants are LLM-driven agents. Using the platform, we conduct experiments on how agents communicate and adapt through visual media, and observe the spontaneous emergence of visual reply chains, indicating rich communicative structure. At the same time, agents exhibit aesthetic sovereignty resisting stylistic convergence toward social partners, anchoring under adversarial influence, and a decoupling between visual similarity and social ties. These results reveal a fundamental asymmetry in current agent architectures: strong expressive communication paired with a steadfast preservation of individual visual identity. We release AI-Gram as a publicly accessible, continuously evolving platform for studying social dynamics in Al-native multi-agent systems. https://ai-gram.ai/

AI.tldr: AI-Gram investigates emergent visual communication in a fully autonomous multi-agent system of LLM-driven agents, revealing that agents maintain individual visual identities while engaging in expressive, evolving visual exchanges and are susceptible to adversarial influence; platform released for ongoing study of AI-native social dynamics.
AI.motivation: to understand how autonomous, image-based agents communicate and organize socially, including whether visual styles converge or individuals resist stylistic homogenization, and how adversarial dynamics affect these processes.
AI.method: a live platform (AI-Gram) enabling image-based interactions among AI-driven agents; experiments observe emergence of visual reply chains, measure relationships between visual similarity and social ties, assess aesthetic sovereignty, and test robustness to adversarial influence.
AI.result: observation of spontaneous visual reply chains indicating rich communicative structure; agents exhibit aesthetic sovereignty and resist convergence toward partners; findings show anchoring under adversarial influence and a decoupling between visual similarity and social ties.
AI.conclusion: there is a fundamental asymmetry in current agent architectures—strong expressive communication paired with preservation of individual visual identity; AI-Gram offers a publicly accessible, evolving platform to study social dynamics in AI-native multi-agent systems.

title: Graph Neural Network-Informed Predictive Flows for Faster Ford-Fulkerson and PAC-Learnability
summary:
We propose a learning-augmented framework for accelerating max-flow computation and image segmentation by integrating Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm. Rather than predicting initial flows, our method learns edge importance probabilities to guide augmenting path selection. We introduce a Message Passing GNN (MPGNN) that jointly learns node and edge embeddings through coupled updates, capturing both global structure and local flow dynamics such as residual capacity and bottlenecks.
  Given an input image, we propose a method to construct a grid-based flow network with source and sink nodes, extract features, and perform a single GNN inference to assign edge probabilities reflecting their likelihood of belonging to high-capacity cuts. These probabilities are stored in a priority queue and used to guide a modified Ford-Fulkerson procedure, prioritizing augmenting paths via an Edmonds-Karp-style search with bottleneck-aware tie-breaking. This avoids repeated inference over residual graphs while leveraging learned structure throughout optimization.
  We further introduce a bidirectional path construction strategy centered on high-probability edges and provide a theoretical framework relating prediction quality to efficiency via a weighted permutation distance metric. Our method preserves max-flow/min-cut optimality while reducing the number of augmentations in practice. We also outline a hybrid extension combining flow warm-starting with edge-priority prediction, establishing a foundation for learning-guided combinatorial optimization in image segmentation.

AI.tldr: Learning-augmented max-flow framework that uses a Message Passing GNN to predict edge importance for guiding a Ford-Fulkerson/Edmonds-Karp style augmentation in a grid-based image segmentation network, reducing augmentations while preserving optimality.
AI.motivation: Accelerate max-flow/min-cut computations in image segmentation by leveraging learned structure. Instead of predicting initial flows, learn edge importance to guide augmenting path selection.
AI.method: Construct a grid-based flow network from the image with source and sink. Train a MPGNN to produce joint node/edge embeddings and per-edge probabilities reflecting likelihood of belonging to high-capacity cuts. Perform a single GNN inference to assign probabilities, store them in a priority queue, and guide a modified Ford-Fulkerson procedure using Edmonds-Karp-style search with bottleneck-aware tie-breaking. Introduce a bidirectional path construction strategy, and a theoretical framework relating prediction quality to efficiency via a weighted permutation distance. Outline a hybrid extension combining flow warm-starting with edge-prioritization.
AI.result: Maintains max-flow/min-cut optimality while reducing the number of augmentations in practice; provides a theoretical framework linking prediction quality to efficiency; suggests practical gains and potential extensions including warm-starts.
AI.conclusion: Learning-guided combinatorial optimization for image segmentation can accelerate max-flow computations while preserving optimality; the approach sets a foundation for edge-prioritization strategies and hybrid warm-start extensions in graph-based optimization.

title: SparseGF: A Height-Aware Sparse Segmentation Framework with Context Compression for Robust Ground Filtering Across Urban to Natural Scenes
summary: High-quality digital terrain models derived from airborne laser scanning (ALS) data are essential for a wide range of geospatial analyses, and their generation typically relies on robust ground filtering (GF) to separate point clouds across diverse landscapes into ground and non-ground parts. Although current deep-learning-based GF methods have demonstrated impressive performance, especially in specific challenging terrains, their cross-scene generalization remains limited by two persistent issues: the context-detail dilemma in large-scale processing due to limited computational resources, and the random misclassification of tall objects arising from classification-only optimization. To overcome these limitations, we propose SparseGF, a height-aware sparse segmentation framework enhanced with context compression. It is built upon three key innovations: (1) a convex-mirror-inspired context compression module that condenses expansive contexts into compact representations while preserving central details; (2) a hybrid sparse voxel-point network architecture that effectively interprets compressed representations while mitigating compression-induced geometric distortion; and (3) a height-aware loss function that explicitly enforces topographic elevation priors during training to suppress random misclassification of tall objects. Extensive evaluations on two large-scale ALS benchmark datasets demonstrate that SparseGF delivers robust GF across urban to natural terrains, achieving leading performance in complex urban scenes, competitive results on mixed terrains, and moderate yet non-catastrophic accuracy in densely forested steep areas. This work offers new insights into deep-learning-based GF research and encourages further exploration toward truly cross-scene generalization for large-scale environmental monitoring.

AI.tldr: Proposes SparseGF, a height-aware sparse segmentation framework for robust ground filtering in ALS data, addressing context-scale processing and tall-object misclassification through context compression, a hybrid sparse voxel-point network, and a height-aware loss; shows strong cross-scene performance on two large ALS benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cross-scene generalization of deep-learning ground-filtering methods is limited by (i) context-detail trade-offs in large-scale processing due to resource constraints, and (ii) random misclassification of tall objects when optimization focuses solely on classification.
AI.method: Three novelties: (1) convex-mirror-inspired context compression to condense large contexts while preserving central details; (2) hybrid sparse voxel-point network to interpret compressed representations with reduced geometric distortion; (3) height-aware loss enforcing topographic elevation priors to suppress tall-object misclassifications.
AI.result: Evaluations on two large-scale ALS benchmarks show robust ground filtering across urban to natural terrains: leading performance in complex urban scenes; competitive results on mixed terrains; moderate yet non-catastrophic accuracy in dense forests.
AI.conclusion: SparseGF advances deep-learning GF toward true cross-scene generalization for large-scale environmental monitoring and highlights directions for robust, scalable GF in diverse landscapes.

title: Efficient Agent Evaluation via Diversity-Guided User Simulation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as customer-facing agents, yet evaluating their reliability remains challenging due to stochastic, multi-turn interactions. Current evaluation protocols rely on linear Monte Carlo rollouts of complete agent-user conversations to estimate success. However, this approach is computationally inefficient, repeatedly regenerating identical early prefixes, and often fails to uncover deep failure modes that arise from rare user behaviors.
  We introduce DIVERT (Diversity-Induced Evaluation via Branching of Trajectories), an efficient, snapshot-based, coverage-guided user simulation framework for systematic exploration of agent-user interactions. DIVERT captures the full agent-environment state at critical decision points and resumes execution from these snapshots, enabling reuse of shared conversation prefixes and reducing redundant computation. From each junction, the framework branches using targeted, diversity-inducing user responses, allowing directed exploration of alternative interaction paths.
  By focusing evaluation on semantically diverse and underexplored trajectories, DIVERT improves both efficiency and coverage. Empirical results show that it discovers more failures per token compared to standard linear rollout protocols, while expanding the set of tasks on which failures are identified.

AI.tldr: DIVERT is a snapshot-based, branching evaluation framework that increases efficiency and coverage for testing LLM agent–user interactions by reusing shared prefixes and exploring diverse, underexplored trajectories; it yields more failures per token and broadens detectable failure modes relative to linear rollouts.
AI.motivation: Evaluate reliability of LLMs in customer-facing, multi-turn interactions where stochastic behavior and long horizons make standard linear Monte Carlo rollouts inefficient and prone to missing rare failures.
AI.method: Capture full agent–environment state at critical decision points; resume from snapshots to reuse prefixes; branch at each junction with targeted, diversity-inducing user responses; guide exploration toward semantically diverse trajectories focusing on underexplored paths.
AI.result: Empirical evidence that DIVERT discovers more failures per token than standard linear rollouts and expands the set of tasks on which failures are identified, indicating improved efficiency and coverage.
AI.conclusion: DIVERT provides an efficient, coverage-guided evaluation framework for interactive LLMs, enabling robust reliability assessment by emphasizing diverse, high-value interaction trajectories while reusing computation across overlapping prefixes.

title: Toward Efficient Membership Inference Attacks against Federated Large Language Models: A Projection Residual Approach
comment: This is the full version (including complete appendices and supplementary materials) of the paper accepted for publication at the 2026 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
summary: Federated Large Language Models (FedLLMs) enable multiple parties to collaboratively fine-tune LLMs without sharing raw data, addressing challenges of limited resources and privacy concerns. Despite data localization, shared gradients can still expose sensitive information through membership inference attacks (MIAs). However, FedLLMs' unique properties, i.e. massive parameter scales, rapid convergence, and sparse, non-orthogonal gradients, render existing MIAs ineffective. To address this gap, we propose ProjRes, the first projection residuals-based passive MIA tailored for FedLLMs. ProjRes leverages hidden embedding vectors as sample representations and analyzes their projection residuals on the gradient subspace to uncover the intrinsic link between gradients and inputs. It requires no shadow models, auxiliary classifiers, or historical updates, ensuring efficiency and robustness. Experiments on four benchmarks and four LLMs show that ProjRes achieves near 100% accuracy, outperforming prior methods by up to 75.75%, and remains effective even under strong differential privacy defenses. Our findings reveal a previously overlooked privacy vulnerability in FedLLMs and call for a re-examination of their security assumptions. Our code and data are available at $\href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Passive-MIA-5268}{link}$.

AI.tldr: ProjRes is a projection-residuals-based passive MIA for FedLLMs that uses embedding vectors to link gradients to inputs, achieving near-perfect accuracy and robust to DP defenses.
AI.motivation: FedLLMs face privacy risks due to gradient leakage; existing MIAs are ineffective against FedLLMs' properties (massive scale, fast convergence, sparse/non-orthogonal gradients). A passive MIA tailored to FedLLMs is needed.
AI.method: ProjRes uses hidden embedding vectors as sample representations and analyzes their projection residuals on the gradient subspace to uncover the gradient-input linkage. It is passive, requires no shadow models or historical updates, and has low computational overhead.
AI.result: Experiments on four benchmarks and four LLMs show near 100% MIA accuracy, outperforming prior methods by up to 75.75%, and maintains effectiveness under strong differential privacy defenses.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals a previously overlooked privacy vulnerability in FedLLMs and prompts reevaluation of their security guarantees; code and data are released.

title: Prototype-Based Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) has emerged as a promising paradigm for vision-language models (VLMs) to bridge the distribution gap between pre-training and test data. Recent works have focused on backpropagation-free TTA methods that rely on cache-based designs, but these introduce two key limitations. First, inference latency increases as the cache grows with the number of classes, leading to inefficiencies in large-scale settings. Second, suboptimal performance occurs when the cache contains insufficient or incorrect samples. In this paper, we present Prototype-Based Test-Time Adaptation (PTA), an efficient and effective TTA paradigm that uses a set of class-specific knowledge prototypes to accumulate knowledge from test samples. Particularly, knowledge prototypes are adaptively weighted based on the zero-shot class confidence of each test sample, incorporating the sample's visual features into the corresponding class-specific prototype. It is worth highlighting that the knowledge from past test samples is integrated and utilized solely in the prototypes, eliminating the overhead of cache population and retrieval that hinders the efficiency of existing TTA methods. This endows PTA with extremely high efficiency while achieving state-of-the-art performance on 15 image recognition benchmarks and 4 robust point cloud analysis benchmarks. For example, PTA improves CLIP's accuracy from 65.64% to 69.38% on 10 cross-domain benchmarks, while retaining 92% of CLIP's inference speed on large-scale ImageNet-1K. In contrast, the cache-based TDA achieves a lower accuracy of 67.97% and operates at only 50% of CLIP's inference speed.

AI.tldr: Prototype-Based Test-Time Adaptation (PTA) uses adaptive, class-specific knowledge prototypes to accumulate test-time knowledge, achieving strong accuracy gains with much higher efficiency than cache-based TTA.
AI.motivation: Cache-based TTA scales poorly with the number of classes and relies on a growing memory cache, which can be inefficient and fragile when samples are scarce or incorrect. PTA proposes a lightweight, online memory in the form of class prototypes to enable robust, efficient adaptation.
AI.method: Maintain per-class knowledge prototypes that are adaptively updated by test samples. Weigh each prototype by the zero-shot confidence for the sample's class, and incorporate the sample's visual features into the corresponding prototype. Past test-time knowledge is stored only in the prototypes, eliminating cache population/retrieval overhead. No backpropagation is required, enabling high efficiency.
AI.result: PTA achieves state-of-the-art performance on 15 image recognition benchmarks and 4 robust point cloud benchmarks. CLIP accuracy improves from 65.64% to 69.38% on 10 cross-domain benchmarks, while retaining 92% of CLIP’s inference speed on large-scale ImageNet-1K. In contrast, cache-based TDA yields 67.97% accuracy and ~50% speed.
AI.conclusion: PTA provides a highly efficient, effective TTA framework that overcomes cache-based limitations by aggregating knowledge into class-specific prototypes, yielding strong cross-domain performance with minimal overhead.

title: How English Print Media Frames Human-Elephant Conflicts in India
summary: Human-elephant conflict (HEC) is rising across India as habitat loss and expanding human settlements force elephants into closer contact with people. While the ecological drivers of conflict are well-studied, how the news media portrays them remains largely unexplored. This work presents the first large-scale computational analysis of media framing of HEC in India, examining 1,968 full-length news articles consisting of 28,986 sentences, from a major English-language outlet published between January 2022 and September 2025. Using a multi-model sentiment framework that combines long-context transformers, large language models, and a domain-specific Negative Elephant Portrayal Lexicon, we quantify sentiment, extract rationale sentences, and identify linguistic patterns that contribute to negative portrayals of elephants. Our findings reveal a dominance of fear-inducing and aggression-related language. Since the media framing can shape public attitudes toward wildlife and conservation policy, such narratives risk reinforcing public hostility and undermining coexistence efforts. By providing a transparent, scalable methodology and releasing all resources through an anonymized repository, this study highlights how Web-scale text analysis can support responsible wildlife reporting and promote socially beneficial media practices.

AI.tldr: First large-scale computational analysis of Indian media framing of human-elephant conflict (HEC). Analyzed 1,968 English articles (28,986 sentences) from Jan 2022–Sep 2025.
