Finds dominant fear/aggression framing via a multi-model sentiment pipeline (transformers, LLMs, domain lexicon). Provides transparent, scalable methodology and releases resources to promote responsible wildlife reporting.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in understanding how media framing of HEC shapes public attitudes and conservation policy, going beyond ecological drivers to examine linguistic portrayals at scale.
AI.method: Dataset: 1,968 full-length news articles (28,986 sentences) from a major English-language outlet, Jan 2022–Sep 2025. Methods: multi-model sentiment framework combining long-context transformers, large language models, and a domain-specific Negative Elephant Portrayal Lexicon; tasks include sentiment quantification, extraction of rationale sentences, and analysis of linguistic patterns. Resources released via anonymized repository.
AI.result: Found predominance of fear-inducing and aggression-related language in portrayals of elephants. Framing potentially influences public hostility and conservation policy. Methodology is transparent and scalable; resources provided to support responsible wildlife reporting.
AI.conclusion: Media portrayal of elephants as dangerous can undermine coexistence efforts; the paper offers a replicable, open framework for monitoring media framing of wildlife and guiding ethical reporting and policy discussions.

title: ARFBench: Benchmarking Time Series Question Answering Ability for Software Incident Response
summary: Time series question-answering (TSQA), in which we ask natural language questions to infer and reason about properties of time series, is a promising yet underexplored capability of foundation models. In this work, we present ARFBench, a TSQA benchmark that evaluates the understanding of multimodal foundation models (FMs) on time series anomalies prevalent in software incident data. ARFBench consists of 750 questions across 142 time series and 5.38M data points from 63 production incidents sourced exclusively from internal telemetry at Datadog. We evaluate leading proprietary and open-source LLMs, VLMs, and time series FMs and observe that frontier VLMs perform markedly better than existing baselines; the leading model (GPT-5) achieves a 62.7% accuracy and 51.9% F1. We next demonstrate the promise of specialized multimodal approaches. We develop a novel TSFM + VLM hybrid prototype which we post-train on a small set of synthetic and real data that yields comparable overall F1 and accuracy with frontier models. Lastly, we find models and human domain experts exhibit complementary strengths. We define a model-expert oracle, a best-of-2 oracle selector over model and expert answers, yielding 82.8% F1 and 87.2% accuracy and establishing a new superhuman frontier for future TSQA models. The benchmark is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Datadog/ARFBench.

AI.tldr: ARFBench benchmarks TSQA on time-series anomalies using multimodal foundation models, showing frontier VLMs beat baselines; a TSFM+VLM hybrid reaches frontier-level performance, and a model-expert oracle yields superhuman F1/accuracy.
AI.motivation: To evaluate multimodal foundation models' capacity to understand and reason about time-series anomalies in real incident data, addressing a gap in TSQA research and assessing potential gains from specialized multimodal approaches.
AI.method: Construct ARFBench with 750 questions across 142 time-series and 5.38M data points from 63 internal Datadog incidents; evaluate LLMs, VLMs, and TSFM-based models; develop a TSFM+VLM hybrid post-trained on synthetic and real data; define a model-expert oracle combining model and human expert answers.
AI.result: Frontier VLMs outperform baselines; GPT-5 achieves 62.7% accuracy and 51.9% F1; TSFM+VLM hybrid reaches comparable accuracy/F1 to frontier models after post-training; the model-expert oracle achieves 82.8% F1 and 87.2% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: ARFBench reveals complementary strengths between models and humans; a model-expert oracle can surpass single-model performance, establishing a new superhuman frontier for TSQA; dataset available publicly.

title: KD-CVG: A Knowledge-Driven Approach for Creative Video Generation
summary: Creative Generation (CG) leverages generative models to automatically produce advertising content that highlights product features, and it has been a significant focus of recent research. However, while CG has advanced considerably, most efforts have concentrated on generating advertising text and images, leaving Creative Video Generation (CVG) relatively underexplored. This gap is largely due to two major challenges faced by Text-to-Video (T2V) models: (a) \textbf{ambiguous semantic alignment}, where models struggle to accurately correlate product selling points with creative video content, and (b) \textbf{inadequate motion adaptability}, resulting in unrealistic movements and distortions. To address these challenges, we develop a comprehensive Advertising Creative Knowledge Base (ACKB) as a foundational resource and propose a knowledge-driven approach (KD-CVG) to overcome the knowledge limitations of existing models. KD-CVG consists of two primary modules: Semantic-Aware Retrieval (SAR) and Multimodal Knowledge Reference (MKR). SAR utilizes the semantic awareness of graph attention networks and reinforcement learning feedback to enhance the model's comprehension of the connections between selling points and creative videos. Building on this, MKR incorporates semantic and motion priors into the T2V model to address existing knowledge gaps. Extensive experiments have demonstrated KD-CVG's superior performance in achieving semantic alignment and motion adaptability, validating its effectiveness over other state-of-the-art methods. The code and dataset will be open source at https://kdcvg.github.io/KDCVG/.

AI.tldr: Introduces Advertising Creative Knowledge Base (ACKB) and a knowledge-driven CVG framework (KD-CVG) with two modules—Semantic-Aware Retrieval (SAR) and Multimodal Knowledge Reference (MKR)—to improve semantic alignment and motion realism in text-to-video advertising; outperforms state-of-the-art methods; code and data released at the project site.
AI.motivation: Creative Video Generation (CVG) for advertising is underexplored due to two main challenges: ambiguous alignment between product selling points and video semantics, and inadequate motion realism. Existing text-to-video models lack a robust knowledge foundation to connect marketing points with creative video content, limiting performance and generalization.
AI.method: Construct an Advertising Creative Knowledge Base (ACKB) as a foundational resource. Propose KD-CVG with two core modules: (1) Semantic-Aware Retrieval (SAR) that uses graph attention networks and reinforcement learning feedback to better map selling points to relevant video semantics; (2) Multimodal Knowledge Reference (MKR) that injects semantic and motion priors into the T2V model to address knowledge gaps and improve temporal consistency and motion realism.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate KD-CVG achieves superior semantic alignment and motion adaptability compared with state-of-the-art methods, validating the effectiveness of the knowledge-driven approach.
AI.conclusion: KD-CVG provides a robust framework for CVG in advertising by leveraging ACKB and targeted priors to bridge knowledge gaps; the approach yields improved alignment and motion feel in generated videos. The authors release code and dataset at the project site https://kdcvg.github.io/KDCVG/ for reproducibility and further development.

title: GeoMind: An Agentic Workflow for Lithology Classification with Reasoned Tool Invocation
summary: Lithology classification in well logs is a fundamental geoscience data mining task that aims to infer rock types from multi dimensional geophysical sequences. Despite recent progress, existing approaches typically formulate the problem as a static, single-step discriminative mapping. This static paradigm limits evidence-based diagnostic reasoning against geological standards, often yielding predictions that are detached from geological reality due to a lack of domain priors. In this work, we propose GeoMind, a tool-augmented agentic framework that models lithology classification as a sequential reasoning process. GeoMind organizes its toolkit into perception, reasoning, and analysis modules, which respectively translate raw logs into semantic trends, infer lithology hypotheses from multi-source evidence, and verify predictions against stratigraphic constraints. A global planner adaptively coordinates these modules based on input characteristics, enabling geologically plausible and evidence-grounded decisions. To guarantee the logical consistency of GeoMind, we introduce a fine-grained process supervision strategy. Unlike standard methods that focus solely on final outcomes, our approach optimizes intermediate reasoning steps, ensuring the validity of decision trajectories and alignment to geological constraints. Experiments on four benchmark well-log datasets demonstrate that GeoMind consistently outperforms strong baselines in classification performance while providing transparent and traceable decision-making processes.

AI.tldr: A tool-augmented, agentic framework GeoMind redefines lithology classification as sequential reasoning, outperforming baselines and delivering traceable decisions.
AI.motivation: Static, single-step discriminative models neglect geological priors and evidence-based reasoning. There is a need for sequential, priors-informed reasoning that yields geologically plausible and interpretable decisions.
AI.method: Perception translates raw logs into semantic trends; Reasoning infers lithology hypotheses from multi-source evidence; Analysis verifies predictions against stratigraphic constraints; A global planner adaptively coordinates modules; Fine-grained process supervision optimizes intermediate reasoning steps for logical consistency and alignment with geological constraints.
AI.result: GeoMind consistently outperforms strong baselines on four benchmark well-log datasets in classification accuracy while providing transparent, traceable decision-making processes.
AI.conclusion: Sequential, tool-augmented agentic frameworks with domain priors and process supervision can yield geologically plausible, interpretable lithology classifications and offer a scalable approach for geoscience data mining.

title: The Recurrent Transformer: Greater Effective Depth and Efficient Decoding
summary: Transformers process tokens in parallel but are temporally shallow: at position $t$, each layer attends to key-value pairs computed based on the previous layer, yielding a depth capped by the number of layers. Recurrent models offer unbounded temporal depth but suffer from optimization instability and historically underutilize modern accelerators. We introduce the Recurrent Transformer, a simple architectural change where each layer attends to key-value pairs computed off its own activations, yielding layerwise recurrent memory while preserving standard autoregressive decoding cost. We show that the architecture can emulate both (i) a conventional Transformer and (ii) token-to-token recurrent updates under mild assumptions, while avoiding optimization instability. Naively, prefill/training appears bandwidth-bound with effective arithmetic intensity near $1$ because keys and values are revealed sequentially; we give an exact tiling-based algorithm that preserves the mathematical computation while reducing HBM traffic from $Θ(N^2)$ to $Θ(N\log N)$, increasing effective arithmetic intensity to $Θ(N/\log N)$ for sequence length $N$. On 150M and 300M parameter C4 pretraining, Recurrent Transformers improve cross-entropy over a parameter-matched Transformer baseline and achieve the improvement with fewer layers (fixed parameters), suggesting that recurrence can trade depth for width, thus reducing KV cache memory footprint and inference latency.

AI.tldr: Recurrent Transformer introduces layerwise recurrence to enable unbounded temporal depth while maintaining autoregressive decoding; it reduces KV cache traffic and improves efficiency, achieving better perplexity with fewer layers on large pretraining models.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limited temporal depth of standard Transformers and instability of recurrent models; leverage recurrence to enable memory across layers without increasing decoding cost, and improve hardware efficiency (HBM bandwidth, arithmetic intensity).
AI.method: Make each layer attend to keys/values computed from its own activations, creating layerwise recurrent memory. Demonstrate equivalence to standard Transformer and to token-to-token recurrence under mild assumptions. Develop a tiling-based algorithm to reduce KV-cache traffic from Θ(N^2) to Θ(N log N) while preserving the same computation. Analyze arithmetic intensity and show practical gains.
AI.result: The architecture can emulate both a conventional Transformer and token-to-token recurrence; avoids optimization instability; demonstrates reduced KV cache traffic and higher arithmetic intensity. Pretraining on 150M and 300M parameter models shows improved cross-entropy over a parameter-matched Transformer baseline, with fewer layers (same parameter count).
AI.conclusion: Recurrence can trade depth for width, providing unbounded temporal depth with practical training/inference benefits, reduced memory footprint, and potential latency reductions without sacrificing autoregressive decoding performance.

title: EdgeFormer: local patch-based edge detection transformer on point clouds
comment: 22 pages, 9 figures. Published in Pattern Analysis and Applications
summary: Edge points on 3D point clouds can clearly convey 3D geometry and surface characteristics, therefore, edge detection is widely used in many vision applications with high industrial and commercial demands. However, the fine-grained edge features are difficult to detect effectively as they are generally densely distributed or exhibit small-scale surface gradients. To address this issue, we present a learning-based edge detection network, named EdgeFormer, which mainly consists of two stages. Based on the observation that spatially neighboring points tend to exhibit high correlation, forming the local underlying surface, we convert the edge detection of the entire point cloud into a point classification based on local patches. Therefore, in the first stage, we construct local patch feature descriptors that describe the local neighborhood around each point. In the second stage, we classify each point by analyzing the local patch feature descriptors generated in the first stage. Due to the conversion of the point cloud into local patches, the proposed method can effectively extract the finer details. The experimental results show that our model demonstrates competitive performance compared to six baselines.

AI.tldr: EdgeFormer is a two-stage learning-based edge detector for 3D point clouds that converts edge detection into local patch-based point classification, achieving finer edge detail and competitive performance against six baselines.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained edge features in 3D point clouds are difficult to detect due to dense distributions and small-scale surface gradients. Exploiting local neighborhood correlations to form a local surface can simplify edge detection by reframing it as a patch-based classification problem.
AI.method: Stage 1 constructs local patch feature descriptors describing the neighborhood around each point. Stage 2 classifies each point by analyzing the local patch descriptors, turning point-cloud edge detection into a patch-based classification task to capture finer details.
AI.result: Experimental results show competitive performance of EdgeFormer compared with six baselines.
AI.conclusion: EdgeFormer demonstrates that local patch-based representations can effectively extract finer edge details in 3D point clouds, offering a promising approach for high-precision edge detection in vision applications.

title: BioMiner: A Multi-modal System for Automated Mining of Protein-Ligand Bioactivity Data from Literature
summary: Protein-ligand bioactivity data published in the literature are essential for drug discovery, yet manual curation struggles to keep pace with rapidly growing literature. Automated bioactivity extraction remains challenging because it requires not only interpreting biochemical semantics distributed across text, tables, and figures, but also reconstructing chemically exact ligand structures (e.g., Markush structures). To address this bottleneck, we introduce BioMiner, a multi-modal extraction framework that explicitly separates bioactivity semantic interpretation from ligand structure construction. Within BioMiner, bioactivity semantics are inferred through direct reasoning, while chemical structures are resolved via a chemical-structure-grounded visual semantic reasoning paradigm, in which multi-modal large language models operate on chemically grounded visual representations to infer inter-structure relationships, and exact molecular construction is delegated to domain chemistry tools. For rigorous evaluation and method development, we further establish BioVista, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 16,457 bioactivity entries curated from 500 publications. BioMiner validates its extraction ability and provides a quantitative baseline, achieving an F1 score of 0.32 for bioactivity triplets. BioMiner's practical utility is demonstrated via three applications: (1) extracting 82,262 data from 11,683 papers to build a pre-training database that improves downstream models performance by 3.9%; (2) enabling a human-in-the-loop workflow that doubles the number of high-quality NLRP3 bioactivity data, helping 38.6% improvement over 28 QSAR models and identification of 16 hit candidates with novel scaffolds; and (3) accelerating protein-ligand complex bioactivity annotation, achieving a 5.59-fold speed increase and 5.75% accuracy improvement over manual workflows in PoseBusters dataset.

AI.tldr: BioMiner is a multi-modal extraction framework that separates bioactivity interpretation from ligand structure construction, using semantic reasoning for bioactivity and a chemistry-grounded visual reasoning for structures, evaluated on BioVista with strong downstream benefits in pretraining, human-in-the-loop curation, and rapid bioactivity annotation, albeit with a modest F1 on bioactivity triplets.
AI.motivation: Manual curation of protein–ligand bioactivity data is slow; automated extraction must handle text, tables, and figures, and reconstruct precise ligand structures including Markush forms.
AI.method: BioMiner explicitly decouples semantics and structure. Bioactivity semantics are inferred via direct reasoning. Structures are resolved through a chemical-structure-grounded visual semantic reasoning pipeline using multi-modal LLMs on chemically grounded visuals, with exact molecular construction delegated to domain chemistry tools. A benchmark BioVista with 16,457 entries from 500 pubs is developed.
AI.result: BioMiner achieves F1=0.32 for bioactivity triplets. Applications: (1) extracts 82,262 data from 11,683 papers to form a pretraining DB improving downstream models by 3.9%; (2) enables a human-in-the-loop workflow doubling high-quality NLRP3 data, improving 28 QSAR models by 38.6% and identifying 16 novel-scaffold hits; (3) accelerates protein–ligand complex bioactivity annotation with 5.59x speed and 5.75% accuracy gain over manual in PoseBusters.
AI.conclusion: BioMiner demonstrates extraction capability and provides a quantitative baseline; its modular separation of semantics and structures offers practical utility for large-scale bioactivity curation and downstream modeling, with potential for broader adoption and further improvements.

title: Learning Dynamic Representations and Policies from Multimodal Clinical Time-Series with Informative Missingness
summary: Multimodal clinical records contain structured measurements and clinical notes recorded over time, offering rich temporal information about the evolution of patient health. Yet these observations are sparse, and whether they are recorded depends on the patient's latent condition. Observation patterns also differ across modalities, as structured measurements and clinical notes arise under distinct recording processes. While prior work has developed methods that accommodate missingness in clinical time series, how to extract and use the information carried by the observation process itself remains underexplored. We therefore propose a patient representation learning framework for multimodal clinical time series that explicitly leverages informative missingness. The framework combines (1) a multimodal encoder that captures signals from structured and textual data together with their observation patterns, (2) a Bayesian filtering module that updates a latent patient state over time from observed multimodal signals, and (3) downstream modules for offline treatment policy learning and patient outcome prediction based on the learned patient state. We evaluate the framework on ICU sepsis cohorts from MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, and eICU. It improves both offline treatment policy learning and adverse outcome prediction, achieving FQE 0.679 versus 0.528 for clinician behavior and AUROC 0.886 for post-72-hour mortality prediction on MIMIC-III.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multimodal patient representation learning framework that exploits informative missingness in structured time series and clinical notes, using a multimodal encoder, Bayesian filtering, and downstream policy learning/outcome prediction; evaluated on ICU sepsis cohorts with improved policy imitation (FQE) and mortality AUROC.
AI.motivation: Observation patterns and missingness in multimodal clinical data reflect latent patient state and recording processes. Existing methods typically treat missing data as noise and ignore the informative structure of observation processes.
AI.method: 1) Multimodal encoder integrating structured measurements and text with observation patterns; 2) Bayesian filtering to update a latent patient state over time from observed multimodal signals; 3) downstream modules for offline treatment policy learning and patient outcome prediction based on the learned state.
AI.result: On MIMIC-III/MIMIC-IV/eICU sepsis cohorts, shows improved offline policy learning and outcome prediction: FQE of 0.679 vs 0.528 for clinician behavior, AUROC of 0.886 for post-72-hour mortality prediction (on MIMIC-III).
AI.conclusion: Leveraging informative missingness in multimodal clinical time series yields better representations for decision support, enhancing policy evaluation and adverse outcome prediction in ICU sepsis.

title: VG-CoT: Towards Trustworthy Visual Reasoning via Grounded Chain-of-Thought
summary: The advancement of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) requires precise local region-based reasoning that faithfully grounds the model's logic in actual visual evidence. However, existing datasets face limitations in scalability due to extensive manual annotation and lack of explicit alignment between multi-step reasoning and corresponding image regions, which constrains the evaluation of model trustworthiness. To address these challenges, we propose the Visual Grounding Chain-of-Thought (VG-CoT) dataset, which explicitly links each reasoning step to real visual evidence within the image through a fully automated three-stage pipeline. The pipeline first extracts object- and text-level visual evidence using state-of-the-art detection and OCR models, then generates step-by-step grounded reasoning with GPT-4o, and finally refines the grounding through a rationale-driven open-set detection process. In addition, we introduce a new benchmark that comprehensively evaluates LVLMs reasoning across three complementary dimensions: Rationale Quality, Answer Accuracy, and Reasoning-Answer Alignment. Experiments with representative LVLMs, including LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2-VL, demonstrate consistent improvements on most evaluation metrics, confirming that VG-CoT effectively enhances trustworthy, evidence-based reasoning while maintaining scalable and cost-efficient dataset construction. The dataset and code will be released publicly upon acceptance to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: VG-CoT automates grounding of LVLM reasoning to image regions via a 3-stage pipeline and proposes a tri-dimensional benchmark (Rationale Quality, Answer Accuracy, Reasoning-Answer Alignment) to evaluate trustworthiness and scalability.
AI.motivation: Current LVLM evaluation is limited by manual annotation and poor alignment between multi-step reasoning and visual evidence; there is a need for scalable, explicit grounding to improve trustworthiness of LVLMs.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) extract object- and text-level visual evidence using state-of-the-art detectors and OCR; (2) generate step-by-step grounded reasoning with GPT-4o; (3) refine grounding via rationale-driven open-set detection; plus a new benchmark evaluating three dimensions: Rationale Quality, Answer Accuracy, Reasoning-Answer Alignment.
AI.result: LVLMs such as LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2-VL show consistent improvements on most evaluation metrics, indicating VG-CoT enhances trustworthy, evidence-based reasoning; the approach is scalable and cost-efficient for dataset construction; code will be released publicly upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: VG-CoT provides an effective grounding and evaluation framework for LVLMs, enabling more trustworthy reasoning while maintaining scalability; the dataset and code will be publicly released to support further research.

title: CAP: Controllable Alignment Prompting for Unlearning in LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained on unfiltered corpora inherently risk retaining sensitive information, necessitating selective knowledge unlearning for regulatory compliance and ethical safety. However, existing parameter-modifying methods face fundamental limitations: high computational costs, uncontrollable forgetting boundaries, and strict dependency on model weight access. These constraints render them impractical for closed-source models, yet current non-invasive alternatives remain unsystematic and reliant on empirical experience. To address these challenges, we propose the Controllable Alignment Prompting for Unlearning (CAP) framework, an end-to-end prompt-driven unlearning paradigm. CAP decouples unlearning into a learnable prompt optimization process via reinforcement learning, where a prompt generator collaborates with the LLM to suppress target knowledge while preserving general capabilities selectively. This approach enables reversible knowledge restoration through prompt revocation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAP achieves precise, controllable unlearning without updating model parameters, establishing a dynamic alignment mechanism that overcomes the transferability limitations of prior methods.

AI.tldr: A prompt-driven, RL-optimized unlearning framework (CAP) that suppresses targeted knowledge in LLMs without updating weights, enabling reversible and controllable unlearning suitable for closed-source models.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained on unfiltered data risk leaking sensitive information. Parameter-modifying methods are costly, have uncertain forgetting boundaries, and require access to model weights; non-invasive methods lack systematic, repeatable approaches. There is a need for reversible, controllable unlearning that works with closed-source models.
AI.method: CAP decouples unlearning into a learnable prompt optimization process via reinforcement learning. A prompt generator co-designs with the LLM to suppress target knowledge while preserving general capabilities, all without updating model parameters. Revocation of prompts allows reversible restoration of knowledge.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate CAP achieves precise and controllable unlearning without modifying model weights, providing a dynamic alignment mechanism that mitigates transferability issues seen in prior methods. The approach preserves general capabilities while suppressing targeted knowledge, and supports reversible restoration through prompt revocation.
AI.conclusion: CAP offers a practical, end-to-end prompt-driven unlearning framework that enables parameter-free, reversible, and controllable suppression of sensitive knowledge in LLMs, with strong potential for deployment on closed-source models and improved safety and regulatory compliance.

title: You Only Gaussian Once: Controllable 3D Gaussian Splatting for Ultra-Densely Sampled Scenes
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized neural rendering, yet existing methods remain predominantly research prototypes ill-suited for production-level deployment. We identify a critical "Industry-Academia Gap" hindering real-world application: unpredictable resource consumption from heuristic Gaussian growth, the "sparsity shield" of current benchmarks that rewards hallucination over physical fidelity, and severe multi-sensor data pollution. To bridge this gap, we propose YOGO (You Only Gaussian Once), a system-level framework that reformulates the stochastic growth process into a deterministic, budget-aware equilibrium. YOGO integrates a novel budget controller for hardware-constrained resource allocation and an availability-registration protocol for robust multi-sensor fusion. To push the boundaries of reconstruction fidelity, we introduce Immersion v1.0, the first ultra-dense indoor dataset specifically designed to break the "sparsity shield." By providing saturated viewpoint coverage, Immersion v1.0 forces algorithms to focus on extreme physical fidelity rather than viewpoint interpolation, and enables the community to focus on the upper limits of high-fidelity reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that YOGO achieves state-of-the-art visual quality while maintaining a strictly deterministic profile, establishing a new standard for production-grade 3DGS. To facilitate reproducibility, part scenes of Immersion v1.0 dataset and source code of YOGO has been publicly released. The project link is https://jjrcn.github.io/YOGO/.

AI.tldr: YOGO introduces a deterministic, budget-aware framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting to close the Industry-Academia Gap, paired with Immersion v1.0—a dense indoor dataset—to counter the sparsity bias and push realism. It reports state-of-the-art visual quality with reproducible, production-friendly behavior and releases code and parts of the dataset for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Address three bottlenecks hindering production deployment: (1) unpredictable resource consumption from heuristic Gaussian growth, (2) the sparsity shield in benchmarks that rewards hallucination over physical fidelity, and (3) severe multi-sensor data pollution.
AI.method: Propose a system-level reformulation of the growth process into a deterministic, budget-aware equilibrium (YOGO) with a budget controller for hardware-constrained resource allocation and an availability-registration protocol for robust multi-sensor fusion; introduce Immersion v1.0 with saturated viewpoint coverage to force high-fidelity reconstruction; release parts of dataset and source code for reproducibility.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows YOGO achieves state-of-the-art visual quality under a strictly deterministic profile, establishing a production-grade baseline for 3DGaussian Splatting; Immersion v1.0 challenges the sparsity bias and accelerates research toward extreme fidelity; public release enhances reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Bridges the Industry-Academia Gap in 3DGS by delivering a production-oriented framework and dataset, setting a new standard for deterministic, resource-bounded high-fidelity reconstruction and encouraging reproducibility.

title: The CriticalSet problem: Identifying Critical Contributors in Bipartite Dependency Networks
summary: Identifying critical nodes in complex networks is a fundamental task in graph mining. Yet, methods addressing an all-or-nothing coverage mechanics in a bipartite dependency network, a graph with two types of nodes where edges represent dependency relationships across the two groups only, remain largely unexplored. We formalize the CriticalSet problem: given an arbitrary bipartite graph modeling dependencies of items on contributors, identify the set of k contributors whose removal isolates the largest number of items. We prove that this problem is NP-hard and requires maximizing a supermodular set function, for which standard forward greedy algorithms provide no approximation guarantees. Consequently, we model CriticalSet as a coalitional game, deriving a closed-form centrality, ShapleyCov, based on the Shapley value. This measure can be interpreted as the expected number of items isolated by a contributor's departure. Leveraging these insights, we propose MinCov, a linear-time iterative peeling algorithm that explicitly accounts for connection redundancy, prioritizing contributors who uniquely support many items. Extensive experiments on synthetic and large-scale real datasets, including a Wikipedia graph with over 250 million edges, reveal that MinCov and ShapleyCov significantly outperform traditional baselines. Notably, MinCov achieves near-optimal performance, within 0.02 AUC of a Stochastic Hill Climbing metaheuristic, while remaining several orders of magnitude faster.

AI.tldr: Defines CriticalSet in bipartite dependency graphs, proves NP-hardness and intractability of standard greedy guarantees for maximizing the ensuing supermodular objective, introduces ShapleyCov centrality from a coalitional-game framing, and presents MinCov—a scalable peeling algorithm. Empirical results on synthetic and large real networks (including a 250M-edge Wikipedia graph) show MinCov near-optimal to a stochastic hill-climbing baseline but far faster, with ShapleyCov offering a principled contributor centrality.
AI.motivation: Explore node-importance in bipartite dependency networks where coverage is all-or-nothing, a setting with two node types and cross-group dependencies, and where existing coverage-based methods lack theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Formalize CriticalSet as selecting k contributors whose removal maximizes isolated items; prove NP-hard and that the objective is a maximization of a supermodular function (lacking greedy guarantees). Model as a coalitional game and derive a closed-form Shapley value-based centrality (ShapleyCov). Develop MinCov, a linear-time iterative peeling algorithm that accounts for redundancy and highlights contributors uniquely supporting many items.
AI.result: Definition of ShapleyCov as the expected number of items isolated by a contributor’s departure; MinCov provides a scalable, near-linear-time solution. Extensive experiments on synthetic data and large-scale real data (Wikipedia with >250M edges) show MinCov and ShapleyCov outperform baselines, with MinCov achieving near-optimal AUC within 0.02 of Stochastic Hill Climbing while being orders of magnitude faster.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a principled, scalable framework for identifying critical contributors in bipartite dependency networks, combining a theoretically grounded centrality measure with an efficient peeling algorithm that accounts for redundancy and delivers strong empirical performance.

title: Improving Performance in Classification Tasks with LCEN and the Weighted Focal Differentiable MCC Loss
summary: The LASSO-Clip-EN (LCEN) algorithm was previously introduced for nonlinear, interpretable feature selection and machine learning. However, its design and use was limited to regression tasks. In this work, we create a modified version of the LCEN algorithm that is suitable for classification tasks and maintains its desirable properties, such as interpretability. This modified LCEN algorithm is evaluated on four widely used binary and multiclass classification datasets. In these experiments, LCEN is compared against 10 other model types and consistently reaches high test-set macro F$_1$ score and Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) metrics, higher than that of the majority of investigated models. LCEN models for classification remain sparse, eliminating an average of 56% of all input features in the experiments performed. Furthermore, LCEN-selected features are used to retrain all models using the same data, leading to statistically significant performance improvements in three of the experiments and insignificant differences in the fourth when compared to using all features or other feature selection methods. Simultaneously, the weighted focal differentiable MCC (diffMCC) loss function is evaluated on the same datasets. Models trained with the diffMCC loss function are always the best-performing methods in these experiments, and reach test-set macro F$_1$ scores that are, on average, 4.9% higher and MCCs that are 8.5% higher than those obtained by models trained with the weighted cross-entropy loss. These results highlight the performance of LCEN as a feature selection and machine learning algorithm also for classification tasks, and how the diffMCC loss function can train very accurate models, surpassing the weighted cross-entropy loss in the tasks investigated.

AI.tldr: Classification-enabled LCEN is introduced; achieves sparse feature selection and strong performance across four datasets, outperforming many baselines; diffMCC loss further improves macro F1 and MCC over cross-entropy.
AI.motivation: Extend LCEN from regression to classification while preserving interpretability and sparsity; leverage differentiable MCC loss to improve accuracy.
AI.method: Adapt LCEN for classification; evaluate on four binary/multiclass datasets; compare with 10 models; measure macro F1 and MCC; assess feature selection by retraining with LCEN-selected features; implement weighted diffMCC loss and compare to weighted cross-entropy.
AI.result: LCEN-C models achieve high test-set macro F1 and MCC, eliminating about 56% of input features on average; retraining with LCEN-selected features yields significant improvements in 3/4 experiments; diffMCC models outperform weighted cross-entropy by ~4.9% in macro F1 and ~8.5% in MCC.
AI.conclusion: LCEN is effective for classification with interpretable, sparse feature selection; diffMCC loss further enhances predictive performance across tasks.

title: S1-VL: Scientific Multimodal Reasoning Model with Thinking-with-Images
summary: We present S1-VL, a multimodal reasoning model for scientific domains that natively supports two complementary reasoning paradigms: Scientific Reasoning, which relies on structured chain-of-thought, and Thinking-with-Images, which enables the model to actively manipulate images through Python code execution during reasoning. In the Thinking-with-Images mode, the model generates and executes image-processing code in a sandbox environment, obtains intermediate visual results, and continues reasoning in a multi-turn iterative manner. This design is particularly effective for challenging scenarios such as high-resolution scientific chart interpretation, microscopic image understanding, and geometry-assisted reasoning. To construct the training data, we collect scientific multimodal datasets spanning six disciplines: mathematics, physics, chemistry, astronomy, geography, and biology. We further develop a six-dimensional quality filtering framework for reasoning trajectories. To mitigate redundant, ineffective, and erroneous visual operations commonly found in existing datasets, we propose a multi-stage filtering pipeline together with an adaptive data routing strategy. This strategy converts samples with low visual information gain into pure Reasoning-mode data, enabling the model to learn when image operations are truly necessary. S1-VL is trained through a four-stage progressive pipeline: scientific multimodal SFT, Thinking-with-Images cold-start SFT, and two stages of reinforcement learning with SAPO. We build S1-VL-32B on top of Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking and evaluate it on 13 benchmarks. Experimental results show that S1-VL-32B achieves state-of-the-art performance on all five Thinking-with-Images benchmarks, including HRBench-4K, HRBench-8K, MME-RealWorld-CN, MME-RealWorld-Lite, and V*, and outperforms compared systems on scientific reasoning benchmarks such as Physics and VRSBench.

AI.tldr: S1-VL introduces dual reasoning modes (Scientific Reasoning with chain-of-thought and Thinking-with-Images via Python-driven image manipulation) for scientific multimodal tasks, achieving SOTA on multiple benchmarks after a multi-stage data collection, filtering, and training pipeline.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of static reasoning in scientific multimodal tasks and leverage active image processing to interpret complex visuals, while ensuring data quality through filtering and routing.
AI.method: Architectural design with Thinking-with-Images mode; sand-boxed Python execution for image-processing; six-domain multimodal dataset; six-dimension quality filtering; multi-stage data pipeline; adaptive routing; four-stage training including reinforcement learning with SAPO; fine-tuned on Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking base; evaluation on 13 benchmarks.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on five Thinking-with-Images benchmarks; improved performance on physics and VRSBench for scientific reasoning; overall strong cross-domain results.
AI.conclusion: Integrating programmable image manipulation with structured reasoning across disciplines yields robust multimodal scientific reasoning; quality control and adaptive data routing are key to efficiency and performance.

title: Unbiased Prevalence Estimation with Multicalibrated LLMs
summary: Estimating the prevalence of a category in a population using imperfect measurement devices (diagnostic tests, classifiers, or large language models) is fundamental to science, public health, and online trust and safety. Standard approaches correct for known device error rates but assume these rates remain stable across populations. We show this assumption fails under covariate shift and that multicalibration, which enforces calibration conditional on the input features rather than just on average, is sufficient for unbiased prevalence estimation under such shift. Standard calibration and quantification methods fail to provide this guarantee. Our work connects recent theoretical work on fairness to a longstanding measurement problem spanning nearly all academic disciplines. A simulation confirms that standard methods exhibit bias growing with shift magnitude, while a multicalibrated estimator maintains near-zero bias. While we focus the discussion mostly on LLMs, our theoretical results apply to any classification model. Two empirical applications -- estimating employment prevalence across U.S. states using the American Community Survey, and classifying political texts across four countries using an LLM -- demonstrate that multicalibration substantially reduces bias in practice, while highlighting that calibration data should cover the key feature dimensions along which target populations may differ.

AI.tldr: Under covariate shift, multicalibration yields unbiased prevalence estimates, unlike standard calibration; validated through simulations and two real-world applications.
AI.motivation: Bias in prevalence estimates arises when device error rates vary across populations; extend calibration theory to be robust under distribution shift; connect fairness calibration with measurement problems across disciplines.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing multicalibration suffices for unbiased prevalence under covariate shift; compare with standard calibration; perform simulations; apply to two empirical datasets (ACS employment prevalence; LLM-based political text classification across four countries); emphasize data coverage of key feature dimensions.
AI.result: Multicalibrated estimators display near-zero bias under shift; standard methods show bias growing with shift; calibration data should cover the dimensions most relevant to population differences; multicalibration improves practical bias reduction.
AI.conclusion: Multicalibration provides robust prevalence estimation across domains; links fairness and measurement theory; guidance on calibrating data coverage for shift-prone tasks.

title: Hyperloop Transformers
summary: LLM architecture research generally aims to maximize model quality subject to fixed compute/latency budgets. However, many applications of interest such as edge and on-device deployment are further constrained by the model's memory footprint, thus motivating parameter-efficient architectures for language modeling. This paper describes a simple architecture that improves the parameter-efficiency of LLMs. Our architecture makes use of looped Transformers as a core primitive, which reuse Transformer layers across depth and are thus more parameter-efficient than ordinary (depth-matched) Transformers. We organize the looped Transformer into three blocks--begin, middle, and end blocks--where each block itself consists of multiple Transformer layers, and only the middle block is applied recurrently across depth. We augment the looped middle block with hyper-connections (Xie et al., 2026), which expand the residual stream into matrix-valued residual streams. Hyper-connections are applied only after each loop, and therefore add minimal new parameters and compute cost. Across various model scales, we find that our Hyper-Connected Looped Transformer (Hyperloop Transformer) is able to outperform depth-matched Transformer and mHC Transformer baselines despite using approximately 50% fewer parameters. The outperformance persists through post-training weight quantization, thus positioning Hyperloop Transformers as an attractive architecture for memory-efficient language modeling.

AI.tldr: Hyperloop Transformer introduces a memory-efficient LLM by looping Transformer depths and using matrix-valued residuals via hyper-connections, achieving ~50% fewer parameters with strong performance, including under quantization.
AI.motivation: Edge/on-device deployment constraints demand parameter-efficient architectures; standard deep transformers incur large memory footprints.
AI.method: A three-block looped Transformer with begin/middle/end blocks; only the middle block is applied recurrently across depth; hyper-connections augment the middle block to create matrix-valued residual streams; hyper-connections added after each loop with minimal parameter/compute overhead.
AI.result: Across model scales, Hyperloop Transformer outperforms depth-matched Transformer and mHC baselines while using ~50% fewer parameters; performance persists after post-training weight quantization.
AI.conclusion: Hyperloop Transformer is a memory-efficient, quantization-robust architecture for language modeling, suitable for memory-constrained deployments.

title: Pre-process for segmentation task with nonlinear diffusion filters
summary:
This paper deals with the case of using nonlinear diffusion filters to obtain piecewise constant images as a previous process for segmentation techniques.
  We first show an intrinsic formulation for the nonlinear diffusion equation to provide some design conditions on the diffusion filters. According to this theoretical framework, we propose a new family of diffusivities; they are obtained from nonlinear diffusion techniques and are related with backward diffusion. Their goal is to split the image in closed contours with a homogenized grey intensity inside and with no blurred edges.
  We also prove that our filters satisfy the well-posedness semi-discrete and full discrete scale-space requirements. This shows that by using semi-implicit schemes, a forward nonlinear diffusion equation is solved, instead of a backward nonlinear diffusion equation, connecting with an edge-preserving process. Under the conditions established for the diffusivity and using a stopping criterion for the diffusion time, we get piecewise constant images with a low computational effort.
  Finally, we test our filter with real images and we illustrate the effects of our diffusivity function as a method to get piecewise constant images.
  The code is available at https://github.com/cplatero/NonlinearDiffusion.

AI.tldr: Proposes a nonlinear diffusion-based preprocessing to obtain piecewise-constant images for segmentation, with a new family of diffusivities linked to backward diffusion, proves well-posedness for semi-discrete and fully discrete scale-space, uses semi-implicit forward diffusion, and validates on real images; code available.
AI.motivation: To create preprocessing filters that produce piecewise-constant images with clear edges and homogeneous interiors, enabling robust segmentation, by grounding diffusivity design in an intrinsic diffusion framework.
AI.method: Formulates an intrinsic nonlinear diffusion equation; derives design conditions for diffusion filters; introduces a new class of diffusivities related to backward diffusion; proves well-posedness of semi-discrete and fully discrete scale-space; employs semi-implicit schemes to solve forward diffusion; uses a stopping criterion on diffusion time to achieve piecewise-constant outputs.
AI.result: The proposed diffusivities yield piecewise-constant images with low computational cost; validated on real images, showing edge preservation and interior homogenization; publicly available code.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a theoretically sound and computationally efficient pathway to obtain segmentation-ready, piecewise-constant images via edge-preserving forward diffusion, with potential as a preprocessing step before segmentation.

title: Engaged AI Governance: Addressing the Last Mile Challenge Through Internal Expert Collaboration
summary: Under the EU AI Act, translating AI governance requirements into software development practice remains challenging. While AI governance frameworks exist at industry and organizational levels, empirical evidence of team-level implementation is scarce. We address this "Last Mile" Challenge through insider action research embedded within an AI startup. We present a legal-text-to-action pipeline that translates EU AI Act requirements into actionable strategies through internal expert collaboration by extracting requirements from legal text, engaging practitioners in assessment and ideation, and prioritizing implementation through collective evaluation. Our analysis reveals three patterns in how practitioners perceive regulatory requirements: convergence (compliance aligns with development priorities), existing practice (current work already satisfies requirements), and disconnection (requirements perceived as administrative overhead). Based on these patterns, we discuss when governance might be treated genuinely or performatively. Practitioners prioritize requirements that serve end-users or their own development needs, but view verification-oriented requirements as box-ticking exercises. This distinction suggests a translation challenge: regulatory requirements risk superficial treatment unless practitioners understand how compliance serves system quality and user protection. Expert collaboration offers a practical mechanism for transforming governance from external imposition to shared ownership and making previously invisible governance work visible and collective.

AI.tldr: A legal-text-to-action pipeline to translate EU AI Act requirements into actionable software development strategies via insider action research; identifies three practitioner perception patterns (convergence, existing practice, disconnection) and argues for expert collaboration to turn governance from external imposition into shared ownership.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between governance frameworks and day-to-day software development; empirical team-level implementation evidence is scarce.
AI.method: Insider action research in an AI startup; develop a pipeline that extracts regulatory requirements from legal text, engages practitioners in assessment and ideation, and prioritizes implementation through collective evaluation.
AI.result: Three patterns in practitioner perception: convergence (compliance aligns with development priorities), existing practice (current work already satisfies requirements), disconnection (requirements seen as administrative overhead). Practitioners favor end-user or development needs; verification-oriented requirements seen as box-ticking. The translation challenge is to show how compliance enhances system quality and user protection. Expert collaboration can transform governance into shared ownership and reveal governance work.
AI.conclusion: Expert collaboration enables embedding governance in development, moving from external imposition to legitimate, visible, and collective practice; governance should be treated as an integral driver of system quality and user protection rather than mere administrative overhead.

title: Measure Twice, Click Once: Co-evolving Proposer and Visual Critic via Reinforcement Learning for GUI Grounding
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) grounding requires mapping natural language instructions to precise pixel coordinates. However, due to visually homogeneous elements and dense layouts, models typically grasp semantic intent yet struggle with achieving precise localization. While scaling sampling attempts (Pass@k) reveals potential gains, static self-consistency strategies derived from geometric clustering often yield limited improvements, as the model's predictions tend to be spatially dispersed. In this paper, we propose replacing static consistency strategies with a learnable selection mechanism that selects the optimal target by critiquing its own proposals rendered on the screenshot. Given the significant disparity between the model's grounding and critiquing capabilities, we propose a co-evolving Propose-then-Critic framework. To jointly optimize these, we introduce a maturity-aware adaptive co-evolutionary reinforcement learning paradigm. This approach dynamically balances the training objectives of proposer and critic, where the diversity of the proposer's outputs enhances critic robustness, while the critic's maturing discrimination capability conversely unlocks the proposer's potential for extensive spatial exploration, fostering the mutual reinforcement and co-evolution of both capabilities, thereby ensuring generalizability to adapt to diverse and complex interface layouts. Extensive experiments over 6 benchmarks show that our method significantly enhances both grounding accuracy and critic reliability.

AI.tldr: A co-evolving Propose-then-Critic RL framework replaces static geometric consistency with a learnable critique mechanism to improve GUI grounding localization; demonstrates improved accuracy and critic reliability across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Geometric clustering-based static consistency poorly constrains spatial localization in visually dense, homogeneous GUI layouts; dynamic, learnable feedback between proposer and critic is needed to boost precision and generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a Proposer that suggests candidate grounding regions and a Critic that critiques and ranks rendered proposals; employ a maturity-aware adaptive co-evolutionary reinforcement learning regime to balance training objectives; co-evolution enhances diversity of proposals and critic discrimination, enabling broader spatial exploration.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks, the method yields significant improvements in grounding accuracy and critic reliability; demonstrates generalization to diverse interface layouts.
AI.conclusion: Mutual reinforcement through co-evolution of proposer and critic yields robust GUI grounding under challenging layouts; the approach generalizes beyond training distributions.

title: UHR-DETR: Efficient End-to-End Small Object Detection for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery has become essential for modern remote sensing, offering unprecedented spatial coverage. However, detecting small objects in such vast scenes presents a critical dilemma: retaining the original resolution for small objects causes prohibitive memory bottlenecks. Conversely, conventional compromises like image downsampling or patch cropping either erase small objects or destroy context. To break this dilemma, we propose UHR-DETR, an efficient end-to-end transformer-based detector designed for UHR imagery. First, we introduce a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder that dynamically allocates finite computational resources to informative high-resolution regions, ensuring maximum object coverage with minimal spatial redundancy. Second, we design a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder. By integrating macroscopic scene awareness with microscopic object details, this module resolves semantic ambiguities and prevents scene fragmentation. Extensive experiments on the UHR imagery datasets (e.g., STAR and SODA-A) demonstrate the superiority of UHR-DETR under strict hardware constraints (e.g., a single 24GB RTX 3090). It achieves a 2.8\% mAP improvement while delivering a 10$\times$ inference speedup compared to standard sliding-window baselines on the STAR dataset. Our codes and models will be available at https://github.com/Li-JingFang/UHR-DETR.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based detector for ultra-high-resolution imagery that uses a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder and a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder to efficiently detect small objects in large scenes under hardware constraints, achieving 2.8% mAP improvement and 10x speed over sliding-window baselines on STAR, with code release.
AI.motivation: Detecting tiny objects in ultra-high-resolution (UHR) remote sensing imagery is severely challenged by memory and computation when retaining full resolution; naïve downsampling or patch cropping sacrifices context or object visibility.
AI.method: Introduce a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder that adaptively allocates compute to informative high-resolution regions to maximize object coverage with minimal spatial redundancy. Propose a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder that fuses macroscopic scene-level information with microscopic object details to resolve semantic ambiguities and prevent scene fragmentation. Implemented as an end-to-end transformer-based detector (UHR-DETR) and evaluated under constrained hardware (single 24GB RTX 3090) on UHR datasets STAR and SODA-A.
AI.result: Empirical results show a 2.8% mAP gain and a 10x inference speedup over standard sliding-window baselines on the STAR dataset; demonstrated on STAR and SODA-A datasets under strict hardware constraints.
AI.conclusion: UHR-DETR provides an efficient, accurate solution for UHR object detection by combining a coverage-maximizing sparse encoding strategy with a global-local decoding scheme, enabling full-scene detection within practical compute budgets; code and models to be released publicly.

title: Probabilistic Verification of Neural Networks via Efficient Probabilistic Hull Generation
summary: The problem of probabilistic verification of a neural network investigates the probability of satisfying the safe constraints in the output space when the input is given by a probability distribution. It is significant to answer this problem when the input is affected by disturbances often modeled by probabilistic variables. In the paper, we propose a novel neural network probabilistic verification framework which computes a guaranteed range for the safe probability by efficiently finding safe and unsafe probabilistic hulls. Our approach consists of three main innovations: (1) a state space subdivision strategy using regression trees to produce probabilistic hulls, (2) a boundary-aware sampling method which identifies the safety boundary in the input space using samples that are later used for building regression trees, and (3) iterative refinement with probabilistic prioritization for computing a guaranteed range for the safe probability. The accuracy and efficiency of our approach are evaluated on various benchmarks including ACAS Xu and a rocket lander controller. The result shows an obvious advantage over the state of the art.

AI.tldr: A neural network probabilistic verification framework that computes guaranteed safe-probability bounds via regression-tree-based probabilistic hulls, boundary-aware sampling, and probabilistic-prioritized iterative refinement; demonstrated on ACAS Xu and rocket lander controllers with apparent state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Certify neural network safety under input disturbances modeled as probability distributions, by providing guaranteed probability bounds for satisfying safety constraints in the output space.
AI.method: (1) Use regression-tree driven state-space subdivision to build probabilistic hulls that bound the safe and unsafe regions; (2) Implement boundary-aware sampling to locate safety boundaries and generate informative samples for regression trees; (3) Apply iterative refinement with probabilistic prioritization to tighten bounds and guarantee the range of the safe probability.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on standard benchmarks (ACAS Xu, rocket lander controller) showing clear advantages over existing methods in accuracy and efficiency, with guaranteed bounds on the safe-probability.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable approach to probabilistic neural network verification that delivers guaranteed safety-probability estimates and outperforms prior techniques on benchmark tasks.

title: Understanding and Mitigating Spurious Signal Amplification in Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for Math Reasoning
summary: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) always adapts models at inference time via pseudo-labeling, leaving it vulnerable to spurious optimization signals from label noise. Through an empirical study, we observe that responses with medium consistency form an ambiguity region and constitute the primary source of reward noise. Crucially, we find that such spurious signals can be even amplified through group-relative advantage estimation. Motivated by these findings, we propose a unified framework, Debiased and Denoised test-time Reinforcement Learning (DDRL), to mitigate spurious signals. Concretely, DDRL first applies a frequency-based sampling strategy to exclude ambiguous samples while maintaining a balanced set of positive and negative examples. It then adopts a debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages, removing the bias introduced by group-relative policy optimization. Finally, DDRL incorporates a consensus-based off-policy refinement stage, which leverages the rejection-sampled dataset to enable efficient and stable model updates. Experiments on three large language models across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that DDRL consistently outperforms existing TTRL baselines. The code will soon be released at https://github.com/yuyongcan/DDRL.

AI.tldr: DDRL: a unified approach to debias and denoise test-time RL by frequency-based sampling, fixed-advantage debiasing, and consensus off-policy refinement, yielding improved performance on LLM math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: TTRL is vulnerable to reward-noise from label noise; medium-consistency responses create an ambiguity region; group-relative advantage estimation amplifies spurious signals. A robust framework is needed to mitigate these issues to improve stability and performance.
AI.method: 1) Frequency-based sampling to discard ambiguous samples while preserving balanced positives/negatives; 2) Debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages to remove bias from group-relative policy optimization; 3) Consensus-based off-policy refinement using rejection-sampled data for stable, efficient updates.
AI.result: Empirical results on three LLMs across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks show DDRL consistently outperforms existing TTRL baselines; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: DDRL effectively mitigates spurious reward signals in TTRL via denoising and debiasing, improving robustness and performance; the approach has potential applicability to broader TTRL settings.

title: 2L-LSH: A Locality-Sensitive Hash Function-Based Method For Rapid Point Cloud Indexing
comment: 13 pages, 13 figures. Published in The Computer Journal
summary: The development of 3D scanning technology has enabled the acquisition of massive point cloud models with diverse structures and large scales, thereby presenting significant challenges in point cloud processing. Fast neighboring points search is one of the most common problems, which is frequently used in model reconstruction, classification, retrieval and feature visualization. Hash function is well known for its high-speed and accurate performance in searching high-dimensional data, which is also the core of the proposed 2L-LSH. Specifically, the 2L-LSH algorithm adopts a two-step hash function strategy, in which the popular step divides the bounding box of the point cloud model and the second step constructs a generalized table-based data structure. The proposed 2L-LSH offers a highly efficient and accurate solution for fast neighboring points search in large-scale 3D point cloud models, making it a promising technique for various applications in the field. The proposed algorithm is compared with the well-known methods including Kd-tree and Octree; the obtained results demonstrated that the proposed method outperforms Kd-tree and Octree in terms of speed, i.e. the time consumption of kNN search can be 51.111% and 94.159% lower than Kd-tree and Octree, respectively. And the RN search time can be 54.519% and 41.840% lower than Kd-tree and Octree, respectively.

AI.tldr: 2L-LSH provides a fast two-step hash-based method for kNN and radius neighbor searches in large-scale 3D point clouds, outperforming Kd-tree and Octree in speed.
AI.motivation: Growing, diverse, large-scale 3D point clouds require efficient neighbor Search; traditional methods like Kd-tree and Octree incur high latency for large datasets.
AI.method: A two-step hash strategy: (1) partition the point cloud bounding box, (2) build a generalized table-based data structure to support fast neighbor queries.
AI.result: Empirical results show substantial speedups: kNN time reduced by 51.1% vs Kd-tree and 94.2% vs Octree; radius neighbor search reduced by 54.5% vs Kd-tree and 41.8% vs Octree.
AI.conclusion: 2L-LSH offers an efficient and accurate solution for fast neighbor searches in large-scale 3D point clouds, with broad applicability to reconstruction, classification, retrieval, and visualization.

title: Separable Expert Architecture: Toward Privacy-Preserving LLM Personalization via Composable Adapters and Deletable User Proxies
summary: Current model training approaches incorporate user information directly into shared weights, making individual data removal computationally infeasible without retraining. This paper presents a three-layer architecture that decouples personal data from shared weights by combining a static base model, composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that shape behavior without imparting user data, and per-user proxy artefacts whose deletion constitutes deterministic unlearning. Evaluation on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B confirms per-user differentiation in which personal data influences outputs while remaining isolated, verified by a return to baseline after proxy removal (KL divergence of approximately 0.21 nats, 82-89% verification pass rate) and near-zero cross-user contamination. Because user-specific information never enters shared weights, the architecture mitigates model inversion, membership inference, and training-data extraction against shared model components by construction. The approach converts machine unlearning from an intractable weight-editing problem into a deterministic deletion operation that preserves personalization alongside privacy-enhancing guarantees and is compatible with differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) for privacy-preserving shared model improvement.

AI.tldr: A 3-layer architecture decouples personal data from shared model weights using a static base model, composable domain-expert LoRA adapters, and per-user proxy artefacts, enabling deterministic per-user unlearning via artefact deletion while preserving personalization; validated on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B with low cross-user contamination and baseline restoration after artefact removal.
AI.motivation: To overcome the infeasibility of removing user data from models trained with shared weights, by separating user data from shared parameters and providing a deterministic deletion pathway (unlearning) that preserves personalization and supports privacy guarantees.
AI.method: Proposes a three-layer architecture: a static base model (shared core), composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that shape behavior without embedding user data, and per-user proxy artefacts whose deletion deterministically unlearns user data. Evaluation on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B assesses per-user differentiation, baseline restoration after proxy removal, and privacy implications, including compatibility with DP-SGD.
AI.result: The approach yields detectable per-user influence on outputs while keeping that influence isolated to the per-user artefacts; removing the per-user proxies returns outputs to baseline (KL divergence ~0.21 nats). Verification pass rate is 82-89%, with near-zero cross-user contamination; shared weights remain free of user data, reducing risks of model inversion, membership inference, and data extraction. The method converts unlearning into deterministic deletion and is compatible with DP-SGD for privacy-preserving shared improvements.
AI.conclusion: Unlearning becomes a deterministic deletion of per-user artefacts rather than weight editing, preserving personalization while enhancing privacy. The approach offers robust privacy guarantees, mitigates common inference attacks against shared components, and integrates with DP-SGD for private continual improvement.

title: Sub-Token Routing in LoRA for Adaptation and Query-Aware KV Compression
summary: Sub-token routing offers a finer control axis for transformer efficiency than the coarse units used in most prior work, such as tokens, pages, heads, or layers. In this paper, we study routing within a token representation itself in LoRA-adapted transformers. The motivation is that a relevant token need not be internally uniform: under a retention budget, preserved value groups are distributed unevenly both across tokens and within tokens, which suggests that KV compression need not be an all-or-nothing decision at token level. We study this fine-grained routing mechanism in two settings. For compression-aware language modeling, we introduce a query-independent design that combines routed subspace LoRA with value-group routing on the KV path. For downstream-task-preserving KV compression, we introduce a query-aware design in which a predictor-based selector allocates a global retention budget over context-token/value-group pairs using query-conditioned relevance. Experiments show that the query-independent design improves the quality-compression tradeoff for language modeling, while the query-aware design preserves downstream behavior under reduced KV budgets. We further examine the relation between token-level and sub-token-level query-aware routing, and show that they form complementary compression axes: token-level methods determine which tokens survive globally, while sub-token routing determines how the surviving tokens are compressed internally.

AI.tldr: Fine-grained sub-token routing in LoRA-adapted transformers enables more efficient KV compression, improving quality under compression and preserving downstream behavior, with token- and sub-token-level routing acting as complementary axes.
AI.motivation: To address inefficient KW/attention compression in transformers by exploiting intra-token heterogeneity. Under budget constraints, relevant information is unevenly distributed both across tokens and within them, so sub-token routing can yield finer-grained control than coarse units (tokens, pages, heads, layers).
AI.method: Investigates routing within token representations in LoRA-adapted transformers under two designs: (1) query-independent design combining routed subspace LoRA with value-group routing on the KV path for compression-aware language modeling; (2) query-aware design with a predictor-based selector that allocates a global retention budget over context-token/value-group pairs using query-conditioned relevance for downstream-task-preserving KV compression.
AI.result: Experiments show that the query-independent design improves the quality–compression tradeoff for language modeling, while the query-aware design preserves downstream behavior under reduced KV budgets. Analysis indicates token-level routing determines which tokens survive globally, whereas sub-token routing determines how surviving tokens are compressed internally; the two axes are complementary.
AI.conclusion: Sub-token routing provides a finer control axis for transformer efficiency beyond coarse units, enabling more nuanced KV compression. Token-level routing selects surviving tokens globally; sub-token routing handles internal compression, together offering improved efficiency without sacrificing performance.

title: VARestorer: One-Step VAR Distillation for Real-World Image Super-Resolution
comment: Accepted in ICLR 2026. Code is available at https://github.com/EternalEvan/VARestorer
summary: Recent advancements in visual autoregressive models (VAR) have demonstrated their effectiveness in image generation, highlighting their potential for real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR). However, adapting VAR for ISR presents critical challenges. The next-scale prediction mechanism, constrained by causal attention, fails to fully exploit global low-quality (LQ) context, resulting in blurry and inconsistent high-quality (HQ) outputs. Additionally, error accumulation in the iterative prediction severely degrades coherence in ISR task. To address these issues, we propose VARestorer, a simple yet effective distillation framework that transforms a pre-trained text-to-image VAR model into a one-step ISR model. By leveraging distribution matching, our method eliminates the need for iterative refinement, significantly reducing error propagation and inference time. Furthermore, we introduce pyramid image conditioning with cross-scale attention, which enables bidirectional scale-wise interactions and fully utilizes the input image information while adapting to the autoregressive mechanism. This prevents later LQ tokens from being overlooked in the transformer. By fine-tuning only 1.2\% of the model parameters through parameter-efficient adapters, our method maintains the expressive power of the original VAR model while significantly enhancing efficiency. Extensive experiments show that VARestorer achieves state-of-the-art performance with 72.32 MUSIQ and 0.7669 CLIPIQA on DIV2K dataset, while accelerating inference by 10 times compared to conventional VAR inference.

AI.tldr: A distillation framework (VARestorer) converts a pre-trained visual autoregressive model into a one-step image super-resolution model via distribution matching, using pyramid conditioning with cross-scale attention and adapters to achieve state-of-the-art performance with 10x faster inference and minimal parameter updates.
AI.motivation: VARs suffer from limited global context due to causal next-scale prediction and error accumulation during iterative refinement, hindering ISR quality and efficiency.
AI.method: Distill a pre-trained text-to-image VAR into a one-step ISR model via distribution matching; introduce pyramid image conditioning with cross-scale attention for bidirectional scale interactions; fine-tune only 1.2% of parameters using adapters.
AI.result: On DIV2K, VARestorer achieves 72.32 MUSIQ and 0.7669 CLIPIQA, with a 10x speedup over conventional VAR inference.
AI.conclusion: The approach preserves the expressive power of the original VAR while dramatically improving efficiency and ISR quality; it highlights effective one-shot distillation with cross-scale conditioning for VAR-based ISR.

title: CoFEE: Reasoning Control for LLM-Based Feature Discovery
summary: Feature discovery from complex unstructured data is fundamentally a reasoning problem: it requires identifying abstractions that are predictive of a target outcome while avoiding leakage, proxies, and post-outcome signals. With the introduction of ever-improving Large Language Models (LLMs), our method provides a structured method for addressing this challenge. LLMs are well suited for this task by being able to process large amounts of information, but unconstrained feature generation can lead to weak features. In this work, we study reasoning control in LLMs by inducing cognitive behaviors for improving feature discovery. We introduce CoFEE (Cognitive Feature Engineering Engine), a reasoning control framework that enforces cognitive behaviors in how the LLM reasons during feature discovery. From a machine learning perspective, these cognitive behaviors act as structured inductive biases over the space of candidate features generated by the model. These behaviors have been exploited with success in ML models, and include backward chaining from outcomes, subgoal decomposition, verification against observability and leakage criteria, and explicit backtracking of rejected reasoning paths. In a controlled comparison, we show that enforcing cognitive behaviors yields features with higher empirical predictability than those under unconstrained vanilla LLM prompts. CoFEE achieves an average Success Rate Score that is 15.2% higher than the vanilla approach, while generating 29% fewer features and reducing costs by 53.3%. Using held-out feature evaluation, we assess whether cognitively induced features generalize beyond the data used for discovery. Our results indicate that, in our evaluated setting, reasoning control is associated with improvements in quality and efficiency of LLM-based feature discovery.

AI.tldr: CoFEE enforces cognitive reasoning biases in LLM-driven feature discovery to improve feature quality and efficiency, showing higher predictive power with fewer features and lower costs than vanilla prompts.
AI.motivation: Feature discovery from complex unstructured data requires predictive abstractions while avoiding leakage and post-outcome signals. LLMs offer processing power but unconstrained prompts create weak or leaked features. Imposing cognitive reasoning controls can act as structured inductive biases.
AI.method: Introduce CoFEE (Cognitive Feature Engineering Engine) that enforces cognitive behaviors in LLM reasoning during feature discovery. Implement behaviors such as backward chaining from outcomes, subgoal decomposition, observability/leakage verification, and explicit backtracking of rejected paths. Evaluate via controlled comparison against vanilla prompts, measuring empirical predictability, feature count, and cost; use held-out evaluation to test generalization.
AI.result: Features generated under cognitive constraints show higher empirical predictability. CoFEE yields an average 15.2% higher Success Rate Score than vanilla prompts, produces 29% fewer features, and reduces costs by 53.3%.
