Held-out evaluation suggests cognitive behavior enforcement improves generalization and overall quality/efficiency of LLM-based feature discovery.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning control through cognitively motivated biases improves LLM-based feature discovery, delivering better feature quality and efficiency and generalizable improvements in held-out settings.

title: Decoupled Travel Planning with Behavior Forest
summary: Behavior sequences, composed of executable steps, serve as the operational foundation for multi-constraint planning problems such as travel planning. In such tasks, each planning step is not only constrained locally but also influenced by global constraints spanning multiple subtasks, leading to a tightly coupled and complex decision process. Existing travel planning methods typically rely on a single decision space that entangles all subtasks and constraints, failing to distinguish between locally acting constraints within a subtask and global constraints that span multiple subtasks. Consequently, the model is forced to jointly reason over local and global constraints at each decision step, increasing the reasoning burden and reducing planning efficiency. To address this problem, we propose the Behavior Forest method. Specifically, our approach structures the decision-making process into a forest of parallel behavior trees, where each behavior tree is responsible for a subtask. A global coordination mechanism is introduced to orchestrate the interactions among these trees, enabling modular and coherent travel planning. Within this framework, large language models are embedded as decision engines within behavior tree nodes, performing localized reasoning conditioned on task-specific constraints to generate candidate subplans and adapt decisions based on coordination feedback. The behavior trees, in turn, provide an explicit control structure that guides LLM generation. This design decouples complex tasks and constraints into manageable subspaces, enabling task-specific reasoning and reducing the cognitive load of LLM. Experimental results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 6.67% on the TravelPlanner and by 11.82% on the ChinaTravel benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness in increasing LLM performance for complex multi-constraint travel planning.

AI.tldr: A modular travel planning framework, Behavior Forest, uses parallel behavior trees to handle subtasks with a global coordination mechanism. LLMs serve as decision engines within tree nodes to generate subplans; this decouples local/global constraints, reducing cognitive load and improving planning efficiency. Wins 6.67% on TravelPlanner and 11.82% on ChinaTravel.
AI.motivation: Current travel planning methods entangle local (subtask) and global (cross-subtask) constraints in a single decision space, increasing reasoning burden and reducing efficiency. A modular, tree-structured approach with coordinated subplans can improve scalability and performance.
AI.method: Introduce Behavior Forest: a forest of parallel behavior trees, each responsible for a subtask. A global coordination mechanism orchestrates interactions among trees. LLMs are embedded as decision engines within behavior tree nodes to produce candidate subplans conditioned on task constraints, with trees guiding generation and providing coordination feedback.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows Performance gains over state-of-the-art: +6.67% on TravelPlanner benchmark and +11.82% on ChinaTravel benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling complex, constraint-rich planning into subspaces via behavior trees with a global coordinator reduces cognitive load for LLMs and enhances multi-constraint travel planning performance.

title: Instance-level Visual Active Tracking with Occlusion-Aware Planning
summary: Visual Active Tracking (VAT) aims to control cameras to follow a target in 3D space, which is critical for applications like drone navigation and security surveillance. However, it faces two key bottlenecks in real-world deployment: confusion from visually similar distractors caused by insufficient instance-level discrimination and severe failure under occlusions due to the absence of active planning. To address these, we propose OA-VAT, a unified pipeline with three complementary modules. First, a training-free Instance-Aware Offline Prototype Initialization aggregates multi-view augmented features via DINOv3 to construct discriminative instance prototypes, mitigating distractor confusion. Second, an Online Prototype Enhancement Tracker enhances prototypes online and integrates a confidence-aware Kalman filter for stable tracking under appearance and motion changes. Third, an Occlusion-Aware Trajectory Planner, trained on our new Planning-20k dataset, uses conditional diffusion to generate obstacle-avoiding paths for occlusion recovery. Experiments demonstrate OA-VAT achieves 0.93 average SR on UnrealCV (+2.2% vs. SOTA TrackVLA), 90.8% average CAR on real-world datasets (+12.1% vs. SOTA GC-VAT), and 81.6% TSR on a DJI Tello drone. Running at 35 FPS on an RTX 3090, it delivers robust, real-time performance for practical deployment.

AI.tldr: OA-VAT is a unified three-module pipeline for visual active tracking that integrates training-free instance prototypes, online prototype enhancement with a confidence-aware Kalman filter, and an occlusion-aware trajectory planner using conditional diffusion; it achieves strong real-time performance on benchmarks and real drones.
AI.motivation: To overcome distractor confusion and occlusion-induced failures in Visual Active Tracking (VAT) by combining instance-level discrimination, online adaptation, and planning-based occlusion recovery.
AI.method: Module 1: training-free Instance-Aware Offline Prototype Initialization aggregates multi-view augmented features via DINOv3 to form discriminative instance prototypes. Module 2: Online Prototype Enhancement Tracker updates prototypes online and uses a confidence-aware Kalman filter for robust tracking under appearance and motion changes. Module 3: Occlusion-Aware Trajectory Planner, trained on Planning-20k dataset, uses conditional diffusion to generate obstacle-avoiding paths for occlusion recovery. Datasets include UnrealCV, real-world datasets, and DJI Tello; evaluations on SR, CAR, TSR with real-time operation at 35 FPS on an RTX 3090.
AI.result: 0.93 average SR on UnrealCV (+2.2% vs. SOTA TrackVLA), 90.8% average CAR on real-world datasets (+12.1% vs. SOTA GC-VAT), and 81.6% TSR on a DJI Tello drone; runtime ~35 FPS on RTX 3090, demonstrating robustness and practical real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: OA-VAT delivers a robust, real-time VAT solution by integrating discriminative instance prototypes, online adaptation, and occlusion-aware planning, enabling effective deployment in drone navigation and security contexts.

title: To See the Unseen: on the Generalization Ability of Transformers in Symbolic Reasoning
summary: We investigate the ability of decoder-only transformer models to perform abstract symbolic reasoning; specifically solving propositional logic reasoning problems given in-context. Previous work demonstrated that models fail to generalize to problems involving variable names that were not observed during training, and it was shown that one reason behind this is the difficulty of copying (or generating) unseen tokens. We show both theoretically and empirically that a particular representational collapse also has a crucial role: the unembeddings (last-layer weights) of unseen tokens collapse to nearly the same vector during training. The collapse makes distinguishing multiple unseen variables difficult for the model (especially when the embedding and unembedding parameters are shared), and provides a mechanistic explanation for the effectiveness of existing heuristic interventions like "active forgetting", which periodically reset the token (un)embeddings. Based on these observations, we devise a combination of techniques, involving a small architecture change facilitating copying, data diversity, and freezing or resetting (un)embeddings, that achieves generalization to unseen tokens. We support our claims with extensive controlled experiments on propositional logic reasoning problems. Beyond synthetic experiments, we also observe evidence of (un)embedding collapse in the open-weight models in the Gemma 3 family, which includes 99 unused tokens reserved for downstream use. Empirically we find that the correlated embeddings of these tokens are a poor initialization for finetuning applications.

AI.tldr: Decoder-only transformers struggle to generalize to unseen tokens in propositional logic due to unembedding collapse; a small architecture tweak plus data diversity and unembedding management enables generalization to unseen tokens; validated by controlled experiments and observed in Gemma 3 open weights.
AI.motivation: Address why abstract symbolic reasoning with in-context learning fails for unseen variable names; connect failure to token copying challenges and a representational collapse of unseen-token unembeddings.
AI.method: Theoretical and empirical analysis of last-layer (un)embeddings, identification of collapse; propose a composite intervention: small architecture change to facilitate copying, increased data diversity, and freezing or resetting (un)embeddings; validate on propositional logic reasoning tasks; extend investigation to open-weight models.
AI.result: Demonstrates generalization to unseen tokens under the proposed regime; supports the unembedding-collapse mechanism as a contributor to failure and shows the interventions' effectiveness; additional evidence of unembedding collapse in Gemma 3 open-weight models with 99 reserved tokens; correlated embeddings among unused tokens are poor initialization for fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Unembedding collapse of unseen tokens is a key mechanism limiting generalization in decoder-only transformers for symbolic reasoning. Targeted interventions—copy-facilitating architecture, diverse data, and regimens for unembedding management—can enable robust generalization to unseen tokens, with broader implications for open-weight models.

title: mcdok at SemEval-2026 Task 13: Finetuning LLMs for Detection of Machine-Generated Code
summary: Multi-domain detection of the machine-generated code snippets in various programming languages is a challenging task. SemEval-2026 Task~13 copes with this challenge in various angles, as a binary detection problem as well as attribution of the source. Specifically, its subtasks also cover generator LLM family detection, as well as a hybrid code co-generated by humans and machines, or adversarially modified codes hiding its origin. Our submitted systems adjusted the existing mdok approach (focused on machine-generated text detection) to these specific kinds of problems by exploring various base models, more suitable for code understanding. The results indicate that the submitted systems are competitive in all three subtasks. However, the margins from the top-performing systems are significant, and thus further improvements are possible.

AI.tldr: Adapting the mdok framework to multi-domain detection of machine-generated code across languages yields competitive results on SemEval-2026 Task 13's binary detection and attribution subtasks, but top margins indicate room for significant improvement.
AI.motivation: Detect the prevalence and origin of machine-generated code across languages, including source-model attribution and handling co-generated or adversarially modified code.
AI.method: Extend the existing mdok approach (originally for machine-generated text) to code, exploring various base models suitable for code understanding and applying them to all Subtasks of SemEval-2026 Task 13.
AI.result: Submitted systems are competitive across binary detection, generator-family attribution, and co-generated/adversarial code subproblems, but margins to top-performing systems are sizable.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows promise for cross-domain machine-generated code detection; further gains are achievable through improved code representations, model ensembles, and robustness to adversarial modifications.

title: Do MLLMs Understand Pointing? Benchmarking and Enhancing Referential Reasoning in Egocentric Vision
summary: Egocentric AI agents, such as smart glasses, rely on pointing gestures to resolve referential ambiguities in natural language commands. However, despite advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), current systems often fail to precisely ground the spatial semantics of pointing. Instead, they rely on spurious correlations with visual proximity or object saliency, a phenomenon we term "Referential Hallucination." To address this gap, we introduce EgoPoint-Bench, a comprehensive question-answering benchmark designed to evaluate and enhance multimodal pointing reasoning in egocentric views. Comprising over 11k high-fidelity simulated and real-world samples, the benchmark spans five evaluation dimensions and three levels of referential complexity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that while state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source models struggle with egocentric pointing, models fine-tuned on our synthetic data achieve significant performance gains and robust sim-to-real generalization. This work highlights the importance of spatially aware supervision and offers a scalable path toward precise egocentric AI assistants. Project page: https://guyyyug.github.io/EgoPoint-Bench/

AI.tldr: EgoPoint-Bench is a new 11k-sample benchmark to evaluate and improve multimodal pointing reasoning in egocentric views; fine-tuning on synthetic data yields significant gains and robust sim-to-real generalization, addressing referential hallucination in grounding commands.
AI.motivation: Egocentric AI like smart glasses relies on pointing gestures to resolve referential ambiguities, but current MLLMs ground spatial semantics poorly, leading to referential hallucination where proximity or saliency rather than true pointing guides grounding.
AI.method: Introduce EgoPoint-Bench, a comprehensive QA benchmark with 11k high-fidelity simulated and real-world samples, covering five evaluation dimensions and three levels of referential complexity; evaluate state-of-the-art models and fine-tune on synthetic data to assess gains and sim-to-real generalization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art proprietary and open-source models struggle with egocentric pointing; models fine-tuned on synthetic data achieve significant performance gains and robust sim-to-real generalization across the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Spatially aware supervision is crucial for precise egocentric grounding; EgoPoint-Bench provides a scalable path toward improved egocentric AI assistants and a framework for evaluating pointing-grounding capabilities.

title: GS-Quant: Granular Semantic and Generative Structural Quantization for Knowledge Graph Completion
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown immense potential in Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC), yet bridging the modality gap between continuous graph embeddings and discrete LLM tokens remains a critical challenge. While recent quantization-based approaches attempt to align these modalities, they typically treat quantization as flat numerical compression, resulting in semantically entangled codes that fail to mirror the hierarchical nature of human reasoning. In this paper, we propose GS-Quant, a novel framework that generates semantically coherent and structurally stratified discrete codes for KG entities. Unlike prior methods, GS-Quant is grounded in the insight that entity representations should follow a linguistic coarse-to-fine logic. We introduce a Granular Semantic Enhancement module that injects hierarchical knowledge into the codebook, ensuring that earlier codes capture global semantic categories while later codes refine specific attributes. Furthermore, a Generative Structural Reconstruction module imposes causal dependencies on the code sequence, transforming independent discrete units into structured semantic descriptors. By expanding the LLM vocabulary with these learned codes, we enable the model to reason over graph structures isomorphically to natural language generation. Experimental results demonstrate that GS-Quant significantly outperforms existing text-based and embedding-based baselines. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/mikumifa/GS-Quant.

AI.tldr: GS-Quant introduces hierarchically structured, semantically coherent discrete codes for KG entities to bridge the modality gap with LLMs, enabling coarse-to-fine reasoning and causal dependencies; it outperforms text- and embedding-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses the modality gap between continuous graph embeddings and discrete LLM tokens in Knowledge Graph Completion. Existing quantization approaches treat codes as flat numerical compression, leading to semantically entangled, non-hierarchical representations that fail to mirror human hierarchical reasoning.
AI.method: Proposes Granular Semantic Enhancement to inject hierarchical knowledge into the codebook, with earlier codes capturing global semantic categories and later codes refining attributes. Proposes Generative Structural Reconstruction to impose causal dependencies across the code sequence, turning independent discrete units into structured semantic descriptors. Expands the LLM vocabulary with learned codes to enable isomorphic reasoning to natural language generation over graphs.
AI.result: Experimental results show GS-Quant significantly outperforms existing text-based and embedding-based baselines on KG Completion tasks. Code is publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: GS-Quant demonstrates that semantically coherent, stratified discrete codes can enable structured, coarse-to-fine reasoning over KG representations within LLMs, improving KGC performance and bridging modality gaps; it highlights a viable path for isomorphic graph-to-language reasoning.

title: Channel-Free Human Activity Recognition via Inductive-Bias-Aware Fusion Design for Heterogeneous IoT Sensor Environments
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables, Preprint. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Human activity recognition (HAR) in Internet of Things (IoT) environments must cope with heterogeneous sensor settings that vary across datasets, devices, body locations, sensing modalities, and channel compositions. This heterogeneity makes conventional channel-fixed models difficult to reuse across sensing environments because their input representations are tightly coupled to predefined channel structures. To address this problem, we investigate strict channel-free HAR, in which a single shared model performs inference without assuming a fixed number, order, or semantic arrangement of input channels, and without relying on sensor-specific input layers or dataset-specific channel templates. We argue that fusion design is the central issue in this setting. Accordingly, we propose a channel-free HAR framework that combines channel-wise encoding with a shared encoder, metadata-conditioned late fusion via conditional batch normalization, and joint optimization of channel-level and fused predictions through a combination loss. The proposed model processes each channel independently to handle varying channel configurations, while sensor metadata such as body location, modality, and axis help recover structural information that channel-independent processing alone cannot retain. In addition, the joint loss encourages both the discriminability of individual channels and the consistency of the final fused prediction. Experiments on PAMAP2, together with robustness analysis on six HAR datasets, ablation studies, sensitivity analysis, efficiency evaluation, and cross-dataset transfer learning, demonstrate three main findings...

AI.tldr: A channel-free HAR framework combining per-channel encoding with a shared encoder, metadata-conditioned late fusion via conditional batch normalization, and a joint loss to jointly optimize channel-level and fused predictions; demonstrated across PAMAP2 and six HAR datasets with cross-dataset transfer and robustness analyses.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous IoT HAR settings make fixed-channel models nontransferable; need a single model that handles varying channel configurations without channel templates.
AI.method: Process each channel independently with channel-wise encoders; share a global encoder; use metadata (body location, modality, axis) to condition fusion through conditional batch normalization; jointly optimize channel-level and fused predictions via a combination loss.
AI.result: Empirical findings on PAMAP2 and six HAR datasets show improved robustness and transferability across channels and datasets; ablations and analyses confirm effectiveness of channel-wise encoding, metadata conditioning, and joint optimization; enhances cross-dataset transfer learning and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Strict channel-free HAR is feasible and beneficial; the proposed framework enables reusability across heterogeneous sensors without channel templates or sensor-specific input layers, while maintaining discriminability and fusion consistency.

title: ID-Eraser: Proactive Defense Against Face Swapping via Identity Perturbation
summary: Deepfake technologies have rapidly advanced with modern generative AI, and face swapping in particular poses serious threats to privacy and digital security. Existing proactive defenses mostly rely on pixel-level perturbations, which are ineffective against contemporary swapping models that extract robust high-level identity embeddings. We propose ID-Eraser, a feature-space proactive defense that removes identifiable facial information to prevent malicious face swapping. By injecting learnable perturbations into identity embeddings and reconstructing natural-looking protection images through a Face Revive Generator (FRG), ID-Eraser produces visually realistic results for humans while rendering the protected identities unusable for Deepfake models. Experiments show that ID-Eraser substantially disrupts identity recognition across diverse face recognition and swapping systems under strict black-box settings, achieving the lowest Top-1 accuracy (0.30) with the best FID (1.64) and LPIPS (0.020). Compared with swaps generated from clean inputs, the identity similarity of protected swaps drops sharply to an average of 0.504 across five representative face swapping models. ID-Eraser further demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization, robustness to common distortions, and practical effectiveness on commercial APIs, reducing Tencent API similarity from 0.76 to 0.36.

AI.tldr: A feature-space proactive defense (ID-Eraser) perturbs identity embeddings and uses a Face Revive Generator to produce photorealistic protection images, significantly reducing identity recognition in deepfake face swapping while preserving visual realism.
AI.motivation: Pixel-level defenses are insufficient against swapping models that rely on high-level identity embeddings; a robust feature-space defense is needed to protect privacy and security against deepfake face swapping.
AI.method: Inject learnable perturbations into identity embeddings and reconstruct protection images via a Face Revive Generator (FRG), yielding protection that remains visually realistic to humans while blocking identity leakage in face-swapping attacks.
AI.result: Under strict black-box settings, achieves the lowest Top-1 recognition accuracy (0.30), best FID (1.64) and LPIPS (0.020); protected swaps have an average identity similarity of ~0.504 across five swapping models; demonstrates cross-dataset generalization, robustness to distortions, and effectiveness on commercial APIs (Tencent) reducing similarity from 0.76 to 0.36.
AI.conclusion: ID-Eraser effectively disrupts identity recognition for face-swapping systems while preserving visual realism, offering practical, generalizable protection with strong empirical support.

title: Enabling and Inhibitory Pathways of University Students' Willingness to Disclose AI Use: A Cognition-Affect-Conation Perspective
summary: The increasing integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has raised important questions regarding students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work. This study investigates the psychological mechanisms underlying university students' willingness to disclose AI use by applying the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. A sequential explanatory mixed-methods design was employed. In the quantitative phase, survey data were collected from 546 university students and analysed using structural equation modelling to examine the relationships among cognitive perceptions, affective responses, and disclosure intention. In the qualitative phase, semi-structured interviews with 22 students were conducted to further interpret the quantitative findings. The results indicate that psychological safety significantly increases students' willingness to disclose AI use and is positively shaped by perceived fairness, perceived teacher support, and perceived organisational support. Conversely, evaluation apprehension reduces disclosure intention and psychological safety, and is strengthened by perceived stigma, perceived uncertainty, and privacy concern. Qualitative findings further reveal that institutional clarity and supportive instructional practices encourage openness, whereas policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation often lead students to adopt cautious or strategic disclosure practices. Overall, the study highlights the dual role of enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms in shaping AI-use disclosure and underscores the importance of supportive institutional environments and clear guidance for promoting responsible AI transparency in higher education.

AI.tldr: A sequential explanatory mixed-methods study using the CAC framework to examine university students' willingness to disclose AI use; psychological safety and fairness perceptions promote disclosure, while evaluation apprehension hinders it; institutional clarity and supportive practices foster openness; policy ambiguity increases strategic disclosure.
AI.motivation: Rising AI use in higher education raises privacy/transparency concerns; understanding cognitive, affective, and conative drivers of disclosure to inform policies and practices.
AI.method: Sequential explanatory mixed-methods design: quantitative phase with 546 students and SEM to model cognitive-perceptual drivers of disclosure; qualitative phase with 22 students via semi-structured interviews to interpret quantitative findings.
AI.result: Psychological safety increases disclosure willingness and is enhanced by perceived fairness, teacher support, and organizational support; evaluation apprehension decreases disclosure and safety, driven by stigma, uncertainty, and privacy concerns; qualitative data show institutional clarity and supportive practices promote openness, whereas policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation lead to cautious disclosure.
AI.conclusion: Dual role of enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms shapes AI-use disclosure; emphasis on supportive institutional environments and clear guidance to promote responsible AI transparency in higher education.

title: Relocation of compact sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ by diffeomorphisms and linear separability of datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$
summary:
Relocation of compact sets in an $n$-dimensional manifold by self-diffeomorphism is of its own interest as well as significant potential applications to data classification in data science. This paper presents a theory for relocating a finite number of compact sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to be relocated to arbitrary target domains in $\mathbb{R}^n$ by diffeomorphisms of $\mathbb{R}^n$. Furthermore, we prove that for any such collection, there exists a differentiable embedding into $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ such that their images become linearly separable.
  As applications of the established theory, we show that a finite number of compact datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be made linearly separable by width-$n$ deep neural networks (DNNs) with Leaky-ReLU, ELU, or SELU activation functions, under a mild condition. In addition, we show that any finite number of mutually disjoint compact datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be made linearly separable in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ by a width-$(n+1)$ DNN.

AI.tldr: A theory that relocates finite collections of compact sets in R^n via diffeomorphisms, plus an embedding into R^{n+1} that yields linear separability. Applications claim that finite datasets can be made separable by width-n DNNs with common activations, and disjoint datasets by width-(n+1) DNNs.
AI.motivation: Bridge differential topology with neural-network expressivity: enable simple linear separability for finite datasets by geometric embedding/relocation.
AI.method: Construct a diffeomorphism-based relocation of compact sets to arbitrary target domains; prove existence of a differentiable embedding into R^{n+1} achieving linear separability of the images; translate these geometric results into neural-network realizations using width-n or width-(n+1) architectures with Leaky-ReLU, ELU, SELU under mild conditions.
AI.result: 1) Finite collections of compact sets in R^n can be relocated to arbitrary targets via ambient diffeomorphisms. 2) There exists a C^1 embedding into R^{n+1} making the images linearly separable. 3) Finite datasets can be made linearly separable by width-n DNNs (with specified activations) under a mild condition; disjoint finite sets can be separated in R^{n+1} by width-(n+1) DNNs.
AI.conclusion: Differential-topology-driven embeddings and diffeomorphisms can yield practical neural-network separability results, linking geometric manipulation of data with network width/activation choices and providing a framework for achieving linear separability of finite datasets.

title: Rethinking Cross-Domain Evaluation for Face Forgery Detection with Semantic Fine-grained Alignment and Mixture-of-Experts
comment: The source code is available at https://github.com/Yuhan-Luo/Semantic-Fine-grained-Alignment-and-Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Nowadays, visual data forgery detection plays an increasingly important role in social and economic security with the rapid development of generative models. Existing face forgery detectors still can't achieve satisfactory performance because of poor generalization ability across datasets. The key factor that led to this phenomenon is the lack of suitable metrics: the commonly used cross-dataset AUC metric fails to reveal an important issue where detection scores may shift significantly across data domains. To explicitly evaluate cross-domain score comparability, we propose \textbf{Cross-AUC}, an evaluation metric that can compute AUC across dataset pairs by contrasting real samples from one dataset with fake samples from another (and vice versa). It is interesting to find that evaluating representative detectors under the Cross-AUC metric reveals substantial performance drops, exposing an overlooked robustness problem. Besides, we also propose the novel framework \textbf{S}emantic \textbf{F}ine-grained \textbf{A}lignment and \textbf{M}ixture-of-Experts (\textbf{SFAM}), consisting of a patch-level image-text alignment module that enhances CLIP's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts, and the facial region mixture-of-experts module, which routes features from different facial regions to specialized experts for region-aware forgery analysis. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments on the public datasets prove that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared with the state-of-the-art methods with various suitable metrics.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cross-AUC for cross-domain score comparability and SFAM detector with patch-level alignment and region-wise experts to improve face forgery detection and cross-dataset generalization.
AI.motivation: Cross-dataset AUC fails to reveal distribution shift in detection scores, masking robustness gaps; existing detectors generalize poorly across datasets.
AI.method: Propose Cross-AUC metric: compute AUC across dataset pairs by swapping real vs fake samples across datasets. Propose SFAM framework: patch-level image-text alignment to enhance CLIP's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts; facial region mixture-of-experts routing regional features to specialized experts for region-aware forgery analysis.
AI.result: Empirical results on public datasets show Cross-AUC reveals performance drops; SFAM achieves superior performance vs state-of-the-art across metrics; robust across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Cross-AUC is a valuable metric for cross-domain evaluation; SFAM provides improved cross-dataset generalization for face forgery detection.

title: Bridging the Training-Deployment Gap: Gated Encoding and Multi-Scale Refinement for Efficient Quantization-Aware Image Enhancement
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at the Mobile AI (MAI) 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Image enhancement models for mobile devices often struggle to balance high output quality with the fast processing speeds required by mobile hardware. While recent deep learning models can enhance low-quality mobile photos into high-quality images, their performance is often degraded when converted to lower-precision formats for actual use on mobile phones. To address this training-deployment mismatch, we propose an efficient image enhancement model designed specifically for mobile deployment. Our approach uses a hierarchical network architecture with gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement to preserve fine-grained visual features. Moreover, we incorporate Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to simulate the effects of low-precision representation during the training process. This allows the network to adapt and prevents the typical drop in quality seen with standard post-training quantization (PTQ). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces high-fidelity visual output while maintaining the low computational overhead needed for practical use on standard mobile devices. The code will be available at https://github.com/GenAI4E/QATIE.git.

AI.tldr: Proposes an efficient mobile image enhancement model with a hierarchical gated encoder and multiscale refinement, augmented by Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to bridge training-deployment gaps between high-quality enhancement and low-precision mobile deployment; claims high fidelity with low compute, code released.
AI.motivation: To overcome the training-deployment mismatch where high-quality enhancement models suffer quality loss when quantized for mobile hardware; standard post-training quantization (PTQ) degrades output quality.
AI.method: A hierarchical network with gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement, trained with Quantization-Aware Training to simulate low-precision representations during training.
AI.result: Demonstrates high-fidelity visual output and low computational overhead on standard mobile devices; QAT mitigates quality loss during quantization; code available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables high-quality mobile image enhancement with efficient run-time requirements, and QAT effectively closes the training-deployment gap for low-precision deployment.

title: Supervised Learning Has a Necessary Geometric Blind Spot: Theory, Consequences, and Minimal Repair
comment: 29 pages. Code: https://github.com/vishalstark512/PMH. Preprint, not peer-reviewed. Affiliation: KU Leuven, Belgium
summary:
We prove that empirical risk minimisation (ERM) imposes a necessary geometric constraint on learned representations: any encoder that minimises supervised loss must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity in directions that are label-correlated in training data but nuisance at test time. This is not a contingent failure of current methods; it is a mathematical consequence of the supervised objective itself. We call this the geometric blind spot of supervised learning (Theorem 1), and show it holds across proper scoring rules, architectures, and dataset sizes.
  This single theorem unifies four lines of prior empirical work that were previously treated separately: non-robust predictive features, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff. In this framing, adversarial vulnerability is one consequence of a broader structural fact about supervised learning geometry.
  We introduce Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a diagnostic that measures the theorem's bounded quantity directly, and show why common alternatives miss the key failure mode. PGD adversarial training reaches Jacobian Frobenius 2.91 yet has the worst clean-input geometry (TDI 1.336), while PMH achieves TDI 0.904. TDI is the only metric that detects this dissociation because it measures isotropic path-length distortion -- the exact quantity Theorem 1 bounds.
  Across seven vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones used by CLIP, DINO, and SAM, the blind spot is measurable and repairable. It is present at foundation-model scale, worsens monotonically across language-model sizes (blind-spot ratio 0.860 to 0.765 to 0.742 from 66M to 340M), and is amplified by task-specific ERM fine-tuning (+54%), while PMH repairs it by 11x with one additional training term whose Gaussian form Proposition 5 proves is the unique perturbation law that uniformly penalises the encoder Jacobian.

AI.tldr: ERM imposes a geometric blind spot in learned representations: encoders must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity along label-correlated directions in training data that are nuisance at test time, a fundamental consequence of the supervised objective. The work introduces the Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) to diagnose this bound, proves a universal Theorem 1, and shows PMH regularization can repair the geometry; the phenomenon persists across architectures, tasks, and scale, including foundation-models.
AI.motivation: Unify and explain disparate empirical observations (non-robust features, texture bias, corruption fragility, robustness-accuracy tradeoffs) under a single geometric framework of supervised learning; provide a diagnostic and a repair mechanism for the geometry of learned representations.
AI.method: Theoretical: prove Theorem 1 bounding encoder Jacobian in directions that are label-correlated in training but nuisance at test time; define Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) as a diagnostic for the bounded quantity. Empirical: compare PGD adversarial training and PMH across seven vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones; evaluate Jacobian Frobenius norm and TDI; test scale effects from 66M to 340M parameters; prove in Proposition 5 that a Gaussian perturbation law uniquely penalises the encoder Jacobian.
AI.result: Theorem 1 holds across proper scoring rules, architectures, and dataset sizes, revealing a consistent geometric blind spot in supervised learning. TDI uniquely detects this dissociation between adversarial robustness and clean-input geometry, whereas other metrics miss it. Adversarial training reduces some losses but exhibits poor TDI; PMH achieves substantially better TDI (≈11x improvement). The phenomenon is measurable and repairable across tasks and scales, persists in foundation-model regimes, worsens with language-model size, and is mitigated by PMH with a Gaussian perturbation as per Proposition 5.
AI.conclusion: Supervised ERM enforces a structural geometric constraint on representations, linking robustness issues to a fundamental property of the objective. Adversarial vulnerability and related phenomena are manifestations of this geometric blind spot. A targeted regularization (PMH with Gaussian perturbation) can repair the geometry, and TDI serves as the essential diagnostic to detect the dissociation between robust and clean geometry, which persists even at large-scale foundation models.

title: Frozen LLMs as Map-Aware Spatio-Temporal Reasoners for Vehicle Trajectory Prediction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities and attracted increasing research attention in the field of autonomous driving (AD). However, safe application of LLMs on AD perception and prediction still requires a thorough understanding of both the dynamic traffic agents and the static road infrastructure. To this end, this study introduces a framework to evaluate the capability of LLMs in understanding the behaviors of dynamic traffic agents and the topology of road networks. The framework leverages frozen LLMs as the reasoning engine, employing a traffic encoder to extract spatial-level scene features from observed trajectories of agents, while a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) encodes the local high-definition (HD) maps. To assess the intrinsic reasoning ability of LLMs, the extracted scene features are then transformed into LLM-compatible tokens via a reprogramming adapter. By residing the prediction burden with the LLMs, a simpler linear decoder is applied to output future trajectories. The framework enables a quantitative analysis of the influence of multi-modal information, especially the impact of map semantics on trajectory prediction accuracy, and allows seamless integration of frozen LLMs with minimal adaptation, thereby demonstrating strong generalizability across diverse LLM architectures and providing a unified platform for model evaluation.

AI.tldr: A framework to evaluate frozen LLMs’ reasoning for autonomous driving by converting trajectory and map data into LLM-compatible tokens, using a lightweight adder to predict future trajectories with a simple decoder, enabling analysis of multi-modal information with strong cross-LLM generalizability.
AI.motivation: Understand and quantify how LLMs can reason about dynamic agents and static road topology in AD, and assess how map semantics influence trajectory prediction while maintaining safe, minimal adaptation.
AI.method: Use frozen LLMs as the reasoning core. Employ a traffic encoder to extract spatial features from observed agent trajectories and a lightweight CNN to encode local HD maps. Transform these scene features into LLM-compatible tokens via a reprogramming adapter. A linear decoder, rather than heavy training, outputs future trajectories.
AI.result: The framework enables quantitative analysis of the impact of multi-modal information (notably map semantics) on trajectory prediction accuracy and demonstrates strong generalizability across diverse LLM architectures, providing a unified evaluation platform.
AI.conclusion: This approach offers a practical, low-adaptation pipeline that leverages frozen LLMs for reasoning in AD perception/prediction and supports broad generalizability across LLM families while enabling systematic evaluation of modality contributions.

title: Thinking with Reasoning Skills: Fewer Tokens, More Accuracy
comment: 10 pages, The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics -- Industry Track
summary: Reasoning LLMs often spend substantial tokens on long intermediate reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought) when solving new problems. We propose to summarize and store reusable reasoning skills distilled from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error exploration, and to retrieve these skills at inference time to guide future reasoning. Unlike the prevailing \emph{reasoning from scratch} paradigm, our approach first recalls relevant skills for each query, helping the model avoid redundant detours and focus on effective solution paths. We evaluate our method on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks, and find that it significantly reduces reasoning tokens while improving overall performance. The resulting lower per-request cost indicates strong practical and economic potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Retrieving distilled, reusable reasoning skills to guide inference reduces token usage and improves performance, moving away from reasoning from scratch.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiency of chain-of-thought and long intermediate traces by leveraging stored skills to direct problem solving.
AI.method: Construct a library of distilled reasoning skills from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error; retrieve relevant skills per query to guide reasoning rather than starting from scratch.
AI.result: Significant reduction in reasoning tokens and improved task performance on coding and mathematical reasoning; lower cost per request.
AI.conclusion: Skill retrieval is a practical and economically attractive enhancement for LLM reasoning, enabling scalable, cost-effective deployment.

title: Even More Guarantees for Variational Inference in the Presence of Symmetries
comment: Accepted for presentation at the OPTIMAL Workshop at AISTATS 2026
summary: When approximating an intractable density via variational inference (VI) the variational family is typically chosen as a simple parametric family that very likely does not contain the target. This raises the question: Under which conditions can we recover characteristics of the target despite misspecification? In this work, we extend previous results on robust VI with location-scale families under target symmetries. We derive sufficient conditions guaranteeing exact recovery of the mean when using the forward Kullback-Leibler divergence and $α$-divergences. We further show how and why optimization can fail to recover the target mean in the absence of our sufficient conditions, providing initial guidelines on the choice of the variational family and $α$-value.

AI.tldr: Under misspecified variational families, the paper identifies conditions under which the mean of the target distribution is exactly recovered by VI using forward KL and α-divergences, for location-scale priors with target symmetries; it also shows when optimization can fail and offers guidelines for choosing the variational family and α.
AI.motivation: Misspecification in variational inference is common in practice. Understanding when essential target characteristics (notably the mean) can still be recovered helps practitioners select divergences and variational families that are robust to misspecification.
AI.method: The paper derives sufficient conditions under which VI with forward KL (and α-divergences) yields exact mean recovery for target distributions with location-scale structure and symmetry. It analyzes cases where these conditions fail and explains how optimization can fail to recover the target mean, providing guidance on choosing the variational family and α-values.
AI.result: Establishes sufficient conditions guaranteeing exact mean recovery under misspecification for forward KL and α-divergences; demonstrates that optimization can fail to recover the target mean when these conditions are violated; offers practical guidelines on selecting variational families and α-values to promote robust mean recovery.
AI.conclusion: The work provides theoretical guarantees for robust VI under model misspecification, emphasizing the role of target symmetries and the choice of divergence. It also translates these results into practical guidelines for variational family design and α-parameter selection.

title: VFM$^{4}$SDG: Unveiling the Power of VFMs for Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection
summary: In real-world scenarios, continual changes in weather, illumination, and imaging conditions cause significant domain shifts, leading detectors trained on a single source domain to degrade severely in unseen environments. Existing single-domain generalized object detection (SDGOD) methods mainly rely on data augmentation or domain-invariant representation learning, but pay limited attention to detector mechanisms, leaving clear limitations under complex domain shifts. Through analytical experiments, we find that performance degradation is dominated by increasing missed detections, which fundamentally arises from reduced cross-domain stability of the detector: object-background and inter-instance relations become less stable in the encoding stage, while semantic-spatial alignment of query representations also becomes harder to maintain in the decoding stage. To this end, we propose VFM$^{4}$SDG, a dual-prior learning framework for SDGOD, which introduces a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) as a transferable cross-domain stability prior into detector representation learning and query modeling. In the encoding stage, we propose Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation to enhance the robustness of object-background and inter-instance relational modeling. In the decoding stage, we propose Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement, which injects category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries to improve their semantic recognition and spatial localization stability in unseen domains. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing SOTA methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating its effectiveness, robustness, and generality.

AI.tldr: A dual-prior SDGOD framework (VFM^4SDG) uses a frozen vision foundation model to stabilize cross-domain detections via (1) cross-domain stable relational prior distillation in encoding and (2) semantic-contextual query enhancement in decoding, achieving state-of-the-art generalization on SDGOD benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Continual domain shifts (weather, illumination, imaging) cause severe detector degradation when trained on a single source. Existing SDGOD methods rely on data augmentation or invariant representations but largely ignore detector mechanics, leading to unstable object-background/inter-instance relations and semantic-spatial misalignment in queries.
AI.method: Propose VFM^4SDG with a frozen vision foundation model as a cross-domain stability prior. Encoding stage: Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation strengthens object-background and inter-instance relations. Decoding stage: Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement injects category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries to improve semantic recognition and spatial localization in unseen domains.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating effectiveness, robustness, and generality.
AI.conclusion: The method highlights the importance of detector-centric priors and cross-domain stability for SDGOD, with a dual-prior framework that generalizes across detectors and domains.

title: Who Defines "Best"? Towards Interactive, User-Defined Evaluation of LLM Leaderboards
comment: Accepted to the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT 2026)
summary: LLM leaderboards are widely used to compare models and guide deployment decisions. However, leaderboard rankings are shaped by evaluation priorities set by benchmark designers, rather than by the diverse goals and constraints of actual users and organizations. A single aggregate score often obscures how models behave across different prompt types and compositions. In this work, we conduct an in-depth analysis of the dataset used in the LMArena (formerly Chatbot Arena) benchmark and investigate this evaluation challenge by designing an interactive visualization interface as a design probe. Our analysis reveals that the dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics, that model rankings vary across prompt slices, and that preference-based judgments are used in ways that blur their intended scope. Building on this analysis, we introduce a visualization interface that allows users to define their own evaluation priorities by selecting and weighting prompt slices and to explore how rankings change accordingly. A qualitative study suggests that this interactive approach improves transparency and supports more context-specific model evaluation, pointing toward alternative ways to design and use LLM leaderboards.

AI.tldr: Dataset-skew and slice-dependent rankings reveal limitations of LLM leaderboards; an interactive, user-weighted visualization approach is proposed to improve transparency.
AI.motivation: Benchmark leaderboards influence deployment decisions but often reflect designers' priorities; a single aggregate score masks performance variation across prompt types and user needs.
AI.method: In-depth analysis of the LMArena (Chatbot Arena) dataset; development of an interactive visualization interface as a design probe; qualitative study evaluating its usefulness.
AI.result: The dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics; model rankings vary across prompt slices; judgments are used in ways that blur their intended scope. The interactive interface lets users select and weight prompt slices to define evaluation priorities and observe how rankings change.
AI.conclusion: An interactive, user-driven visualization approach can complement traditional leaderboards by improving transparency and enabling context-specific evaluation, suggesting alternative ways to design and use LLM leaderboards.

title: A Green-Integral-Constrained Neural Solver with Stochastic Physics-Informed Regularization
summary: Standard physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) struggle to simulate highly oscillatory Helmholtz solutions in heterogeneous media because pointwise minimization of second-order PDE residuals is computationally expensive, biased toward smooth solutions, and requires artificial absorbing boundary layers to restrict the solution. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a Green-Integral (GI) neural solver for the acoustic Helmholtz equation. It departs from the PDE-residual-based formulation by enforcing wave physics through an integral representation that imposes a nonlocal constraint. Oscillatory behavior and outgoing radiation are encoded directly through the integral kernel, eliminating second-order spatial derivatives and enforcing physical solutions without additional boundary layers. Theoretically, optimizing this GI loss via a neural network acts as a spectrally tuned preconditioned iteration, enabling convergence in heterogeneous media where the classical Born series diverges. By exploiting FFT-based convolution to accelerate the GI loss evaluation, our approach substantially reduces GPU memory usage and training time. However, this efficiency relies on a fixed regular grid, which can limit local resolution. To improve local accuracy in strong scattering regions, we also propose a hybrid GI+PDE loss, enforcing a lightweight Helmholtz residual at a small number of nonuniformly sampled collocation points. We evaluate our method on seismic benchmark models characterized by structural contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity at frequencies up to 20Hz. GI-based training consistently outperforms PDE-based PINNs, reducing computational cost by over a factor of ten. In models with localized scattering, the hybrid loss yields the most accurate reconstructions, providing a stable, efficient, and physically grounded alternative.

AI.tldr: Green-Integral neural solver for Helmholtz; enforces integral representation rather than PDE residuals; FFT-accelerated, fixed grid; hybrid GI+PDE loss for local accuracy; shows >10x speedup over PDE PINNs in seismic-like models with subwavelength heterogeneity, up to 20 Hz.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of standard PINNs in oscillatory Helmholtz problems in heterogeneous media, including computational cost, bias toward smooth solutions, and need for absorbing boundaries.
AI.method: Nonlocal Green-Integral loss enforcing integral form; spectrally tuned preconditioned iteration perspective; FFT-based convolution for efficient GI loss; fixed regular grid; hybrid GI+PDE loss at a few nonuniform collocation points to improve local resolution in strong scattering.
AI.result: GI-based training outperforms PDE-based PINNs by >10x in computational cost; robust performance on seismic benchmark models with structural contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity up to 20 Hz; the hybrid GI+PDE loss delivers best accuracy in localized scattering.
AI.conclusion: GI framework provides a physically grounded, efficient alternative to PINNs for heterogeneous Helmholtz problems, with clear computational advantages and a practical way to improve local accuracy; limitations include reliance on a fixed grid, addressed by the proposed hybrid loss.

title: Gmd: Gaussian mixture descriptor for pair matching of 3D fragments
comment: 24 pages, 10 figures. Published in Multimedia Systems
summary: In the automatic reassembly of fragments acquired using laser scanners to reconstruct objects, a crucial step is the matching of fractured surfaces. In this paper, we propose a novel local descriptor that uses the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to fit the distribution of points, allowing for the description and matching of fractured surfaces of fragments. Our method involves dividing a local surface patch into concave and convex regions for estimating the k value of GMM. Then the final Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) of the fractured surface is formed by merging the regional GMDs. To measure the similarities between GMDs for determining adjacent fragments, we employ the L2 distance and align the fragments using Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) and Iterative Closest Point (ICP). The extensive experiments on real-scanned public datasets and Terracotta datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach; furthermore, the comparisons with several existing methods also validate the advantage of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) for local surface patches in 3D fragment reassembly, modeling point distributions with GMM, merging regional descriptors, and using L2 distance with RANSAC/ICP for alignment; validated on real-scanned public and Terracotta datasets with favorable comparisons.
AI.motivation: Automatic reassembly of fractured fragments from laser-scanned data requires robust, discriminative local surface descriptors that can handle variability in fragment surfaces and noise. Existing descriptors may not capture local curved geometry effectively, hindering correct fragment adjacency detection.
AI.method: For each local surface patch, divide into concave and convex regions to adaptively estimate the GMM component count (k). Fit a Gaussian Mixture Model to the regional point distribution to obtain a Regional GMD. Merge regional GMDs to form the final Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) for the fractured surface. Measure similarity between GMDs via L2 distance and align fragments using RANSAC followed by ICP to compute the final pose.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-scanned public datasets and Terracotta datasets demonstrate the method’s effectiveness. Comparisons with several existing methods validate the proposed approach, showing advantages in descriptor quality and fragment matching/adjoining performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GMD provides a robust, adaptive local descriptor for fractured-surface matching in automatic fragment reassembly, enabling more accurate adjacency determination and improved reassembly outcomes compared to established baselines.

title: Inferring High-Level Events from Timestamped Data: Complexity and Medical Applications
comment: This is the full version (with appendix) of a paper appearing at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026)
summary: In this paper, we develop a novel logic-based approach to detecting high-level temporally extended events from timestamped data and background knowledge. Our framework employs logical rules to capture existence and termination conditions for simple temporal events and to combine these into meta-events. In the medical domain, for example, disease episodes and therapies are inferred from timestamped clinical observations, such as diagnoses and drug administrations stored in patient records, and can be further combined into higher-level disease events. As some incorrect events might be inferred, we use constraints to identify incompatible combinations of events and propose a repair mechanism to select preferred consistent sets of events. While reasoning in the full framework is intractable, we identify relevant restrictions that ensure polynomial-time data complexity. Our prototype system implements core components of the approach using answer set programming. An evaluation on a lung cancer use case supports the interest of the approach, both in terms of computational feasibility and positive alignment of our results with medical expert opinions. While strongly motivated by the needs of the healthcare domain, our framework is purposely generic, enabling its reuse in other areas.

AI.tldr: A logic-based, ASP-driven framework for detecting temporally extended events from timestamped data via existence/termination rules and meta-events, with constraint-based repair for consistency; tractable under restrictions; demonstrated on a lung cancer use case; generic to other domains.
AI.motivation: To reason about high-level, temporally extended events in noisy, timestamped clinical data by composing simple events into meta-events, while ensuring consistency and tractability.
AI.method: Define logical rules for event existence and termination; compose into meta-events; apply constraints to reject incompatible combinations; propose a repair mechanism to select preferred consistent event sets; implement core components in answer set programming; identify restrictions yielding polynomial-time data complexity.
AI.result: Prototype ASP-based system; successful evaluation on lung cancer use case showing computational feasibility and concordance with medical expert opinions; framework demonstrates generic applicability beyond healthcare.
AI.conclusion: The approach is generic and reusable across domains, with acknowledged intractability of the full framework and practical tractability under restrictions; ASP implementation enables feasible, domain-relevant event detection.

title: Seeing Isn't Believing: Uncovering Blind Spots in Evaluator Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly used to evaluate outputs of other models, for image-to-text (I2T) tasks such as visual question answering, and text-to-image (T2I) generation tasks. Despite this growing reliance, the reliability of these Evaluator VLMs remains under explored. In this work, we systematically evaluate the reliability of Evaluator VLMs across both I2T and T2I tasks. We introduce targeted perturbations that degrade output quality along key error dimensions, including object hallucinations, spatial reasoning, factual grounding, and visual fidelity. These perturbations test whether Evaluator VLMs can reliably account for these quality degrading errors in their evaluations. Using a comprehensive benchmark of over 4000 perturbed instances spanning 40 perturbation dimensions, we evaluate 4 prominent VLMs using single-answer scoring, pairwise comparison, and reference-guided paradigms. Our findings reveal that current VLM evaluators exhibit substantial blind spots: they often fail to detect perturbed outputs - in some cases exceeding 50%, struggle particularly with fine-grained compositional and spatial errors, and are often insensitive to hallucinated content that contradicts the input image. Pairwise comparison proves more reliable, though failure rates persist. These results highlight the unreliable nature of current Evaluator VLMs and urge caution in their deployment for benchmarking and development decisions. Code and data have been made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Evaluator VLMs for I2T and T2I tasks are unreliable benchmarks: many perturbations go undetected (often >50%), especially fine-grained compositional/spatial and hallucination cases; pairwise comparisons are more robust but still imperfect; caution urged in deployment; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: As large vision-language models are increasingly used to evaluate other models' outputs, their reliability remains insufficiently studied. A systematic evaluation across varied error modes is needed to trust their use for benchmarking and development decisions.
AI.method: Introduce targeted perturbations along 40 dimensions (e.g., object hallucinations, spatial reasoning, factual grounding, visual fidelity) that degrade output quality. Build a benchmark of over 4000 perturbed instances spanning I2T and T2I tasks. Evaluate 4 prominent VLM evaluators using single-answer scoring, pairwise comparison, and reference-guided paradigms.
AI.result: Evaluator VLMs exhibit substantial blind spots: they often fail to detect perturbed outputs, in some cases by more than 50%. They struggle with fine-grained compositional and spatial errors and are often insensitive to hallucinated content contradicting the input image. Pairwise comparison is more reliable than single-answer or reference-guided scoring, but failure rates persist.
AI.conclusion: Current Evaluator VLMs are unreliable for benchmarking and development decisions; use with caution. The authors release code and data publicly to facilitate further study and improvements.

title: Learning to Communicate: Toward End-to-End Optimization of Multi-Agent Language Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems built on large language models have shown strong performance on complex reasoning tasks, yet most work focuses on agent roles and orchestration while treating inter-agent communication as a fixed interface. Latent communication through internal representations such as key-value caches offers a promising alternative to text-based protocols, but existing approaches do not jointly optimize communication with multi-agent reasoning. Therefore we propose DiffMAS, a training framework that treats latent communication as a learnable component of multi-agent systems. DiffMAS performs parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, enabling agents to jointly learn how information should be encoded and interpreted across interactions. Experiments on mathematical reasoning, scientific QA, code generation, and commonsense benchmarks show that DiffMAS consistently improves reasoning accuracy and decoding stability over single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods, achieving 26.7% on AIME24, 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, and consistent gains across reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DiffMAS trains multi-agent LLMs with learnable latent communication, enabling agents to jointly optimize information encoding/interpretation and improving reasoning across tasks.
AI.motivation: Latent communication via internal representations (e.g., key-value caches) offers a scalable alternative to text-based protocols, but prior work fixes the communication interface and does not jointly optimize it with multi-agent reasoning, limiting performance and stability.
AI.method: Parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, enabling joint learning of how information is encoded and interpreted across interactions (learnable latent communication channels).
AI.result: Across mathematical reasoning, scientific QA, code generation, and commonsense benchmarks, DiffMAS yields consistent gains over single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent-communication methods, e.g., 26.7% on AIME24 and 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing latent inter-agent communication with reasoning improves accuracy and decoding stability, demonstrating DiffMAS as a scalable framework for multi-agent LLM systems across diverse reasoning domains.

title: Conditional anomaly detection with soft harmonic functions
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM 2011). 10.1109/ICDM.2011.40
summary: In this paper, we consider the problem of conditional anomaly detection that aims to identify data instances with an unusual response or a class label. We develop a new non-parametric approach for conditional anomaly detection based on the soft harmonic solution, with which we estimate the confidence of the label to detect anomalous mislabeling. We further regularize the solution to avoid the detection of isolated examples and examples on the boundary of the distribution support. We demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method on several synthetic and UCI ML datasets in detecting unusual labels when compared to several baseline approaches. We also evaluate the performance of our method on a real-world electronic health record dataset where we seek to identify unusual patient-management decisions.

AI.tldr: Non-parametric conditional anomaly detection using a soft harmonic solution with regularization to detect unusual labels, validated on synthetic/UCI data and an EHR dataset.
AI.motivation: Identify instances with unusual responses/labels, including mislabeling and unusual clinical decisions, while avoiding spurious detections on boundary or isolated points.
AI.method: Proposes a non-parametric approach based on a soft harmonic solution to estimate label confidence; includes regularization to suppress detection of isolated points and boundary cases; graph-based or kernel-based.
AI.result: Empirical results show efficacy over baselines on synthetic and UCI datasets for unusual labels; real-world EHR dataset shows potential for flagging unusual management decisions.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a robust, non-parametric framework for conditional anomaly detection with improved reliability due to regularization; suitable for detecting mislabeling and unusual decisions across domains.

title: Attention-based multiple instance learning for predominant growth pattern prediction in lung adenocarcinoma wsi using foundation models
summary: Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) grading depends on accurately identifying growth patterns, which are indicators of prognosis and can influence treatment decisions. Common deep learning approaches to determine the predominant pattern rely on patch-level classification or segmentation, requiring extensive annotations. This study proposes an attention-based multiple instance learning (ABMIL) framework to predict the predominant LUAD growth pattern at the whole slide level to reduce annotation burden. Our approach integrates pretrained pathology foundation models as patch encoders, used either frozen or fine-tuned on annotated patches, to extract discriminative features that are aggregated through attention mechanisms. Experiments show that fine-tuned encoders improve performance, with Prov-GigaPath achieving the highest agreement (\k{appa} = 0.699) under ABMIL. Compared to simple patch-aggregation baselines, ABMIL yields more robust predictions by leveraging slide-level supervision and spatial attention. Future work will extend this framework to estimate the full distribution of growth patterns and validate performance on external cohorts.

AI.tldr: Attention-based multiple instance learning (ABMIL) for predicting predominant LUAD growth patterns at the whole-slide level using pretrained pathology foundation models as patch encoders; fine-tuning improves accuracy, achieving Cohen's kappa 0.699 with Prov-GigaPath; reduces annotation burden compared to patch-level segmentation.
AI.motivation: Reduce annotation burden and enable slide-level grading of LUAD growth patterns that inform prognosis and treatment without requiring exhaustive patch-level annotations.
AI.method: ABMIL framework with patch embeddings from pretrained pathology foundation models (frozen or fine-tuned on annotated patches); attention-based aggregation across patches to yield slide-level predictions; comparison to simple patch-aggregation baselines.
AI.result: Fine-tuned encoders outperform frozen ones; Prov-GigaPath achieves the highest agreement (kappa = 0.699) under ABMIL; ABMIL yields more robust slide-level predictions by leveraging spatial attention and slide-level supervision compared to patch-aggregation baselines.
AI.conclusion: ABMIL with fine-tuned patch encoders is effective for predicting predominant LUAD growth patterns at the whole-slide level and can reduce labeling burden; future work includes estimating the full distribution of growth patterns and validating on external cohorts.

title: Dynamical Priors as a Training Objective in Reinforcement Learning
comment: Supplementary material can be accessed here: https://github.com/drsukeshs/esd-rl
summary: Standard reinforcement learning (RL) optimizes policies for reward but imposes few constraints on how decisions evolve over time. As a result, policies may achieve high performance while exhibiting temporally incoherent behavior such as abrupt confidence shifts, oscillations, or degenerate inactivity. We introduce Dynamical Prior Reinforcement Learning (DP-RL), a training framework that augments policy gradient learning with an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics that implement evidence accumulation and hysteresis. Without modifying the reward, environment, or policy architecture, this prior shapes the temporal evolution of action probabilities during learning. Across three minimal environments, we show that dynamical priors systematically alter decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior that cannot be explained by generic smoothing. These results demonstrate that training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents.

AI.tldr: DP-RL adds a dynamical prior as an auxiliary loss to policy gradient methods to enforce temporally structured decision-making, using external state dynamics that implement evidence accumulation and hysteresis; it alters decision trajectories without changing reward, environment, or policy architecture, demonstrated in three minimal tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard RL optimizes reward with little constraint on temporal evolution of decisions, which can yield temporally incoherent behavior such as abrupt shifts, oscillations, or inactivity.
AI.method: Introduce Dynamical Prior Reinforcement Learning (DP-RL) by adding an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics that model evidence accumulation and hysteresis. This prior shapes the evolution of action probabilities during learning, while keeping the reward, environment, and policy architecture unchanged.
AI.result: In three minimal environments, DP-RL systematically alters decision trajectories to produce temporally structured behavior that cannot be explained by generic smoothing, showing that training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents.
AI.conclusion: Training objectives can control the temporal dynamics of decision-making in RL agents; DP-RL provides a framework to impose temporal priors via auxiliary dynamics without modifying core components.

title: Component-Based Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection requires sensitivity to subtle shifts without overreacting to natural In-Distribution (ID) diversity. However, from the viewpoint of detection granularity, global representation inevitably suppress local OOD cues, while patch-based methods are unstable due to entangled spurious-correlation and noise. And neither them is effective in detecting compositional OODs composed of valid ID components. Inspired by recognition-by-components theory, we present a training-free Component-Based OOD Detection (CoOD) framework that addresses the existing limitations by decomposing inputs into functional components. To instantiate CoOD, we derive Component Shift Score (CSS) to detect local appearance shifts, and Compositional Consistency Score (CCS) to identify cross-component compositional inconsistencies. Empirically, CoOD achieves consistent improvements on both coarse- and fine-grained OOD detection.

AI.tldr: A training-free framework for OOD detection that decomposes inputs into functional components to detect local shifts and compositional inconsistencies, addressing limits of global and patch-based approaches with demonstrated improvements on coarse- and fine-grained OOD tasks.
AI.motivation: Overly global representations mask local OOD cues and patch-based methods are unstable due to spurious correlations and noise; existing methods struggle with compositional OODs built from ID components.
AI.method: Decompose inputs into functional components (Component-Based OOD Detection, CoOD). Introduce Component Shift Score (CSS) for local appearance shifts and Compositional Consistency Score (CCS) for cross-component compositional inconsistencies. Training-free framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show CoOD achieves consistent improvements on both coarse- and fine-grained OOD detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: CoOD offers a robust, training-free approach to detect OOD by leveraging components, addressing granularity and compositional challenges that global and patch-based methods encounter.

title: Alignment has a Fantasia Problem
summary: Modern AI assistants are trained to follow instructions, implicitly assuming that users can clearly articulate their goals and the kind of assistance they need. Decades of behavioral research, however, show that people often engage with AI systems before their goals are fully formed. When AI systems treat prompts as complete expressions of intent, they can appear to be useful or convenient, but not necessarily aligned with the users' needs. We call these failures Fantasia interactions. We argue that Fantasia interactions demand a rethinking of alignment research: rather than treating users as rational oracles, AI should provide cognitive support by actively helping users form and refine their intent through time. This requires an interdisciplinary approach that bridges machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science. We synthesize insights from these fields to characterize the mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions. We then show why existing interventions are insufficient, and propose a research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that better help humans navigate uncertainty in their tasks.

AI.tldr: Fantasia interactions reveal that users often engage with AI before goals are fully formed; AI should help users form and refine intent over time, not just execute fixed prompts, requiring interdisciplinary, cognitive-supportive alignment across ML, UI, and behavioral science.
AI.motivation: Behavioral research shows people frequently interact with AI while their goals are still under formation. Treating prompts as complete intent can yield misalignment with users' evolving needs. This motivates a rethinking of alignment research toward cognitive support that helps users form and refine their intent over time.
AI.method: Synthesize and integrate insights from machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science; characterize mechanisms and failure modes of Fantasia interactions; critique existing interventions; propose a cross-disciplinary research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that assist users in navigating task uncertainty.
