AI.result: Proposes a conceptual framework for Fantasia interactions, identifies key failure modes of current prompts-as-intent approaches, demonstrates insufficiency of existing interventions, and outlines evaluation criteria and a design space for cognitive-supportive AI systems.
AI.conclusion: Reframe alignment as providing cognitive support for evolving user intent. AI should assist users in forming and refining goals over time, requiring interdisciplinary collaboration across ML, design, and behavioral sciences, along with new evaluation paradigms for uncertainty navigation.

title: Drug Synergy Prediction via Residual Graph Isomorphism Networks and Attention Mechanisms
summary: In the treatment of complex diseases, treatment regimens using a single drug often yield limited efficacy and can lead to drug resistance. In contrast, combination drug therapies can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes through synergistic effects. However, experimentally validating all possible drug combinations is prohibitively expensive, underscoring the critical need for efficient computational prediction methods. Although existing approaches based on deep learning and graph neural networks (GNNs) have made considerable progress, challenges remain in reducing structural bias, improving generalization capability, and enhancing model interpretability. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a collaborative prediction graph neural network that integrates molecular structural features and cell-line genomic profiles with drug-drug interactions to enhance the prediction of synergistic effects. We introduce a novel model named the Residual Graph Isomorphism Network integrated with an Attention mechanism (ResGIN-Att). The model first extracts multi scale topological features of drug molecules using a residual graph isomorphism network, where residual connections help mitigate over-smoothing in deep layers. Subsequently, an adaptive Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) module fuses structural information from local to global scales. Finally, a cross-attention module is designed to explicitly model drug-drug interactions and identify key chemical substructures. Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets demonstrate that ResGIN-Att achieves competitive performance, comparing favorably against key baseline methods while exhibiting promising generalization capability and robustness.

AI.tldr: A novel ResGIN-Att model for multi-modal drug synergy prediction leveraging residual GIN for drug graphs, adaptive LSTM for scale fusion, and cross-attention for DDIs, integrating molecular, cell-line genomic data, and drug interactions; shows competitive performance and generalization on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Drug combination therapies can yield synergistic effects; single-drug regimens limited; experimental validation is expensive; need computational methods with reduced bias, better generalization, and interpretability.
AI.method: Use Residual Graph Isomorphism Network to extract multi-scale molecular topological features with residual connections to avoid over-smoothing; adaptive LSTM to fuse local-to-global structural information; cross-attention to model drug-drug interactions and identify substructures; integrate molecular features, cell-line genomics, and DDIs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five public datasets show competitive performance vs baselines, with good generalization and robustness.
AI.conclusion: ResGIN-Att improves synergy prediction by addressing structural bias and interpretability via cross-attention; demonstrates robustness; suitable for efficient drug synergy prediction, though further work may assess ablations and real-world generalization.

title: Deep kernel video approximation for unsupervised action segmentation
summary: This work focuses on per-video unsupervised action segmentation, which is of interest to applications where storing large datasets is either not possible, or nor permitted. We propose to segment videos by learning in deep kernel space, to approximate the underlying frame distribution, as closely as possible. To define this closeness metric between the original video distribution and its approximation, we rely on maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) which is a geometry-preserving metric in distribution space, and thus gives more reliable estimates. Moreover, unlike the commonly used optimal transport metric, MMD is both easier to optimize, and faster. We choose to use neural tangent kernels (NTKs) to define the kernel space where MMD operates, because of their improved descriptive power as opposed to fixed kernels. And, also, because NTKs sidestep the trivial solution, when jointly learning the inputs (video approximation) and the kernel function. Finally, we show competitive results when compared to state-of-the-art per-video methods, on six standard benchmarks. Additionally, our method has higher F1 scores than prior agglomerative work, when the number of segments is unknown.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised per-video action segmentation via deep kernel space with MMD and neural tangent kernels; competitive on six benchmarks; outperforms agglomerative methods when segment count is unknown.
AI.motivation: Per-video unsupervised segmentation is valuable when large datasets cannot be stored; opt for a distributional alignment metric that is geometry-preserving and efficient. MMD offers a reliable, scalable alternative to OT; NTKs provide expressive, trainable kernels and avoid trivial solutions when jointly optimizing inputs and kernels.
AI.method: Learn in a deep kernel space to approximate the frame distribution; use maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to quantify closeness between the original video distribution and its approximation; define the kernel in neural tangent kernel (NTK) space for stronger descriptive power; NTKs mitigate the trivial solution when jointly learning inputs and kernels; evaluate against six standard benchmarks.
AI.result: The approach yields competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art per-video methods; achieves higher F1 scores than prior agglomerative methods when the number of segments is unknown.
AI.conclusion: Shows that combining MMD with NTK-based deep kernels is effective for unsupervised per-video action segmentation, delivering robust performance across multiple benchmarks and robustness to unknown segment counts.

title: Bounding the Black Box: A Statistical Certification Framework for AI Risk Regulation
summary:
Artificial intelligence now decides who receives a loan, who is flagged for criminal investigation, and whether an autonomous vehicle brakes in time. Governments have responded: the EU AI Act, the NIST Risk Management Framework, and the Council of Europe Convention all demand that high-risk systems demonstrate safety before deployment. Yet beneath this regulatory consensus lies a critical vacuum: none specifies what ``acceptable risk'' means in quantitative terms, and none provides a technical method for verifying that a deployed system actually meets such a threshold. The regulatory architecture is in place; the verification instrument is not.
  This gap is not theoretical. As the EU AI Act moves into full enforcement, developers face mandatory conformity assessments without established methodologies for producing quantitative safety evidence - and the systems most in need of oversight are opaque statistical inference engines that resist white-box scrutiny.
  This paper provides the missing instrument. Drawing on the aviation certification paradigm, we propose a two-stage framework that transforms AI risk regulation into engineering practice. In Stage One, a competent authority formally fixes an acceptable failure probability $δ$ and an operational input domain $\varepsilon$ - a normative act with direct civil liability implications. In Stage Two, the RoMA and gRoMA statistical verification tools compute a definitive, auditable upper bound on the system's true failure rate, requiring no access to model internals and scaling to arbitrary architectures. We demonstrate how this certificate satisfies existing regulatory obligations, shifts accountability upstream to developers, and integrates with the legal frameworks that exist today.

AI.tldr: A two-stage framework transforming AI risk regulation into engineering practice: Stage 1 regulatory act fixes an acceptable failure probability δ and input domain ε; Stage 2 RoMA/gRoMA verification computes an auditable upper bound on the true failure rate without model internals, scalable to arbitrary architectures.
AI.motivation: Regulators lack quantitative acceptability thresholds and practical verification methods for AI safety, especially for opaque, high-risk systems. A formal, auditable safety certificate is needed to bridge policy and deployment.
AI.method: Stage One: a competent authority fixes δ (acceptable failure probability) and ε (operational input domain). Stage Two: RoMA and gRoMA statistical verification compute a definitive upper bound on the system's true failure rate with no access to internals, producing an auditable certificate that scales to any architecture.
AI.result: Conceptual framework demonstrated to satisfy regulatory obligations, shift accountability to developers, and integrate with current legal frameworks; provides a verifiable safety certificate for high-risk AI without requiring white-box access.
AI.conclusion: The proposed instrument fills a critical verification gap by codifying a quantitative, auditable safety certificate for AI systems, aligning risk regulation with implementable engineering practice.

title: Generalizing Numerical Reasoning in Table Data through Operation Sketches and Self-Supervised Learning
comment: Accepted to TACL. This is a pre-MIT Press publication version
summary: Numerical reasoning over expert-domain tables often exhibits high in-domain accuracy but limited robustness to domain shift. Models trained with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on specific datasets tend to rely on header-operation shortcuts rather than structural reasoning. We introduce TaNOS, a continual pre-training framework comprising three components: (i) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (ii) operation sketches that provide minimal structural cues, and (iii) self-supervised pretraining that constructs correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from given tables in a program-first manner. By decoupling domain semantics and numerical operation structure, TaNOS improves the transferability of numerical reasoning. Applied to an 8B instruction-tuned model, TaNOS achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% train data, outperforming SFT baseline (73.97%) with full train data and proprietary models such as GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro. Furthermore, in the domain-shift experiments, TaNOS displays nearly-negligible cross-domain gap (<2pp) when standard SFT shows over 10pp gap. These results suggest that structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision can improve the robustness of numerical reasoning across diverse expert-domain tables.

AI.tldr: TaNOS is a continual pre-training framework that decouples numeric reasoning from table semantics using header anonymization, operation sketches, and self-supervised program-question pairing, yielding strong data-efficient transferability on numerical reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Address overreliance on header-operation shortcuts and weak robustness to domain shift in supervised fine-tuning on expert-domain tables.
AI.method: Three components: (i) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (ii) operation sketches providing minimal structural cues, (iii) self-supervised pretraining generating correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from tables in a program-first manner; decouples domain semantics from numerical operation structure.
AI.result: On an 8B instruction-tuned model, achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% train data; outperforms SFT baseline (73.97%) with full data and proprietary models.
AI.conclusion: Structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision improves robustness of numerical reasoning across expert-domain tables.

title: CHRep: Cross-modal Histology Representation and Post-hoc Calibration for Spatial Gene Expression Prediction
summary: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) enables spatially resolved gene profiling but remains expensive and low-throughput, limiting large-cohort studies and routine clinical use. Predicting spatial gene expression from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) slides is a promising alternative, yet under realistic leave-one-slide-out evaluation, existing models often suffer from slide-level appearance shifts and regression-driven over-smoothing that suppress biologically meaningful variation. CHRep is a two-phase framework for robust histology-to-expression prediction. In the training phase, CHRep learns a structure-aware representation by jointly optimizing correlation-aware regression, symmetric image-expression alignment, and coordinate-induced spatial topology regularization. In the inference phase, cross-slide robustness is improved without backbone fine-tuning through a lightweight calibration module trained on the training slides, which combines a non-parametric estimate from a training gallery with a magnitude-regularized correction module. Unlike prior embedding-alignment or retrieval-based transfer methods that rely on a single prediction route, CHRep couples topology-preserving representation learning with post-hoc calibration, enabling stable neighborhood retrieval and controlled bias correction under slide-level shifts. Across the three cohorts, CHRep consistently improves gene-wise correlation under leave-one-slide-out evaluation, with the largest gains observed on Alex+10x. Relative to HAGE, the Pearson correlation coefficient on all considered genes [PCC(ACG)] increases by 4.0% on cSCC and 9.8% on HER2+. Relative to mclSTExp, PCC(ACG) further improves by 39.5% on Alex+10x, together with 9.7% and 9.0% reductions in mean squared error (MSE) and mean absolute error (MAE), respectively.

AI.tldr: CHRep is a two-phase framework for robust histology-to-expression prediction in spatial transcriptomics that improves cross-slide robustness and gene-wise correlations by combining structure-aware representation learning with post-hoc calibration, outperforming HAGE and mclSTExp, especially on Alex+10x.
AI.motivation: Predict spatial gene expression from H&E slides to overcome the high cost/low throughput of spatial transcriptomics; address slide-level appearance shifts and over-smoothing in leave-one-slide-out evaluation.
AI.method: Training phase learns a structure-aware representation by jointly optimizing correlation-aware regression, symmetric image-expression alignment, and coordinate-induced spatial topology regularization. Inference uses a lightweight calibration module that merges a non-parametric training-gallery estimate with a magnitude-regularized correction, enabling cross-slide robustness without backbone fine-tuning.
AI.result: CHRep yields consistent improvements in gene-wise correlation under leave-one-slide-out across three cohorts, with the largest gains on Alex+10x. PCC(ACG) improves by 4.0% (cSCC) and 9.8% (HER2+) versus HAGE, and by 39.5% on Alex+10x versus mclSTExp, along with 9.7% and 9.0% reductions in MSE and MAE respectively.
AI.conclusion: By coupling topology-preserving representation learning with post-hoc calibration, CHRep enables stable neighborhood retrieval and bias control under slide shifts, achieving robust cross-slide gene-expression prediction beyond prior embedding-alignment or retrieval-based methods.

title: Nemobot Games: Crafting Strategic AI Gaming Agents for Interactive Learning with Large Language Models
summary: This paper introduces a new paradigm for AI game programming, leveraging large language models (LLMs) to extend and operationalize Claude Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines. Central to this paradigm is Nemobot, an interactive agentic engineering environment that enables users to create, customize, and deploy LLM-powered game agents while actively engaging with AI-driven strategies. The LLM-based chatbot, integrated within Nemobot, demonstrates its capabilities across four distinct classes of games. For dictionary-based games, it compresses state-action mappings into efficient, generalized models for rapid adaptability. In rigorously solvable games, it employs mathematical reasoning to compute optimal strategies and generates human-readable explanations for its decisions. For heuristic-based games, it synthesizes strategies by combining insights from classical minimax algorithms (see, e.g., shannon1950chess) with crowd-sourced data. Finally, in learning-based games, it utilizes reinforcement learning with human feedback and self-critique to iteratively refine strategies through trial-and-error and imitation learning. Nemobot amplifies this framework by offering a programmable environment where users can experiment with tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning of strategic game agents. From strategic games to role-playing games, Nemobot demonstrates how AI agents can achieve a form of self-programming by integrating crowdsourced learning and human creativity to iteratively refine their own logic. This represents a step toward the long-term goal of self-programming AI.

AI.tldr: Nemobot is a programmable LLM-driven environment that operationalizes Shannon's game-playing taxonomy, enabling creation and deployment of adversarial agents across dictionary-, solvable-, heuristic-, and learning-based games; demonstrates self-programming with crowdsourced feedback.
AI.motivation: Extend Claude Shannon's taxonomy into practical AI game agents and explore self-programming via interactive, tool-augmented agent environments.
AI.method: LLM-powered chatbot integrated in Nemobot; four game classes with tailored strategies: dictionary-based compression of state-action mappings; solvable games with mathematical reasoning and explanations; heuristic games combining minimax insights with crowd data; learning-based games using reinforcement learning with human feedback and self-critique; programmable environment for tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning.
AI.result: Demonstrates capability across the four classes, with compressed mappings, optimal reasoning and explanations, hybrid strategies from minimax and crowdsourcing, and iterative learning via RL with human feedback; highlights progress toward self-programming AI.
AI.conclusion: Nemobot represents a step toward self-programming AI by integrating crowdsourced learning and human creativity to iteratively refine its own logic within a flexible agent-engineering framework.

title: A temporal deep learning framework for calibration of low-cost air quality sensors
summary: Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS) provide a practical alternative to expensive regulatory-grade instruments, making dense urban monitoring networks possible. Yet their adoption is limited by calibration challenges, including sensor drift, environmental cross-sensitivity, and variability in performance from device to device. This work presents a deep learning framework for calibrating LCS measurements of PM$_{2.5}$, PM$_{10}$, and NO$_2$ using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, trained on co-located reference data from the OxAria network in Oxford, UK. Unlike the Random Forest (RF) baseline, which treats each observation independently, the proposed approach captures temporal dependencies and delayed environmental effects through sequence-based learning, achieving higher $R^2$ values across training, validation, and test sets for all three pollutants. A feature set is constructed combining time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms to improve generalization on unseen temporal windows. Validation of unseen calibrated values against the Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1 demonstrates regulatory compliance with expanded uncertainties of 22.11% for NO$_2$, 12.42% for PM$_{10}$, and 9.1% for PM$_{2.5}$.

AI.tldr: An LSTM-based calibration framework for low-cost sensors (LCS) measuring PM2.5, PM10, and NO2 shows improved accuracy over RF baselines by modeling temporal dependencies and environmental delays, validated on OxAria co-located data with regulatory compliance demonstrated via Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1 uncertainties.
AI.motivation: To address calibration challenges of LCS (drift, cross-sensitivity, device variance) and enable reliable dense urban air-quality networks by leveraging sequence-based learning to improve calibration accuracy and generalization.
AI.method: Develop an LSTM network trained on co-located OxAria reference data, with a feature set that includes time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms; compare against a Random Forest baseline that treats observations independently; assess performance on training, validation, and test sets.
AI.result: LSTM achieves higher R^2 than RF across all pollutants and data splits; temporal modeling captures delayed environmental effects and improves generalization; regulatory compliance validated with expanded uncertainties: NO2 22.11%, PM10 12.42%, PM2.5 9.1% via Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1.
AI.conclusion: Sequence-based LCS calibration leveraging temporal dependencies yields better predictive accuracy and regulatory-aligned uncertainty bounds, suggesting LSTM as a robust approach for calibrating dense networks of low-cost sensors.

title: From Research Question to Scientific Workflow: Leveraging Agentic AI for Science Automation
summary: Scientific workflow systems automate execution -- scheduling, fault tolerance, resource management -- but not the semantic translation that precedes it. Scientists still manually convert research questions into workflow specifications, a task requiring both domain knowledge and infrastructure expertise. We propose an agentic architecture that closes this gap through three layers: an LLM interprets natural language into structured intents (semantic layer); validated generators produce reproducible workflow DAGs (deterministic layer); and domain experts author ``Skills'': markdown documents encoding vocabulary mappings, parameter constraints, and optimization strategies (knowledge layer). This decomposition confines LLM non-determinism to intent extraction: identical intents always yield identical workflows. We implement and evaluate the architecture on the 1000 Genomes population genetics workflow and Hyperflow WMS running on Kubernetes. In an ablation study on 150 queries, Skills raise full-match intent accuracy from 44% to 83%; skill-driven deferred workflow generation reduces data transfer by 92\%; and the end-to-end pipeline completes queries on Kubernetes with LLM overhead below 15 seconds and cost under $0.001 per query.

AI.tldr: Agentic, three-layer architecture aligns NL intent extraction with reproducible DAG generation using domain-authored Skills; validated on 1000 Genomes and Hyperflow on Kubernetes; ablation shows substantial gains in intent accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Close the semantic translation gap in scientific workflows by decoupling natural-language intent extraction from deterministic workflow generation, using a knowledge layer of domain-authored Skills to constrain LLM nondeterminism.
AI.method: Three-layer architecture: (1) Semantic layer uses an LLM to convert natural language into structured intents; (2) Deterministic layer uses validated generators to produce reproducible workflow DAGs; (3) Knowledge layer where domain experts author 'Skills' in Markdown encoding vocabulary mappings, parameter constraints, and optimization strategies; implemented and evaluated on the 1000 Genomes population genetics workflow and Hyperflow WMS on Kubernetes; includes an ablation study on 150 queries and metrics for accuracy, data transfer, latency, and cost.
AI.result: Skills raise full-match intent accuracy from 44% to 83%; skill-driven deferred workflow generation reduces data transfer by 92%; end-to-end latency on Kubernetes with LLM overhead under 15 seconds and cost per query under $0.001.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling nondeterminism to the intent layer with a knowledge layer yields reproducible workflows, improves accuracy, and achieves low latency and cost; demonstrates practical viability for scalable, domain-specific workflow automation.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Coupled Demand Forecasting and Supply Chain Optimization
comment: The paper is accepted in the Computers, Materials & Continua journal
summary: Supply chain resilience and efficiency are vital in industries characterized by volatile demand and uncertain supply, such as textiles and personal protective equipment (PPE). Traditional forecasting and optimization approaches often operate in isolation, limiting their real-world effectiveness. This paper proposes a Hybrid AI Framework for Demand-Supply Forecasting and Optimization (HAF-DS), which integrates a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based demand forecasting module with a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) optimization layer. The LSTM captures temporal and contextual demand dependencies, while the optimization layer prescribes cost-efficient replenishment and allocation decisions. The framework jointly minimizes forecasting error and operational cost through embedding-based feature representation and recurrent neural architectures. Experiments on textile sales and supply chain datasets show significant performance gains over statistical and deep learning baselines. On the combined dataset, HAF-DS reduced Mean Absolute Error (MAE) from 15.04 to 12.83 (14.7%), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) from 19.53 to 17.11 (12.4%), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) from 9.5% to 8.1%. Inventory cost decreased by 5.4%, stockouts by 27.5%, and service level rose from 95.5% to 97.8%. These results confirm that coupling predictive forecasting with prescriptive optimization enhances both accuracy and efficiency, providing a scalable and adaptable solution for modern textile and PPE supply chains.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LSTM-MILP framework (HAF-DS) for integrated demand forecasting and replenishment optimization, yielding substantial accuracy and cost-efficiency gains in textile and PPE supply chains.
AI.motivation: Supply chains in textiles and PPE face volatile demand and uncertain supply. Traditional siloed forecasting and optimization limit responsiveness and resilience; an integrated approach can improve service levels and reduce costs.
AI.method: A hybrid framework that combines LSTM-based demand forecasting with a MILP optimization layer. Embedding-based feature representations and recurrent architectures enable joint minimization of forecast error and operational cost, producing cost-efficient replenishment and allocation decisions.
AI.result: On combined textile datasets, MAE dropped from 15.04 to 12.83 (−14.7%), RMSE from 19.53 to 17.11 (−12.4%), and MAPE from 9.5% to 8.1%. Inventory cost down 5.4%, stockouts down 27.5%, service level up from 95.5% to 97.8%. Demonstrates that forecasting and prescriptive optimization synergy improves accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Coupling predictive forecasting with prescriptive optimization yields tangible improvements in accuracy and operational performance, offering a scalable, adaptable solution for modern textile and PPE supply chains.

title: Sculpt4D: Generating 4D Shapes via Sparse-Attention Diffusion Transformers
summary: Recent breakthroughs in 3D generative modeling have yielded remarkable progress in static shape synthesis, yet high-fidelity dynamic 4D generation remains elusive, hindered by temporal artifacts and prohibitive computational demand. We present Sculpt4D, a native 4D generative framework that seamlessly integrates efficient temporal modeling into a pretrained 3D Diffusion Transformer (Hunyuan3D 2.1), thereby mitigating the scarcity of 4D training data. At its core lies a Block Sparse Attention mechanism that preserves object identity by anchoring to the initial frame while capturing rich motion dynamics via a time-decaying sparse mask. This design faithfully models complex spatiotemporal dependencies with high fidelity, while sidestepping the quadratic overhead of full attention and reducing network total computation by 56%. Consequently, Sculpt4D establishes a new state-of-the-art in temporally coherent 4D synthesis and charts a path toward efficient and scalable 4D generation.

AI.tldr: Sculpt4D introduces a native 4D generative framework that augments a pretrained 3D Diffusion Transformer with Block Sparse Attention for temporally coherent 4D synthesis, achieving 56% less computation and new state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Dynamic 4D generation lags behind static 3D synthesis due to temporal artifacts and prohibitive compute, exacerbated by scarce 4D training data.
AI.method: Integrates temporal modeling into Hunyuan3D 2.1; uses Block Sparse Attention anchored to the initial frame with a time-decaying sparse mask to model spatiotemporal dependencies while avoiding full quadratic attention.
AI.result: Achieves temporally coherent 4D generation with substantially reduced computation (56% fewer ops) and sets a new state-of-the-art in 4D synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Sculpt4D demonstrates a scalable, efficient path for 4D generation by coupling a robust 3D diffusion backbone with targeted temporal sparsity, enabling broader 4D applications.

title: Promoting Simple Agents: Ensemble Methods for Event-Log Prediction
summary: We compare lightweight automata-based models (n-grams) with neural architectures (LSTM, Transformer) for next-activity prediction in streaming event logs. Experiments on synthetic patterns and five real-world process mining datasets show that n-grams with appropriate context windows achieve comparable accuracy to neural models while requiring substantially fewer resources. Unlike windowed neural architectures, which show unstable performance patterns, n-grams provide stable and consistent accuracy. While we demonstrate that classical ensemble methods like voting improve n-gram performance, they require running many agents in parallel during inference, increasing memory consumption and latency. We propose an ensemble method, the promotion algorithm, that dynamically selects between two active models during inference, reducing overhead compared to classical voting schemes. On real-world datasets, these ensembles match or exceed the accuracy of non-windowed neural models with lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: N-gram models with contextual windows can rival LSTM/Transformer in next-activity prediction for streaming event logs, with far lower resource usage and more stable accuracy; a promotion-based ensemble reduces inference overhead compared to voting.
AI.motivation: Alleviate computational cost and instability of windowed neural models in live process mining; provide a lightweight, stable alternative with competitive accuracy.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on synthetic patterns and five real-world process mining datasets, comparing lightweight n-grams with neural architectures (LSTM, Transformer). Investigates classical ensemble methods (voting) and introduces a promotion algorithm that dynamically selects between two active models during inference.
AI.result: N-grams with appropriate context windows achieve comparable accuracy to neural models while using substantially fewer resources. Windowed neural models show unstable performance patterns. Voting improves n-gram performance but increases memory/latency; promotion algorithm reduces overhead and maintains accuracy. On real-world datasets, ensembles match or exceed non-windowed neural models at lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Classical n-gram approaches can be competitive with neural models for next-activity prediction in streaming logs, especially when paired with an efficient promotion-based ensemble that minimizes inference overhead while delivering stable, robust accuracy.

title: Local Neighborhood Instability in Parametric Projections: Quantitative and Visual Analysis
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, LaTeX, to appear at the 17th International EuroVis Workshop on Visual Analytics
summary: Parametric projections let analysts embed new points in real time, but input variations from measurement noise or data drift can produce unpredictable shifts in the 2D layout. Whether and where a projection is locally stable remains largely unexamined. In this paper, we present a stability evaluation framework that probes parametric projections with Gaussian perturbations around selected anchor points and assesses how neighborhoods deform in the 2D embedding. Our approach combines quantitative measures of mean displacement, bias, and nearest-anchor assignment error with per-anchor visualizations of displacement vectors, local PCA ellipsoids, and Voronoi misassignment for detailed inspection. We demonstrate the framework's effectiveness on UMAP- and t-SNE-based neural projectors of varying network sizes and study the effect of Jacobian regularization as a gradient-based robustness strategy. We apply our framework to the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets. The results show that our framework identifies unstable projection regions invisible to reconstruction error or neighborhood-preservation metrics.

AI.tldr: Introduces a stability evaluation framework for parametric projections using Gaussian perturbations around anchor points; reveals unstable regions not captured by reconstruction error or neighborhood metrics; validated on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST with UMAP/t-SNE neural projectors and Jacobian regularization.
AI.motivation: Local stability of parametric projections under input noise and data drift is underexplored; identifying unstable regions is crucial for reliable real-time embedding and downstream analyses.
AI.method: Perturb selected anchors with Gaussian noise; quantify mean displacement, bias, and nearest-anchor misassignment in the 2D embedding; provide per-anchor diagnostics (displacement vectors, local PCA ellipsoids, Voronoi misassignment); experiment with different network sizes; study Jacobian regularization as a robustness strategy; evaluate on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets.
AI.result: The framework identifies unstable projection regions that standard metrics (reconstruction error, neighborhood preservation) miss; Jacobian regularization impacts robustness; findings demonstrated on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST with neural-projector variants of UMAP/t-SNE.
AI.conclusion: Localized stability diagnostics complement conventional metrics and guide the design of more robust parametric projection systems; the approach enables targeted improvements to reduce sensitivity to input perturbations.

title: DCMorph: Face Morphing via Dual-Stream Cross-Attention Diffusion
summary: Advancing face morphing attack techniques is crucial to anticipate evolving threats and develop robust defensive mechanisms for identity verification systems. This work introduces DCMorph, a dual-stream diffusion-based morphing framework that simultaneously operates at both identity conditioning and latent space levels. Unlike image-level methods suffering from blending artifacts or GAN-based approaches with limited reconstruction fidelity, DCMorph leverages identity-conditioned latent diffusion models through two mechanisms: (1) decoupled cross-attention interpolation that injects identity-specific features from both source faces into the denoising process, enabling explicit dual-identity conditioning absent in existing diffusion-based methods, and (2) DDIM inversion with spherical interpolation between inverted latent representations from both source faces, providing geometrically consistent initial latent representation that preserves structural attributes. Vulnerability analyses across four state-of-the-art face recognition systems demonstrate that DCMorph achieves the highest attack success rates compared to existing methods at both operational thresholds, while remaining challenging to detect by current morphing attack detection solutions.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream diffusion-based face morphing framework (DCMorph) conditions identities at both source latent spaces to maximize realism and attack success, via decoupled cross-attention interpolation and DDIM inversion with latent spherical interpolation; outperforms baselines on attack efficacy and remains hard to detect by current morphing detectors.
AI.motivation: Face morphing poses risks to identity verification systems. Existing approaches (image-level blending, GAN-based methods) struggle with artifacts or limited reconstruction fidelity. A diffusion-based, dual-identity conditioning framework aims to improve morph realism and attack strength while challenging detectors.
AI.method: DCMorph employs dual-stream identity-conditioned latent diffusion with two mechanisms: (1) decoupled cross-attention interpolation that injects identity-specific features from both source faces into the denoising process, enabling explicit dual-identity conditioning; (2) DDIM inversion with spherical interpolation between inverted latent representations from the two sources, yielding a geometrically consistent initial latent that preserves structural attributes.
AI.result: Vulnerability analyses across four state-of-the-art face recognition systems show DCMorph achieves the highest attack success rates compared with existing methods at both operational thresholds, and remains challenging to detect by current morphing attack detectors.
AI.conclusion: The dual-stream diffusion-based design improves morph realism and attack effectiveness over prior methods, highlighting the need for stronger defenses in identity verification. Further work should assess generalizability, computational costs, and detection robustness against advanced diffusion-based morphing.

title: DualSplat: Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting via Pseudo-Mask Bootstrapping from Reconstruction Failures
comment: 10 pages,6 figures, accepted to Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Conference 2026
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves real-time photorealistic rendering, its performance degrades significantly when training images contain transient objects that violate multi-view consistency. Existing methods face a circular dependency: accurate transient detection requires a well-reconstructed static scene, while clean reconstruction itself depends on reliable transient masks. We address this challenge with DualSplat, a Failure-to-Prior framework that converts first-pass reconstruction failures into explicit priors for a second reconstruction stage. We observe that transients, which appear in only a subset of views, often manifest as incomplete fragments during conservative initial training. We exploit these failures to construct object-level pseudo-masks by combining photometric residuals, feature mismatches, and SAM2 instance boundaries. These pseudo-masks then guide a clean second-pass 3DGS optimization, while a lightweight MLP refines them online by gradually shifting from prior supervision to self-consistency. Experiments on RobustNeRF and NeRF On-the-go show that DualSplat outperforms existing baselines, demonstrating particularly clear advantages in transient-heavy scenes and transient regions.

AI.tldr: DualSplat converts first-pass reconstruction failures caused by transients into explicit priors for a second-pass 3DGS, improving transient handling without requiring perfect initial masks.
AI.motivation: Transient objects violate multi-view consistency and create a circular dependency: accurate masking requires a good static reconstruction, while clean reconstruction needs reliable transient masks.
AI.method: Run a conservative first-pass 3DGS to induce reconstruction failures; extract object-level pseudo-masks from photometric residuals, feature mismatches, and SAM2 instance boundaries; perform a second-pass 3DGS optimization guided by these pseudo-masks; employ a lightweight MLP to refine masks online, gradually shifting from priors to self-consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on RobustNeRF and NeRF On-the-go show DualSplat outperforms baselines, with especially clear advantages in scenes with many transients and in transient regions.
AI.conclusion: A practical failure-to-prior framework that leverages first-pass reconstruction gaps to stabilize and improve 3DGS in dynamic scenes; effectiveness hinges on quality of pseudo-masks (e.g., from SAM2) and their integration into the second-pass optimization.

title: Large-Scale Data Parallelization of Product Quantization and Inverted Indexing Using Dask
comment: To be published in the CSCE 2022 proceedings
summary: Large-scale Nearest Neighbor (NN) search, though widely utilized in the similarity search field, remains challenged by the computational limitations inherent in processing large scale data. In an effort to decrease the computational expense needed, Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search is often used in applications that do not require the exact similarity search, but instead can rely on an approximation. Product Quantization (PQ) is a memory-efficient ANN effective for clustering all sizes of datasets. Clustering large-scale, high dimensional data requires a heavy computational expense, in both memory-cost and execution time. This work focuses on a unique way to divide and conquer the large scale data in Python using PQ, Inverted Indexing and Dask, combining the results without compromising the accuracy and reducing computational requirements to the level required when using medium-scale data.

AI.tldr: A PQ-based ANN framework using Inverted Indexing and Dask to partition large-scale, high-dimensional data and merge results with preserved accuracy, achieving medium-scale-level computation.
AI.motivation: Address the computational and memory challenges of large-scale NN search and clustering in high dimensions; provide a scalable, memory-efficient approximate approach.
AI.method: Combine Product Quantization (PQ) with inverted indexing and Dask for distributed processing; partition data, perform PQ-based ANN on chunks, and fuse results to preserve accuracy while reducing compute.
AI.result: Demonstrates scalability and reduced computational cost to levels comparable with medium-scale data processing, while maintaining comparable accuracy through result combination.
AI.conclusion: A Python-based, divide-and-conquer PQ framework with inverted indexing and Dask can enable scalable, memory-efficient ANN for very large high-dimensional datasets without significant loss in accuracy.

title: Causal Disentanglement for Full-Reference Image Quality Assessment
summary: Existing deep network-based full-reference image quality assessment (FR-IQA) models typically work by performing pairwise comparisons of deep features from the reference and distorted images. In this paper, we approach this problem from a different perspective and propose a novel FR-IQA paradigm based on causal inference and decoupled representation learning. Unlike typical feature comparison-based FR-IQA models, our approach formulates degradation estimation as a causal disentanglement process guided by intervention on latent representations. We first decouple degradation and content representations by exploiting the content invariance between the reference and distorted images. Second, inspired by the human visual masking effect, we design a masking module to model the causal relationship between image content and degradation features, thereby extracting content-influenced degradation features from distorted images. Finally, quality scores are predicted from these degradation features using either supervised regression or label-free dimensionality reduction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves highly competitive performance on standard IQA benchmarks across fully supervised, few-label, and label-free settings. Furthermore, we evaluate the approach on diverse non-standard natural image domains with scarce data, including underwater, radiographic, medical, neutron, and screen-content images. Benefiting from its ability to perform scenario-specific training and prediction without labeled IQA data, our method exhibits superior cross-domain generalization compared to existing training-free FR-IQA models.

AI.tldr: A causal-inference FR-IQA framework with decoupled content/degradation representations and a masking module, achieving competitive results and strong cross-domain generalization in both supervised and label-free settings.
AI.motivation: Standard FR-IQA methods rely on pairwise deep feature comparisons and often struggle with cross-domain or data-scarce settings. There is a need to disentangle content and degradation and to model content-aware degradation via causal interventions.
AI.method: 1) Decouple degradation and content via content-invariance across reference and distorted images. 2) Use a masking module inspired by human visual masking to capture the causal relation between content and degradation, deriving content-influenced degradation features. 3) Predict quality scores from degradation features using supervised regression or label-free dimensionality reduction.
AI.result: Demonstrates highly competitive performance on standard IQA benchmarks in fully supervised, few-label, and label-free settings; shows strong cross-domain generalization on domains with scarce data (underwater, radiographic, medical, neutron, screen-content), outperforming existing training-free FR-IQA models in cross-domain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The causal-disentanglement approach provides robust, flexible FR-IQA with broad applicability across domains and data regimes, enabling scenario-specific training/prediction without labeled IQA data.

title: Dilated CNNs for Periodic Signal Processing: A Low-Complexity Approach
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures, the use of deep learning in IoT devices
summary: Denoising of periodic signals and accurate waveform estimation are core tasks across many signal processing domains, including speech, music, medical diagnostics, radio, and sonar. Although deep learning methods have recently shown performance improvements over classical approaches, they require substantial computational resources and are usually trained separately for each signal observation. This study proposes a computationally efficient method based on DCNN and Re-sampling, termed R-DCNN, designed for operation under strict power and resource constraints. The approach targets signals with varying fundamental frequencies and requires only a single observation for training. It generalizes to additional signals via a lightweight resampling step that aligns time scales in signals with different frequencies to re-use the same network weights. Despite its low computational complexity, R-DCNN achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical methods, such as autoregressive (AR)-based techniques, as well as conventional DCNNs trained individually for each observation. This combination of efficiency and performance makes the proposed method particularly well suited for deployment in resource-constrained environments without sacrificing denoising or estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: A light-weight R-DCNN approach for denoising/ waveform estimation of periodic signals across varying frequencies using one-shot training and a resampling step to reuse weights, achieving competitive performance with low resources.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational burden of deep learning for denoising across signals with changing fundamental frequencies; enable single-observation training and resource-constrained deployment while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: A deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) paired with a lightweight resampling step (R-DCNN). Train on a single observation; use resampling to align time scales across signals with different frequencies so the same network weights can be reused for different signals; low computational footprint.
AI.result: Performance on par with state-of-the-art classical autoregressive (AR) methods and conventional DCNNs trained per observation, despite reduced computation; generalizes to other signals via resampling.
AI.conclusion: R-DCNN offers an efficient denoising and waveform estimation solution suitable for resource-constrained environments, achieving accuracy comparable to heavier methods; extensible to additional signals through lightweight resampling, though relies on effective time-scale alignment.

title: Sapiens2
summary: We present Sapiens2, a model family of high-resolution transformers for human-centric vision focused on generalization, versatility, and high-fidelity outputs. Our model sizes range from 0.4 to 5 billion parameters, with native 1K resolution and hierarchical variants that support 4K. Sapiens2 substantially improves over its predecessor in both pretraining and post-training. First, to learn features that capture low-level details (for dense prediction) and high-level semantics (for zero-shot or few-label settings), we combine masked image reconstruction with self-distilled contrastive objectives. Our evaluations show that this unified pretraining objective is better suited for a wider range of downstream tasks. Second, along the data axis, we pretrain on a curated dataset of 1 billion high-quality human images and improve the quality and quantity of task annotations. Third, architecturally, we incorporate advances from frontier models that enable longer training schedules with improved stability. Our 4K models adopt windowed attention to reason over longer spatial context and are pretrained with 2K output resolution. Sapiens2 sets a new state-of-the-art and improves over the first generation on pose (+4 mAP), body-part segmentation (+24.3 mIoU), normal estimation (45.6% lower angular error) and extends to new tasks such as pointmap and albedo estimation. Code: https://github.com/facebookresearch/sapiens2

AI.tldr: Sapiens2 is a high-resolution transformer family for human-centric vision, unifying dense-prediction and semantic tasks through joint masked reconstruction and self-distilled contrastive pretraining, trained on 1B high-quality human images, with native 1K and 4K windowed-attention variants achieving state-of-the-art on pose, body-part segmentation, and normal estimation, and expanding to new tasks like pointmap and albedo estimation.
AI.motivation: To improve generalization across dense (pixel-level) and high-level (semantic) human-centric vision tasks, by learning features that capture both fine-grained details and semantic structure, and by scaling data, architecture, and training stability.
AI.method: A multi-object pretraining objective combining masked image reconstruction with self-distilled contrastive learning. Pretraining on a curated 1B-image dataset of high-quality human images. Architecturally, 4K models use windowed attention to expand receptive field; longer training schedules with improved stability; native 1K resolution with 4K hierarchical variants up to 2K output resolution at pretraining. Code provided: https://github.com/facebookresearch/sapiens2
AI.result: Sapiens2 sets new state-of-the-art across multiple human-centric tasks: pose (+4 mAP), body-part segmentation (+24.3 mIoU), normal estimation (45.6% lower angular error), and extends to new tasks such as pointmap and albedo estimation.
AI.conclusion: Sapiens2 demonstrates that a unified pretraining objective combined with large-scale, high-quality human-centric data and architecture innovations yields broad generalization and high-fidelity outputs for diverse vision tasks, establishing a new baseline for future work in human-centric vision.

title: Geometric Monomial (GEM): a family of rational 2N-differentiable activation functions
summary: The choice of activation function plays a crucial role in the optimization and performance of deep neural networks. While the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) remains the dominant choice due to its simplicity and effectiveness, its lack of smoothness may hinder gradient-based optimization in deep architectures. In this work we propose a family of $C^{2N}$-smooth activation functions whose gate follows a log-logistic CDF, achieving ReLU-like performance with purely rational arithmetic. We introduce three variants: GEM (the base family), E-GEM (an $ε$-parameterized generalization enabling arbitrary $L^p$-approximation of ReLU), and SE-GEM (a piecewise variant eliminating dead neurons with $C^{2N}$ junction smoothness). An $N$-ablation study establishes $N=1$ as optimal for standard-depth networks, reducing the GELU deficit on CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56 from 6.10% to 2.12%. The smoothness parameter $N$ further reveals a CNN-transformer tradeoff: $N=1$ is preferred for deep CNNs, while $N=2$ is preferred for transformers. On MNIST, E-GEM ties the best baseline (99.23%). On CIFAR-10 + ResNet-56, SE-GEM ($ε=10^{-4}$) surpasses GELU (92.51% vs 92.44%) -- the first GEM-family activation to outperform GELU. On CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56, E-GEM reduces the GELU deficit from 6.10% (GEM $N=2$) to just 0.62%. On GPT-2 (124M), GEM achieves the lowest perplexity (72.57 vs 73.76 for GELU), with GEM $N=1$ also beating GELU (73.32). On BERT-small, E-GEM ($ε=10$) achieves the best validation loss (6.656) across all activations. The $ε$-parameterization reveals a scale-dependent optimum: small $ε$ ($10^{-4}$--$10^{-6}$) for deep CNNs and larger transformers, with the special case of small transformers (BERT-small) benefiting from large $ε$ ($ε=10$) due to its limited depth and unconstrained gradients.

AI.tldr: GEM-family activations provide C^{2N}-smooth, log-logistic gated activations that emulate ReLU with rational arithmetic, delivering state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art results across CNNs, transformers, and language models; optimal smoothness N and epsilon depend on architecture.
AI.motivation: To overcome ReLU's non-smoothness while preserving ReLU-like performance, enabling better gradient flow and potentially more stable optimization in deep nets; also to explore a family of smooth activations with tunable smoothness and an ε-parameterization for flexible L^p approximations.
AI.method: Introduce GEM (base), E-GEM (ε-parameterized L^p-approximation of ReLU), and SE-GEM (piecewise, with C^{2N} junction smoothness). Gate activation uses a log-logistic CDF; N controls smoothness order (C^{2N}). Conduct extensive ablations (N) on standard CNNs and transformers, and evaluate on MNIST, CIFAR-10/100 with ResNet-56, GPT-2 (124M), BERT-small; vary ε to observe scale-dependent optima.
AI.result: N=1 generally optimal for standard-depth CNNs; deeper CNNs may favor N=1 while transformers benefit from N=2. On CIFAR-100+ResNet-56, E-GEM reduces GELU deficit from 6.10% (GEM N=2) to 0.62%. On CIFAR-10+ResNet-56, SE-GEM (ε=1e-4) slightly surpasses GELU (92.51% vs 92.44%). On CIFAR-100+ResNet-56, E-GEM more than halves the GELU gap. On MNIST, E-GEM matches top baseline 99.23%. On GPT-2 (124M), GEM achieves the lowest perplexity (72.57 vs 73.76 for GELU), with N=1 also beating GELU (73.32). On BERT-small, E-GEM (ε=10) achieves best validation loss (6.656) among activations. The ε-parameterization yields a scale-dependent optimum: small ε (1e-4 to 1e-6) for deep CNNs and larger ε for transformers; very large ε benefits small transformers due to limited depth and gradient freedom.
AI.conclusion: GEM-family activations can outperform GELU across tasks, with a tunable tradeoff between smoothness and approximation quality. Selecting N and ε based on architecture yields best performance; N=1 suffices for most CNNs, while N=2 can help transformers. The framework provides a practical, rational-arithmetic-friendly alternative to GELU with competitive or superior results.

title: WorldMark: A Unified Benchmark Suite for Interactive Video World Models
summary: Interactive video generation models such as Genie, YUME, HY-World, and Matrix-Game are advancing rapidly, yet every model is evaluated on its own benchmark with private scenes and trajectories, making fair cross-model comparison impossible. Existing public benchmarks offer useful metrics such as trajectory error, aesthetic scores, and VLM-based judgments, but none supplies the standardized test conditions -- identical scenes, identical action sequences, and a unified control interface -- needed to make those metrics comparable across models with heterogeneous inputs. We introduce WorldMark, the first benchmark that provides such a common playing field for interactive Image-to-Video world models. WorldMark contributes: (1) a unified action-mapping layer that translates a shared WASD-style action vocabulary into each model's native control format, enabling apples-to-apples comparison across six major models on identical scenes and trajectories; (2) a hierarchical test suite of 500 evaluation cases covering first- and third-person viewpoints, photorealistic and stylized scenes, and three difficulty tiers from Easy to Hard spanning 20-60s; and (3) a modular evaluation toolkit for Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency, designed so that researchers can reuse our standardized inputs while plugging in their own metrics as the field evolves. We will release all data, evaluation code, and model outputs to facilitate future research. Beyond offline metrics, we launch World Model Arena (warena.ai), an online platform where anyone can pit leading world models against each other in side-by-side battles and watch the live leaderboard.

AI.tldr: WorldMark provides standardized cross-model benchmarking for interactive Image-to-Video world models, featuring a unified action-mapping layer, a 500-case test suite, a modular evaluation toolkit, and an online World Model Arena for live comparisons.
AI.motivation: To overcome non-comparability of current benchmarks due to model-specific inputs and private test scenes, enabling apples-to-apples comparisons across diverse models.
AI.method: Introduce a unified action-mapping layer translating a shared WASD-style control vocabulary into each model's native interface; build a hierarchical 500-case test suite spanning first/third-person viewpoints, photorealistic and stylized scenes, and 3 difficulty levels (Easy–Hard) over 20–60s; provide modular evaluation toolkit (Visual Quality, Control Alignment, World Consistency); release data/code/model outputs; launch World Model Arena (warena.ai) for side-by-side benchmarking.
AI.result: A standardized, reusable benchmarking platform enabling fair cross-model comparisons and accelerating progress by providing identical scenes/trajectories and a common interface; public data and online arena.
AI.conclusion: WorldMark establishes a reproducible foundation for evaluating interactive video generation, promoting transparency and community-driven advancement in World Models.

title: Evaluating Post-hoc Explanations of the Transformer-based Genome Language Model DNABERT-2
comment: Accepted at the 4th World Conference on Explainable Artificial Intelligence, XAI-2026
summary: Explaining deep neural network predictions on genome sequences enables biological insight and hypothesis generation-often of greater interest than predictive performance alone. While explanations of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been shown to capture relevant patterns in genome sequences, it is unclear whether this transfers to more expressive Transformer-based genome language models (gLMs). To answer this question, we adapt AttnLRP, an extension of layer-wise relevance propagation to the attention mechanism, and apply it to the state-of-the-art gLM DNABERT-2. Thereby, we propose strategies to transfer explanations from token and nucleotide level. We evaluate the adaption of AttnLRP on genomic datasets using multiple metrics. Further, we provide an extensive comparison between the explanations of DNABERT-2 and a baseline CNN. Our results demonstrate that AttnLRP yields reliable explanations corresponding to known biological patterns. Hence, like CNNs, gLMs can also help derive biological insights. This work contributes to the explainability of gLMs and addresses the comparability of relevance attributions across different architectures.

AI.tldr: AttnLRP applied to DNABERT-2 provides reliable, biology-consistent explanations for genome language models, enabling transfer of interpretability strategies from CNNs to gLMs and enabling cross-architecture comparability.
AI.motivation: Explainability of deep learning models on genome sequences and assessment of whether Transformer-based gLMs convey interpretable patterns akin to CNNs; enabling biological insight beyond predictive performance.
AI.method: Adapt AttnLRP (layer-wise relevance propagation for attention) to DNABERT-2; develop token- and nucleotide-level explanation transfer strategies; evaluate on genomic datasets with multiple metrics; compare explanations to a CNN baseline.
AI.result: AttnLRP yields explanations aligned with known biological patterns; gLMs can provide insights comparable to CNNs; explanations transferable across token and nucleotide levels; supports improved explainability and cross-architecture comparability.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that Transformer-based gLM explanations are reliable and biologically meaningful, advancing gLM explainability and enabling fair cross-architecture interpretation.

title: Efficient Logic Gate Networks for Video Copy Detection
summary: Video copy detection requires robust similarity estimation under diverse visual distortions while operating at very large scale. Although deep neural networks achieve strong performance, their computational cost and descriptor size limit practical deployment in high-throughput systems. In this work, we propose a video copy detection framework based on differentiable Logic Gate Networks (LGNs), which replace conventional floating-point feature extractors with compact, logic-based representations. Our approach combines aggressive frame miniaturization, binary preprocessing, and a trainable LGN embedding model that learns both logical operations and interconnections. After training, the model can be discretized into a purely Boolean circuit, enabling extremely fast and memory-efficient inference. We systematically evaluate different similarity strategies, binarization schemes, and LGN architectures across multiple dataset folds and difficulty levels. Experimental results demonstrate that LGN-based models achieve competitive or superior accuracy and ranking performance compared to prior models, while producing descriptors several orders of magnitude smaller and delivering inference speeds exceeding 11k samples per second. These findings indicate that logic-based models offer a promising alternative for scalable and resource-efficient video copy detection.

AI.tldr: LGN-based video copy detection framework using differentiable logic gate networks
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost and descriptor size for large-scale video copy detection while maintaining robustness to distortions and high accuracy
AI.method: Combine aggressive frame miniaturization, binary preprocessing, and a trainable LGN embedding model that learns logical operations and interconnections; during/after training, discretize the model to a purely Boolean circuit for fast, memory-efficient inference
AI.result: LGN-based descriptors are orders of magnitude smaller than traditional features, achieve competitive or superior accuracy and ranking, and enable extremely fast inference (>11k samples per second) across multiple dataset folds/difficulties
AI.conclusion: Logic-based models offer a scalable, resource-efficient alternative for video copy detection with strong performance and very low inference cost

title: Towards Universal Tabular Embeddings: A Benchmark Across Data Tasks
summary: Tabular foundation models aim to learn universal representations of tabular data that transfer across tasks and domains, enabling applications such as table retrieval, semantic search and table-based prediction. Despite the growing number of such models, it remains unclear which approach works best in practice, as existing methods are often evaluated under task-specific settings that make direct comparison difficult. To address this, we introduce TEmBed, the Tabular Embedding Test Bed, a comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating tabular embeddings across four representation levels: cell, row, column, and table. Evaluating a diverse set of tabular representation learning models, we show that which model to use depends on the task and representation level. Our results offer practical guidance for selecting tabular embeddings in real-world applications and lay the groundwork for developing more general-purpose tabular representation models.

AI.tldr: Introduces TEmBed, a four-level benchmark for tabular embeddings (cell, row, column, table) to enable fair cross-model comparison, showing that model choice is task- and representation-level dependent and providing guidance for real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: There is no unified benchmark for tabular embeddings that allows fair cross-model comparison across tasks and representation levels; current evaluations are task-specific and hinder generalization.
AI.method: Define TEmBed; compile tasks for each representation level; evaluate a diverse set of tabular representation learning models across retrieval, semantic search, and table-based prediction; analyze results to derive guidance.
AI.result: Model performance depends on representation level and task; no single model excels universally; the benchmark yields practical recommendations and sets groundwork for more general tabular representations.
AI.conclusion: TEmBed provides a systematic, extensible evaluation framework that supports fair comparison and accelerates development of universal tabular embeddings.

title: Discriminative-Generative Synergy for Occlusion Robust 3D Human Mesh Recovery
summary: 3D human mesh recovery from monocular RGB images aims to estimate anatomically plausible 3D human models for downstream applications, but remains challenging under partial or severe occlusions. Regression-based methods are efficient yet often produce implausible or inaccurate results in unconstrained scenarios, while diffusion-based methods provide strong generative priors for occluded regions but may weaken fidelity to rare poses due to over-reliance on generation. To address these limitations, we propose a brain-inspired synergistic framework that integrates the discriminative power of vision transformers with the generative capability of conditional diffusion models. Specifically, the ViT-based pathway extracts deterministic visual cues from visible regions, while the diffusion-based pathway synthesizes structurally coherent human body representations. To effectively bridge the two pathways, we design a diverse-consistent feature learning module to align discriminative features with generative priors, and a cross-attention multi-level fusion mechanism to enable bidirectional interaction across semantic levels. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on key metrics and shows strong robustness in complex real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: A brain-inspired framework combines ViT discriminative cues with diffusion-based generative priors for robust 3D human mesh recovery under occlusion, using diverse-consistent feature learning and cross-attention fusion.
AI.motivation: Under partial or severe occlusions, regression methods are efficient but produce implausible/ inaccurate results; diffusion priors help occluded regions but may reduce fidelity for rare poses. A hybrid approach aims to capitalize on strengths of both.
AI.method: Two-pathway architecture: a ViT-based pathway extracts deterministic visual cues from visible regions; a diffusion-based pathway synthesizes structurally coherent body representations. Bridges include a diverse-consistent feature learning module to align discriminative features with generative priors and a cross-attention multi-level fusion mechanism for bidirectional interaction across semantic levels.
AI.result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show superior performance on key metrics and strong robustness in complex real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed brain-inspired synergistic framework improves 3D mesh recovery by integrating discriminative and generative priors with cross-pathway interaction, outperforming baselines.

title: Fairness under uncertainty in sequential decisions
comment: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 2026
summary:
Fair machine learning (ML) methods help identify and mitigate the risk that algorithms encode or automate social injustices. Algorithmic approaches alone cannot resolve structural inequalities, but they can support socio-technical decision systems by surfacing discriminatory biases, clarifying trade-offs, and enabling governance. Although fairness is well studied in supervised learning, many real ML applications are online and sequential, with prior decisions informing future ones. Each decision is taken under uncertainty due to unobserved counterfactuals and finite samples, with dire consequences for under-represented groups, systematically under-observed due to historical exclusion and selective feedback. A bank cannot know whether a denied loan would have been repaid, and may have less data on marginalized populations.
  This paper introduces a taxonomy of uncertainty in sequential decision-making -- model, feedback, and prediction uncertainty -- providing shared vocabulary for assessing systems where uncertainty is unevenly distributed across groups. We formalize model and feedback uncertainty via counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning, and illustrate harms to decision makers (unrealized gains/losses) and subjects (compounding exclusion, reduced access) of policies that ignore the unobserved space. Algorithmic examples show it is possible to reduce outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while preserving institutional objectives (e.g. expected utility). Experiments on data simulated with varying bias show how unequal uncertainty and selective feedback produce disparities, and how uncertainty-aware exploration alters fairness metrics. The framework equips practitioners to diagnose, audit, and govern fairness risks. Where uncertainty drives unfairness rather than incidental noise, accounting for it is essential to fair and effective decision-making.

AI.tldr: A taxonomy of uncertainty in sequential decision-making is introduced to study fair ML, formalizing model and feedback uncertainty via counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning; it shows harms of ignoring unobserved spaces, demonstrates possible variance reduction for disadvantaged groups under utility constraints, and provides a framework for diagnosing, auditing, and governing fairness risks in online decision systems.
AI.motivation: Many ML deployments are online and sequential, where decisions affect future data and outcomes; fairness research has focused on static settings, but uncertainty is unevenly distributed across groups due to unobserved counterfactuals and historical exclusion. A principled treatment of model, feedback, and prediction uncertainty is needed to prevent compounding injustice and to guide governance.
AI.method: Propose a taxonomy of three uncertainties (model, feedback, prediction) in sequential decision-making. Formalize model and feedback uncertainty using counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning. Provide algorithmic examples demonstrating how uncertainty-aware exploration can reduce outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while preserving institutional objectives. Validate with experiments on data simulated with varying bias to show how unequal uncertainty and selective feedback create disparities and how accounting for uncertainty aids diagnosis and governance.
AI.result: Unequal uncertainty and selective feedback generate disparities in outcomes; uncertainty-aware exploration can alter fairness metrics and reduce variance for disadvantaged groups without sacrificing expected utility. The framework enables practitioners to diagnose, audit, and govern fairness risks in sequential decisions where uncertainty is heterogeneously distributed.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for uncertainty in sequential decision-making is essential for fair and effective decision systems. The proposed taxonomy, formalization, and algorithms equip practitioners to diagnose, audit, and govern fairness risks, particularly when unobserved counterfactuals and limited data drive unequal outcomes.

title: Unlocking the Power of Critical Factors for 3D Visual Geometry Estimation
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. GitHub Page: https://github.com/aim-uofa/CARVE
summary: Feed-forward visual geometry estimation has recently made rapid progress. However, an important gap remains: multi-frame models usually produce better cross-frame consistency, yet they often underperform strong per-frame methods on single-frame accuracy. This observation motivates our systematic investigation into the critical factors driving model performance through rigorous ablation studies, which reveals several key insights: 1) Scaling up data diversity and quality unlocks further performance gains even in state-of-the-art visual geometry estimation methods; 2) Commonly adopted confidence-aware loss and gradient-based loss mechanisms may unintentionally hinder performance; 3) Joint supervision through both per-sequence and per-frame alignment improves results, while local region alignment surprisingly degrades performance. Furthermore, we introduce two enhancements to integrate the advantages of optimization-based methods and high-resolution inputs: a consistency loss function that enforces alignment between depth maps, camera parameters, and point maps, and an efficient architectural design that leverages high-resolution information. We integrate these designs into CARVE, a resolution-enhanced model for feed-forward visual geometry estimation. Experiments on point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsic estimation show that CARVE achieves strong and robust performance across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CARVE: a resolution-enhanced, feed-forward visual geometry estimator that shows data diversity, supervision strategy, and loss choices crucial for performance; integrates a consistency loss and high-res design to outperform baselines across tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where multi-frame models offer cross-frame consistency but often underperform single-frame methods in per-frame accuracy, through systematic ablations and architectural enhancements.
AI.method: Rigorous ablation studies on data, losses, and supervision; introduce CARVE with a consistency loss that aligns depth maps, camera parameters, and point maps; design an efficient high-resolution architecture; joint per-sequence and per-frame supervision; evaluation on point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsic estimation.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Increasing data diversity/quality yields further gains even for SOTA methods; (2) Confidence-aware and gradient-based losses may hinder performance; (3) Joint per-sequence and per-frame supervision helps, while local region alignment degrades; CARVE with consistency loss and high-res design achieves strong, robust performance across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CARVE demonstrates effective integration of optimization-based advantages with high-resolution inputs, highlighting data diversity and supervision strategies as critical drivers, and offering a robust, scalable approach for feed-forward visual geometry estimation across tasks.

title: Transferable Physics-Informed Representations via Closed-Form Head Adaptation
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have garnered significant interest for their potential in solving partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern a wide range of physical phenomena. By incorporating physical laws into the learning process, PINN models have demonstrated the ability to learn physical outcomes reasonably well. However, current PINN approaches struggle to predict or solve new PDEs effectively when there is a lack of training examples, indicating they do not generalize well to unseen problem instances. In this paper, we present a transferable learning approach for PINNs premised on a fast Pseudoinverse PINN framework (Pi-PINN). Pi-PINN learns a transferable physics-informed representation in a shared embedding space and enables rapid solving of both known and unknown PDE instances via closed-form head adaptation using a least-squares-optimal pseudoinverse under PDE constraints. We further investigate the synergies between data-driven multi-task learning loss and physics-informed loss, providing insights into the design of more performant PINNs. We demonstrate the effectiveness of Pi-PINN on various PDE problems, including Poisson's equation, Helmholtz equation, and Burgers' equation, achieving fast and accurate physics-informed solutions without requiring any data for unseen instances. Pi-PINN can produce predictions 100-1000 times faster than a typical PINN, while producing predictions with 10-100 times lower relative error than a typical data-driven model even with only two training samples. Overall, our findings highlight the potential of transferable representations with closed-form head adaptation to enhance the efficiency and generalization of PINNs across PDE families and scientific and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: Pi-PINN: a transferable physics-informed representation with closed-form head adaptation via least-squares pseudoinverse enables rapid solving of unseen PDE instances across PDE families, outperforming typical PINNs and data-driven models in speed and accuracy with minimal (two) training samples.
AI.motivation: Address poor generalization of PINNs to unseen PDE instances and data scarcity by learning a shared, transferable physics-informed embedding and using a closed-form pseudoinverse head for fast adaptation to new PDEs.
AI.method: Train a Pi-PINN that learns a shared physics-informed representation in an embedding space. For new PDE instances, solve with a closed-form head adaptation using a least-squares optimal pseudoinverse under PDE constraints. Investigate synergy between data-driven multi-task loss and physics-informed loss.
AI.result: Pi-PINN achieves 100–1000x faster predictions than typical PINNs and 10–100x lower relative error than typical data-driven models, even with only two training samples, on Poisson, Helmholtz, and Burgers' equations; no data required for unseen PDE instances.
AI.conclusion: Transferable representations with closed-form head adaptation can enhance the efficiency and generalization of PINNs across PDE families, enabling rapid, accurate physics-informed solutions for known and unknown PDEs.

title: Building a Precise Video Language with Human-AI Oversight
summary: Video-language models (VLMs) learn to reason about the dynamic visual world through natural language. We introduce a suite of open datasets, benchmarks, and recipes for scalable oversight that enable precise video captioning.
