First, we define a structured specification for describing subjects, scenes, motion, spatial, and camera dynamics, grounded by hundreds of carefully defined visual primitives developed with professional video creators such as filmmakers. Next, to curate high-quality captions, we introduce CHAI (Critique-based Human-AI Oversight), a framework where trained experts critique and revise model-generated pre-captions into improved post-captions. This division of labor improves annotation accuracy and efficiency by offloading text generation to models, allowing humans to better focus on verification. Additionally, these critiques and preferences between pre- and post-captions provide rich supervision for improving open-source models (Qwen3-VL) on caption generation, reward modeling, and critique generation through SFT, DPO, and inference-time scaling. Our ablations show that critique quality in precision, recall, and constructiveness, ensured by our oversight framework, directly governs downstream performance. With modest expert supervision, the resulting model outperforms closed-source models such as Gemini-3.1-Pro. Finally, we apply our approach to re-caption large-scale professional videos (e.g., films, commercials, games) and fine-tune video generation models such as Wan to better follow detailed prompts of up to 400 words, achieving finer control over cinematography including camera motion, angle, lens, focus, point of view, and framing. Our results show that precise specification and human-AI oversight are key to professional-level video understanding and generation. Data and code are available on our project page: https://linzhiqiu.github.io/papers/chai/

AI.tldr: Defines a structured, primitive-based specification for video subjects, scenes, motion, and dynamics; introduces CHAI, a critique-based human-AI oversight framework to produce high-quality captions; demonstrates improvements in open-model captioning (Qwen3-VL) via expert critiques (SFT, DPO) and outperforming some closed-source baselines; applies to re-captioning professional videos and improving video generation control; emphasizes that precise specification plus oversight boosts professional-level video understanding and generation.
AI.motivation: Video-language models require scalable, precise oversight to produce high-quality, professional, captioned descriptions of dynamic scenes. The work targets improved annotation quality, efficiency, and alignment of open models with professional standards for complex video content.
AI.method: 1) Develop a structured specification with hundreds of visual primitives for subjects, scenes, motion, spatial/camera dynamics, grounded by expert-driven definitions from filmmakers. 2) CHAI framework: experts critique model-generated pre-captions and revise into post-captions, enabling better annotation accuracy and efficiency. 3) Use critiques and pre/post caption preferences as supervision to improve open-source models (Qwen3-VL) via supervised fine-tuning (SFT), direct policy optimization (DPO), and inference-time scaling. 4) Conduct ablations on critique quality (precision, recall, constructiveness) and show impact on downstream tasks. 5) Apply approach to re-caption large-scale professional videos and fine-tune video generation models (Wan) for detailed prompts (up to 400 words) to control cinematography.
AI.result: CHAI’s structured specification plus critique-based oversight yields higher-quality captions; improved precision/recall/constructiveness aligns with downstream performance; open-models (Qwen3-VL) surpass some closed-model baselines (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro) in captioning; enables fine-grained control in video generation; data and code released on project page.
AI.conclusion: Precise, structured visual primitives combined with human-AI critique loops are essential for professional-level video understanding and generation, enabling scalable, accurate captioning and controllable video synthesis; the approach demonstrates the value of expert oversight in open-model development.

title: PrismaDV: Automated Task-Aware Data Unit Test Generation
summary:
Data is a central resource for modern enterprises, and data validation is essential for ensuring the reliability of downstream applications. However, existing automated data unit testing frameworks are largely task-agnostic: they validate datasets without considering the semantics and requirements of the code that consumes the data.
  We present PrismaDV, a compound AI system that analyzes downstream task code together with dataset profiles to identify data access patterns, infer implicit data assumptions, and generate task-aware executable data unit tests. To further adapt the data unit tests over time to specific datasets and downstream tasks, we propose "Selective Informative Feedback for Task Adaptation" (SIFTA), a prompt-optimization framework that leverages the scarce outcomes from the execution of data unit tests and downstream tasks. We evaluate PrismaDV on two new benchmarks spanning 60 tasks across five datasets, where it consistently outperforms both task-agnostic and task-aware baselines in generating unit tests that reflect the end-to-end impact of data errors. Furthermore, we show that with SIFTA, we can automatically learn prompts for PrismaDV's modules that outperform prompts written by hand or generated from a generic prompt optimizer. We publicly release our benchmarks and prototype implementation.

AI.tldr: PrismaDV introduces task-aware data unit testing by coupling downstream task code with dataset profiles to generate executable tests that reflect end-to-end data errors; complemented by SIFTA, a prompt-optimization framework that uses scarce feedback to adapt prompts over time. Evaluated on 60 tasks across five datasets, it outperforms baselines; benchmarks and prototype released.
AI.motivation: Data validation often ignores the semantics of the downstream code consuming the data, leading to tests that miss real-world failure modes. There is a need for task-aware data unit tests that capture end-to-end data impact.
AI.method: PrismaDV analyzes downstream task code and dataset profiles to identify data access patterns and implicit data assumptions, generating executable, task-aware data unit tests. SIFTA optimizes prompts based on scarce feedback from test executions and downstream task outcomes to adapt prompts over time.
AI.result: Consistent outperformance of both task-agnostic and task-aware baselines in generating unit tests aligned with end-to-end data error impact across 60 tasks and five datasets. SIFTA learns prompts that outperform hand-written prompts and generic prompt optimizers. Benchmarks and a prototype implementation are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware data unit testing coupled with prompt-optimization for adaptation can better align validation tests with downstream data usage, improving reliability and enabling continual improvement through SIFTA.

title: Ramen: Robust Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models with Active Sample Selection
summary: Pretrained vision-language models such as CLIP exhibit strong zero-shot generalization but remain sensitive to distribution shifts. Test-time adaptation adapts models during inference without access to source data or target labels, offering a practical way to handle such shifts. However, existing methods typically assume that test samples come from a single, consistent domain, while in practice, test data often include samples from mixed domains with distinct characteristics. Consequently, their performance degrades under mixed-domain settings. To address this, we present Ramen, a framework for robust test-time adaptation through active sample selection. For each incoming test sample, Ramen retrieves a customized batch of relevant samples from previously seen data based on two criteria: domain consistency, which ensures that adaptation focuses on data from similar domains, and prediction balance, which mitigates adaptation bias caused by skewed predictions. To improve efficiency, Ramen employs an embedding-gradient cache that stores the embeddings and sample-level gradients of past test images. The stored embeddings are used to retrieve relevant samples, and the corresponding gradients are aggregated for model updates, eliminating the need for any additional forward or backward passes. Our theoretical analysis provides insight into why the proposed adaptation mechanism is effective under mixed-domain shifts. Experiments on multiple image corruption and domain-shift benchmarks demonstrate that Ramen achieves strong and consistent performance, offering robust and efficient adaptation in complex mixed-domain scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/baowenxuan/Ramen .

AI.tldr: Ramen: a robust test-time adaptation framework for mixed-domain shifts in pretrained vision–language models, using active sample selection and an embedding–gradient cache to enable efficient, label-free updates.
AI.motivation: Address distribution shifts at test time, especially when test data come from mixed domains. Existing test-time adaptation methods assume a single domain and can degrade under mixed-domain shifts; aim to reduce adaptation bias and improve robustness.
AI.method: For each test sample, retrieve a customized batch of past samples based on (1) domain consistency to align adaptation with similar domains and (2) prediction balance to mitigate bias from skewed predictions. Use an embedding–gradient cache that stores embeddings and sample-level gradients of past test images; use stored embeddings for retrieval and aggregate corresponding gradients to update the model, avoiding extra forward/backward passes.
AI.result: Empirical results on image corruption and domain-shift benchmarks show strong and consistent performance under mixed-domain settings; efficient due to avoiding additional forward/backward passes; code released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Ramen provides robust, efficient test-time adaptation in mixed-domain scenarios by combining targeted sample retrieval with gradient aggregation, supported by theoretical insights and strong empirical results.

title: An effective variant of the Hartigan $k$-means algorithm
summary: The k-means problem is perhaps the classical clustering problem and often synonymous with Lloyd's algorithm (1957). It has become clear that Hartigan's algorithm (1975) gives better results in almost all cases, Telgarsky-Vattani note a typical improvement of $5\%$ -- $10\%$. We point out that a very minor variation of Hartigan's method leads to another $2\%$ -- $5\%$ improvement; the improvement tends to become larger when either dimension or $k$ increase.

AI.tldr: Minor variant of Hartigan's k-means yields an additional 2-5% improvement over standard Hartigan, with larger gains as dimension or k increase.
AI.motivation: To push clustering quality beyond existing Hartigan improvements and quantify how a small modification performs across dimensionality and the number of clusters (k).
AI.method: Apply a very minor variation to Hartigan's algorithm (details not provided in the abstract) and compare its performance to Lloyd's and standard Hartigan's method, reporting percentage improvements.
AI.result: Hartigan's method typically improves over Lloyd's algorithm by about 5-10% (Telgarsky-Vattani). The minor variant adds an extra 2-5% improvement, with larger gains when either the data dimension or k increases.
AI.conclusion: A small modification to Hartigan's algorithm can yield additional clustering accuracy gains beyond the standard Hartigan improvement, particularly in higher-dimensional or higher‑k settings; this warrants practical consideration for improved clustering performance.

title: Interpretable facial dynamics as behavioral and perceptual traces of deepfakes
comment: Main paper: 19 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. SI Appendix: 11 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables
summary: Deepfake detection research has largely converged on deep learning approaches that, despite strong benchmark performance, offer limited insight into what distinguishes real from manipulated facial behavior. This study presents an interpretable alternative grounded in bio-behavioral features of facial dynamics and evaluates how computational detection strategies relate to human perceptual judgments. We identify core low-dimensional patterns of facial movement, from which temporal features characterizing spatiotemporal structure were derived. Traditional machine learning classifiers trained on these features achieved modest but significant above-chance deepfake classification, driven by higher-order temporal irregularities that were more pronounced in manipulated than real facial dynamics. Notably, detection was substantially more accurate for videos containing emotive expressions than those without. An emotional valence classification analysis further indicated that emotive signals are systematically degraded in deepfakes, explaining the differential impact of emotive dynamics on detection. Furthermore, we provide an additional and often overlooked dimension of explainability by assessing the relationship between model decisions and human perceptual detection. Model and human judgments converged for emotive but diverged for non-emotive videos, and even where outputs aligned, underlying detection strategies differed. These findings demonstrate that face-swapped deepfakes carry a measurable behavioral fingerprint, most salient during emotional expression. Additionally, model-human comparisons suggest that interpretable computational features and human perception may offer complementary rather than redundant routes to detection.

AI.tldr: Interpretable bio-behavioral facial dynamics reveal detectable patterns in deepfakes, especially with emotion; model–human judgments align for emotive content but diverge for non-emotive; interpretable features offer complementary explainability to detection.
AI.motivation: To move beyond opaque DL detectors by grounding detection in interpretable facial dynamics and to examine how algorithmic decisions relate to human perceptual judgments.
AI.method: Identify core low-dimensional facial movement patterns, derive temporal spatiotemporal features, train traditional ML classifiers on these features, assess performance across emotive vs non-emotive videos, perform emotional valence classification, and compare model decisions with human perceptual judgments.
AI.result: Detectors using the interpretable features achieve above-chance deepfake classification; higher-order temporal irregularities are more pronounced in manipulated faces; detection is markedly more accurate for emotive expressions; emotive signals are degraded in deepfakes; model–human judgments converge for emotive videos but diverge for non-emotive ones; relatively independent, supplementary explainability from computational features and human perception.
AI.conclusion: Face-swapped deepfakes exhibit a measurable behavioral fingerprint, especially during emotional expression; interpretable computational features and human perception provide complementary routes to detection and explainability.

title: Quotient-Space Diffusion Models
comment: ICLR 2026 Oral Presentation; 40 pages, 5 figures, 6 tables
summary: Diffusion-based generative models have reformed generative AI, and have enabled new capabilities in the science domain, for example, generating 3D structures of molecules. Due to the intrinsic problem structure of certain tasks, there is often a symmetry in the system, which identifies objects that can be converted by a group action as equivalent, hence the target distribution is essentially defined on the quotient space with respect to the group. In this work, we establish a formal framework for diffusion modeling on a general quotient space, and apply it to molecular structure generation which follows the special Euclidean group $\text{SE}(3)$ symmetry. The framework reduces the necessity of learning the component corresponding to the group action, hence simplifies learning difficulty over conventional group-equivariant diffusion models, and the sampler guarantees recovering the target distribution, while heuristic alignment strategies lack proper samplers. The arguments are empirically validated on structure generation for small molecules and proteins, indicating that the principled quotient-space diffusion model provides a new framework that outperforms previous symmetry treatments.

AI.tldr: Diffusion on quotient spaces for SE(3) symmetry in molecular structure generation; reduces learning of group-action components, provides guaranteed samplers, and outperforms prior symmetry treatments.
AI.motivation: Symmetry induces equivalence classes; learning on the full space is inefficient due to redundant group-action components. A quotient-space formulation targets the true distribution and enables principled sampling.
AI.method: Develop a formal diffusion framework on general quotient spaces and instantiate it for SE(3) symmetry in molecular generation; factor out the group action, derive a sampler with convergence guarantees, and compare against heuristic alignment approaches.
AI.result: Empirical validation on small molecules and proteins demonstrates that quotient-space diffusion outperforms previous symmetry-handling methods, indicating practical benefits of the principled approach.
AI.conclusion: A principled quotient-space diffusion framework for symmetry-aware generative modeling; reduces learning difficulty and improves performance, with potential applicability beyond SE(3) to other group symmetries.

title: Back to Source: Open-Set Continual Test-Time Adaptation via Domain Compensation
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to mitigate distributional shifts between training and test domains during inference time. However, existing TTA methods fall short in the realistic scenario where models face both continually changing domains and the simultaneous emergence of unknown semantic classes, a challenging setting we term Open-set Continual Test-Time Adaptation (OCTTA). The coupling of domain and semantic shifts often collapses the feature space, severely degrading both classification and out-of-distribution detection. To tackle this, we propose DOmain COmpensation (DOCO), a lightweight and effective framework that robustly performs domain adaptation and OOD detection in a synergistic, closed loop. DOCO first performs dynamic, adaptation-conditioned sample splitting to separate likely ID from OOD samples. Then, using only the ID samples, it learns a domain compensation prompt by aligning feature statistics with the source domain, guided by a structural preservation regularizer that prevents semantic distortion. This learned prompt is then propagated to the OOD samples within the same batch, effectively isolating their semantic novelty for more reliable detection. Extensive experiments on multiple challenging benchmarks demonstrate that DOCO outperforms prior CTTA and OSTTA methods, establishing a new state-of-the-art for the demanding OCTTA setting.

AI.tldr: DOCO offers a lightweight OCTTA framework using adaptation-conditioned sample splitting and a domain-compensation prompt learned from in-domain samples to align feature statistics while preserving semantics, enabling improved CTTA/OSTTA performance under open-set continual shifts.
AI.motivation: In OCTTA, simultaneous distributional shifts and emergence of unknown semantic classes degrade the feature space, hurting both classification and OOD detection; existing TTA methods struggle with joint domain and open-set changes.
AI.method: DOCO uses dynamic, adaptation-conditioned sample splitting to separate likely ID from OOD samples. It learns a domain compensation prompt from ID samples by aligning feature statistics with the source domain, guided by a structural preservation regularizer to prevent semantic distortion. The learned prompt is then propagated to OOD samples within the batch to isolate their semantic novelty for detection.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple challenging benchmarks show that DOCO outperforms prior CTTA and OSTTA methods, establishing a new state-of-the-art for OCTTA.
AI.conclusion: DOCO provides a synergistic, closed-loop framework for robust domain adaptation and OOD detection in OCTTA with a lightweight approach.

title: Probably Approximately Consensus: On the Learning Theory of Finding Common Ground
comment: Accepted to the Social Choice and Learning Algorithms Workshop at IJCAI 2025
summary: A primary goal of online deliberation platforms is to identify ideas that are broadly agreeable to a community of users through their expressed preferences. Yet, consensus elicitation should ideally extend beyond the specific statements provided by users and should incorporate the relative salience of particular topics. We address this issue by modelling consensus as an interval in a one-dimensional opinion space derived from potentially high-dimensional data via embedding and dimensionality reduction. We define an objective that maximizes expected agreement within a hypothesis interval where the expectation is over an underlying distribution of issues, implicitly taking into account their salience. We propose an efficient Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) algorithm and establish PAC-learning guarantees. Our initial experiments demonstrate the performance of our algorithm and examine more efficient approaches to identifying optimal consensus regions. We find that through selectively querying users on an existing sample of statements, we can reduce the number of queries needed to a practical number.

AI.tldr: Models consensus as a 1D interval in an embedded opinion space, optimizing for expected agreement with PAC guarantees; introduces an efficient ERM algorithm and shows selective querying reduces required data.
AI.motivation: To capture consensus that reflects topic salience, not just explicit statements, by embedding high-dimensional issues and modeling agreement over a hypothesis interval with salience-aware expectations.
AI.method: Embed and reduce dimensionality to a 1D opinion space; define a hypothesis interval objective that maximizes expected agreement under the distribution of issues; develop an efficient Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) algorithm with PAC guarantees; conduct experiments and explore efficient strategies for identifying optimal consensus regions; show that selective querying on an existing sample reduces required queries.
AI.result: The approach yields promising performance in experiments; efficient strategies for locating optimal consensus intervals; selective querying reduces the number of queries to a practical level.
AI.conclusion: Consensus can be effectively elicited by modeling it as salience-aware intervals in an embedded opinion space, with an ERM-based algorithm providing PAC guarantees and scalable querying strategies for online deliberation platforms.

title: Reshoot-Anything: A Self-Supervised Model for In-the-Wild Video Reshooting
summary: Precise camera control for reshooting dynamic videos is bottlenecked by the severe scarcity of paired multi-view data for non-rigid scenes. We overcome this limitation with a highly scalable self-supervised framework capable of leveraging internet-scale monocular videos. Our core contribution is the generation of pseudo multi-view training triplets, consisting of a source video, a geometric anchor, and a target video. We achieve this by extracting distinct smooth random-walk crop trajectories from a single input video to serve as the source and target views. The anchor is synthetically generated by forward-warping the first frame of the source with a dense tracking field, which effectively simulates the distorted point-cloud inputs expected at inference. Because our independent cropping strategy introduces spatial misalignment and artificial occlusions, the model cannot simply copy information from the current source frame. Instead, it is forced to implicitly learn 4D spatiotemporal structures by actively routing and re-projecting missing high-fidelity textures across distinct times and viewpoints from the source video to reconstruct the target. At inference, our minimally adapted diffusion transformer utilizes a 4D point-cloud derived anchor to achieve state-of-the-art temporal consistency, robust camera control, and high-fidelity novel view synthesis on complex dynamic scenes.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised framework for dynamic video reshooting that builds pseudo multi-view triplets from a single monocular video using random-walk crops; a synthetic 4D anchor from forward-warped frames, forcing the model to learn 4D spatiotemporal structure; inference uses a diffusion transformer with a 4D point-cloud anchor to deliver state-of-the-art temporal consistency and novel view synthesis.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of paired multi-view data for non-rigid scenes hinders precise camera control in reshooting dynamic videos. A scalable, self-supervised approach is needed to leverage vast internet-scale monocular videos.
AI.method: Generate pseudo multi-view triplets (source, anchor, target) by extracting distinct smooth random-walk crop trajectories from a single video. Create the anchor by forward-warping the first source frame using a dense tracking field to simulate distorted inputs. Force the model to infer high-fidelity textures across times/views due to induced spatial misalignment and occlusions. Train a diffusion transformer with a 4D point-cloud derived anchor; at inference, adapt minimally for state-of-the-art results.
AI.result: Demonstrates a scalable self-supervised pipeline that achieves strong temporal consistency, robust camera control, and high-fidelity novel view synthesis on complex dynamic scenes, using internet-scale monocular data.
AI.conclusion: Shows that pseudo multi-view training on monocular videos can unlock effective 4D spatiotemporal reasoning for camera control and novel view synthesis, with a diffusion-transformer backbone leveraged by a 4D point-cloud anchor.

title: GFlowState: Visualizing the Training of Generative Flow Networks Beyond the Reward
summary: We present GFlowState, a visual analytics system designed to illuminate the training process of Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets or GFNs). GFlowNets are a probabilistic framework for generating samples proportionally to a reward function. While GFlowNets have proved to be powerful tools in applications such as molecule and material discovery, their training dynamics remain difficult to interpret. Standard machine learning tools allow metric tracking but do not reveal how models explore the sample space, construct sample trajectories, or shift sampling probabilities during training. Our solution, GFlowState, allows users to analyze sampling trajectories, compare the sample space relative to reference datasets, and analyze the training dynamics. To this end, we introduce multiple views, including a chart of candidate rankings, a state projection, a node-link diagram of the trajectory network, and a transition heatmap. These visualizations enable GFlowNet developers and users to investigate sampling behavior and policy evolution, and to identify underexplored regions and sources of training failure. Case studies demonstrate how the system supports debugging and assessing the quality of GFlowNets across application domains. By making the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable, our work enhances their interpretability and can accelerate GFlowNet development in practice.

AI.tldr: GFlowState is a visual analytics system that makes the training dynamics of Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) interpretable by visualizing sampling trajectories, policy evolution, and space exploration to aid debugging and development.
AI.motivation: GFlowNets can generate samples proportional to a reward but their training dynamics are opaque; existing metrics do not reveal trajectory construction or state-space exploration, hindering interpretability and debugging.
AI.method: Develop GFlowState with multiple visual views: candidate rankings chart, state projection, trajectory network node-link diagram, and transition heatmap to analyze sampling behavior, policy evolution, and training failures.
AI.result: Case studies demonstrate debugging support and quality assessment of GFlowNets across application domains, showing enhanced interpretability and potential acceleration of development.
AI.conclusion: Making the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable improves interpretability and can accelerate practical development and debugging of GFlowNets.

title: From Codebooks to VLMs: Evaluating Automated Visual Discourse Analysis for Climate Change on Social Media
summary: Social media platforms have become primary arenas for climate communication, generating millions of images and posts that - if systematically analysed - can reveal which communication strategies mobilise public concern and which fall flat. We aim to facilitate such research by analysing how computer vision methods can be used for social media discourse analysis. This analysis includes application-based taxonomy design, model selection, prompt engineering, and validation. We benchmark six promptable vision-language models and 15 zero-shot CLIP-like models on two datasets from X (formerly Twitter) - a 1,038-image expert-annotated set and a larger corpus of over 1.2 million images, with 50,000 labels manually validated - spanning five annotation dimensions: animal content, climate change consequences, climate action, image setting, and image type. Among the models benchmarked, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite outperforms all others across all super-categories and both datasets, while the gap to open-weight models of moderate size remains relatively small. Beyond instance-level metrics, we advocate for distributional evaluation: VLM predictions can reliably recover population level trends even when per-image accuracy is moderate, making them a viable starting point for discourse analysis at scale. We find that chain-of-thought reasoning reduces rather than improves performance, and that annotation dimension specific prompt design improves performance. We release tweet IDs and labels along with our code at https://github.com/KathPra/Codebooks2VLMs.git.

AI.tldr: Promptable vision-language models enable scalable analysis of climate discourse on social media; Gemini-3.1-flash-lite leads; distributional evaluation preserves population-level signals; chain-of-thought not beneficial; dimension-specific prompts help; data and code released.
AI.motivation: Climate change communication on social media is prolific; manual annotation is infeasible at scale; VLMs can approximate per-image labels and reveal trends at population level; need to evaluate performance and robust evaluation strategies.
AI.method: Benchmark six promptable VLMs and 15 zero-shot CLIP-like models on two datasets from X: 1,038 expert-annotated images and 1.2M images with 50k labels validated; five annotation dimensions; perform prompt design experiments, test chain-of-thought vs direct prompting; perform distributional evaluation and report per-image accuracy and population trends; release data and code.
AI.result: Gemini-3.1-flash-lite outperforms others across super-categories and both datasets; gap to open-weight models of moderate size is relatively small; distributional evaluation recovers population-level trends even with moderate per-image accuracy; chain-of-thought reasoning reduces performance; annotation-dimension-specific prompt design improves performance; provides data/code.
AI.conclusion: Vision-language models can support scalable discourse analysis of climate communication; distributional evaluation is valuable; careful prompt design per annotation dimension boosts performance; resources released to enable replication and further research.

title: A Scale-Adaptive Framework for Joint Spatiotemporal Super-Resolution with Diffusion Models
summary: Deep-learning video super-resolution has progressed rapidly, but climate applications typically super-resolve (increase resolution) either space or time, and joint spatiotemporal models are often designed for a single pair of super-resolution (SR) factors (upscaling spatial and temporal ratio between the low-resolution sequence and the high-resolution sequence), limiting transfer across spatial resolutions and temporal cadences (frame rates). We present a scale-adaptive framework that reuses the same architecture across factors by decomposing spatiotemporal SR into a deterministic prediction of the conditional mean, with attention, and a residual conditional diffusion model, with an optional mass-conservation (same precipitation amount in inputs and outputs) transform to preserve aggregated totals. Assuming that larger SR factors primarily increase underdetermination (hence required context and residual uncertainty) rather than changing the conditional-mean structure, scale adaptivity is achieved by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters before retraining: the diffusion noise schedule amplitude beta (larger for larger factors to increase diversity), the temporal context length L (set to maintain comparable attention horizons across cadences) and optionally a third, the mass-conservation function f (tapered to limit the amplification of extremes for large factors). Demonstrated on reanalysis precipitation over France (Comephore), the same architecture spans super-resolution factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, yielding a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across scales.

AI.tldr: A scale-adaptive framework for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution in climate data that reuses a single architecture across spatial and temporal SR factors by decomposing the problem into a deterministic mean predictor with attention and a residual conditional diffusion model, with an optional mass-conservation transform. Scale adaptivity is achieved by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters (diffusion schedule amplitude beta, temporal context length L, and the mass-conservation function f) before retraining. Demonstrated on France precipitation (Comephore), spanning spatial factors 1–25 and temporal factors 1–6, providing a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for cross-scale SR.
AI.motivation: Climate applications require joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across many scales, but existing deep-learning approaches typically target fixed SR factors and do not readily transfer across different spatial resolutions and frame rates. A unified, scale-adaptive framework would enable reuse of architectures and minimize retraining while preserving important physical quantities in precipitation data.
AI.method: Decompose spatiotemporal SR into a deterministic conditional mean (with attention) and a residual conditional diffusion model. Include an optional mass-conservation transform to preserve total precipitation. Achieve scale adaptivity by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters before retraining: (i) diffusion noise schedule amplitude beta (larger for larger SR factors to promote diversity), (ii) temporal context length L (to maintain comparable attention horizons across cadences), and (iii) the mass-conservation function f (tapered to limit amplification of extremes for large factors). The architecture remains the same across scales; only hyperparameters are adjusted per factor, followed by retraining.
AI.result: On reanalysis precipitation over France (Comephore), the same architecture spans SR factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, demonstrating broad transferability and a practical tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal SR across scales.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, reusable framework for joint spatiotemporal SR in climate data is proposed, enabling cross-scale transfer with minimal architectural changes and explicit hyperparameter retuning guidance; the approach is validated on France precipitation across a wide range of SR factors.

title: SyMTRS: Benchmark Multi-Task Synthetic Dataset for Depth, Domain Adaptation and Super-Resolution in Aerial Imagery
summary: Recent advances in deep learning for remote sensing rely heavily on large annotated datasets, yet acquiring high-quality ground truth for geometric, radiometric, and multi-domain tasks remains costly and often infeasible. In particular, the lack of accurate depth annotations, controlled illumination variations, and multi-scale paired imagery limits progress in monocular depth estimation, domain adaptation, and super-resolution for aerial scenes. We present SyMTRS, a large-scale synthetic dataset generated using a high-fidelity urban simulation pipeline. The dataset provides high-resolution RGB aerial imagery (2048 x 2048), pixel-perfect depth maps, night-time counterparts for domain adaptation, and aligned low-resolution variants for super-resolution at x2, x4, and x8 scales. Unlike existing remote sensing datasets that focus on a single task or modality, SyMTRS is designed as a unified multi-task benchmark enabling joint research in geometric understanding, cross-domain robustness, and resolution enhancement. We describe the dataset generation process, its statistical properties, and its positioning relative to existing benchmarks. SyMTRS aims to bridge critical gaps in remote sensing research by enabling controlled experiments with perfect geometric ground truth and consistent multi-domain supervision. The results obtained in this work can be reproduced from this Github repository: https://github.com/safouaneelg/SyMTRS.

AI.tldr: A synthetic, multi-task remote sensing dataset (SyMTRS) offering high-res RGB, depth maps, night-domain data, and multi-scale low-resolution variants to enable unified benchmarking across geometry, domain adaptation, and super-resolution for aerial scenes.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of accurate ground-truth geometric, radiometric, and multi-domain annotations in real remote sensing data, by providing a controlled, comprehensive synthetic benchmark with perfect geometry and cross-domain supervision.
AI.method: Generate a large-scale, high-fidelity urban scene dataset via an advanced simulation pipeline; provide 2048×2048 RGB images, pixel-perfect depth maps, aligned night-time imagery, and LR variants at x2, x4, and x8; design the dataset as a unified multi-task benchmark and document its generation, statistics, and relation to existing datasets.
AI.result: Introduction of SyMTRS with detailed dataset properties and a positioning relative to existing benchmarks; emphasis on reproducibility and multi-task utility, with a GitHub repository hosting the materials and code.
AI.conclusion: SyMTRS aims to bridge critical gaps in remote sensing research by enabling controlled experiments with perfect ground truth and consistent multi-domain supervision, fostering progress in geometry, domain adaptation, and super-resolution.

title: Low-Rank Adaptation Redux for Large Models
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as the de facto standard for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) of foundation models, enabling the adaptation of billion-parameter networks with minimal computational and memory overhead. Despite its empirical success and rapid proliferation of variants, it remains elusive which architectural choices, optimization techniques, and deployment constraints should guide practical method selection. This overview revisits LoRA through the lens of signal processing (SP), bridging modern adapter designs with classical low-rank modeling tools and inverse problems, as well as highlighting how SP principles can inform principled advances of fine-tuning approaches. Rather than providing a comprehensive enumeration and empirical comparisons of LoRA variants, emphasis is placed on the technical mechanisms underpinning these approaches to justify their effectiveness. These advances are categorized into three complementary axes: architectural design, efficient optimization, and pertinent applications. The first axis builds on singular value decomposition (SVD)-based factorization, rank-augmentation constructions, and cross-layer tensorization, while the second axis deals with initialization, alternating solvers, gauge-invariant optimization, and parameterization-aware methods. Beyond fine-tuning, emerging applications of LoRA are accounted across the entire lifecycle of large models, ranging from pre- and post-training to serving/deployment. Finally, open research directions are outlined at the confluence of SP and deep learning to catalyze a bidirectional frontier: classical SP tools provide a principled vocabulary for designing principled PEFT methods, while the unique challenges facing modern deep learning, especially the overwhelming scale and prohibitive overhead, also offer new research lines benefiting the SP community in return.

AI.tldr: A signal-processing view of LoRA reframes parameter-efficient fine-tuning as a structured low-rank/inverse-problem approach, organized into three guiding axes—architecture, optimization, and deployment—providing principled interpretations and directions rather than exhaustive variant comparisons.
AI.motivation: Despite LoRA’s empirical success, there is limited principled guidance on which architectural choices, optimization strategies, and deployment constraints yield robust, scalable PEFT in practice. A SP-based perspective can unify diverse LoRA variants under classical low-rank and inverse-problem tools.
AI.method: Provide a structured overview that links LoRA variants to SP concepts, categorized into three axes: (1) architectural design (SVD-based factorization, rank augmentation, cross-layer tensorization), (2) efficient optimization (initialization, alternating solvers, gauge-invariant optimization, parameterization-aware methods), and (3) applications across the model lifecycle (pre-training, fine-tuning, serving/deployment). Emphasize the mechanisms behind effectiveness over exhaustive empirical comparison.
AI.result: A synthesized framework that clarifies how LoRA variants map to SP principles, justifies design choices via low-rank and inverse-problem tools, and outlines open SP–DL research directions and principled avenues for advancing PEFT.
AI.conclusion: SP principles can guide principled PEFT development, providing a bidirectional bridge between classical signal processing and modern large-model fine-tuning, and highlighting directions to address scale and overhead in practical deployments.

title: TEMA: Anchor the Image, Follow the Text for Multi-Modification Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is an important image retrieval paradigm that enables users to retrieve a target image using a multimodal query that consists of a reference image and modification text. Although research on CIR has made significant progress, prevailing setups still rely simple modification texts that typically cover only a limited range of salient changes, which induces two limitations highly relevant to practical applications, namely Insufficient Entity Coverage and Clause-Entity Misalignment. In order to address these issues and bring CIR closer to real-world use, we construct two instruction-rich multi-modification datasets, M-FashionIQ and M-CIRR. In addition, we propose TEMA, the Text-oriented Entity Mapping Architecture, which is the first CIR framework designed for multi-modification while also accommodating simple modifications. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that TEMA's superiority in both original and multi-modification scenarios, while maintaining an optimal balance between retrieval accuracy and computational efficiency. Our codes and constructed multi-modification dataset (M-FashionIQ and M-CIRR) are available at https://github.com/lee-zixu/ACL26-TEMA/.

AI.tldr: We propose TEMA, a Text-oriented Entity Mapping Architecture for multi-modification Composed Image Retrieval, together with two instruction-rich datasets (M-FashionIQ, M-CIRR); it achieves state-of-the-art results and efficient retrieval across four benchmarks, with code and data released.
AI.motivation: Current CIR systems rely on simple modification texts that cover a limited set of changes, leading to Insufficient Entity Coverage and Clause-Entity Misalignment. There is a need for instruction-rich, multi-modification queries and robust entity-text mapping to better reflect real-world usage.
AI.method: Introduce TEMA, a Text-oriented Entity Mapping Architecture designed for multi-modification CIR while also handling simple modifications. Construct two instruction-rich multi-modification datasets, M-FashionIQ and M-CIRR. Evaluate on four benchmark datasets comparing to baselines to demonstrate improved accuracy and efficiency.
AI.result: TEMA achieves superior performance in both original and multi-modification scenarios across four benchmarks, with a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off. The proposed datasets effectively address Insufficient Entity Coverage and Clause-Entity Misalignment, and code/datasets are released.
AI.conclusion: TEMA is the first CIR framework explicitly built for multi-modification (while accommodating simple edits), supported by two new datasets. It advances practical CIR and provides resources for broader adoption; future work may explore further expansion of modification types and scalability.

title: The Sample Complexity of Multicalibration
summary:
We study the minimax sample complexity of multicalibration in the batch setting. A learner observes $n$ i.i.d. samples from an unknown distribution and must output a (possibly randomized) predictor whose population multicalibration error, measured by Expected Calibration Error (ECE), is at most $\varepsilon$ with respect to a given family of groups. For every fixed $κ> 0$, in the regime $|G|\le \varepsilon^{-κ}$, we prove that $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-3})$ samples are necessary and sufficient, up to polylogarithmic factors. The lower bound holds even for randomized predictors, and the upper bound is realized by a randomized predictor obtained via an online-to-batch reduction. This separates the sample complexity of multicalibration from that of marginal calibration, which scales as $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-2})$, and shows that mean-ECE multicalibration is as difficult in the batch setting as it is in the online setting, in contrast to marginal calibration which is strictly more difficult in the online setting. In contrast we observe that for $κ= 0$, the sample complexity of multicalibration remains $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-2})$ exhibiting a sharp threshold phenomenon.
  More generally, we establish matching upper and lower bounds, up to polylogarithmic factors, for a weighted $L_p$ multicalibration metric for all $1 \le p \le 2$, with optimal exponent $3/p$. We also extend the lower-bound template to a regular class of elicitable properties, and combine it with the online upper bounds of Hu et al. (2025) to obtain matching bounds for calibrating properties including expectiles and bounded-density quantiles.

AI.tldr: In batch multicalibration, minimax sample complexity is Θ~(ε^{-3}) for |G| ≤ ε^{-κ} with κ>0; a κ=0 threshold yields Θ~(ε^{-2}). This matches online complexity for mean-ECE multicalibration and separates it from marginal calibration (ε^{-2}). Extends to weighted L_p metrics with optimal exponent 3/p (1≤p≤2) and to elicitable properties such as expectiles and bounded-density quantiles via a lower-bound template and online upper bounds.
AI.motivation: Resolve the batch-sample complexity of multicalibration with respect to a family of groups, compare to marginal calibration and online results, and generalize to weighted L_p metrics and elicitable properties.
AI.method: Minimax lower bounds for randomized predictors; online-to-batch reduction to achieve matching upper bounds; use of existing online bounds (Hu et al. 2025) for calibration; generalization to weighted L_p multicalibration; extension of lower-bound templates to elicitable properties.
AI.result: For fixed κ>0 and |G| ≤ ε^{-κ}, the minimax sample complexity is Θ~(ε^{-3}). The lower bound holds for randomized predictors; upper bound obtained via online-to-batch reduction. There is a threshold at κ=0 where complexity becomes Θ~(ε^{-2}). For weighted L_p (1≤p≤2), optimal exponent is 3/p. The lower-bound template extends to elicitable properties, enabling matching bounds for expectiles and bounded-density quantiles.
AI.conclusion: Shows that mean-ECE multicalibration in batch is as hard as in the online setting, unlike marginal calibration. Provides a unified view across η-L_p metrics and elicitable properties, with clear regime-based dependencies on group size and ε.

title: Multiscale Super Resolution without Image Priors
summary: We address the ambiguities in the super-resolution problem under translation. We demonstrate that combinations of low-resolution images at different scales can be used to make the super-resolution problem well posed. Such differences in scale can be achieved using sensors with different pixel sizes (as demonstrated here) or by varying the effective pixel size through changes in optical magnification (e.g., using a zoom lens). We show that images acquired with pairwise coprime pixel sizes lead to a system with a stable inverse, and furthermore, that super-resolution images can be reconstructed efficiently using Fourier domain techniques or iterative least squares methods. Our mathematical analysis provides an expression for the expected error of the least squares reconstruction for large signals assuming i.i.d. noise that elucidates the noise-resolution tradeoff. These results are validated through both one- and two-dimensional experiments that leverage charge-coupled device (CCD) hardware binning to explore reconstructions over a large range of effective pixel sizes. Finally, two-dimensional reconstructions for a series of targets are used to demonstrate the advantages of multiscale super-resolution, and implications of these results for common imaging systems are discussed.

AI.tldr: Multiscale super-resolution using measurements at coprime pixel sizes yields a well-posed inverse problem, enabling fast Fourier/least-squares reconstructions and a quantifiable noise–resolution tradeoff; validated experimentally with 1D/2D targets via CCD binning.
AI.motivation: SR under pure translation is ill-posed; introducing scale diversity (via different pixel sizes or magnification) provides independent information that stabilizes the inverse problem.
AI.method: Acquire multiple low-resolution images at different scales (coprime pixel sizes). Analyze via Fourier-domain techniques or iterative least-squares; prove stability of the inverse for pairwise coprime scales; derive an explicit error expression for LS reconstruction under large-signal, i.i.d. noise; validate with 1D/2D CCD-binning experiments across a wide range of effective pixel sizes.
AI.result: The combination of scale-diverse measurements yields a well-posed system with stable inversion. Efficient reconstructions are achievable through Fourier-domain methods or LS. Theoretical LS error bounds match empirical performance, demonstrating a favorable noise–resolution tradeoff. 1D and 2D experiments confirm multiscale SR advantages across many pixel-sizes, with 2D reconstructions illustrating practical gains for imaging targets.
AI.conclusion: Multiscale, coprime-pixel SR resolves translation ambiguities, enabling stable, efficient reconstructions and offering practical guidance for imaging system design to exploit scale diversity.

title: Fine-Tuning Regimes Define Distinct Continual Learning Problems
summary: Continual learning (CL) studies how models acquire tasks sequentially while retaining previously learned knowledge. Despite substantial progress in benchmarking CL methods, comparative evaluations typically keep the fine-tuning regime fixed. In this paper, we argue that the fine-tuning regime, defined by the trainable parameter subspace, is itself a key evaluation variable. We formalize adaptation regimes as projected optimization over fixed trainable subspaces, showing that changing the trainable depth alters the effective update signal through which both current task fitting and knowledge preservation operate. This analysis motivates the hypothesis that method comparisons need not be invariant across regimes. We test this hypothesis in task incremental CL, five trainable depth regimes, and four standard methods: online EWC, LwF, SI, and GEM. Across five benchmark datasets, namely MNIST, Fashion MNIST, KMNIST, QMNIST, and CIFAR-100, and across 11 task orders per dataset, we find that the relative ranking of methods is not consistently preserved across regimes. We further show that deeper adaptation regimes are associated with larger update magnitudes, higher forgetting, and a stronger relationship between the two. These results show that comparative conclusions in CL can depend strongly on the chosen fine-tuning regime, motivating regime-aware evaluation protocols that treat trainable depth as an explicit experimental factor.

AI.tldr: Adaptation regimes defined by trainable depth act as a key experimental variable in continual learning; method rankings vary with regime, and deeper adaptation increases update magnitudes and forgetting, warranting regime-aware evaluations.
AI.motivation: To reveal that fixed fine-tuning regimes bias continual-learning comparisons; to formalize adaptation regimes and quantify their impact on learning dynamics and forgetting.
AI.method: Formally model adaptation regimes as projected optimization over fixed trainable subspaces (trainable depth). Analyze how changing subspace affects update signals for current task fitting vs. knowledge preservation. Empirically evaluate five trainable-depth regimes across five datasets and 11 task orders using four standard CL methods (online EWC, LwF, SI, GEM).
AI.result: Method rankings are not consistent across adaptation regimes; deeper adaptation correlates with larger update magnitudes and greater forgetting; stronger link between update magnitude and forgetting observed. This demonstrates regime-dependent conclusions and motivates regime-aware evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Trainable-depth (adaptation regime) is a crucial evaluation factor in continual learning; evaluations should explicitly test multiple regimes to avoid biased conclusions and to fairly compare methods.

title: Divide-then-Diagnose: Weaving Clinician-Inspired Contexts for Ultra-Long Capsule Endoscopy Videos
summary: Capsule endoscopy (CE) enables non-invasive gastrointestinal screening, but current CE research remains largely limited to frame-level classification and detection, leaving video-level analysis underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce and formally define a new task, diagnosis-driven CE video summarization, which requires extracting key evidence frames that covers clinically meaningful findings and making accurate diagnoses from those evidence frames. This setting is challenging because diagnostically relevant events are extremely sparse and can be overwhelmed by tens of thousands of redundant normal frames, while individual observations are often ambiguous due to motion blur, debris, specular highlights, and rapid viewpoint changes. To facilitate research in this direction, we introduce VideoCAP, the first CE dataset with diagnosis-driven annotations derived from real clinical reports. VideoCAP comprises 240 full-length videos and provides realistic supervision for both key evidence frame extraction and diagnosis. To address this task, we further propose DiCE, a clinician-inspired framework that mirrors the standard CE reading workflow. DiCE first performs efficient candidate screening over the raw video, then uses a Context Weaver to organize candidates into coherent diagnostic contexts that preserve distinct lesion events, and an Evidence Converger to aggregate multi-frame evidence within each context into robust clip-level judgments. Experiments show that DiCE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, producing concise and clinically reliable diagnostic summaries. These results highlight diagnosis-driven contextual reasoning as a promising paradigm for ultra-long CE video summarization.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
