AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: Temporal Taskification in Streaming Continual Learning: A Source of Evaluation Instability
summary: Streaming Continual Learning (CL) typically converts a continuous stream into a sequence of discrete tasks through temporal partitioning. We argue that this temporal taskification step is not a neutral preprocessing choice, but a structural component of evaluation: different valid splits of the same stream can induce different CL regimes and therefore different benchmark conclusions. To study this effect, we introduce a taskification-level framework based on plasticity and stability profiles, a profile distance between taskifications, and Boundary-Profile Sensitivity (BPS), which diagnoses how strongly small boundary perturbations alter the induced regime before any CL model is trained. We evaluate continual finetuning, Experience Replay, Elastic Weight Consolidation, and Learning without Forgetting on network traffic forecasting with CESNET-Timeseries24, keeping the stream, model, and training budget fixed while varying only the temporal taskification. Across 9-, 30-, and 44-day splits, we observe substantial changes in forecasting error, forgetting, and backward transfer, showing that taskification alone can materially affect CL evaluation. We further find that shorter taskifications induce noisier distribution-level patterns, larger structural distances, and higher BPS, indicating greater sensitivity to boundary perturbations. These results show that benchmark conclusions in streaming CL depend not only on the learner and the data stream, but also on how that stream is taskified, motivating temporal taskification as a first-class evaluation variable.

AI.tldr: Taskification level of streaming data materially affects continual learning evaluation; temporal partitioning is an evaluation variable.
AI.motivation: Demonstrate that how a data stream is split into tasks (taskification) is not neutral and can change CL regimes and benchmark conclusions; introduce a framework to quantify this effect.
AI.method: Propose a taskification-level framework using plasticity and stability profiles, a profile distance between taskifications, and Boundary-Profile Sensitivity (BPS). Evaluate continual finetuning, Experience Replay, Elastic Weight Consolidation, and Learning without Forgetting on network traffic forecasting with CESNET-Timeseries24, keeping stream, model, and training budget fixed while varying only the temporal taskification (9-, 30-, 44-day splits).
AI.result: Taskification alone produced substantial changes in forecasting error, forgetting, and backward transfer across splits. Shorter taskifications yielded noisier distribution-level patterns, larger structural distances between taskifications, and higher BPS, indicating greater sensitivity to boundary perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark conclusions in streaming CL depend on the learner, data stream, and also on how the stream is taskified; temporal taskification should be treated as a first-class evaluation variable in evaluating streaming CL.

title: Grounding Video Reasoning in Physical Signals
comment: Benchmark for Grounding Video Reasoning in Physical Signals
summary: Physical video understanding requires more than naming an event correctly. A model can answer a question about pouring, sliding, or collision from textual regularities while still failing to localize the event in time or space. We introduce a grounded benchmark for physical video understanding that extends the what--when--where evaluation structure of V-STaR to four video sources, six physics domains, three prompt families (physics, vstar_like, and neutral_rstr), and four input conditions (original, shuffled, ablated, and frame-masked). The benchmark contains 1,560 base video clips from SSV2, YouCook2, HoloAssist, and Roundabout-TAU. Each clip is first converted into a shared grounded event record, and the three query families are derived from that record. Temporal and spatial targets are shared across prompt families, while the non-physics families use deterministic family-appropriate semantic a_what targets derived from the same record. Across models and prompt families, physics remains the strongest regime overall, vstar_like is the clearest non-physics semantic comparison, and neutral_rstr behaves as a harder templated control. Prompt-family robustness is selective rather than universal, perturbation gains cluster in weak original cases, and spatial grounding is the weakest across settings. These results suggest that video Q&A reasoning benchmarks shall report physically grounded, prompt-aware, and perturbation-aware diagnostics alongside aggregate accuracy.

AI.tldr: Grounded, multi-source benchmark for physical video understanding; extends V-STaR with 4 sources, 6 physics domains, 3 prompt families, and 4 input conditions; shows physics is the strongest regime, non-physics semantics vary by prompt family, and ground truth grounding (spatial) is the weak point; advocates reporting grounded, prompt-aware, and perturbation-aware diagnostics alongside accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current video QA benchmarks largely assess coarse event recognition (what) across limited settings and do not robustly test temporal/spatial grounding, prompt sensitivity, or perturbation robustness across diverse data sources. This work targets physically grounded video reasoning across multiple domains and prompt styles to push for more diagnostic evaluation.
AI.method: Construct a grounded benchmark for physical video understanding by extending the what–when–where structure of V-STaR to four video sources (SSV2, YouCook2, HoloAssist, Roundabout-TAU), six physics domains, three prompt families (physics, vstar_like, neutral_rstr), and four input conditions (original, shuffled, ablated, frame-masked). Each clip (1,560 base videos) is converted to a shared grounded event record; three query families are derived; temporal/spatial targets are shared across families while non-physics targets are deterministic a_what derived from the same record. Evaluation across models and prompts reveals patterns in robustness and grounding performance.
AI.result: Physics remains the strongest regime across settings; vstar_like yields the clearest non-physics semantic baseline; neutral_rstr provides a harder, templated control. robustness of prompt families is selective; gains from perturbations concentrate in weaker original cases; spatial grounding underperforms relative to temporal grounding and other targets.
AI.conclusion: To advance video Q&A benchmarking, researchers should report physically grounded, prompt-aware, and perturbation-aware diagnostics in addition to aggregate accuracy to properly assess model capabilities in temporal/spatial grounding and reasoning.

title: Handbook of Rough Set Extensions and Uncertainty Models
comment: 159 pages. Peer-Reviewed Book. ISBN: 978-1-59973-867-3. Publisher: Neutrosophic Science International Association (NSIA) Publishing House
summary: Rough set theory models uncertainty by approximating target concepts through lower and upper sets induced by indiscernibility, or more generally, by granulation relations in data tables. This perspective captures vagueness caused by limited observational resolution and supports set-theoretic reasoning about what can be determined with certainty and what remains only possible. This book is written as a map of models. Rather than developing a single algorithmic pipeline in depth, it provides a systematic survey of the main rough set paradigms and their extension routes. More specifically, representative variants are organized according to (i) the underlying granulation mechanism, such as equivalence-based, tolerance-based, covering-based, neighborhood-based, and probabilistic approximations, and (ii) the uncertainty semantics attached to data and relations, such as crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, and plithogenic settings. The book also explains how each choice changes the form of approximations and the interpretation of boundary regions. Throughout the book, small illustrative examples are used to clarify modeling intent and typical use cases in classification and decision support. Finally, an important clarification of scope should be noted. Since the main purpose of this book is to provide a map of models, the Abstract and Introduction should not lead readers to expect that feature reduction and rule induction are primary objectives. Although these topics are central in the rough set literature, they are treated here mainly as motivating applications and as entry points to the broader research landscape. The principal aim of the book is to survey and position rough set models and their extensions in a systematic and coherent manner.

AI.tldr: A systematic map of rough set models and extensions, organized by granulation mechanisms and uncertainty semantics, clarifying how choices affect approximations and boundary regions, with emphasis on survey over algorithmic pipelines.
AI.motivation: Provide a coherent, comprehensive survey and positioning of rough set models and their extensions to enable comparison, integration, and guidance for classification and decision support, rather than focusing on feature reduction or rule induction.
AI.method: Taxonomic survey across granulation mechanisms (equivalence-based, tolerance-based, covering-based, neighborhood-based, probabilistic) and uncertainty semantics (crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, plithogenic). Includes explanation of how these choices modify approximations and boundary interpretations, supplemented by illustrative examples. Treats feature reduction and rule induction as motivating applications rather than primary objectives.
AI.result: A structured map of rough set models and extensions, with a coherent framework that facilitates cross-model comparison, synthesis, and identification of research gaps and future directions.
AI.conclusion: Positions rough set models within a systematic, coherent landscape, offering a reference framework for researchers rather than an algorithm-centric contribution; emphasizes interpretation and scope clarification to guide future work.

title: Addressing Image Authenticity When Cameras Use Generative AI
comment: To appear in CVPR 2026 Workshop on Authenticity and Provenance in the Age of Generative AI
summary: The ability of generative AI (GenAI) methods to photorealistically alter camera images has raised awareness about the authenticity of images shared online. Interestingly, images captured directly by our cameras are considered authentic and faithful. However, with the increasing integration of deep-learning modules into cameras' capture-time hardware -- namely, the image signal processor (ISP) -- there is now a potential for hallucinated content in images directly output by our cameras. Hallucinated capture-time image content is typically benign, such as enhanced edges or texture, but in certain operations, such as AI-based digital zoom or low-light image enhancement, hallucinations can potentially alter the semantics and interpretation of the image content. As a result, users may not realize that the content in their camera images is not authentic. This paper addresses this issue by enabling users to recover the 'unhallucinated' version of the camera image to avoid misinterpretation of the image content. Our approach works by optimizing an image-specific multi-layer perceptron (MLP) decoder together with a modality-specific encoder so that, given the camera image, we can recover the image before hallucinated content was added. The encoder and MLP are self-contained and can be applied post-capture to the image without requiring access to the camera ISP. Moreover, the encoder and MLP decoder require only 180 KB of storage and can be readily saved as metadata within standard image formats such as JPEG and HEIC.

AI.tldr: Proposes a post-capture method to recover unhallucinated content from camera images by using a per-image, encoder-MLP decoder pair that can be stored as metadata (≈180 KB) and does not require access to the camera ISP.
AI.motivation: Address the risk that capture-time hallucinations from ISP processing can alter semantics in photos, potentially misleading users about authenticity; provide a way to retrieve the original, authentic-looking content.
AI.method: Train/optimize an image-specific encoder and a lightweight, per-image MLP decoder to map a potentially hallucinated camera image back to its unhallucinated version. The system is self-contained, operates post-capture, and can be embedded as metadata within JPEG/HEIC (~180 KB), requiring no access to the camera ISP at deployment.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and a practical deployment pipeline for recovering authentic-looking images. The approach yields a compact, post-capture solution that can be applied without ISP access and stores its model as metadata.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a feasible pathway to mitigate misinterpretation of camera content by enabling users to view or recover the pre-hallucination image, leveraging a small, image-specific encoder/decoder pair stored with the image.

title: UniGenDet: A Unified Generative-Discriminative Framework for Co-Evolutionary Image Generation and Generated Image Detection
summary: In recent years, significant progress has been made in both image generation and generated image detection. Despite their rapid, yet largely independent, development, these two fields have evolved distinct architectural paradigms: the former predominantly relies on generative networks, while the latter favors discriminative frameworks. A recent trend in both domains is the use of adversarial information to enhance performance, revealing potential for synergy. However, the significant architectural divergence between them presents considerable challenges. Departing from previous approaches, we propose UniGenDet: a Unified generative-discriminative framework for co-evolutionary image Generation and generated image Detection. To bridge the task gap, we design a symbiotic multimodal self-attention mechanism and a unified fine-tuning algorithm. This synergy allows the generation task to improve the interpretability of authenticity identification, while authenticity criteria guide the creation of higher-fidelity images. Furthermore, we introduce a detector-informed generative alignment mechanism to facilitate seamless information exchange. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code: \href{https://github.com/Zhangyr2022/UniGenDet}{https://github.com/Zhangyr2022/UniGenDet}.

AI.tldr: A unified generative-discriminative framework (UniGenDet) for co-evolving image generation and detection, using symbiotic multimodal self-attention, unified fine-tuning, and detector-informed alignment to achieve state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Generative and discriminative image tasks have evolved under different architectures, hindering cross-task synergy; adversarial information usage suggests potential gains from a unified approach.
AI.method: Proposes UniGenDet with a symbiotic multimodal self-attention mechanism, a unified fine-tuning algorithm, and a detector-informed generative alignment mechanism to enable cross-task information exchange between generation and detection.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple datasets show state-of-the-art performance; code released.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework can enhance authenticity detection and generation fidelity through cross-task guidance, with interpretability improved for detection and higher fidelity images produced.

title: Directional Confusions Reveal Divergent Inductive Biases Through Rate-Distortion Geometry in Human and Machine Vision
summary: Humans and modern vision models can reach similar classification accuracy while making systematically different kinds of mistakes - differing not in how often they err, but in who gets mistaken for whom, and in which direction. We show that these directional confusions reveal distinct inductive biases that are invisible to accuracy alone. Using matched human and deep vision model responses on a natural-image categorization task under 12 perturbation types, we quantify asymmetry in confusion matrices and link it to generalization geometry through a Rate-Distortion (RD) framework, summarized by three geometric signatures (slope (beta), curvature (kappa)) and efficiency (AUC). We find that humans exhibit broad but weak asymmetries, whereas deep vision models show sparser, stronger directional collapses. Robustness training reduces global asymmetry but fails to recover the human-like breadth-strength profile of graded similarity. Mechanistic simulations further show that different asymmetry organizations shift the RD frontier in opposite directions, even when matched for performance. Together, these results position directional confusions and RD geometry as compact, interpretable signatures of inductive bias under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Humans and deep vision models share similar accuracy but differ in error structure; directional confusions reveal inductive biases invisible to accuracy alone; analyzing asymmetry in confusion matrices with a rate-distortion (RD) framework yields interpretable geometric signatures (beta, kappa, AUC) that distinguish humans from models and reveal effects of robustness training.
AI.motivation: To move beyond accuracy as the sole metric for classification, by examining the directional structure of mistakes under distribution shift to infer inductive biases of humans vs. machines.
AI.method: Collect matched human and deep vision model responses on a natural-image categorization task under 12 perturbations. Compute confusion-matrix asymmetry (directional confusions) and map to a rate-distortion (RD) framework. Extract geometric signatures: slope (beta), curvature (kappa), and efficiency (AUC). Compare humans vs. deep models; assess impact of robustness training; run mechanistic simulations to see how asymmetry arrangements shift the RD frontier while keeping performance fixed.
AI.result: Humans show broad but weak directional asymmetries in confusions; deep vision models exhibit sparse, stronger directional collapses. Robustness training reduces global asymmetry but does not recover the human-like breadth-strength profile of graded similarity. Mechanistic simulations indicate that different asymmetry organizations shift the RD frontier in opposite directions, even when performance is matched. These outcomes demonstrate that directional confusions and RD geometry serve as compact, interpretable signatures of inductive bias under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Directional confusions coupled with RD-geometry metrics provide concise, interpretable signatures of inductive bias under distribution shift, enabling comparison across humans and models and guiding the development of training regimes to shape inductive biases.

title: When Prompts Override Vision: Prompt-Induced Hallucinations in LVLMs
summary: Despite impressive progress in capabilities of large vision-language models (LVLMs), these systems remain vulnerable to hallucinations, i.e., outputs that are not grounded in the visual input. Prior work has attributed hallucinations in LVLMs to factors such as limitations of the vision backbone or the dominance of the language component, yet the relative importance of these factors remains unclear. To resolve this ambiguity, We propose HalluScope, a benchmark to better understand the extent to which different factors induce hallucinations. Our analysis indicates that hallucinations largely stem from excessive reliance on textual priors and background knowledge, especially information introduced through textual instructions. To mitigate hallucinations induced by textual instruction priors, we propose HalluVL-DPO, a framework for fine-tuning off-the-shelf LVLMs towards more visually grounded responses. HalluVL-DPO leverages preference optimization using a curated training dataset that we construct, guiding the model to prefer grounded responses over hallucinated ones. We demonstrate that our optimized model effectively mitigates the targeted hallucination failure mode, while preserving or improving performance on other hallucination benchmarks and visual capability evaluations. To support reproducibility and further research, we will publicly release our evaluation benchmark, preference training dataset, and code at https://pegah-kh.github.io/projects/prompts-override-vision/ .

AI.tldr: HalluScope identifies textual priors/instruction priors as the main source of LVLM hallucinations; introduces HalluVL-DPO to ground responses via preference optimization; reports improved grounding with maintained/performance on other metrics; will release benchmarks and data for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Unclear which factors (vision backbone vs language priors) drive LVLM hallucinations; quantify and mitigate to improve reliability.
AI.method: Develop HalluScope benchmark to diagnose sources of hallucinations; propose HalluVL-DPO fine-tuning with preference optimization on a curated dataset to encourage grounded responses.
AI.result: Findings indicate hallucinations largely stem from textual priors and instruction priors; HalluVL-DPO reduces targeted hallucinations and preserves or improves performance on other benchmarks and visual tasks; resources to be released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Textual priors dominate LVLM hallucinations; targeted, preference-based fine-tuning can substantially mitigate these errors while preserving overall performance; promotes reproducibility through released datasets and code.

title: Vista4D: Video Reshooting with 4D Point Clouds
comment: 24 pages, 20 figures, CVPR 2026, see project page at https://eyeline-labs.github.io/Vista4D
summary: We present Vista4D, a robust and flexible video reshooting framework that grounds the input video and target cameras in a 4D point cloud. Specifically, given an input video, our method re-synthesizes the scene with the same dynamics from a different camera trajectory and viewpoint. Existing video reshooting methods often struggle with depth estimation artifacts of real-world dynamic videos, while also failing to preserve content appearance and failing to maintain precise camera control for challenging new trajectories. We build a 4D-grounded point cloud representation with static pixel segmentation and 4D reconstruction to explicitly preserve seen content and provide rich camera signals, and we train with reconstructed multiview dynamic data for robustness against point cloud artifacts during real-world inference. Our results demonstrate improved 4D consistency, camera control, and visual quality compared to state-of-the-art baselines under a variety of videos and camera paths. Moreover, our method generalizes to real-world applications such as dynamic scene expansion and 4D scene recomposition. See our project page for results, code, and models: https://eyeline-labs.github.io/Vista4D

AI.tldr: Vista4D: A 4D-grounded video reshooting framework that uses a 4D point cloud with static pixel segmentation to re-synthesize a scene from a different camera trajectory while preserving content and dynamics.
AI.motivation: To overcome depth estimation artifacts and content degradation in dynamic video reshooting, and to enable precise control of new camera trajectories while preserving seen content.
AI.method: Construct a 4D-grounded point cloud representation with static pixel segmentation and 4D reconstruction. Ground the input video and target camera poses, train with reconstructed multiview dynamic data to mitigate point-cloud artifacts, and re-synthesize the scene from a new trajectory and viewpoint while maintaining dynamics.
AI.result: Improved 4D consistency, camera control, and visual quality compared to state-of-the-art baselines across diverse videos and camera paths; demonstrated generalization to dynamic scene expansion and 4D scene recomposition.
AI.conclusion: Vista4D provides a robust, flexible framework for video reshooting grounded in 4D geometry, achieving better consistency and visual fidelity and enabling real-world applications such as dynamic scene expansion.

title: Context Unrolling in Omni Models
summary: We present Omni, a unified multimodal model natively trained on diverse modalities, including text, images, videos, 3D geometry, and hidden representations. We find that such training enables Context Unrolling, where the model explicitly reasons across multiple modal representations before producing predictions. This process enables the model to aggregate complementary information across heterogeneous modalities, facilitating a more faithful approximation of the shared multimodal knowledge manifold and improving downstream reasoning fidelity. As a result, Omni achieves strong performance on both multimodal generation and understanding benchmarks, while demonstrating advanced multimodal reasoning capabilities, including in-context generation of text, image, video, and 3D geometry.

AI.tldr: Omni is a unified multimodal model trained across text, images, video, 3D geometry, and hidden representations, enabling Context Unrolling to reason across modalities before predicting, leading to superior multimodal generation and understanding.
AI.motivation: Address the fragmentation of AI systems across modalities by learning a single model that shares representations across text, visual, audio/video, 3D geometry, and hidden representations, to improve reasoning fidelity and data efficiency.
AI.method: Train a single model natively on diverse modalities and introduce Context Unrolling: explicit, stepwise reasoning across multiple modal representations prior to prediction, enabling aggregation of complementary information from heterogeneous modalities and alignment with a shared multimodal knowledge manifold.
AI.result: Omni achieves strong performance on multimodal generation and understanding benchmarks and exhibits advanced multimodal reasoning capabilities, including in-context generation of text, image, video, and 3D geometry.
AI.conclusion: Unified, cross-modality training with Context Unrolling can improve reasoning fidelity and generation across modalities, suggesting a scalable path toward broadly capable multimodal AI.

title: Seeing Without Eyes: 4D Human-Scene Understanding from Wearable IMUs
summary: Understanding human activities and their surrounding environments typically relies on visual perception, yet cameras pose persistent challenges in privacy, safety, energy efficiency, and scalability. We explore an alternative: 4D perception without vision. Its goal is to reconstruct human motion and 3D scene layouts purely from everyday wearable sensors. For this we introduce IMU-to-4D, a framework that repurposes large language models for non-visual spatiotemporal understanding of human-scene dynamics. IMU-to-4D uses data from a few inertial sensors from earbuds, watches, or smartphones and predicts detailed 4D human motion together with coarse scene structure. Experiments across diverse human-scene datasets show that IMU-to-4D yields more coherent and temporally stable results than SoTA cascaded pipelines, suggesting wearable motion sensors alone can support rich 4D understanding.

AI.tldr: A framework that uses inertial sensors to achieve 4D perception (4D human motion + coarse scene structure) by repurposing large language models, offering a privacy-preserving alternative to vision-based methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome privacy, safety, energy, and scalability challenges of camera-based perception by enabling 4D understanding from wearable sensors.
AI.method: Collect inertial data from earbuds, watches, or smartphones and feed it into a repurposed large language model to predict detailed 4D human motion and coarse scene layout, achieving coherence and temporal stability that surpasses state-of-the-art cascaded pipelines across diverse datasets.
AI.result: IMU-to-4D produces more coherent and temporally stable 4D reconstructions than competing pipelines, demonstrating that wearable motion data can support rich 4D understanding without visual input.
AI.conclusion: Wearable inertial sensors, when combined with LLM-based reasoning, can enable viable 4D perception with privacy advantages, suggesting a new direction for non-visual human-scene understanding.

title: Seeing Fast and Slow: Learning the Flow of Time in Videos
summary: How can we tell whether a video has been sped up or slowed down? How can we generate videos at different speeds? Although videos have been central to modern computer vision research, little attention has been paid to perceiving and controlling the passage of time. In this paper, we study time as a learnable visual concept and develop models for reasoning about and manipulating the flow of time in videos. We first exploit the multimodal cues and temporal structure naturally present in videos to learn, in a self-supervised manner, to detect speed changes and estimate playback speed. We then show that these learned temporal reasoning models enable us to curate the largest slow-motion video dataset to date from noisy in-the-wild sources. Such slow-motion footage, typically filmed by high-speed cameras, contains substantially richer temporal detail than standard videos. Using this data, we further develop models capable of temporal control, including speed-conditioned video generation, which produces motion at specified playback speed, and temporal super-resolution, which tranforms low-FPS, blurry videos into high-FPS sequences with fine-grained temporal details. Our findings highlight time as a manipulable, perceptual dimension in video learning, opening doors to temporally controllable video generation, temporal forensics detection, and potentially richer world-models that understand how events unfold over time.

AI.tldr: Treat time as a learnable visual concept to detect speed changes, estimate playback speed, and control tempo in video generation and temporal super-resolution, enabling a large slow-motion dataset from in-the-wild sources.
AI.motivation: Time perception in videos is underexplored. Exploiting temporal cues to detect speed changes and manipulate playback speed could enable temporally controllable generation, better forensics, and richer temporal world-models.
AI.method: Self-supervised learning using multimodal cues and intrinsic temporal structure to detect speed changes and estimate playback speed; curate a large slow-motion video dataset from noisy in-the-wild sources; develop models for speed-conditioned video generation and temporal super-resolution.
AI.result: Demonstrates temporal reasoning models that detect speed changes, estimate playback speed, and enable speed-conditioned generation and temporal super-resolution; builds the largest slow-motion dataset from in-the-wild footage, enhancing temporal detail.
AI.conclusion: Time is a manipulable perceptual dimension in video learning, unlocking temporally controllable generation, temporal forensics, and richer world-models that capture event progression over time.
